topics.title: Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Microcontroller — Formal Definition

A **Microcontroller** is a single-chip, self-contained microcomputer that integrates a **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Read-Only Memory (ROM)**, **Random-Access Memory (RAM)**, **Input/Output (I/O) ports**, **Timers/Counters**, and **serial communication peripherals** onto a single integrated circuit (IC). Unlike a general-purpose microprocessor, which requires external memory and peripherals, a microcontroller is purpose-built for **embedded control applications**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PCBMT402):**
> "A microcontroller is a complete computer system on a chip, optimized for real-time control of embedded systems, integrating CPU, memory, and I/O on a single VLSI die."

## 1.2 The 8051 Microcontroller — Overview

The **Intel 8051** (and its modern derivatives such as AT89C51, P89V51RD2) is an **8-bit Harvard architecture** single-chip microcontroller introduced by Intel in 1980. It remains the de-facto teaching platform in Indian engineering curricula, including KTU.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Specifications of the Standard 8051**
> - **CPU**: 8-bit ALU
> - **On-chip ROM (Program Memory)**: **4 KB**
> - **On-chip RAM (Data Memory)**: **128 bytes**
> - **I/O Lines**: **32** (arranged as four 8-bit ports — P0, P1, P2, P3)
> - **Timers/Counters**: Two 16-bit (T0, T1)
> - **Serial Port**: One full-duplex UART
> - **Interrupt Sources**: **5** (2 external, 2 timer, 1 serial)
> - **External Memory Expandability**: **64 KB** code + **64 KB** data
> - **Clock**: **12 MHz** crystal typical; machine cycle = **12 oscillator periods**

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Toaster" View

Imagine a **toaster with a tiny in-built brain** that watches the bread, decides when to stop heating, and signals "DONE" via a buzzer — all without needing an external laptop. That integrated "tiny brain" is a microcontroller.

| Analogy Element | Real Microcontroller Part |
|---|---|
| Brain that decides | CPU (ALU + Control Unit) |
| Recipe book (permanent) | ROM/Flash (Program Memory) |
| Mixing bowl (temporary) | RAM (Data Memory) |
| Buttons & dials | I/O Ports (P0–P3) |
| Built-in kitchen timer | Timers/Counters (T0, T1) |
| Walkie-talkie to other devices | Serial Port (UART) |

> [!TIP]
> A **microprocessor** is like a powerful desktop CPU that needs you to plug in RAM, a hard disk, and a graphics card separately. A **microcontroller** is like a microwave oven — everything is already inside, and the chip is dedicated to one specific job (heating).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of the 8051

The 8051 follows the **Harvard Architecture** — meaning it has **separate physical buses and separate memory spaces** for *code* (program) and *data*. This allows simultaneous fetch of instruction and operand, doubling throughput at the same clock rate.

### 2.1.1 Major Functional Blocks

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**
   - **8-bit ALU** — performs arithmetic (`ADD`, `SUBB`, `MUL`, `DIV`), logic (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`), rotate, and bit operations.
   - **Control Unit (CU)** — fetches, decodes, and orchestrates instruction execution.
   - **Register banks** — Four banks (Bank 0–3), each with 8 registers `R0`–`R7`; default is **Bank 0**.

2. **Program Counter (PC)**
   - **16-bit register** → can address **65,536 bytes (64 KB)** of code memory.
   - Always points to the *next* instruction to be fetched.
   - **Reset value**: `0000_{H}` (first instruction of program).

3. **Stack Pointer (SP)**
   - **8-bit register** pointing to the *top* of the stack in on-chip RAM.
   - **Reset value**: `07_{H}` (stack begins at internal RAM `08_{H}`).
   - Stack **grows upward** (PUSH increments SP; POP decrements SP).

4. **Data Pointer Register (DPTR)**
   - **16-bit register** (composed of `DPH` high byte + `DPL` low byte).
   - Used to access external data memory and look-up tables in code memory.
   - DPTR can hold any address in the **64 KB** external memory range.

5. **Program Status Word (PSW)**
   - **8-bit flag register** containing carry, auxiliary carry, parity, overflow, register bank select, and general-purpose flags.

6. **On-Chip Memory**
   - **ROM (4 KB)** for program storage; mapped at `0000_{H}`–`0FFF_{H}`.
   - **RAM (128 bytes)** for data; mapped at `00_{H}`–`7F_{H}`.

7. **SFR (Special Function Register) Bank**
   - **128 bytes** mapped at `80_{H}`–`FF_{H}`; controls on-chip peripherals.
   - Accessible only via direct addressing.

8. **I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)** — 32 bidirectional lines.
9. **Timers/Counters (T0, T1)** — 16-bit, programmable.
10. **Serial Port (UART)** — full-duplex, programmable baud rate.
11. **Interrupt Controller** — 5 sources, 2 priority levels.
12. **Oscillator & Clock Circuit** — drives internal timing.

## 2.2 Oscillator and Machine Cycle Timing

The 8051 divides the external crystal oscillator frequency by an **internal factor of 12** to generate the **machine cycle**, which is the basic unit of instruction timing.

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}}$$

Where:
- $T_{MC}$ = Duration of one machine cycle in seconds.
- $f_{OSC}$ = External crystal oscillator frequency in Hz.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $11.0592\ \text{MHz}$ Crystal?**
> The crystal $11.0592\ \text{MHz}$ is universally used with the 8051 because it is **divisible by standard baud rates** (e.g., 9600, 4800, 19200). At this frequency, one machine cycle is:
>
> $$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} = 1.085\ \mu s$$
>
> This makes serial communication timing exact, with **zero error** at standard baud rates — a frequent **2-mark question** in KTU.

## 2.3 Memory Organization

The 8051 has **three distinct address spaces**:

| Memory Type | Size | Address Range | Bus Used | Stack Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| On-chip Code (ROM) | 4 KB | $0000_{H}$ – $0FFF_{H}$ | PSEN (active) | No |
| External Code | Up to 60 KB | $1000_{H}$ – $FFFF_{H}$ | PSEN (active) | No |
| On-chip Data RAM | 128 B | $00_{H}$ – $7F_{H}$ | RD/WR | Yes |
| SFR | 128 B | $80_{H}$ – $FF_{H}$ | RD/WR | No |
| External Data (XRAM) | Up to 64 KB | $0000_{H}$ – $FFFF_{H}$ | RD/WR | No |

### 2.3.1 On-Chip RAM (Lower 128 Bytes) — Detailed Layout

| Address Range | Width | Region | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| $00_{H}$ – $07_{H}$ | 8 B | Register Bank 0 | `R0`–`R7` of Bank 0 (default after reset) |
| $08_{H}$ – $0F_{H}$ | 8 B | Register Bank 1 | `R0`–`R7` of Bank 1 |
| $10_{H}$ – $17_{H}$ | 8 B | Register Bank 2 | `R0`–`R7` of Bank 2 |
| $18_{H}$ – $1F_{H}$ | 8 B | Register Bank 3 | `R0`–`R7` of Bank 3 |
| $20_{H}$ – $2F_{H}$ | 16 B | Bit-Addressable | 128 individual bit addresses $00_{H}$ – $7F_{H}$ |
| $30_{H}$ – $7F_{H}$ | 80 B | General Purpose RAM | Scratchpad storage |

### 2.3.2 Special Function Registers (SFR) — Key Locations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SFRs must be accessed using direct addressing only.** They are **not bit-addressable** except for those marked (B) below.

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addressable | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| `A` (Accumulator) | `E0H` | Yes (B) | Primary ALU operand |
| `B` | `F0H` | Yes (B) | Used in `MUL`/`DIV` |
| `PSW` | `D0H` | Yes (B) | Program Status Word |
| `SP` | `81H` | No | Stack Pointer |
| `DPH` | `83H` | No | Data Pointer High |
| `DPL` | `82H` | No | Data Pointer Low |
| `P0` | `80H` | Yes (B) | Port 0 latch |
| `P1` | `90H` | No | Port 1 latch |
| `P2` | `A0H` | Yes (B) | Port 2 latch |
| `P3` | `B0H` | Yes (B) | Port 3 latch |
| `TCON` | `88H` | Yes (B) | Timer/Counter control |
| `TMOD` | `89H` | No | Timer/Counter mode |
| `TL0, TH0` | `8AH, 8CH` | No | Timer 0 low/high |
| `TL1, TH1` | `8BH, 8DH` | No | Timer 1 low/high |
| `SCON` | `98H` | Yes (B) | Serial control |
| `SBUF` | `99H` | No | Serial data buffer |
| `IE` | `A8H` | Yes (B) | Interrupt enable |
| `IP` | `B8H` | Yes (B) | Interrupt priority |
| `PCON` | `87H` | No | Power control |

## 2.4 PSW (Program Status Word) Register — Bit-by-Bit

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | `CY` | `D7H` | Carry Flag |
| 6 | `AC` | `D6H` | Auxiliary Carry (for BCD) |
| 5 | `F0` | `D5H` | User-definable flag |
| 4 | `RS1` | `D4H` | Register Bank Select 1 |
| 3 | `RS0` | `D3H` | Register Bank Select 0 |
| 2 | `OV` | `D2H` | Overflow Flag |
| 1 | – | `D1H` | Reserved (undefined) |
| 0 | `P` | `D0H` | Parity Flag (even parity of A) |

> [!TIP]
> **Register Bank Selection**:
> - `RS1,RS0 = 00` → Bank 0 (default), registers at $00_{H}$–$07_{H}$.
> - `RS1,RS0 = 01` → Bank 1, registers at $08_{H}$–$0F_{H}$.
> - `RS1,RS0 = 10` → Bank 2, registers at $10_{H}$–$17_{H}$.
> - `RS1,RS0 = 11` → Bank 3, registers at $18_{H}$–$1F_{H}$.

## 2.5 Pin Diagram of 8051 (40-Pin DIP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Pins You Must Memorize for KTU:**
> - `P1.0` and `P1.1` → alternate functions `T2` and `T2EX` (only on 8052).
> - `P3.0 = RxD`, `P3.1 = TxD`, `P3.2 = INT0`, `P3.3 = INT1`, `P3.4 = T0`, `P3.5 = T1`, `P3.6 = WR`, `P3.7 = RD`.
> - `EA` (pin 31) tied LOW → forces all code fetches to *external* ROM.
> - `ALE` (pin 30) → emits the low byte of address during external memory access.
> - `PSEN` (pin 29) → Program Store Enable — read strobe for external code memory.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Machine cycle period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | seconds | For $f_{OSC} = 12\ \text{MHz}$, $T_{MC} = 1\ \mu s$ |
| Timer tick period (Mode 1) | $T_{tick} = T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | seconds | Each tick is 1 machine cycle |
| Timer overflow (Mode 1) | $T_{overflow} = (2^{16} - \text{initial value}) \cdot T_{MC}$ | seconds | 16-bit timer rolls over at $FFFF_{H} \rightarrow 0000_{H}$ |
| Baud Rate (Timer 1 Mode 2) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | bps | Set `SMOD = 1` in `PCON.7` to double rate |
| External data address capacity | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes | bytes | Addressed via 16-bit `DPTR` |
| Program code address capacity | $2^{16} = 64\ \text{KB}$ | bytes | Addressed via 16-bit `PC` |
| Number of bit-addressable bits in RAM | $16 \times 8 = 128$ | bits | Located in $20_{H}$–$2F_{H}$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs**: 8051 derivatives control fuel injection, anti-lock braking, and dashboard indicators.
- **Consumer Electronics**: Washing machines, microwave ovens, remote controls.
- **Industrial Automation**: PLC front-end, sensor signal conditioning, relay driver logic.
- **IoT Edge Devices**: Low-power variants (e.g., TI CC2530, Silicon Labs EFM8) act as sensor pre-processors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section walks through the **most commonly asked analytical derivations** in KTU Module 1 — oscillator timing, memory address calculation, and stack pointer movement.

## 3.1 Derivation: Machine Cycle and Instruction Execution Time

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

> A system uses an 8051 with a **crystal of $16\ \text{MHz}$**. Compute:
> (a) The duration of one **machine cycle**.
> (b) The execution time of a single-cycle instruction (`NOP`, `INC A`).
> (c) The execution time of a two-byte, two-cycle instruction (`ADD A, R0`).

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of machine cycle.**

The 8051 internally divides the oscillator frequency by 12. Therefore:

$$
f_{MC} = \frac{f_{OSC}}{12}
$$

Where $f_{MC}$ is the machine cycle frequency.

**Step 2 — Substitute the given oscillator frequency.**

Substituting $f_{OSC} = 16\ \text{MHz} = 16 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Hz}$:

$$
f_{MC} = \frac{16 \times 10^{6}}{12}\ \text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert frequency to time period.**

The period is the reciprocal of frequency:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{1}{f_{MC}} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{16 \times 10^{6}}\ \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify to a clean numerical value.**

$$
T_{MC} = 0.75\ \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Apply to single-cycle instruction (1 machine cycle).**

Single-cycle instructions like `NOP`, `INC A`, `DEC A` execute in **one machine cycle**:

$$
T_{\text{single}} = 1 \times T_{MC} = 0.75\ \mu s
$$

**Step 6 — Apply to two-cycle instruction (2 machine cycles).**

Two-cycle instructions like `ADD A, R0` execute in two machine cycles:

$$
T_{\text{double}} = 2 \times T_{MC} = 2 \times 0.75\ \mu s = 1.5\ \mu s
$$

### 3.1.3 Result Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Machine cycle period | $0.75\ \mu s$ |
| NOP / INC A execution time | $0.75\ \mu s$ |
| ADD A, R0 execution time | $1.5\ \mu s$ |

## 3.2 Derivation: Stack Pointer Movement on PUSH and POP

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup

> After reset, the 8051 starts with **SP = $07_{H}$**. After executing the following sequence:
>
> `PUSH 30H`  →  `PUSH 31H`  →  `PUSH 32H`  →  `POP ACC`  →  `POP B`
>
> Find: (a) the **final value of SP**, and (b) the **addresses of valid stack bytes**.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Understand the reset condition.**

Reset sets:
$$
SP_{initial} = 07_{H}
$$
So the first available stack location is internal RAM address $08_{H}$.

**Step 2 — PUSH operation semantics.**

A `PUSH` instruction:
1. Increments SP first: $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$.
2. Writes the data byte to the new SP location.

**Step 3 — Apply three PUSH operations.**

| Operation | SP after operation | Data written at address |
|---|---|---|
| `PUSH 30H` | $08_{H}$ | RAM[$08_{H}$] $\leftarrow$ `30H` content |
| `PUSH 31H` | $09_{H}$ | RAM[$09_{H}$] $\leftarrow$ `31H` content |
| `PUSH 32H` | $0A_{H}$ | RAM[$0A_{H}$] $\leftarrow$ `32H` content |

**Step 4 — POP operation semantics.**

A `POP` instruction:
1. Reads the data byte from the current SP location.
2. Decrements SP: $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$.

**Step 5 — Apply two POP operations.**

| Operation | SP after operation | Data read from address |
|---|---|---|
| `POP ACC` | $09_{H}$ | ACC $\leftarrow$ RAM[$0A_{H}$] |
| `POP B` | $08_{H}$ | B $\leftarrow$ RAM[$09_{H}$] |

**Step 6 — State the final answer.**

After the complete sequence:

$$
SP_{final} = 08_{H}
$$

Valid stack bytes reside in internal RAM locations $08_{H}$, $09_{H}$, $0A_{H}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Timer Overflow Period (Mode 1)

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup

> A 8051 system uses $f_{OSC} = 12\ \text{MHz}$. Timer 0 is in **Mode 1** (16-bit) and is loaded with the initial value $3C_{H}$ for low byte and `B0H` for high byte (i.e., initial count register = `$B03C_{H}$`). Compute the time elapsed when the timer overflows.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Calculate the machine cycle period.**

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1\ \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the initial 16-bit value to decimal.**

$$
B03C_{H} = (B \times 16^{3}) + (0 \times 16^{2}) + (3 \times 16) + (12 \times 1)
$$

$$
B03C_{H} = (11 \times 4096) + 0 + 48 + 12 = 45056 + 60 = 45116
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the number of counts required for overflow.**

A 16-bit timer overflows at $2^{16} = 65536$. The counts needed are:

$$
N_{counts} = 65536 - 45116 = 20420
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by the machine cycle to get the time interval.**

$$
T_{overflow} = N_{counts} \times T_{MC} = 20420 \times 1\ \mu s
$$

$$
T_{overflow} = 20.42\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer.**

The timer will overflow and roll over from `$FFFF_{H}$` to `$0000_{H}$` after **20.42 milliseconds**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Memory-Mapped Bit-Addressable Locations

The 8051 has **128 bit-addressable bits** in the on-chip RAM (addresses $20_{H}$–$2F_{H}$). The mapping is linear:

$$
\text{Bit address} = \text{(Byte address} - 20_{H}\text{)} \times 8 + \text{bit position}
$$

For example, bit position 3 of byte $25_{H}$ has bit address:

$$
\text{BitAddr} = (25_{H} - 20_{H}) \times 8 + 3 = 05_{H} \times 8 + 3 = 40_{H} + 3 = 43_{H}
$$

So you can write instructions like:
```
SETB  43H     ; sets the bit at RAM address 25H, bit 3
```

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of the 8051

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram provides a **block-level functional architecture** of the 8051, illustrating the relationships between the CPU, memory, peripherals, and external buses. This is the **standard KTU Module 1 diagram** asked in exams.

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph CPU
        ALU[ALU 8 bit]
        CU[Control Unit]
        REG[Register Banks R0 R7]
    end

    PC[Program Counter 16 bit]
    SP[Stack Pointer 8 bit]
    DPTR[Data Pointer DPTR 16 bit]
    PSW[Program Status Word PSW]

    subgraph ONCHIP_MEM[On Chip Memory]
        ROM[ROM 4 KB Code]
        RAM[RAM 128 B Data]
        SFR[SFR Bank 128 B]
    end

    subgraph PERIPHERALS[On Chip Peripherals]
        P0[Port 0 8 bit]
        P1[Port 1 8 bit]
        P2[Port 2 8 bit]
        P3[Port 3 8 bit]
        T0[Timer 0 16 bit]
        T1[Timer 1 16 bit]
        UART[Serial Port UART]
        INTC[Interrupt Controller]
    end

    OSC[Oscillator and Clock]
    RST[Reset Circuit]

    PC --> CU
    OSC --> CU
    RST --> CPU
    RST --> PC
    RST --> SP

    CU --> ALU
    ALU --> REG
    REG --> PSW

    ALU <--> RAM
    ALU <--> SFR
    PC --> ROM
    DPTR <--> RAM
    SP <--> RAM

    SFR --> P0
    SFR --> P1
    SFR --> P2
    SFR --> P3
    SFR --> T0
    SFR --> T1
    SFR --> UART
    SFR --> INTC

    P0 --> EXT[(External Memory and Devices)]
    P1 --> EXT
    P2 --> EXT
    P3 --> EXT
```

## 4.2 Memory Map of the 8051 (Logical View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph CODE[Code Memory 64 KB]
        A0000[0000H 0000H Reset Vector]
        A0001[0003H External Int 0]
        A0002[000BH Timer 0]
        A0003[0013H External Int 1]
        A0004[001BH Timer 1]
        A0005[0023H Serial Port]
        AFFFF[1000H FFFFH User Program]
    end

    subgraph DATA[Data Memory]
        subgraph IRAM[Internal RAM 00H 7FH]
            RB0[00H 07H Bank 0]
            RB1[08H 0FH Bank 1]
            RB2[10H 17H Bank 2]
            RB3[18H 1FH Bank 3]
            BA[20H 2FH Bit Addressable]
            GP[30H 7FH General Purpose]
        end
        subgraph SFRAREA[SFR Area 80H FFH]
            SFR1[80H 90H A0H B0H Ports]
            SFR2[D0H E0H F0H PSW ACC B]
            SFR3[88H 89H 8AH 8BH TCON TMOD TL0 TH0]
            SFR4[98H 99H SCON SBUF]
        end
        XRAM[External Data XRAM 64 KB]
    end
```

## 4.3 Oscillator and Reset Interface

```mermaid
graph TD
    XTAL[Crystal 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz]
    C1[Cap 30 pF]
    C2[Cap 30 pF]
    GND[GND]
    VCC[VCC 5V]
    RST_BTN[Pushbutton Reset Switch]
    R10[Resistor 10 k ohm]
    ELEC[Electrolytic Capacitor 10 uF]
    RST_PIN[RST Pin 9]

    XTAL --- XTAL1[X1 Pin 19]
    XTAL --- XTAL2[X2 Pin 18]
    C1 --- XTAL1
    C1 --- GND
    C2 --- XTAL2
    C2 --- GND

    VCC --- R10
    R10 --- RST_PIN
    ELEC --- RST_PIN
    ELEC --- GND
    RST_BTN --- RST_PIN
    RST_BTN --- GND
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Power-On Reset** circuit works because at power-on, the capacitor `10 µF` is uncharged and acts like a short circuit, pulling the `RST` pin HIGH for a brief moment. This HIGH pulse (≥ 2 machine cycles) resets the 8051. The `10 kΩ` resistor then slowly charges the capacitor back to `0V`.

## 4.4 I/O Port Alternate Functions (Port 3)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P3_0[P3.0 RxD] --> SER_IN[Serial Input]
    P3_1[P3.1 TxD] --> SER_OUT[Serial Output]
    P3_2[P3.2 INT0] --> EXT0[External Interrupt 0]
    P3_3[P3.3 INT1] --> EXT1[External Interrupt 1]
    P3_4[P3.4 T0] --> TM0[Timer 0 External Input]
    P3_5[P3.5 T1] --> TM1[Timer 1 External Input]
    P3_6[P3.6 WR] --> XMEM_W[External Memory Write Strobe]
    P3_7[P3.7 RD] --> XMEM_R[External Memory Read Strobe]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`

> **Q: List the features of the 8051 microcontroller. Why is Harvard architecture preferred for the 8051?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Features of 8051:**
1. 8-bit ALU with 8-bit data bus.
2. 4 KB on-chip ROM, 128 bytes on-chip RAM.
3. Four 8-bit I/O ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) giving 32 I/O lines.
4. Two 16-bit timers/counters (T0, T1).
5. Full-duplex UART, 5 interrupt sources with 2 priority levels.
6. 64 KB external code and 64 KB external data memory addressable.
7. On-chip oscillator and clock circuit; one machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods.

**Why Harvard Architecture (1 Mark):**
The 8051 uses **Harvard architecture** with separate program and data memory buses. This allows **simultaneous fetching of an instruction and an operand**, doubling throughput at the same clock speed compared to Von Neumann architecture.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

> **Q: Explain the role of the Program Status Word (PSW) register in 8051. How are register banks selected?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**PSW Register:**
The Program Status Word (SFR at `D0H`) is an **8-bit flag register** that reflects the result of the most recent ALU operation. It contains:
- `CY` (bit 7) — Carry Flag.
- `AC` (bit 6) — Auxiliary Carry (used in BCD arithmetic).
- `F0` (bit 5) — User-defined flag.
- `RS1, RS0` (bits 4, 3) — Register Bank Select bits.
- `OV` (bit 2) — Overflow Flag.
- `P` (bit 0) — Even Parity Flag of Accumulator.

**Register Bank Selection (1 Mark):**
The bits `RS1` and `RS0` choose one of the four register banks located in on-chip RAM:
- `RS1 RS0 = 00` → Bank 0 at $00_{H}$–$07_{H}$ (default after reset).
- `RS1 RS0 = 01` → Bank 1 at $08_{H}$–$0F_{H}$.
- `RS1 RS0 = 10` → Bank 2 at $10_{H}$–$17_{H}$.
- `RS1 RS0 = 11` → Bank 3 at $18_{H}$–$1F_{H}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Compulsory]`

> **Q (a)** Draw the functional block diagram of the 8051 microcontroller and explain the function of each block. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **Q (b)** Explain the memory organization of the 8051 with a neat diagram. Differentiate between the data memory and program memory. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. CPU (ALU + Control Unit + Registers)** — *1 Mark*
The Central Processing Unit executes instructions fetched from program memory. The **8-bit ALU** performs arithmetic, logical, and bit operations. The **Control Unit** decodes opcodes and generates control signals. The **Register banks** provide fast on-chip storage for operands.

**2. Program Counter (PC) and Stack Pointer (SP)** — *1 Mark*
The 16-bit **PC** holds the address of the next instruction. The 8-bit **SP** tracks the top of the stack in on-chip RAM, default value $07_{H}$.

**3. Data Pointer (DPTR)** — *1 Mark*
A 16-bit register (DPH + DPL) used to access external data memory and look-up tables.

**4. On-Chip Memory** — *1 Mark*
- **ROM (4 KB)**: Stores program code; non-volatile.
- **RAM (128 B)**: Stores variables, stack, and working registers.
- **SFR bank (128 B)**: Provides control registers for peripherals.

**5. I/O Ports (P0–P3)** — *1 Mark*
Four 8-bit bidirectional ports providing 32 I/O lines. Port 3 has alternate functions (RxD, TxD, INT0/1, T0/1, RD, WR).

**6. Timers/Counters and Serial Port** — *1 Mark*
Two 16-bit timers (T0, T1) for timing/counting; one full-duplex UART for serial communication.

**7. Interrupt Controller and Oscillator** — *1 Mark*
Manages 5 interrupt sources with 2 priority levels. The on-chip oscillator, when connected to a crystal, generates the system clock.

*(The full Mermaid block diagram from Section 4.1 must be drawn on the answer sheet for full credit.)*

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Program Memory vs. Data Memory** — *2 Marks*

| Aspect | Program Memory (Code ROM) | Data Memory (RAM) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Stores executable code | Stores variables, stack, operands |
| Read Signal | `PSEN` active | `RD` active |
| Write Signal | Not writable | `WR` active |
| Reset Vector | $0000_{H}$ | Stack begins at $08_{H}$ |
| Size | 4 KB on-chip, 60 KB external | 128 B on-chip, 64 KB external |

**On-Chip RAM Layout** — *3 Marks* (see Section 2.3.1 table).

**SFR Layout** — *2 Marks* (see Section 2.3.2 table — mention `A`, `B`, `PSW`, `SP`, `DPTR`, `P0–P3`, `TCON`, `TMOD`, `SCON`, `IE`, `IP`).

---

### **Question B** `[Internal Choice – KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Q (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the oscillator and clock circuit of the 8051. A 12 MHz crystal is used. Calculate the time for one machine cycle and the time taken to execute (i) `NOP` and (ii) `MUL AB`. **\[7 Marks\]**
>
> **Q (b)** Explain the role of pins `EA`, `ALE`, `PSEN`, `RST`, `XTAL1`, and `XTAL2` in the 8051. Describe the power-on reset circuit. **\[7 Marks\]**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Oscillator Circuit** — *2 Marks*
The 8051 has an on-chip oscillator that requires an external crystal connected between pins **XTAL1 (pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (pin 18)**. Two 30 pF capacitors connect each crystal pin to ground. This forms a **Pierce oscillator** that generates the master clock.

**Machine Cycle Calculation** — *2 Marks*
$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1\ \mu s
$$

**Execution Times** — *3 Marks*

- `NOP` = 1 machine cycle = **1 µs**.
- `MUL AB` = 4 machine cycles = **4 µs**.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Pin Functions** — *3 Marks*

| Pin | Function |
|---|---|
| `EA` (External Access, pin 31) | When HIGH → first 4 KB from on-chip ROM, rest from external. When LOW → all code from external ROM. |
| `ALE` (Address Latch Enable, pin 30) | Latches low-byte address into external latch (74373) for demultiplexing address/data bus of P0. |
| `PSEN` (Program Store Enable, pin 29) | Read strobe for external code memory. Active LOW. |
| `RST` (Reset, pin 9) | A HIGH pulse for ≥ 2 machine cycles resets the 8051 (PC ← $0000_{H}$, SP ← $07_{H}$, all ports ← `FFH`). |
| `XTAL1, XTAL2` (pins 19, 18) | Connect to external crystal; form the on-chip oscillator. |

**Power-On Reset Circuit** — *4 Marks*

At power-on, the **10 µF electrolytic capacitor** is uncharged and pulls `RST` HIGH. The **10 kΩ resistor** slowly charges the capacitor, eventually bringing `RST` LOW. This HIGH-to-LOW transition provides the required reset pulse. The **reset pushbutton** allows manual reset by shorting `RST` to ground (creating a HIGH-LOW-HIGH transition).

```
   VCC ── 10 kΩ ── RST pin ── 10 µF ── GND
                     │
                  Pushbutton
                     │
                    GND
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the divide-by-12 rule**: Many students write $T_{MC} = \dfrac{1}{f_{OSC}}$, which is **wrong**. The 8051 divides by 12.
> 2. **Confusing `PSEN` with `RD`**: `PSEN` is for *code* memory; `RD` is for *data* memory. Mixing them up costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Writing the wrong reset value of SP**: SP resets to `$07_{H}`, **not** `$00_{H}$`. The stack *occupies* addresses starting from $08_{H}$.
> 4. **Saying P3 has no alternate function**: This loses 1 mark — every pin of P3 has a dedicated alternate function.
> 5. **Confusing Harvard with Von Neumann**: Harvard = **separate** buses for code/data. Do NOT say "separate CPUs".
> 6. **Wrong register bank addresses**: Bank 0 starts at $00_{H}$, Bank 1 at $08_{H}$, etc. The offset is exactly 8 bytes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The 8051 is an **8-bit Harvard architecture** microcontroller with **4 KB ROM** and **128 B RAM** on-chip.
- The **PC** is 16-bit (can address 64 KB code); **DPTR** is 16-bit (can address 64 KB external data); **SP** is 8-bit (points to on-chip RAM stack, resets to $07_{H}$).
- One **machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**. Formula: $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$.
- On-chip RAM $00_{H}$–$1F_{H}$ is divided into **four 8-byte register banks**; the rest of the lower 128 bytes is general purpose (with $20_{H}$–$2F_{H}$ being **bit-addressable**).
- The **SFR bank** ($80_{H}$–$FF_{H}$) provides control registers for ports, timers, serial port, and interrupts.
- **Port 3** has dual functions: each of its 8 pins serves as RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, or RD.
- **PSW flags** to remember: `CY`, `AC`, `OV`, `P`, plus `RS1, RS0` for bank selection and `F0` for user use.
- **EA** controls whether code is fetched from internal or external ROM; **ALE** latches the low byte of external addresses; **PSEN** strobes external code memory reads.
- **Reset** sets PC to $0000_{H}$, SP to $07_{H}$, and all ports to `FFH` (ready for input).
- **Harvard architecture** = separate program and data memories with separate buses → faster than Von Neumann for the same clock.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Function Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Special Function Registers # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** are dedicated on-chip memory-mapped registers in the **Intel 8051 microcontroller** that control the core CPU functions, peripheral configuration, I/O port latching, timer/counter operation, serial communication, and interrupt handling. They occupy the upper **128 bytes** of the internal RAM address space, specifically from address **80H to FFH**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Board Definition:** > "SFRs are the **control knobs and dashboard indicators** of the 8051 microcontroller. Unlike general-purpose RAM (used for storing user data), every SFR has a specific, predefined hardware function — flipping a bit in a specific SFR directly triggers a physical hardware action." ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Car Dashboard Imagine a car (the 8051 chip). The car has many mechanical parts working invisibly, but to **drive and control** it, you need a dashboard. Special Function Registers are exactly that dashboard: - The **Steering Wheel** → Port registers (P0, P1, P2, P3) — direct physical control of external pins. - The **Speedometer & Fuel Gauge** → PSW (Program Status Word) — instantly tells you the *current state* (carry flag, zero flag, register bank selected). - The **Ignition Key & Gearbox** → TCON, TMOD, SCON — start/stop and configure the engine subsystems (timers, serial). - The **Engine ECU (Brain)** → ACC, B, SP, DPTR — the internal computational workspace used by the ALU. Without the dashboard, you cannot interact with the engine. Without SFRs, the CPU cannot interact with the on-chip peripherals. ### 1.3 Physical Address Map Overview | Memory Region | Address Range | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Lower RAM (General Purpose) | 00H – 7FH | User data, stack, register banks | | **SFR Area** | **80H – FFH** | **Hardware control registers (128 bytes)** | | Bit-Addressable SFRs | 80H, 88H, 90H, 98H, A0H, A8H, B0H, B8H, D0H | 16 SFRs individually bit-manipulable | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Favorite:** The 8051 has **128 SFR locations (80H–FFH)** but only **21 are physically implemented** in the standard 8051. The remaining addresses are reserved and writing to them has no effect (or is implementation-dependent). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of SFRs by Function ### A. Core CPU Registers **1. Accumulator (ACC, Address E0H)** - The primary register for all **ALU arithmetic and logic operations**. - Bit-addressable (bits E0H–E7H). - Most instructions implicitly or explicitly use ACC. **2. B Register (Address F0H)** - Used **only** in two instructions: `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`. - For `MUL AB`: High byte of result → B, Low byte → A. - For `DIV AB`: Quotient → A, Remainder → B. - Otherwise, it acts as a general-purpose scratch register. **3. Program Status Word (PSW, Address D0H)** The most critical "state" register. It is **bit-addressable**. | Bit | Symbol | Address | Function | |---|---|---|---| | PSW.7 | **CY** | D7H | **Carry Flag** — set on unsigned overflow | | PSW.6 | **AC** | D6H | **Auxiliary Carry** — carry from bit 3 to bit 4 (BCD) | | PSW.5 | **F0** | D5H | **User Flag 0** — general purpose, software-defined | | PSW.4 | **RS1** | D4H | Register Bank Select bit 1 | | PSW.3 | **RS0** | D3H | Register Bank Select bit 0 | | PSW.2 | **OV** | D2H | **Overflow Flag** — signed arithmetic overflow | | PSW.1 | — | D1H | Reserved (user-available on some variants) | | PSW.0 | **P** | D0H | **Parity Flag** — even parity of ACC (hardware-set) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Register Bank Selection Logic (RS1:RS0):** > - `00` → Bank 0 (R0–R7 at 00H–07H) — **Default after RESET** > - `01` → Bank 1 (R0–R7 at 08H–0FH) > - `10` → Bank 2 (R0–R7 at 10H–17H) > - `11` → Bank 3 (R0–R7 at 18H–1FH) **4. Stack Pointer (SP, Address 81H)** - 8-bit register pointing to the top of the stack in internal RAM. - **Reset Value = 07H** (stack starts at 08H). - Increments before PUSH, decrements after POP. - Can be moved to any location 07H–7FH. **5. Data Pointer (DPTR — DPH:DPL)** - A **16-bit register** formed by combining two 8-bit SFRs: - **DPH** (High byte, Address 83H) - **DPL** (Low byte, Address 82H) - Used for **external data memory addressing** via `MOVX` instructions and **look-up table reads** via `MOVC A,@A+DPTR`. - Range: 0000H – FFFFH (covers the entire 64KB external memory space). ### B. I/O Port Latch Registers (P0, P1, P2, P3) | SFR | Address | Bit-Addressable? | Dual Function | |---|---|---|---| | P0 | 80H | Yes | Lower address/data bus (AD0–AD7) during external access | | P1 | 90H | Yes | Pure general-purpose I/O | | P2 | A0H | Yes | Upper address bus (A8–A15) during external access | | P3 | B0H | Yes | **Alternate functions** (RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) | > [!NOTE] > **P3 Alternate Functions (Very High Yield for KTU):** > - P3.0 → **RXD** (Serial Input) > - P3.1 → **TXD** (Serial Output) > - P3.2 → **INT0** (External Interrupt 0) > - P3.3 → **INT1** (External Interrupt 1) > - P3.4 → **T0** (Timer 0 Input) > - P3.5 → **T1** (Timer 1 Input) > - P3.6 → **WR** (External Write Strobe) > - P3.7 → **RD** (External Read Strobe) ### C. Timer/Counter SFRs **6. Timer Mode Register (TMOD, Address 89H) — Not bit-addressable** - Upper nibble (T1) and lower nibble (T0) each have 4 bits: **GATE, C/T, M1, M0**. | M1 | M0 | Mode | Operation | |---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit Timer (8048 compatibility) | | 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit Timer (most commonly used) | | 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit Auto-Reload Timer | | 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split Timer (T0 only) | **7. Timer Control Register (TCON, Address 88H) — Bit-addressable** | Bit | Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | TCON.7 | TF1 | Timer 1 Overflow Flag | | TCON.6 | TR1 | Timer 1 Run Control (1 = Start) | | TCON.5 | TF0 | Timer 0 Overflow Flag | | TCON.4 | TR0 | Timer 0 Run Control | | TCON.3 | IE1 | External Int 1 Edge Flag | | TCON.2 | IT1 | External Int 1 Trigger Type (1 = Edge) | | TCON.1 | IE0 | External Int 0 Edge Flag | | TCON.0 | IT0 | External Int 0 Trigger Type | **8. Timer Registers (TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1)** - 8-bit high/low bytes for Timer 0 and Timer 1. - TH0=8CH, TL0=8AH, TH1=8DH, TL1=8BH. ### D. Serial Port SFRs **9. Serial Control Register (SCON, Address 98H) — Bit-addressable** | Bit | Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | SCON.7 | **SM0** | Serial Mode bit 0 | | SCON.6 | **SM1** | Serial Mode bit 1 | | SCON.5 | **SM2** | Multiprocessor Communication Enable | | SCON.4 | **REN** | Receive Enable | | SCON.3 | **TB8** | 9th Transmitted Bit | | SCON.2 | **RB8** | 9th Received Bit | | SCON.1 | **TI** | Transmit Interrupt Flag | | SCON.0 | **RI** | Receive Interrupt Flag | **10. Serial Data Buffer (SBUF, Address 99H)** - Physically two separate registers sharing one address: **one for transmit, one for receive**. - Writing to SBUF loads the transmit register; reading from SBUF reads the receive register. ### E. Interrupt & Power SFRs **11. Interrupt Enable Register (IE, Address A8H) — Bit-addressable** | Bit | Symbol | Function | |---|---|---| | IE.7 | **EA** | Global Enable (must be 1 to enable any interrupt) | | IE.6 | — | Reserved | | IE.5 | — | Reserved | | IE.4 | **ES** | Enable Serial Port Interrupt | | IE.3 | **ET1** | Enable Timer 1 Interrupt | | IE.2 | **EX1** | Enable External Interrupt 1 | | IE.1 | **ET0** | Enable Timer 0 Interrupt | | IE.0 | **EX0** | Enable External Interrupt 0 | **12. Interrupt Priority Register (IP, Address B8H) — Bit-addressable** - Defines High/Low priority for each interrupt source. A `1` = High priority. - Bits: —, —, —, PS, PT1, PX1, PT0, PX0. **13. Power Control Register (PCON, Address 87H) — NOT bit-addressable** - Bit 7 (SMOD) → Doubles the baud rate of Serial Port in Modes 1, 2, 3. - Bit 6 (PCON.6) → Reserved. - Bits 1, 0 (IDL, PD) → **Idle and Power-Down modes** (in original 8051). ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula / Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | SFR Address Range | $80\text{H} \le \text{Addr} \le \text{FFH}$ | 128 byte locations | | Stack Pointer Reset | $\text{SP} = 07\text{H}$ | First PUSH writes to 08H | | DPTR Range | $\text{DPTR} = 0000\text{H} \text{ to } \text{FFFFH}$ | 16-bit: $65536$ unique addresses | | Parity Flag | $P = 1$ if ACC has **odd** number of 1s | Even parity generator | | Baud Rate (Mode 1) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times f_{\text{osc1}}$ (Timer 1 overflow rate) | Timer 1 in Auto-Reload (Mode 2) | | Timer Overflow (Mode 1) | $N = 2^{16} - \text{Initial Value}$ | Number of counts to overflow | | Auto-Reload (Mode 2) | $\text{Reload} = 256 - N$ | Where $N$ = desired count | | MUL AB | $\text{Result} = A \times B$ → High byte: B, Low byte: A | 8-bit × 8-bit = 16-bit result | | DIV AB | $\text{Quotient} \rightarrow A$, $\text{Remainder} \rightarrow B$ | Division by zero sets OV=1 | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility SFRs form the **hardware abstraction layer** of 8051 programming. In industrial applications: - **Automotive ECUs** (older systems): Configure timers via TMOD/TCON for fuel injection timing. - **Consumer electronics (washing machines, microwaves)**: P1 controls keypad, P2 drives LCD, timer interrupt (via TCON) schedules buzzer. - **Embedded sensor nodes**: SCON manages UART communication; PCON enables sleep modes for battery conservation. - **Educational labs**: SFR bit-manipulation (`SETB TR0`, `CLR P1.0`) is the foundation for learning real-time embedded control. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example: Bank Selection via PSW **Problem:** Write the sequence of 8051 instructions to switch from default Register Bank 0 to Register Bank 2, and demonstrate the memory address mapping. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Identify the bit pattern for Bank 2:** From the RS1:RS0 truth table: - Bank 2 requires `RS1 = 1` and `RS0 = 0`. - RS1 is bit D4H; RS0 is bit D3H of PSW. **Step 2 — Construct the instruction using bit-addressable SFRs:** ```assembly SETB RS1 ; Set bit D4H (RS1) of PSW to 1 CLR RS0 ; Clear bit D3H (RS0) of PSW to 0 ``` **Step 3 — Verify the memory remap:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Before:} \quad & \text{R0} \rightarrow 00\text{H}, \quad \text{R7} \rightarrow 07\text{H} \\ \text{After:} \quad & \text{R0} \rightarrow 10\text{H}, \quad \text{R7} \rightarrow 17\text{H} \\ \text{Address Shift:} \quad & \Delta = 16 \text{ bytes} = 10\text{H} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Confirm the algebraic mapping:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bank}_n \text{ base address} &= 8 \times n \quad \text{(decimal)} \\ \text{Bank}_2 \text{ base} &= 8 \times 2 = 16_{10} = 10\text{H} \quad \checkmark \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Logic:** Each register bank occupies 8 consecutive bytes. The base address = $8n$ where $n$ is the bank number (0, 1, 2, 3). This is why RS1:RS0 = `10` maps to addresses 10H–17H. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example: Timer Initial Value Calculation **Problem:** A system clock of $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592$ MHz drives Timer 0 in **Mode 1 (16-bit)**. The desired delay is **50 ms**. Compute the initial value to be loaded into TH0:TL0. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Determine the Timer clock frequency:** The 8051 divides the oscillator by 12 (machine cycle). $$ f_{\text{timer}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12} = 921600 \text{ Hz} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the timer tick period:** $$ T_{\text{tick}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{timer}}} = \frac{1}{921600} \approx 1.085\,\mu s $$ **Step 3 — Calculate the required number of timer counts for 50 ms:** $$ N = \frac{\text{Desired Delay}}{T_{\text{tick}}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46080 \text{ counts} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the 16-bit overflow formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Initial Value} &= 2^{16} - N \\ &= 65536 - 46080 \\ &= 19456 \\ &= 4\text{C}00\text{H} \text{ (in hexadecimal)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Split into TH0 and TL0:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{TH0} &= \text{4CH} \quad \text{(high byte)} \\ \text{TL0} &= \text{00H} \quad \text{(low byte)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6 — Assembly snippet:** ```assembly MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit) MOV TH0, #4CH ; Load high byte MOV TL0, #00H ; Load low byte SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Real-world insight:** The crystal **11.0592 MHz** is intentionally chosen because $11.0592 / 12 = 921600$, which is a **standard baud rate divisor**. This makes UART communication (via SCON) error-free for common baud rates like 9600. --- ## 3.3 Worked Example: Parity Flag Computation **Problem:** Determine the state of the **P (Parity) flag** when the Accumulator contains the value `A3H`. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Convert A3H to binary:** $$ A3H = 1010\,0011_2 $$ **Step 2 — Count the number of 1s in the byte:** $$ \text{Bits set} = 1+0+1+0+0+0+1+1 = 4 $$ **Step 3 — Apply the even-parity rule:** The 8051's P flag is **set to 1** when the number of 1s in the ACC is **odd** (i.e., the hardware maintains **even parity** in the byte: ACC + P always has an even number of 1s). $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Number of 1s} &= 4 \text{ (even)} \\ \therefore P &= 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Verification:** Including the parity bit, total 1s = $4 + 0 = 4$ (even) ✓ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Logic:** $P = \text{bit-count of ACC} \pmod 2$ — specifically, $P=1$ if the count is odd. The 8051 hardware sets/clears P **automatically** after every instruction that writes to ACC. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: SFR Reset Behavior | SFR | Reset Value (Hex) | Binary | Notes | |---|---|---|---| | ACC | 00H | 00000000 | Accumulator cleared | | B | 00H | 00000000 | B register cleared | | PSW | 00H | 00000000 | Bank 0 selected, all flags cleared | | SP | 07H | 00000111 | Stack starts at 08H | | DPTR | 0000H | — | Data Pointer reset to zero | | P0–P3 | FFH | 11111111 | All ports set to logic HIGH (input ready) | | IP | XXX00000B | — | All priorities default to LOW | | IE | 0XX00000B | — | All interrupts **disabled** by default | | TCON | 00H | 00000000 | Timers stopped (TR0=TR1=0) | | TMOD | 00H | 00000000 | Mode 0 for both timers | | SCON | 00H | 00000000 | Mode 0, REN=0 (receive disabled) | | PCON | 0XXX0000B | — | SMOD=0 (normal baud rate) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Internal RAM & SFR Memory Map ```mermaid graph TB subgraph LOWER["LOWER 128 BYTES (00H - 7FH)"] RB0["Register Bank 0
00H-07H"] RB1["Register Bank 1

18H-1FH"] BITAREA["Bit-Addressable Area
20H-2FH (16 bytes = 128 bits)"] GENRAM["General Purpose RAM
30H-7FH (80 bytes)"] end subgraph UPPER["UPPER 128 BYTES (80H - FFH) - SFR REGION"] P0REG["P0

(ALU + Control Unit)"] ACC["ACC

Carry Flag"] AC["Bit 6 (D6H): AC
Auxiliary Carry"] F0["Bit 5 (D5H): F0
User Flag 0"] RS1["Bit 4 (D4H): RS1
Bank Select MSB"] RS0["Bit 3 (D3H): RS0
Bank Select LSB"] OV["Bit 2 (D2H): OV
Overflow Flag"] RES["Bit 1 (D1H): Reserved"] P["Bit 0 (D0H): P
Parity Flag"] PSWBYTE --> CY PSWBYTE --> AC PSWBYTE --> F0 PSWBYTE --> RS1 PSWBYTE --> RS0 PSWBYTE --> OV PSWBYTE --> RES PSWBYTE --> P RS1 --> BANK["Bank Selection:
00=B0, 01=B1, 10=B2, 11=B3"] style PSWBYTE fill:#ffb6c1,stroke:#8b0000,stroke-width:3px style CY fill:#ff6347,color:#fff style AC fill:#ff6347,color:#fff style OV fill:#ff6347,color:#fff style P fill:#ff6347,color:#fff style F0 fill:#ffd700 style RS1 fill:#90ee90 style RS0 fill:#90ee90 style RES fill:#d3d3d3 style BANK fill:#add8e6 ``` ## 4.4 Timer/Counter SFR Interaction Flow ```mermaid graph TB START([User Code Begins]) LOADTMOD["MOV TMOD, #XXH
(Select Mode)"] LOADTH["Load TH0/TL0
(Initial Value)"] SETTR["SETB TR0
(TCON Bit 4)"] COUNT["Timer Increments
Every Machine Cycle"] CHECK{"TF0 = 1?
(TCON Bit 5)"} OVERFLOW["Timer Overflow
Flag Set Automatically"] HANDLER["Interrupt Service Routine
or Polling Code"] RELOAD["Auto-Reload from TH
(Mode 2 Only)"] STOP([Stop / Repeat]) START --> LOADTMOD LOADTMOD --> LOADTH LOADTH --> SETTR SETTR --> COUNT COUNT --> CHECK CHECK -->|No| COUNT CHECK -->|Yes| OVERFLOW OVERFLOW --> HANDLER HANDLER --> RELOAD RELOAD --> COUNT HANDLER --> STOP style START fill:#90ee90 style SETTR fill:#ffd700 style OVERFLOW fill:#ff6347,color:#fff style HANDLER fill:#87ceeb style RELOAD fill:#dda0dd style STOP fill:#ff6347,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Q: What are Special Function Registers (SFRs) in the 8051 microcontroller? List any four SFRs with their addresses.** **Model Answer:** Special Function Registers are dedicated on-chip memory-mapped registers in the 8051 that are used for controlling the CPU, peripherals, I/O ports, timers, and interrupts. They occupy addresses from **80H to FFH** in the internal RAM. Four SFRs: 1. **ACC (Accumulator)** – Address **E0H** 2. **B Register** – Address **F0H** 3. **PSW (Program Status Word)** – Address **D0H** 4. **SP (Stack Pointer)** – Address **81H** --- ### Question 2 **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **Q: Explain the function of the PSW register. What is the significance of the RS0 and RS1 bits?** **Model Answer:** The **Program Status Word (PSW)** at address **D0H** is an 8-bit bit-addressable register that holds the current state of the CPU. It contains the **Carry Flag (CY)**, **Auxiliary Carry (AC)**, **Overflow Flag (OV)**, **Parity (P)**, and **User Flag (F0)**, plus two register bank select bits. The **RS1 and RS0** bits select one of the four register banks in internal RAM: - `RS1 RS0 = 00` → Bank 0 (00H–07H) - `RS1 RS0 = 01` → Bank 1 (08H–0FH) - `RS1 RS0 = 10` → Bank 2 (10H–17H) - `RS1 RS0 = 11` → Bank 3 (18H–1FH) After reset, RS1:RS0 = 00, so Bank 0 is active. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **(a)** Explain the various Special Function Registers available in the 8051 microcontroller with their addresses and primary functions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the bit structure of the PSW register. How is the register bank selection done using RS1 and RS0 bits? **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) SFR Overview:** The 8051 microcontroller has 21 implemented SFRs located in the address range **80H–FFH** of the internal RAM. They are grouped as follows: **1. Core CPU Registers:** - **ACC (E0H):** Primary operand register for all ALU operations. Bit-addressable. - **B (F0H):** Used in MUL AB and DIV AB instructions; otherwise general-purpose. - **PSW (D0H):** Holds CPU status flags (CY, AC, OV, P) and register bank selection bits. - **SP (81H):** 8-bit stack pointer; reset to 07H. - **DPTR (82H, 83H):** 16-bit data pointer for external memory addressing. **2. I/O Port Registers:** - **P0 (80H), P1 (90H), P2 (A0H), P3 (B0H):** Latch I/O pin values; all bit-addressable. P3 has alternate functions (RXD, TXD, INT0/1, T0/T1, RD/WR). **3. Timer Registers:** - **TMOD (89H):** Selects timer mode (0, 1, 2, 3). - **TCON (88H):** Controls timer run/stop and overflow flags. - **TH0/TL0 (8CH/8AH) and TH1/TL1 (8DH/8BH):** Timer high/low byte registers. **4. Serial Port Registers:** - **SCON (98H):** Configures serial mode, receive enable, and interrupt flags (RI, TI). - **SBUF (99H):** Serial data buffer (transmit and receive share one address). **5. Interrupt Registers:** - **IE (A8H):** Enables individual and global interrupts (EA, ES, ET1, EX1, ET0, EX0). - **IP (B8H):** Sets priority (high/low) for each interrupt. **6. Power Control:** - **PCON (87H):** Contains SMOD bit for baud rate doubling and power-saving modes. **(b) PSW Bit Structure and Register Bank Selection:** The PSW register layout: | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Symbol | CY | AC | F0 | RS1 | RS0 | OV | — | P | | Address | D7H | D6H | D5H | D4H | D3H | D2H | D1H | D0H | - **CY (Carry):** Set when arithmetic produces carry-out from bit 7. - **AC (Auxiliary Carry):** Set when carry occurs from bit 3 to bit 4 (used in BCD arithmetic). - **F0:** General-purpose user-defined flag. - **OV (Overflow):** Set on signed arithmetic overflow. - **P (Parity):** Hardware sets if ACC has odd number of 1s. - **RS1, RS0:** Select active register bank: $$ \text{Bank Address Range} = 8 \times n \text{ to } (8 \times n + 7) $$ where $n$ is the decimal value of the 2-bit RS1:RS0 code. **Example:** To select Bank 2: ```assembly SETB RS1 ; D4H = 1 CLR RS0 ; D3H = 0 ; Now R0 = 10H, R7 = 17H ``` --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the TMOD and TCON registers of the 8051. How is Timer 0 configured in Mode 1 with software control? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the SCON and SBUF registers used in serial communication. Compute the initial timer value for generating a baud rate of 9600 with a 11.0592 MHz crystal, using Timer 1 in Mode 2. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) TMOD and TCON Registers:** **TMOD (89H) — Timer Mode Register (NOT bit-addressable):** | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | GATE | C/T | M1 | M0 | GATE | C/T | M1 | M0 | | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 1} | \multicolumn{4}{c}{TIMER 0} | - **GATE:** 1 = hardware control (INTx pin), 0 = software control (TRx). - **C/T:** 1 = Counter mode, 0 = Timer mode. - **M1, M0:** Mode select bits (00=Mode 0, 01=Mode 1, 10=Mode 2, 11=Mode 3). **TCON (88H) — Timer Control Register (Bit-addressable):** | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 8FH | 8EH | 8DH | 8CH | 8BH | 8AH | 89H | 88H | - **TR0/TR1:** Set to 1 to start the timer; cleared to stop. - **TF0/TF1:** Set automatically by hardware when the timer overflows. **Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit) with software control:** For software control, GATE = 0. For Timer mode, C/T = 0. For Mode 1, M1=0, M0=1. Thus, the lower nibble of TMOD = 0001 = 01H. ```assembly MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1, software start, timer mode MOV TH0, #FCH ; Load high byte of initial value MOV TL0, #18H ; Load low byte SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 ; ... main code ... ; Polling loop WAIT: JNB TF0, WAIT ; Wait until overflow flag is set CLR TR0 ; Stop timer CLR TF0 ; Clear overflow flag ``` **(b) SCON, SBUF, and Baud Rate Calculation:** **SCON (98H) — Serial Control Register:** | Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Symbol | SM0 | SM1 | SM2 | REN | TB8 | RB8 | TI | RI | For **Mode 1 (8-bit UART, variable baud rate):** SM0=0, SM1=1. **SBUF (99H):** Writing loads the transmit shift register; reading accesses the receive buffer. **Baud Rate Calculation:** For Mode 1 with Timer 1 in auto-reload (Mode 2): $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} $$ With $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592$ MHz, SMOD = 0, and desired baud = 9600: $$ \begin{aligned} 9600 &= \frac{2^0}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} \\ 9600 &= \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} \\ 9600 &= \frac{921600}{32 \times (256 - \text{TH1})} \\ 9600 &= \frac{28800}{256 - \text{TH1}} \\ 256 - \text{TH1} &= \frac{28800}{9600} = 3 \\ \text{TH1} &= 256 - 3 = 253 = \text{FDH} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Configuration:** ```assembly MOV TMOD, #20H ; Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload) MOV TH1, #FDH ; Reload value for 9600 baud MOV SCON, #50H ; Mode 1, REN enabled SETB TR1 ; Start Timer 1 ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting SMOD factor:** Many students omit $2^{\text{SMOD}}$ in the baud rate formula, leading to wrong TH1 values. Always state SMOD explicitly. > 2. **Confusing SBUF read vs. write:** Writing to SBUF loads the **transmitter**; reading from SBUF reads the **receiver**. The hardware auto-clears neither TI nor RI. > 3. **Reset value of SP confusion:** SP resets to **07H**, not 08H. The first PUSH increments SP to 08H before writing. > 4. **P flag misconception:** P=1 means the ACC has an **odd** number of 1s (the hardware maintains even parity including P). > 5. **Forgetting to set EA:** When configuring IE for interrupts, EA (global enable) must be 1, otherwise no interrupt will fire, regardless of individual bits. > 6. **Bit-addressable confusion:** TMOD and PCON are **NOT** bit-addressable. You must read/write the full byte using `MOV`. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - [x] **SFR Range:** 80H–FFH in internal RAM; **21 registers** are physically implemented in standard 8051. - [x] **ACC (E0H):** Most-used register; primary operand of ALU. Bit-addressable. - [x] **B (F0H):** Used only in `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`; else general-purpose. - [x] **PSW (D0H):** Holds CY, AC, OV, P, F0, RS1, RS0. Reset value = 00H (Bank 0). - [x] **SP (81H):** Resets to **07H**; first PUSH writes to 08H. - [x] **DPTR:** 16-bit (DPH 83H + DPL 82H); used for `MOVX` and `MOVC` table lookups. - [x] **Ports P0–P3:** All are bit-addressable; P3 has alternate functions. - [x] **TMOD (89H):** NOT bit-addressable; M1:M0 selects Mode 0/1/2/3. - [x] **TCON (88H):** Bit-addressable; TR0/TR1 start timers; TF0/TF1 indicate overflow. - [x] **SCON (98H):** Configures serial mode; SM0:SM1 = 01 → Mode 1 (8-bit UART). - [x] **SBUF (99H):** Single address; writing loads TX, reading reads RX. - [x] **IE (A8H):** EA must be 1 to enable any interrupt; EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES are individual enables. - [x] **IP (B8H):** High/Low priority selection for each interrupt source. - [x] **PCON (87H):** Contains SMOD (baud rate doubler) and power-down/idle control bits. - [x] **Bit-addressable SFRs (16 total):** P0, TCON, P1, SCON, P2, IE, P3, IP, PSW, ACC, B. These have addresses ending in 0H or 8H. - [x] **Reset behavior:** All ports default to FFH (input ready), SP = 07H, PSW = 00H, IE = 0X000000B, PCON = 0XXX0000B. - [x] **11.0592 MHz crystal:** Standard for UART — divides cleanly to 9600 baud. - [x] **Baud Rate Formula:** $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$ for Mode 1.

topics.title: Pin Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pin Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Pin Description" in the 8051 Microcontroller?

The **Pin Description** of a microcontroller refers to the **complete functional specification of every physical pin** on its integrated circuit package. For the KTU syllabus, this topic focuses on the **Intel 8051 (or its CMOS derivative, the 8051/8052 family)**, which is housed in a **40-pin Dual In-line Package (DIP)**. Each of the 40 pins has a defined electrical role, a logical name, a pin number, and often one or more *alternate functions*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> *Pin Description* involves studying the **40-pin architecture of the 8051**, identifying the **four I/O ports (P0, P1, P2, P3)**, the **control pins (RST, ALE, PSEN, EA)**, the **oscillator pins (XTAL1, XTAL2)**, and the **power supply pins (Vcc, GND)** along with their alternate functionalities, electrical characteristics, and port-loading capabilities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The 8051 as a Building with 40 Doors

Imagine the 8051 microcontroller as a **highly secure office building** with exactly **40 doors** (its physical pins).

- **Power Doors (Vcc & GND):** Like the main electricity switch and grounding cables of the building — without them, nothing works.
- **I/O Port Doors (P0, P1, P2, P3):** Like the four main entrances/exits where data, control signals, and address information flow in and out of employees (bits) — each port has **8 doors** (8 bits).
- **Special Purpose Doors (RST, ALE, PSEN, EA):** Like security control rooms — they manage the building's reset, address handling, memory selection, and external access permissions.
- **Clock Doors (XTAL1, XTAL2):** Like the master pendulum clock that synchronizes every worker's activity — they determine how fast the "building operates" (clock frequency, typically **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz**).

Just as a building manager must know the purpose of every door, an embedded systems engineer **must** master the pin diagram — it is the **first step** in circuit design, interfacing, and programming.

## 1.3 The 40-Pin Architecture: At a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 is a **40-pin DIP IC**. The pin count is divided as follows:
> - **2 pins** → Power (Vcc, GND)
> - **32 pins** → Four 8-bit I/O Ports (P0.0–P0.7, P1.0–P1.7, P2.0–P2.7, P3.0–P3.7)
> - **2 pins** → Crystal Oscillator (XTAL1, XTAL2)
> - **1 pin** → Reset (RST)
> - **1 pin** → External Access / Programming Voltage (EA/VPP)
> - **1 pin** → Program Store Enable (PSEN)
> - **1 pin** → Address Latch Enable / Programming (ALE/PROG)
>
> **Total = 2 + 32 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 40 pins.**

## 1.4 The "Why" Behind the Pin Count

> [!NOTE]
> **Why exactly 40 pins and not 64?** 
> Because the 8051 was designed in **1980** by Intel to be a **Cost-Effective, Low-Pin-Count Controller** for embedded applications (initially for the MCS-51 family of microcontrollers). A 40-pin DIP was the cheapest package available at the time. To fit 4 ports (32 I/O lines) + control + power + clock in just 40 pins, the designers made **Port 3 dual-function** (alternate functions for serial, interrupts, timers, and read/write strobes) and **Port 0** shared between data and the lower-order address bus via time-multiplexing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pin-by-Pin Functional Breakdown

The 8051's 40 pins can be grouped into **five distinct categories**. We will analyze each one with engineering depth.

### 2.1.1 Power Supply Pins (2 Pins)

| Pin # | Name | Function |
|:-----:|:----:|:---------|
| **40** | **Vcc** | **+5V** DC power supply input |
| **20** | **GND (Vss)** | Ground reference (0V) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Voltage:** The standard 8051 operates on **+5V DC ± 10%**. The CMOS variant (80C51) can operate from **+2V to +5.5V**, making it suitable for battery-powered applications.

### 2.1.2 Port 0 (Pins 32–39) — The "Multiplexed Workhorse"

| Pin # | Name | Type |
|:-----:|:----:|:-----|
| 32 | **P0.0 (AD0)** | Bidirectional, Open-Drain |
| 33 | **P0.1 (AD1)** | Bidirectional, Open-Drain |
| 34 | **P0.2 (AD2)** | Bidirectional, Open-Drain |
| 35 | **P0.3 (AD3)** | Bidirectional, Open-Drain |
| 36 | **P0.4 (AD4)** | Bidirectional, Open-Drain |
| 37 | **P0.5 (AD5)** | Bidirectional, Open-Drain |
| 38 | **P0.6 (AD6)** | Bidirectional, Open-Drain |
| 39 | **P0.7 (AD7)** | Bidirectional, Open-Drain |

**Key Characteristics of Port 0:**

- **True bidirectional** — has no internal pull-up resistors.
- Acts as the **lower-order address/data bus (AD0–AD7)** when external memory is accessed.
- During external memory cycles, it first outputs the **lower 8 bits of the address**, then switches to **input/output of the data byte** — this is called **time-multiplexing**.
- **No internal pull-up** → external **pull-up resistors of 10 kΩ** must be connected when used as a general-purpose output port.
- **Sink current per pin: ~3.2 mA** (low-power variants have lower ratings).
- When used purely as I/O, the port must be written with 1s first (to make it ready for input).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Port 0 is the ONLY port without internal pull-ups.** This is critical knowledge for KTU exams.

### 2.1.3 Port 1 (Pins 1–8) — The "Simple General-Purpose I/O"

| Pin # | Name | Type |
|:-----:|:----:|:-----|
| 1 | **P1.0** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 2 | **P1.1** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 3 | **P1.2** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 4 | **P1.3** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 5 | **P1.4** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 6 | **P1.5** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 7 | **P1.6** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 8 | **P1.7** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |

**Key Characteristics of Port 1:**

- **Dedicated bidirectional I/O port** with **internal pull-up resistors (~30 kΩ)**.
- Has **no alternate functions** in the original 8051 (in the enhanced 8052, P1.0 and P1.1 carry T2 and T2EX for Timer 2).
- **No external pull-ups needed** — can directly drive LEDs, transistors, etc.
- **Quasi-bidirectional:** writing 1 makes the pin input (high impedance); writing 0 drives it low (strong LOW).
- Suitable for **sensors, push-buttons, and simple digital output devices**.

### 2.1.4 Port 2 (Pins 21–28) — The "Upper Address Provider"

| Pin # | Name | Type |
|:-----:|:----:|:-----|
| 21 | **P2.0 (A8)** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 22 | **P2.1 (A9)** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 23 | **P2.2 (A10)** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 24 | **P2.3 (A11)** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 25 | **P2.4 (A12)** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 26 | **P2.5 (A13)** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 27 | **P2.6 (A14)** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |
| 28 | **P2.7 (A15)** | Bidirectional, Internal Pull-up |

**Key Characteristics of Port 2:**

- Bidirectional I/O with **internal pull-up resistors**.
- During external memory access, it emits the **upper 8 bits of the address bus (A8–A15)**.
- Stays at the address value (no time-multiplexing) — it **latches** the upper address, freeing P0 to be used for data.
- When **not accessing external memory**, P2 acts as a normal general-purpose I/O port.
- In 8051 (no on-chip ROM for upper address generation), P2 must be configured carefully when interfacing large external memories.

### 2.1.5 Port 3 (Pins 10–17) — The "Multi-Function Specialist"

| Pin # | Name | Primary Function | Alternate Function |
|:-----:|:----:|:----------------|:-------------------|
| 10 | **P3.0** | I/O | **RxD** (Serial Input) |
| 11 | **P3.1** | I/O | **TxD** (Serial Output) |
| 12 | **P3.2** | I/O | **INT0** (External Interrupt 0) |
| 13 | **P3.3** | I/O | **INT1** (External Interrupt 1) |
| 14 | **P3.4** | I/O | **T0** (Timer 0 Input) |
| 15 | **P3.5** | I/O | **T1** (Timer 1 Input) |
| 16 | **P3.6** | I/O | **WR** (External Data Memory Write Strobe) |
| 17 | **P3.7** | I/O | **RD** (External Data Memory Read Strobe) |

**Key Characteristics of Port 3:**

- **Bidirectional I/O with internal pull-up resistors** (~30 kΩ).
- Each pin has a **dedicated alternate function** that is invoked automatically by setting the corresponding SFR bit or hardware event.
- The **Serial Port (UART)** uses P3.0 and P3.1.
- The **two external hardware interrupts** use P3.2 and P3.3.
- **Timers 0 and 1** accept external clock/count inputs on P3.4 and P3.5.
- **External Data Memory access** is enabled by the active-low **WR** (P3.6) and **RD** (P3.7) strobes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The alternate functions of Port 3 take priority over the general-purpose I/O.** When the SFRs (like SCON, TCON, IE) activate these functions, the corresponding pins are automatically reconfigured. This dual-role design saved 8 pins — a brilliant engineering optimization.

### 2.1.6 Control & Special Function Pins

#### Pin 9: RST (Reset)

- **Active HIGH** input.
- A high pulse of at least **2 machine cycles** (i.e., **24 oscillator periods**) resets the 8051.
- Internally, the pin is connected to a **Schmitt trigger** for noise immunity.
- Pin 9 has an **internal pull-down resistor** (≈ 50 kΩ) — so leaving it floating keeps the chip in normal operation.
- Typical reset circuit: **10 µF electrolytic capacitor + 8.2 kΩ resistor + 10 µF capacitor to GND** for power-on-reset (POR) and a push-button for manual reset.

#### Pin 18: XTAL2 (Output) and Pin 19: XTAL1 (Input)

- These two pins connect to the **external crystal oscillator** or **external clock source**.
- Common crystal frequencies: **11.0592 MHz** (for serial baud rate accuracy), **12 MHz**, **24 MHz**.
- The on-chip oscillator circuit is a **high-gain inverting amplifier** configured as a **Pierce oscillator** using the crystal.
- **Two 30 pF ceramic capacitors** must be connected from each pin to GND for stable oscillation.
- An **external clock source** can also be applied directly to **XTAL1**, leaving **XTAL2 floating**.

#### Pin 29: PSEN (Program Store Enable)

- **Active LOW** output.
- Enables the **external Program (Code) Memory** during a fetch operation.
- Connected to the **Output Enable (OE)** pin of external ROM/EPROM.
- PSEN is activated **twice per machine cycle** during external code fetch.
- **Not activated** for internal program memory access (8051/8751).
- **Not activated** during external data memory access (RD is used instead).

#### Pin 30: ALE/PROG (Address Latch Enable / Program Pulse)

- **ALE** is an output pin that latches the address from the multiplexed P0 bus.
- ALE pulse occurs at a rate of **1/6 of the oscillator frequency** (1/6 × f_osc).
- ALE is used to **demultiplex the address/data bus** by triggering an external latch (typically a **74HC373** or **74LS373** octal transparent latch).
- During **programming of EPROM (8751)**, this pin receives the **programming pulse** (hence the alternate name PROG).
- **One ALE pulse is skipped** during external data memory access (to avoid false latching).
- **ALE can be disabled** by setting bit 0 of the SFR at address 8EH.

#### Pin 31: EA/VPP (External Access / Programming Voltage)

- **EA** is an input pin with dual meaning.
- When **EA = 1 (HIGH)**: The 8051 executes code from **internal ROM** (up to 4 KB for the standard 8051, 8 KB for 8052).
- When **EA = 0 (LOW)**: The 8051 fetches **all code from external program memory**, regardless of internal ROM presence.
- During **EPROM programming**, this pin receives the **+12V (or +21V for newer variants) programming voltage** (VPP).
- **Must be tied to Vcc** for normal operation of 8051 with internal ROM.
- **Must be tied to GND** for 8031 (ROM-less version).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Master the following numerical relationships — at least one numerical question appears in every KTU exam.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Value | Unit |
|:-:|:----------|:--------|:--------------|:-----|
| 1 | Machine cycle frequency | f_mc = f_osc / 12 | 11.0592 MHz / 12 = 0.9216 | MHz |
| 2 | ALE pulse frequency | f_ALE = f_osc / 6 | 11.0592 MHz / 6 = 1.8432 | MHz |
| 3 | Reset pulse duration (min) | t_RST ≥ 2 machine cycles | 24 / f_osc = 2.17 µs (at 11.0592 MHz) | µs |
| 4 | Port 0 sink current | I_OL per pin | 3.2 (TTL), 10 (CMOS HC) | mA |
| 5 | Port 0 source current | I_OH per pin | ≈ 0 (open drain) | mA |
| 6 | Ports 1/2/3 sink current | I_OL per pin | 1.6 (TTL) | mA |
| 7 | Ports 1/2/3 source current | I_OH per pin | 0.06 (TTL) | mA |
| 8 | Port internal pull-up | R_pull-up | 30 to 50 | kΩ |
| 9 | Vcc supply voltage | V_cc | +5 ± 10% | V |
| 10 | Crystal load capacitance | C_L | 20 to 40 | pF |
| 11 | Power-on reset time | t_POR | ≈ RC time constant | ms |
| 12 | External memory addressing | A15–A8 from P2, AD7–AD0 from P0 | 16-bit address = 64 KB space | Bytes |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051's pin architecture directly determines the **hardware design rules** for embedded systems:
> - **PCB Layout:** All 40 pins must be routed on a 2-layer or 4-layer board with proper **decoupling capacitors (0.1 µF ceramic + 10 µF electrolytic)** near Vcc.
> - **External Memory Interfacing:** A **74HC373 latch** captures the lower address from P0; a **74HC138 decoder** activates the correct memory chip using PSEN/RD/WR.
> - **Port Expansion:** When more I/O is needed, **I/O expanders (8255)** are daisy-chained using P0 and P2.
> - **Industrial Applications:** The 8051 still powers **washing machines, microwave ovens, automotive ECUs, and smart meters** — every pin's behavior (sink/source current, drive strength) is critical for relay and motor driving.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Reset Pulse Width

The 8051's reset circuitry requires a minimum pulse width. Let us derive it step by step.

### Given:
- Oscillator frequency: $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$
- 1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods

### Step 1 — Define the machine cycle duration
A machine cycle is the basic time unit for the 8051. It spans **12 oscillator periods**.

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the oscillator frequency
$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}$$

$$T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592} \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the numerical value
$$T_{mc} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \text{ µs}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the reset pulse requirement
The 8051 needs a **minimum of 2 machine cycles** of HIGH on the RST pin to trigger a valid reset.

$$t_{RST(min)} = 2 \times T_{mc}$$

$$t_{RST(min)} = 2 \times 1.085 \text{ µs} = 2.17 \text{ µs}$$

### Result
$$\boxed{t_{RST(min)} \geq 2.17 \text{ µs (for } f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, the reset pulse is held HIGH for several milliseconds to allow the oscillator and internal PLLs to stabilize. A typical RC reset circuit gives a time constant $\tau = R \times C$. For $R = 8.2 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 10 \text{ µF}$:
> $$\tau = 8.2 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 0.082 \text{ s} = 82 \text{ ms}$$
> This is **37,788 times** the minimum required — a huge safety margin.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ALE Pulse Frequency and Timing

### Given:
- Oscillator frequency: $f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}$

### Step 1 — Machine cycle frequency
$$f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{12 \text{ MHz}}{12} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

### Step 2 — ALE pulse frequency
ALE pulses occur at a rate of **1/6 of the oscillator frequency**, equivalently **twice per machine cycle**.

$$f_{ALE} = \frac{f_{osc}}{6} = \frac{12 \text{ MHz}}{6} = 2 \text{ MHz}$$

### Step 3 — ALE period
$$T_{ALE} = \frac{1}{f_{ALE}} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^6} = 0.5 \text{ µs}$$

### Step 4 — ALE duty cycle interpretation
ALE is HIGH for approximately **1 oscillator period** within every 6 oscillator periods. The duty cycle is:

$$D_{ALE} = \frac{1 \text{ osc period}}{6 \text{ osc periods}} = 16.67\%$$

### Result
$$\boxed{f_{ALE} = 2 \text{ MHz}, \quad T_{ALE} = 0.5 \text{ µs, for } f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: External Program Memory Address Generation

When the 8051 accesses external code memory, the **16-bit address** is formed by combining P0 and P2.

### Given:
- A memory location address: $A_{15}A_{14}...A_1A_0$ (16 bits)

### Step 1 — Upper address byte
The upper 8 bits ($A_{15} - A_8$) are provided by **Port 2**.

$$P2 = A_{15}A_{14}A_{13}A_{12}A_{11}A_{10}A_9A_8$$

### Step 2 — Lower address byte
The lower 8 bits ($A_7 - A_0$) are provided by **Port 0** during the **first half** of the memory cycle (latched by ALE).

$$P0_{address} = A_7A_6A_5A_4A_3A_2A_1A_0$$

### Step 3 — Combined address
$$A_{16-bit} = (P2 \text{ value}) \times 256 + (P0 \text{ latched value})$$

$$A_{16-bit} = P2 \cdot 2^8 + P0_{latched}$$

### Step 4 — Example calculation
Suppose we wish to access memory location **0x3F4A**.

$$0x3F4A = (0x3F) \times 256 + 0x4A$$
$$= 63 \times 256 + 74 = 16128 + 74 = 16202 \text{ (decimal)}$$

So:
- **P2 = 0x3F** (pins P2.0 through P2.7 carry bits 0001 1111 mapped to A8–A15)
- **P0 (latched) = 0x4A** (during the first half of the cycle; P0.0–P0.7 carry A0–A7)

### Result
$$\boxed{A_{16} = P2 \cdot 2^8 + P0_{latched} = P2 \cdot 256 + P0_{latched}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum addressable external memory** is $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes = **64 KB** (with PSEN gating ROM and RD/WR gating RAM, the total external memory can be up to **128 KB** split between code and data).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of All Four Ports

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most heavily tested comparison in KTU exams.** Memorize this table thoroughly.

| Feature | Port 0 | Port 1 | Port 2 | Port 3 |
|:--------|:------:|:------:|:------:|:------:|
| Pin numbers | **32–39** | **1–8** | **21–28** | **10–17** |
| Number of pins | 8 | 8 | 8 | 8 |
| Internal pull-up | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (≈ 30 kΩ) | ✅ Yes (≈ 30 kΩ) | ✅ Yes (≈ 30 kΩ) |
| Open-drain? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Alternate function | **AD0–AD7** (Address/Data bus) | None (basic 8051) | **A8–A15** (Upper address) | **RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD** |
| External pull-up needed? | ✅ Yes (10 kΩ) | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| Sink current per pin | **3.2 mA** | 1.6 mA | 1.6 mA | 1.6 mA |
| Used for external memory? | ✅ Yes (LAD bus) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (UAD bus) | ❌ No |
| Direct LED drive? | ❌ Need driver | ✅ Yes (with limit) | ✅ Yes (with limit) | ✅ Yes (with limit) |
| Direction control SFR | P0 (0x80) | P1 (0x90) | P2 (0xA0) | P3 (0xB0) |

---

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Baud Rate Calculation Using Timer 1 (Relation to 11.0592 MHz Crystal)

A KTU-favorite numerical problem. The 8051's serial port uses **Timer 1 overflow** to generate baud rates. The relationship is:

$$Baud \text{ Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

### Step 1 — Assume SMOD = 0 (default)
$$Baud = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for TH1
$$256 - TH1 = \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}$$

$$TH1 = 256 - \frac{f_{osc}}{384 \times Baud}$$

### Step 3 — Compute for f_osc = 11.0592 MHz and Baud = 9600
$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600}$$

$$256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3.6864 \times 10^6}$$

$$256 - TH1 = 3$$

$$TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD$$

### Result
$$\boxed{TH1 = 0xFD \text{ for Baud} = 9600 \text{ at } f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz (SMOD} = 0)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz?** Because 11.0592 / 12 = 0.9216, and 0.9216 / 32 = 28,800. This divides evenly into standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400, 1200, 600, 300) without rounding error. The 12 MHz crystal causes **0.16% error** at 9600 baud — enough to fail RS-232 communication over long distances.

## 4.3 External Memory Interfacing — Architecture Flow

When the 8051 needs more than 4 KB of program memory or 128 bytes of data RAM, **external memory chips** are connected using the address/data bus.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MCU["8051 Microcontroller"]
    P0["Port 0\nPins 32 to 39\nAD0 to AD7"]
    P2["Port 2\nPins 21 to 28\nA8 to A15"]
    P3["Port 3\nPins 16 and 17\nWR and RD"]
    PSENP["Pin 29\nPSEN"]
    ALEA["Pin 30\nALE"]

    LATCH["74HC373 Octal Latch\n(8-bit Transparent Latch)"]
    ROM["External ROM\n(up to 64 KB)"]
    RAM["External RAM\n(up to 64 KB)"]
    DECODER["74HC138\n3-to-8 Decoder"]

    MCU --> P0
    MCU --> P2
    MCU --> P3
    MCU --> PSENP
    MCU --> ALEA

    P0 -->|"Multiplexed\nAddress+Data"| LATCH
    ALEA -->|"Latch\nEnable"| LATCH
    LATCH -->|"A0 to A7\n(Latched)"| ROM
    LATCH -->|"A0 to A7\n(Latched)"| RAM
    P2 -->|"A8 to A15"| ROM
    P2 -->|"A8 to A15"| RAM
    P2 -->|"A13, A14, A15"| DECODER
    PSENP -->|"ROM\nRead Strobe"| ROM
    P3 -->|"WR (P3.6)\nRD (P3.7)"| RAM
    DECODER -->|"Chip Select\nSignals"| ROM
    DECODER -->|"Chip Select\nSignals"| RAM
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the function of pins XTAL1 and XTAL2 of the 8051 microcontroller. What is the role of the capacitors connected to these pins?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**XTAL1 (Pin 19) and XTAL2 (Pin 18)** are the two pins that connect the 8051 to an **external crystal oscillator** (or an external clock source), which provides the **timing reference** for all internal operations of the microcontroller.

- **XTAL2** is the **output** of the on-chip inverting amplifier (Pierce oscillator).
- **XTAL1** is the **input** to the amplifier.

Internally, these pins are connected to a high-gain inverting amplifier that, together with an external **quartz crystal** and two **load capacitors (typically 30 pF each)**, forms a stable oscillator circuit. The capacitors are connected from each pin to **GND** and serve to:
1. Provide the **correct load capacitance** required by the crystal for accurate frequency oscillation.
2. Suppress **harmonic distortion** and ensure the oscillator starts reliably on power-up.

The oscillator frequency ($f_{osc}$) determines the **machine cycle frequency** ($f_{mc} = f_{osc}/12$) and the **ALE pulse rate** ($f_{ALE} = f_{osc}/6$). Standard frequencies are 11.0592 MHz (preferred for serial communication) and 12 MHz.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Port 0 and Port 1 of the 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Port 0 | Port 1 |
|:--------|:-------|:-------|
| Pin numbers | 32 to 39 | 1 to 8 |
| Internal pull-up resistor | **Absent** (open-drain) | **Present** (≈ 30 kΩ) |
| External pull-up needed | Yes (≈ 10 kΩ) | No |
| Alternate function | AD0–AD7 (multiplexed address/data bus) | None (basic 8051) |
| Used for external memory? | Yes (LAD bus) | No (general-purpose only) |
| Sink current per pin | ≈ 3.2 mA | ≈ 1.6 mA |
| Can drive LED directly? | Not recommended | Yes (with current-limiting resistor) |

The **key difference** is that **Port 0 has no internal pull-up resistors** and serves as the **multiplexed address/data bus** during external memory access, while **Port 1 is a general-purpose I/O port** with internal pull-ups and no alternate function in the standard 8051.

---

## 5.2 PART B: Full-Descriptive Questions (Choose ONE — 14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Draw the pin diagram of the 8051 microcontroller and explain the functions of the following pins: (i) RST (ii) EA/VPP (iii) PSEN (iv) ALE/PROG.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Pin Diagram:**

```
                            +------- U -------
            P1.0 (T2)  1 ---|                 |--- 40    Vcc (+5V)
            P1.1 (T2EX) 2 ---|                 |--- 39    P0.7 (AD7)
                P1.2    3 ---|                 |--- 38    P0.6 (AD6)
                P1.3    4 ---|     8051        |--- 37    P0.5 (AD5)
                P1.4    5 ---|   Micro-        |--- 36    P0.4 (AD4)
                P1.5    6 ---|   controller     |--- 35    P0.3 (AD3)
                P1.6    7 ---|                 |--- 34    P0.2 (AD2)
                P1.7    8 ---|                 |--- 33    P0.1 (AD1)
                 RST    9 ---|                 |--- 32    P0.0 (AD0)
```
