```
            P3.0 (RxD) 10 ---|                 |--- 31    EA/VPP
            P3.1 (TxD) 11 ---|                 |--- 30    ALE/PROG
         P3.2 (INT0)  12 ---|                 |--- 29    PSEN
         P3.3 (INT1)  13 ---|                 |--- 28    P2.7 (A15)
            P3.4 (T0)  14 ---|                 |--- 27    P2.6 (A14)
            P3.5 (T1)  15 ---|                 |--- 26    P2.5 (A13)
           P3.6 (WR)   16 ---|                 |--- 25    P2.4 (A12)
           P3.7 (RD)   17 ---|                 |--- 24    P2.3 (A11)
              XTAL2    18 ---|                 |--- 23    P2.2 (A10)
              XTAL1    19 ---|                 |--- 22    P2.1 (A9)
                 GND   20 ---|                 |--- 21    P2.0 (A8)
                            +-----------------+
```

**Function Explanations:**

**(i) RST (Pin 9) — Reset Pin [1.5 marks]:**
- RST is an **active-HIGH** input pin.
- Applying a HIGH pulse of at least **2 machine cycles (24 oscillator periods)** to this pin resets the 8051.
- On reset: PC = 0000H, all SFRs are initialized to 0, Ports 1/2/3 are set to FFH, and execution begins from address 0000H.
- The pin has an internal Schmitt trigger for noise immunity and an internal pull-down resistor (≈ 50 kΩ).

**(ii) EA/VPP (Pin 31) — External Access / Programming Voltage [2 marks]:**
- This pin serves **two purposes**:
  - **EA (External Access) — Input:** When HIGH (1), the 8051 executes code from **internal ROM** (up to 4 KB / 8 KB). When LOW (0), all code is fetched from **external program memory** (used in 8031, the ROM-less version).
  - **VPP (Programming Voltage) — Input:** During **EPROM programming** of the 8751, this pin receives **+12V (or +21V)** as the programming voltage.
- In normal operation of 8051, EA is tied to Vcc.

**(iii) PSEN (Pin 29) — Program Store Enable [1.5 marks]:**
- PSEN is an **active-LOW output** signal.
- It is used to **enable external program memory (ROM/EPROM)** during a code-fetch operation.
- Connected to the **Output Enable (OE)** pin of the external ROM.
- PSEN is activated **twice per machine cycle** for external code fetch, and is **not activated** for internal ROM or external data memory.

**(iv) ALE/PROG (Pin 30) — Address Latch Enable / Program Pulse [2 marks]:**
- **ALE — Output:** Demultiplexes the address/data bus by issuing a **latch enable pulse** to an external 74HC373 latch. ALE occurs at **1/6 of the oscillator frequency**.
- **PROG — Input:** During **EPROM programming** of the 8751, this pin receives the **programming pulse**.
- One ALE pulse is skipped during external data memory access (MOVX) to prevent false latching.
- ALE can be disabled by setting bit 0 of SFR at address 8EH (this frees pin 30 as a normal I/O when using the 8751 in normal mode).

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the alternate functions of Port 3 of the 8051 microcontroller with a neat pin diagram.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Port 3 Pin Diagram:**

```
        P3.0 (Pin 10)  ----> RxD  (Serial Input)
        P3.1 (Pin 11)  ----> TxD  (Serial Output)
        P3.2 (Pin 12)  ----> INT0 (External Interrupt 0)
        P3.3 (Pin 13)  ----> INT1 (External Interrupt 1)
        P3.4 (Pin 14)  ----> T0   (Timer 0 External Input)
        P3.5 (Pin 15)  ----> T1   (Timer 1 External Input)
        P3.6 (Pin 16)  ----> WR   (External Data Memory Write Strobe, active LOW)
        P3.7 (Pin 17)  ----> RD   (External Data Memory Read Strobe, active LOW)
```

**Alternate Function Descriptions:**

| Pin | Name | Alternate Function | Triggered By |
|:---:|:----:|:-------------------|:-------------|
| 10 | P3.0 | **RxD** — Serial Data Input | UART receive mode (SCON.REN = 1) |
| 11 | P3.1 | **TxD** — Serial Data Output | UART transmit operation |
| 12 | P3.2 | **INT0** — External Interrupt 0 | Falling edge / LOW level (IT0 in TCON) |
| 13 | P3.3 | **INT1** — External Interrupt 1 | Falling edge / LOW level (IT1 in TCON) |
| 14 | P3.4 | **T0** — Timer 0 External Input | External pulse counting (TMOD = 0x05/0x06) |
| 15 | P3.5 | **T1** — Timer 1 External Input | External pulse counting (TMOD = 0x50/0x60) |
| 16 | P3.6 | **WR** — Write Strobe | Active LOW during MOVX @DPTR, A |
| 17 | P3.7 | **RD** — Read Strobe | Active LOW during MOVX A, @DPTR |

**Key Engineering Points:**

1. **Port 3 is bidirectional with internal pull-ups (≈ 30 kΩ)** — no external pull-up resistors are needed.
2. The **alternate functions are automatically activated** when the corresponding SFRs (SCON, TCON, IE, TMOD) are configured. There is no manual pin-switching by the programmer.
3. The **serial port** uses **P3.0 (RxD) and P3.1 (TxD)** — these are TTL-level UART signals and require a **MAX232** level converter to interface with an RS-232 port of a PC.
4. The **external interrupts INT0 and INT1** are the only **hardware-triggered interrupts** that can wake the 8051 from idle/power-down mode.
5. The **WR and RD strobes** are active-LOW, so external memory or peripheral devices latch data on the **falling edge** of these signals.
6. If a particular alternate function is **not used**, the corresponding P3 pin can be used as a **normal general-purpose I/O pin**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Many students wrongly assume that **Port 3's alternate functions require explicit programming**. They do not — they are **automatically invoked** by the SFR configuration. Do not waste marks writing "We must set a bit to use P3.0 as RxD" — the SFR SCON automatically reconfigures the pin.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Describe the various functional groups of pins in the 8051 microcontroller. What is the role of Port 0 during external memory access?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

The 40 pins of the 8051 can be classified into **five functional groups**:

**1. Power Supply Pins (2 pins) [1 mark]:**
- **Vcc (Pin 40):** +5V DC power input.
- **GND (Pin 20):** Ground reference (0V).

**2. I/O Port Pins (32 pins) [2 marks]:**
- **Port 0 (Pins 32–39):** 8-bit, open-drain, multiplexed address/data.
- **Port 1 (Pins 1–8):** 8-bit, internal pull-up, general-purpose I/O.
- **Port 2 (Pins 21–28):** 8-bit, internal pull-up, provides upper address bus (A8–A15).
- **Port 3 (Pins 10–17):** 8-bit, internal pull-up, with alternate functions (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD).

**3. Oscillator Pins (2 pins) [1 mark]:**
- **XTAL1 (Pin 19):** Input to the on-chip oscillator amplifier.
- **XTAL2 (Pin 18):** Output from the on-chip oscillator amplifier.
- A crystal and two 30 pF capacitors form a stable clock source.

**4. Control Pins (3 pins) [1 mark]:**
- **RST (Pin 9):** Active-HIGH reset.
- **PSEN (Pin 29):** Active-LOW program store enable (for external code memory).
- **ALE/PROG (Pin 30):** Address latch enable (also used as programming pulse input for EPROM).

**5. External Access Pin (1 pin) [1 mark]:**
- **EA/VPP (Pin 31):** Selects internal vs. external code memory; receives programming voltage.

**Role of Port 0 During External Memory Access [3 marks]:**

Port 0 serves a **dual role** in 8051 systems that use external memory:

1. **Multiplexed Address/Data Bus (AD0–AD7):**
   - During the **first half** of the memory cycle, Port 0 carries the **lower 8 bits of the address (A0–A7)**. This is latched by an external latch (74HC373) when ALE goes HIGH.
   - During the **second half** of the memory cycle, Port 0 carries the **8-bit data byte** to be read from or written to the external memory.

2. **Demultiplexing using ALE:**
   - The **ALE pin (Pin 30)** issues a HIGH-to-LOW transition that triggers the external latch to capture the address and then release the bus for data transfer.

3. **External Pull-up Resistors:**
   - Because Port 0 is **open-drain**, external **10 kΩ pull-up resistors** to Vcc are mandatory when interfacing with external memory devices. Without them, the port cannot drive the bus HIGH.

4. **Memory Mapping:**
   - Combined with Port 2 (which provides A8–A15), the 8051 can address up to **64 KB of external code memory** (using PSEN) and **64 KB of external data memory** (using RD and WR).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2:** Students often forget that **PSEN is for code memory only** and **RD/WR is for data memory only**. Mixing them up is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**With a suitable diagram, explain how external program memory and external data memory are interfaced to the 8051. What is the maximum memory size?**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Interfacing Diagram:**

```
        8051                    74HC373              External ROM (2764)
        -----                   --------             -----------------
P0.0–P0.7 ──────────AD0-AD7───▶│ D0-D7 → A0-A7    │→ A0-A7
                                                  │   D0-D7 ← D0-D7
P2.0–P2.7 ──────A8-A15───────────────────────────▶│   A8-A12
P3.6 (WR) ─────────────────────────────────────────▶│  (Write strobe NOT used)
P3.7 (RD) ─────────────────────────────────────────▶│  (Read strobe NOT used)
   PSEN ──────────────────────────────────────────▶│  OE (Output Enable)
   ALE  ──────▶ G (Latch Enable) of 74HC373
   EA ───────── GND (for 8031) or Vcc (for 8051 with external code)

        8051                                       External RAM (6264)
        -----                                      -----------------
P0.0–P0.7 ──────────AD0-AD7────────────────────────│→ A0-A7
                                                  │   D0-D7 ↔ D0-D7
P2.0–P2.7 ──────A8-A15───────────────────────────▶│   A8-A12
P3.6 (WR) ────────────────────────────────────────▶│  WE (Write Enable)
P3.7 (RD) ────────────────────────────────────────▶│  OE (Output Enable)
   ALE  ──────▶ External latch (74HC373) for lower address
```

**Step-by-Step Interfacing Description [7 marks total]:**

1. **Address Latch (74HC373):**
   - The multiplexed address/data bus from P0 is **demultiplexed** using a 74HC373 transparent latch.
   - The **ALE** signal controls the latch — when ALE is HIGH, the latch is transparent (address flows through); when ALE goes LOW, the latch holds the lower 8 address bits.

2. **External Program Memory Interfacing (Code ROM):**
   - The **16-bit address** is provided by **P0 (A0–A7)** and **P2 (A8–A15)**.
   - The **PSEN** signal from the 8051 is connected to the **OE (Output Enable)** pin of the external ROM.
   - When the CPU fetches an instruction from an address in external ROM, PSEN goes LOW, enabling the ROM to place the opcode byte on the data bus.
   - **Maximum size:** 64 KB (since 16-bit address bus).

3. **External Data Memory Interfacing (XDATA RAM):**
   - The same address bus (P0 + P2) is used.
   - The **P3.6 (WR)** and **P3.7 (RD)** signals are connected to the **WE** and **OE** pins of the RAM.
   - **MOVX** instructions automatically activate WR/RD for external data memory access.
   - **Maximum size:** 64 KB (using a separate chip select).

4. **Chip Select Decoding:**
   - For multiple memory chips, a **74HC138 (3-to-8 decoder)** decodes the upper address bits (A13, A14, A15) to generate individual chip-select signals.
   - Example: 000xxx → CS0, 001xxx → CS1, etc.

5. **Total External Memory:**
   - The 8051 can access a total of **64 KB code + 64 KB data = 128 KB of external memory**, in addition to its internal 4 KB ROM and 128 bytes RAM.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #3:** When drawing the interfacing diagram, students often forget to show the **74HC373 latch** and write the address/data bus as a single line. Examiners **specifically look for the latch and the ALE connection** — losing this loses 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams (Module 1: Pin Description):**

- ✅ The 8051 is a **40-pin DIP IC** organized into **4 ports (32 I/O pins) + 8 special pins**.
- ✅ **Port 0 (Pins 32–39)** is the **only open-drain port** — requires **10 kΩ external pull-up resistors**.
- ✅ **Port 0** serves as the **multiplexed address/data bus (AD0–AD7)** — lower 8 address bits + 8 data bits, demultiplexed using **ALE + 74HC373**.
- ✅ **Port 1 (Pins 1–8)** is a **general-purpose I/O** with **internal pull-ups** and **no alternate functions** in the standard 8051.
- ✅ **Port 2 (Pins 21–28)** provides the **upper address bus (A8–A15)** during external memory access.
- ✅ **Port 3 (Pins 10–17)** has **8 alternate functions**: **RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD** — all activated automatically by SFRs.
- ✅ **Pin 9 — RST:** Active-HIGH reset, minimum pulse **≥ 2 machine cycles (24 oscillator periods)**, internal pull-down resistor.
- ✅ **Pins 18, 19 — XTAL2, XTAL1:** On-chip **Pierce oscillator** amplifier; external crystal + **2 × 30 pF capacitors**.
- ✅ **Pin 29 — PSEN:** Active-LOW output, enables **external code memory (ROM/EPROM)**, connected to **OE** of ROM.
- ✅ **Pin 30 — ALE/PROG:** Latch enable for address demultiplexing; **f_ALE = f_osc/6**; can be disabled via SFR (8EH).
- ✅ **Pin 31 — EA/VPP:** HIGH → internal ROM; LOW → external ROM only; receives **+12V/+21V** during EPROM programming.
- ✅ **Pin 20 — GND, Pin 40 — Vcc (+5V).**
- ✅ **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**, **ALE frequency = f_osc/6**, **Maximum addressable memory = 64 KB** (code) + **64 KB** (data).
- ✅ **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred for accurate UART baud rates (gives zero error for 9600 baud with TH1 = 0xFD).
- ✅ **Maximum total external memory = 128 KB** (64 KB code via PSEN + 64 KB data via RD/WR).
- ✅ **Ports 1, 2, 3 have internal pull-ups (≈ 30 kΩ)**; **Port 0 does NOT** — this is the single most important port comparison fact.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — Module 1, Topic: Pin Description**
> *These notes are aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020), Course PCBMT402, and cover all expected examination patterns for the University ESE (End Semester Examination).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller Architecture (MCS-51 Family)**, an **Interrupt** is defined as an *asynchronous* (or synchronous, for internal events) event that suspends the normal sequential execution of the main program by forcing the CPU to branch to a predefined memory location known as the **Interrupt Vector Address**, where a specialized subroutine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** resides. After the ISR completes execution (terminated by the `RETI` instruction), the CPU resumes the main program from the exact point where it was interrupted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** An interrupt is a hardware- or software-triggered mechanism that temporarily halts the microcontroller's primary execution flow to service a high-priority event, after which control is automatically returned to the main program.

The **8051 Microcontroller** has a total of **5 interrupt sources** (excluding Reset):
1. **External Hardware Interrupt 0** (INT0)
2. **Timer 0 Overflow** (TF0)
3. **External Hardware Interrupt 1** (INT1)
4. **Timer 1 Overflow** (TF1)
5. **Serial Port Transmit/Receive** (TI/RI)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Worker & Phone Call" Model

Imagine you are an office worker (the **CPU**) sitting at your desk working on a report (the **main program**). You have two ways to know if your boss needs you urgently:

- **Polling Method:** You stop working every 2 minutes, get up, walk to your boss's cabin to check if he needs you, then come back. This wastes enormous time and energy.
- **Interrupt Method:** You keep working. When your boss calls you on the phone (the **interrupt signal**), the phone rings, you pause your work, mark exactly where you stopped (the **PC is saved on the stack**), attend to your boss (execute the **ISR**), hang up (`RETI`), and resume work from where you left off (**PC is popped from stack**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Interrupts eliminate the need for the CPU to constantly check (poll) peripheral devices, drastically improving **real-time responsiveness** and **power efficiency** — a core concept in **Embedded Systems Design**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Interrupt Latency:** The time delay between the interrupt request being raised and the execution of the first instruction of the ISR. For the standard **12 MHz crystal oscillator** in 8051, this latency ranges from **3 to 7 machine cycles** (i.e., **3 µs to 7 µs**).
- **Interrupt Vector Address:** The fixed ROM memory location where the ISR for a given interrupt must reside.
- **Interrupt Nesting:** The ability of a higher-priority interrupt to preempt a lower-priority ISR that is currently executing. The 8051 supports this only partially through the **IP register**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the 8051 Interrupt System (Step-by-Step)

The 8051 interrupt system operates through three critical hardware registers located in the **Special Function Register (SFR)** memory space:

**Step 1 — Interrupt Source Activation:** A peripheral event sets a flag bit in the relevant SFR (e.g., `TF0` in TCON, `TI/RI` in SCON, or external pins INT0/INT1).

**Step 2 — Interrupt Enable Verification:** The CPU checks the corresponding enable bit in the **IE Register (Address A8H)**. The Global Enable bit **EA** must be set (`EA = 1`) for any interrupt to be recognized.

**Step 3 — Priority Arbitration:** If two interrupts occur simultaneously, the **IP Register (Address B8H)** is consulted. If priorities are equal, the **natural hardware priority order** is applied.

**Step 4 — Context Save:** The current **Program Counter (PC)** value is automatically pushed onto the **stack** (internal RAM, growing upward from **07H**).

**Step 5 — Vector Fetch & Branch:** The CPU loads the PC with the corresponding **Interrupt Vector Address** and begins ISR execution.

**Step 6 — ISR Execution:** The ISR runs. For external interrupts triggered by **level-sensitive** mode, the external pin must be held low by hardware until the ISR executes.

**Step 7 — Return via RETI:** The `RETI` instruction pops the saved PC from the stack and re-enables the interrupt logic at the current priority level.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet & Register Cheat Sheet

#### Table 2.1 — 8051 Interrupt Vector Addresses

| Interrupt Source | Flag Bit | Vector Address | ROM Bytes Allocated |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Reset (Highest Priority)** | — | **0000H** | — |
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | IE0 | **0003H** | **8 bytes** |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | **000BH** | **8 bytes** |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | IE1 | **0013H** | **8 bytes** |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | **001BH** | **8 bytes** |
| Serial Port (TI or RI) | TI / RI | **0023H** | **8 bytes** |

> [!WARNING]
> **8-Byte Limitation:** Each vector address is separated by only **8 bytes**. A typical ISR exceeds this, so programmers *must* place an `LJMP` or `AJMP` instruction at the vector address to redirect execution to the actual ISR located elsewhere in ROM.

#### Table 2.2 — IE Register (Interrupt Enable, Address A8H, Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Name | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
| Function | Global Enable | Reserved | Reserved | Serial Port | Timer 1 | Ext. Int 1 | Timer 0 | Ext. Int 0 |

#### Table 2.3 — IP Register (Interrupt Priority, Address B8H, Bit-Addressable)

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Name | — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |
| Function | Reserved | Reserved | Reserved | Serial Priority | Timer 1 Priority | Ext 1 Priority | Timer 0 Priority | Ext 0 Priority |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Setting a bit to **1** in IP assigns **High Priority**. Bit = **0** assigns **Low Priority** (default).

#### Table 2.4 — Natural Hardware Priority Order (When IP bits are all 0)

| Priority Rank | Interrupt Source |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 (Highest) | External Interrupt 0 (IE0) |
| 2 | Timer 0 Overflow (TF0) |
| 3 | External Interrupt 1 (IE1) |
| 4 | Timer 1 Overflow (TF1) |
| 5 (Lowest) | Serial Port (TI or RI) |

### 2.3 Interrupt Latency Calculation (Real-World Utility)

The latency formula is critical for **real-time control systems** (e.g., anti-lock braking systems, motor controllers):

$$L_{total} = T_{current} + T_{response} + T_{branch}$$

Where:
- $L_{total}$ = Total interrupt latency (in machine cycles)
- $T_{current}$ = Remaining cycles of the currently executing instruction (max = **4 machine cycles** for the longest 8051 instruction: `MUL AB` or `DIV AB`)
- $T_{response}$ = Interrupt sampling delay (**1 machine cycle** for level-triggered, **2 machine cycles** for edge-triggered)
- $T_{branch}$ = Time to push PC and fetch vector (**2 machine cycles**)

**Conversion to time:** Each machine cycle $= \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Application:** For an 8051 with **11.0592 MHz crystal** (commonly used for exact baud rate generation), one machine cycle = **1.085 µs**. Maximum interrupt latency $\approx 7 \times 1.085 \approx 7.6 \mu s$. This deterministic behavior is vital in **industrial automation**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Interrupt Resolution Logic

### 3.1 Derivation of Interrupt Priority Resolution Logic

When multiple interrupts arrive **simultaneously**, the 8051 follows a deterministic two-stage resolution algorithm. We derive this logic formally.

**Given:**
- $n$ interrupt sources with flags $F_1, F_2, \ldots, F_n$
- Corresponding enable bits $E_1, E_2, \ldots, E_n$ (all gated by global $EA$)
- Corresponding priority bits $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_n \in \{0, 1\}$

**Stage 1 — Priority Class Separation:**

The CPU first segregates active interrupts into two pools:

$$H = \{i \mid P_i = 1 \text{ and } E_i = 1 \text{ and } EA = 1 \text{ and } F_i = 1\}$$

$$L = \{i \mid P_i = 0 \text{ and } E_i = 1 \text{ and } EA = 1 \text{ and } F_i = 1\}$$

If $H \neq \emptyset$, the highest-ranked interrupt in $H$ is serviced first. If $H = \emptyset$, the highest-ranked interrupt in $L$ is serviced.

**Stage 2 — Within-Class Ranking:**

Within any non-empty pool, the **fixed natural priority order** $\rho$ (defined in Table 2.4) is applied. For the 8051's natural ranking:

$$\rho(IE0) = 1 > \rho(TF0) = 2 > \rho(IE1) = 3 > \rho(TF1) = 4 > \rho(TI/RI) = 5$$

The selected interrupt $i^*$ satisfies:

$$i^* = \arg\min_{i \in H \cup L} \{\rho(i)\}$$

### 3.2 Worked Example — Priority Calculation for f = 11.0592 MHz

**Problem:** Compute the maximum interrupt latency for an 8051 system using an **11.0592 MHz** crystal oscillator, assuming the interrupt is edge-triggered and occurs during the execution of the `MUL AB` instruction.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the duration of one machine cycle.

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6}$$

$$T_{MC} = 1.085 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1.085 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2:** Identify the worst-case current instruction duration.

The `MUL AB` instruction requires **4 machine cycles** (the maximum in 8051).

$$T_{current} = 4 \times T_{MC} = 4 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 4.34 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3:** Identify the response time for edge-triggered interrupt.

For edge-triggered external interrupts, the sampling requires **2 machine cycles**.

$$T_{response} = 2 \times T_{MC} = 2 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 2.17 \, \mu s$$

**Step 4:** Identify the branching time (PC push + vector fetch).

$$T_{branch} = 2 \times T_{MC} = 2 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 2.17 \, \mu s$$

**Step 5:** Sum all components.

$$L_{total} = T_{current} + T_{response} + T_{branch}$$

$$L_{total} = 4.34 \, \mu s + 2.17 \, \mu s + 2.17 \, \mu s$$

$$\boxed{L_{total} = 8.68 \, \mu s}$$

### 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Flag Clearing Mechanism

A common KTU question involves which interrupt flags are **automatically cleared by hardware** versus those requiring **software clearing**.

| Interrupt | Flag Bit | Clearing Mechanism |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| External Interrupt 0 (Edge-Triggered) | IE0 | **Auto-cleared by hardware** on vector fetch |
| External Interrupt 0 (Level-Triggered) | IE0 | **NOT auto-cleared** (must de-assert pin externally) |
| External Interrupt 1 (Edge-Triggered) | IE1 | **Auto-cleared by hardware** on vector fetch |
| External Interrupt 1 (Level-Triggered) | IE1 | **NOT auto-cleared** |
| Timer 0 Overflow | TF0 | **Auto-cleared by hardware** on vector fetch |
| Timer 1 Overflow | TF1 | **Auto-cleared by hardware** on vector fetch |
| Serial Port (Transmit) | TI | **NOT auto-cleared** — must be cleared by software |
| Serial Port (Receive) | RI | **NOT auto-cleared** — must be cleared by software |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** "**T**imer and **E**dge-triggered external interrupts are **T**aken care of by hardware. **S**erial and **L**evel-triggered external interrupts require **S**oftware (mnemonic: **TLS** — **T**ake **L**evel **S**erial by software)."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Interrupt Architecture Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the functional flow of the 8051 interrupt subsystem, showing how a peripheral event propagates through the SFR registers to finally invoke the ISR.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[External INT0 Pin P3.2] --> flagA[IE0 in TCON]
    srcB[Timer 0 Overflow] --> flagB[TF0 in TCON]
    srcC[External INT1 Pin P3.3] --> flagC[IE1 in TCON]
    srcD[Timer 1 Overflow] --> flagD[TF1 in TCON]
    srcE[Serial Port TX RX] --> flagE[TI RI in SCON]

    flagA --> gateA{EX0 and EA}
    flagB --> gateB{ET0 and EA}
    flagC --> gateC{EX1 and EA}
    flagD --> gateD{ET1 and EA}
    flagE --> gateE{ES and EA}

    gateA --> prio[IP Register Priority Arbitration]
    gateB --> prio
    gateC --> prio
    gateD --> prio
    gateE --> prio

    prio --> natpri[Natural Hardware Priority if IP bits equal]

    natpri --> vecfetch[Fetch Vector Address from ROM]

    vecfetch --> pushpc[Push Current PC onto Stack]
    pushpc --> loadpc[Load PC with Vector Address]
    loadpc --> isr[Execute Interrupt Service Routine ISR]
    isr --> reti[RETI Instruction Pops PC from Stack]
    reti --> resume[Resume Main Program Execution]
```

### 4.2 Interrupt Execution Timeline (Sequential State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MainExec: CPU executes main program

    MainExec --> Instruction: Current instruction in fetch decode execute
    Instruction --> InstructionCheck: Machine cycle boundaries
    InstructionCheck --> InterruptPending: Interrupt flag sampled

    InterruptPending --> VectorFetch: Interrupt acknowledged
    VectorFetch --> StackPush: SP increments, PC pushed
    StackPush --> ISRRun: PC loaded with vector

    ISRRun --> ISRContinue: More ISR instructions
    ISRContinue --> ISRContinue: Loop within ISR
    ISRContinue --> RETI: RETI opcode encountered

    RETI --> StackPop: PC restored from stack
    StackPop --> MainExec: Resume main program

    InterruptPending --> MainExec: Interrupt not enabled or masked
```

### 4.3 Interrupt Vector Memory Map (ROM Layout)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addr0[0000H Reset Vector] --> space0[8 bytes for Reset]
    space0 --> addr1[0003H External Int 0]
    addr1 --> space1[8 bytes for INT0 ISR]
    space1 --> addr2[000BH Timer 0 Overflow]
    addr2 --> space2[8 bytes for TF0 ISR]
    space2 --> addr3[0013H External Int 1]
    addr3 --> space3[8 bytes for INT1 ISR]
    space3 --> addr4[001BH Timer 1 Overflow]
    addr4 --> space4[8 bytes for TF1 ISR]
    space4 --> addr5[0023H Serial Port]
    addr5 --> space5[8 bytes for Serial ISR]
    space5 --> userprog[User Program Space Beyond 002BH]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Insight:** Notice how the vector addresses are spaced exactly **8 bytes** apart in hex (0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H). This architectural choice reflects the original Intel design constraint where a short `LJMP` (3 bytes) fits exactly into the 8-byte gap, allowing redirection to the actual ISR.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Define an interrupt. List all the interrupts of the 8051 microcontroller along with their vector addresses.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **interrupt** is an asynchronous event that temporarily suspends the main program execution, forcing the CPU to execute a dedicated subroutine called the **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**, and then return to the main program via the `RETI` instruction.

The 8051 has **5 interrupt sources** (excluding Reset):

| Interrupt | Vector Address |
| :--- | :---: |
| Reset | 0000H |
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | 0003H |
| Timer 0 Overflow (TF0) | 000BH |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | 0013H |
| Timer 1 Overflow (TF1) | 001BH |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | 0023H |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between level-triggered and edge-triggered external interrupts in 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Level-Triggered | Edge-Triggered |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Trigger condition | Low level (0V) on INT0/INT1 pin | Falling edge (H→L transition) |
| TCON bit | IT0/IT1 = **0** | IT0/IT1 = **1** |
| Flag clearing | NOT auto-cleared; external pin must be de-asserted by hardware | Auto-cleared by hardware on ISR entry |
| Pulse requirement | Pin must stay low until ISR executes | Pulse must be high then low (min 1 machine cycle each) |

---

### PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **Interrupt Enable (IE) Register** and **Interrupt Priority (IP) Register** of the 8051 microcontroller in detail. Describe the role of each bit with a neat diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal**. An external interrupt is triggered just as the CPU begins executing the `MUL AB` instruction. Calculate the **worst-case interrupt latency** in microseconds, assuming level-triggered mode. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

The **IE Register** at SFR address **A8H** is bit-addressable and controls which interrupts are permitted to be recognized by the CPU.

- **EA (IE.7) — Global Enable:** Must be set to 1 for any interrupt to be acknowledged.
- **ES (IE.4) — Serial Port Enable:** Enables the TI/RI interrupt.
- **ET1 (IE.3) — Timer 1 Interrupt Enable.**
- **EX1 (IE.2) — External Interrupt 1 Enable.**
- **ET0 (IE.1) — Timer 0 Interrupt Enable.**
- **EX0 (IE.0) — External Interrupt 0 Enable.**

A typical initialization: `MOV IE, #82H` enables only Timer 0 overflow (`ET0 = 1`, `EA = 1`).

The **IP Register** at SFR address **B8H** assigns one of two priority levels to each interrupt. Setting a bit to 1 makes the corresponding interrupt **high priority**; otherwise, it is **low priority**.

When two interrupts of different priority occur simultaneously, the high-priority interrupt is serviced first. When two interrupts of the **same priority** occur simultaneously, the natural hardware priority order is used (INT0 > TF0 > INT1 > TF1 > Serial).

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given: $f_{OSC} = 12 \text{ MHz}$, level-triggered mode.

**Step 1:** Calculate machine cycle period.

$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s$$

**Step 2:** Worst-case current instruction = `MUL AB` = **4 machine cycles**.

$$T_{current} = 4 \times 1 \, \mu s = 4 \, \mu s$$

**Step 3:** Level-triggered response time = **1 machine cycle**.

$$T_{response} = 1 \, \mu s$$

**Step 4:** Branch time (PC save + vector fetch) = **2 machine cycles**.

$$T_{branch} = 2 \, \mu s$$

**Step 5:** Total latency.

$$L_{total} = 4 + 1 + 2 = \boxed{7 \, \mu s}$$

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** What is the significance of the `RETI` instruction? Why can't we use a normal `RET` instruction to return from an ISR? Describe the complete **sequence of events** that occurs when an external interrupt INT0 is triggered in the 8051. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **interrupt nesting**. How does the 8051 handle nested interrupts through the IP register? Provide a real-world example where nesting is essential. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) Solution:**

The **`RETI` (Return from Interrupt)** instruction does two critical things:
1. **Pops the PC** from the stack, restoring execution to the main program.
2. **Informs the interrupt controller** that the current ISR has completed, thereby allowing further interrupts of the **same priority level** to be recognized again.

A normal `RET` instruction only pops the PC; it does **not** signal the interrupt hardware, which can lead to logical errors and missed subsequent interrupts.

**Complete sequence for INT0 trigger:**

1. The external hardware pulls the **INT0 (P3.2) pin LOW** (level) or produces a falling edge.
2. The **IE0 flag in TCON** is set.
3. The CPU samples the interrupt at the end of the next machine cycle.
4. It verifies that **EA = 1** and **EX0 = 1** in the IE register.
5. The current instruction completes.
6. The CPU automatically pushes the **16-bit PC** onto the stack (two pushes: low byte then high byte).
7. The PC is loaded with vector address **0003H**.
8. The ISR at 0003H executes.
9. The `RETI` instruction is encountered, popping the PC and returning to main.

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Interrupt Nesting** is the ability of a **higher-priority interrupt** to preempt (interrupt) a **lower-priority ISR** that is currently executing.

**8051 Nesting Mechanism:**

When a high-priority interrupt (IP bit = 1) occurs while a low-priority ISR is running:
- The low-priority ISR is suspended.
- Its PC is pushed onto the stack.
- The high-priority ISR executes to completion.
- The `RETI` of the high-priority ISR returns to the suspended low-priority ISR.
- The low-priority ISR's `RETI` returns to the main program.

**Real-World Example:** In an **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS)** of a car:
- **Low priority:** Routine wheel speed sensor updates (TF0 timer interrupt).
- **High priority:** Emergency brake pedal press (External INT0).

If the driver suddenly slams the brake while routine sensors are being read, the high-priority interrupt must immediately preempt the low-priority ISR to deploy ABS within microseconds. Without nesting, braking safety would be compromised.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting EA = 1:** Students often set EX0/ET1 but forget the **Global Enable bit EA**. Without EA = 1, *no* interrupt will be recognized. This is the #1 reason for "program not entering ISR" complaints.
> 2. **Confusing RET and RETI:** Using `RET` instead of `RETI` will cause the interrupt logic to remain in a "busy" state, blocking all subsequent interrupts of the same priority.
> 3. **Ignoring the 8-byte limit:** Placing ISR code directly at the vector address without an `LJMP` will cause code to overlap with the next interrupt's vector, corrupting both ISRs.
> 4. **Level-triggered flag not cleared:** In level-triggered mode, if the external pin remains LOW after ISR exit, the 8051 will **re-enter the ISR immediately**, causing infinite recursion.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- **Interrupt Sources:** 5 (INT0, TF0, INT1, TF1, TI/RI) + Reset.
- **Vector Addresses:** 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H (each separated by 8 bytes).
- **IE Register (A8H):** EA = global; EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES = individual enables.
- **IP Register (B8H):** PX0, PT0, PX1, PT1, PS = priority assignment (1 = high).
- **Natural Priority Order:** INT0 > TF0 > INT1 > TF1 > Serial Port.
- **Trigger Modes:** IT0/IT1 in TCON = 0 → Level; = 1 → Edge.
- **Auto-cleared Flags:** IE0, IE1 (edge only), TF0, TF1.
- **Software-cleared Flags:** TI, RI, IE0/IE1 (in level mode).
- **Latency Range:** 3–7 machine cycles (3 µs – 7 µs at 12 MHz).
- **RETI vs RET:** RETI both pops PC and clears internal interrupt busy flag.
- **8051 Limitations:** Only 2 priority levels; no native support for >2 levels of nesting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stack and Stack Pointer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stack and Stack Pointer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller Architecture**, the **Stack** is a dedicated, Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) data structure implemented in the **Internal RAM (IRAM)** of the microcontroller, used by the CPU for the temporary storage of critical data, most notably the **Program Counter (PC)** during subroutine calls (`LCALL`, `ACALL`) and interrupt servicing. The **Stack Pointer (SP)** is an **8-bit Special Function Register (SFR)** with the internal address **81H** that always holds the address of the **topmost element (TOS – Top of Stack)** currently present in the stack. Because the SP is only 8 bits wide, the 8051 stack is inherently restricted to the lower **128 bytes (00H to 7FH)** of the Internal RAM, and it is automatically initialized to **07H** upon any system reset (cold or warm).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The 8051 Stack Pointer is a pure 8-bit register. It can only address the lower 128 bytes of Internal RAM. This is fundamentally different from processors like the x86 or ARM, where the stack pointer is 16 bits wide and can address external memory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you walk into a busy cafeteria and pick up a **clean tray from a spring-loaded stack** of trays.

1. You can only access the **topmost tray** (this is the Top of Stack).
2. The cafeteria worker places new, clean trays on the **top** of the pile.
3. When you return a tray, you place it back on the **top** of the pile.
4. The trays at the **bottom** are inaccessible until all the trays above them are removed.

This is precisely how a **LIFO (Last-In, First-Out)** stack operates. The Stack Pointer is like an invisible finger pointing exactly at the topmost tray. The 8051 uses this mechanism to remember where it was in the main program before it "jumps" to execute a subroutine. When the subroutine finishes, the 8051 uses the stack to find its way back to the exact spot in the main program.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The stack is not just a programming convenience; it is the **backbone of all function calls, nested interrupts, and context saving** in embedded systems. In real-time control systems (like ABS braking in cars or industrial PLCs), improperly sized stacks cause silent data corruption and system crashes, a phenomenon called **Stack Overflow**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Default SP value after RESET:** **07H**
- **SP SFR Address:** **81H** (in the Special Function Register space)
- **Maximum usable Internal RAM for stack:** **128 bytes (00H – 7FH)**
- **Maximum stack depth (in bytes):** **121 bytes** (from 08H to 7FH if default is used, or 128 bytes if SP is reinitialized to 00H)
- **Direction of growth:** **Upward** (SP increments as data is pushed, decrements as data is popped)
- **Stack size per PUSH:** **1 byte** (The 8051 PUSH/POP operate on 8-bit data, not 16-bit, unlike some other architectures)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Architectural Role of the Stack

The 8051 uses the stack for **three primary tasks**:

1. **Saving the Return Address** when a subroutine call instruction (`LCALL` or `ACALL`) is executed.
2. **Saving the Current State** (typically the PSW – Program Status Word) at the start of an Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) before the CPU vectors to the interrupt handler.
3. **Temporary Data Storage** for program variables (although this is strongly discouraged in embedded C best practices).

## 2.2 Operational Logic of PUSH and POP

The 8051 provides two dedicated stack instructions: `PUSH` and `POP`.

### 2.2.1 The PUSH Operation
When the 8051 executes a `PUSH direct` instruction (e.g., `PUSH ACC`), the following micro-operations occur sequentially:

1. The **Stack Pointer (SP) is incremented by 1** automatically by the CPU hardware.
2. The contents of the specified **direct internal RAM address** (e.g., the Accumulator) are **copied** into the new RAM location pointed to by the SP.

**Generic Algebraic Expression:**

$$
\text{RAM}_{(\text{SP} + 1)} \leftarrow \text{RAM}_{(\text{direct})}
$$

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1
$$

### 2.2.2 The POP Operation
When the 8051 executes a `POP direct` instruction (e.g., `POP PSW`), the following micro-operations occur:

1. The contents of the RAM location currently pointed to by the **SP are copied** into the specified `direct` address.
2. The **SP is decremented by 1** automatically.

**Generic Algebraic Expression:**

$$
\text{RAM}_{(\text{direct})} \leftarrow \text{RAM}_{(\text{SP})}
$$

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Critical Asymmetry:** Notice that PUSH increments **before** storing, while POP reads **before** decrementing. This subtle order is a classic **KTU 2-mark and 7-mark question** trap. Mixing up the order will completely corrupt the stack state.

## 2.3 Reset Initialization of the Stack Pointer

Upon a hardware reset (power-on or RESET pin HIGH), the 8051 hardware automatically writes the value **07H** into the SP register. This means the very first PUSH will write data to Internal RAM address **08H**, not 07H. This default location is dangerous because Internal RAM addresses 08H to 1FH constitute **Register Banks 1, 2, and 3**, and addresses 20H to 2FH are the **Bit-Addressable area**. Conflicting with these areas can cause severe program errors.

**Best Engineering Practice (KTU Frequently Asked):**

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{\#}60\text{H} \quad \text{(or 70H, depending on application)}
$$

This shifts the stack safely into the upper region of the Internal RAM.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical / Logical Representation | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| SP Register SFR Address | $81\text{H}$ | Special Function Register |
| Default SP after RESET | $\text{SP} = 07\text{H}$ | Set by hardware |
| PUSH operation (2 steps) | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1$; then $\text{RAM}_{\text{SP}} \leftarrow \text{direct}$ | Order is critical |
| POP operation (2 steps) | $\text{direct} \leftarrow \text{RAM}_{\text{SP}}$; then $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 1$ | Order is critical |
| Stack Direction | Upward (Increment on PUSH) | Unique to 8051 (not downward) |
| Stack Memory Range | $00\text{H}$ to $7\text{FH}$ | 128 bytes of IRAM only |
| Return Address Size (LCALL) | 2 bytes (16-bit PC) | Pushed as 2 separate bytes |
| LCALL Stack Effect | $\text{SP} \uparrow$ by 2 | PC-low pushed first, then PC-high |
| RET Stack Effect | $\text{SP} \downarrow$ by 2 | PC-high popped first, then PC-low |
| Safe SP initialization | $\text{MOV SP, } \60\text{H}$ | Avoids register banks |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem: Tracking Stack Evolution

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
Given the following 8051 assembly code snippet, track the state of the Stack Pointer and the corresponding Internal RAM locations. Assume the **Stack Pointer is initially set to 60H**, and the Accumulator (A) contains the value **55H**, and Register R0 contains **AAH**.

```assembly
MOV  A,   #55H       ; Load 55H into A
MOV  R0,  #AAH       ; Load AAH into R0
PUSH ACC             ; (1) Push A onto stack
PUSH 00H             ; (2) Push R0 (SFR 00H) onto stack
POP  B               ; (3) Pop top of stack into B
POP ACC              ; (4) Pop next into A
```

### 3.1.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Execution

**Initial State:**
- Internal RAM address 60H = `xxxx` (unknown garbage)
- Internal RAM address 61H = `xxxx`
- Internal RAM address 62H = `xxxx`
- $\text{SP} = 60\text{H}$
- $\text{A} = 55\text{H}$
- $\text{R0} = \text{AAH}$

**Step 1: `MOV A, #55H`**
This is a non-stack operation. It simply loads the literal value 55H into the Accumulator.
- Result: $\text{A} = 55\text{H}$; $\text{SP} = 60\text{H}$ (unchanged)

**Step 2: `MOV R0, #AAH`**
Loads the literal value AAH into R0 (which is SFR 00H).
- Result: $\text{R0} = \text{AAH}$; $\text{SP} = 60\text{H}$ (unchanged)

**Step 3: `PUSH ACC`**
Per the PUSH operational logic, the CPU first increments SP, then stores.

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 1 = 60\text{H} + 1 = 61\text{H}
$$

$$
\text{RAM}_{61\text{H}} = \text{A} = 55\text{H}
$$

- Result: $\text{SP} = 61\text{H}$; $\text{RAM}_{61\text{H}} = 55\text{H}$

**Step 4: `PUSH 00H`**
This pushes the contents of internal RAM address 00H (which is R0).

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 1 = 61\text{H} + 1 = 62\text{H}
$$

$$
\text{RAM}_{62\text{H}} = \text{RAM}_{00\text{H}} = \text{AAH}
$$

- Result: $\text{SP} = 62\text{H}$; $\text{RAM}_{62\text{H}} = \text{AAH}$

**Step 5: `POP B`**
Per the POP operational logic, the CPU first reads, then decrements.

$$
\text{B} = \text{RAM}_{62\text{H}} = \text{AAH}
$$

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 1 = 62\text{H} - 1 = 61\text{H}
$$

- Result: $\text{SP} = 61\text{H}$; $\text{B} = \text{AAH}$

**Step 6: `POP ACC`**

$$
\text{A} = \text{RAM}_{61\text{H}} = 55\text{H}
$$

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 1 = 61\text{H} - 1 = 60\text{H}
$$

- Result: $\text{SP} = 60\text{H}$ (restored to initial value); $\text{A} = 55\text{H}$

### 3.1.2 State Transition Matrix (KTU 5-Mark Table Style)

| Instruction | A | R0 | B | SP | RAM 60H | RAM 61H | RAM 62H |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Initial State | 55H | AAH | xxH | 60H | xx | xx | xx |
| After `PUSH ACC` | 55H | AAH | xxH | 61H | xx | **55H** | xx |
| After `PUSH 00H` | 55H | AAH | xxH | 62H | xx | 55H | **AAH** |
| After `POP B` | 55H | AAH | **AAH** | 61H | xx | 55H | AAH |
| After `POP ACC` | 55H | AAH | AAH | **60H** | xx | 55H | AAH |

## 3.2 Derivative Case: The LCALL Stack Mechanism

A deeper KTU question involves the `LCALL` instruction. `LCALL` is a 3-byte instruction that pushes the **16-bit Program Counter** onto the stack in **two separate 8-bit operations** (because the stack is 8-bit wide).

**Algorithm:**

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1; \quad \text{RAM}_{\text{SP}} \leftarrow \text{PC}_{7 \text{ to } 0} \quad \text{(Low byte pushed FIRST)}
$$

$$
\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1; \quad \text{RAM}_{\text{SP}} \leftarrow \text{PC}_{15 \text{ to } 8} \quad \text{(High byte pushed SECOND)}
$$

During the `RET` (return) instruction, the process is exactly reversed, which is why the high byte is popped **first** to correctly reconstruct the 16-bit PC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Verification:** You can prove this by hand-tracing a simple subroutine that uses LCALL. If the low byte were pushed second, the return from the subroutine would jump to an address with the correct high byte but the swapped low byte — a classic "off-by-one" bug in embedded firmware.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Stack Subsystem

Because a physical drawing of RAM is repetitive, we use a **Functional Architecture Flow** to map the precise interactions between the CPU, the SP register, and the Internal RAM during PUSH and POP.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CPU["CPU Execution Unit"]
    DECODER["Instruction Decoder"]
    ALU["ALU / Data Bus"]
    SPReg["Stack Pointer Register SFR 81H"]
    RAMBlock["Internal RAM 00H to 7FH"]
    TOS["Top of Stack TOS Location"]
    DirectAddr["Direct Address Operand"]

    CPU --> DECODER
    DECODER -->|Detects PUSH opcode| SPReg
    DECODER -->|Detects POP opcode| SPReg
    SPReg -->|Holds current address| TOS
    TOS -->|Physically resides in| RAMBlock
    DirectAddr -->|Source for PUSH| RAMBlock
    RAMBlock -->|Destination for POP| DirectAddr
    ALU -->|Bidirectional data path| RAMBlock
```

## 4.2 State Diagram: The PUSH / POP Cycle

This diagram illustrates the **strict sequential nature** of the two micro-operations. Note the directional arrows showing the "increment-then-store" and "read-then-decrement" logic.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> Idle: SP = 60H
    Idle --> PUSH_Increment: Executes PUSH ACC
    PUSH_Increment --> PUSH_Write: SP becomes 61H
    PUSH_Write --> Idle: RAM 61H is loaded with A
    Idle --> POP_Read: Executes POP B
    POP_Read --> POP_Decrement: B receives value from RAM at SP
    POP_Decrement --> Idle: SP becomes 60H again
```

## 4.3 Stack Growth Visualization (LIFO Order)

This block diagram shows how a series of PUSH operations physically stack data in upward-growing RAM, mimicking the cafeteria tray analogy.

```mermaid
flowchart BT
    A0["Address 60H : (Empty / Available)"]
    A1["Address 61H : First Pushed Value 55H (Older)"]
    A2["Address 62H : Second Pushed Value AAH (Newer - TOS)"]
    SP["SP points here = 62H"]
    A2 --> SP
```

**Reading the diagram (Bottom-Up):**
- Address 60H is the "floor" of our stack.
- The bottom-most pushed value (55H) sits one byte above the original SP.
- The top-most pushed value (AAH) is where the SP currently points.
- A POP would remove AAH first, then 55H on the subsequent POP, strictly respecting LIFO order.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Stack Pointer in the 8051 microcontroller? What is its default value after a system reset, and why is it considered risky to use this default in real applications?**

**Model Answer:**
The Stack Pointer (SP) is an 8-bit Special Function Register located at SFR address **81H**. It always holds the address of the top of the stack in the 8051's internal RAM. Upon reset, the SP is automatically initialized to **07H** by the 8051 hardware. This default is risky because the first PUSH operation will write to Internal RAM address 08H, which overlaps with **Register Banks 1, 2, and 3** (addresses 08H–1FH) and the **Bit-Addressable Area** (20H–2FH). Pushing data into these regions will corrupt program variables, cause register bank switching errors, and lead to unpredictable embedded system behavior. The standard industry practice is to reinitialize the SP to a safer value, such as **60H or 70H**, using the `MOV SP, #value` instruction.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the PUSH and POP operations in the 8051. State clearly the order of updating the Stack Pointer in each case.**

**Model Answer:**
The **PUSH** instruction stores the contents of a specified internal RAM location onto the stack. It operates in two strict steps: first, the Stack Pointer is **incremented by 1**, and second, the data is written to the new RAM address pointed to by the updated SP. Conversely, the **POP** instruction retrieves data from the top of the stack into a specified internal RAM location. It also operates in two steps: first, the data at the current SP address is **read and copied** to the destination, and second, the SP is **decremented by 1**. The critical distinction is that PUSH updates the SP **before** writing, while POP updates the SP **after** reading. Mixing this order causes irreversible stack corruption.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the organization of the 8051 stack in the Internal RAM. Discuss the consequences of stack overflow in an 8051-based embedded system. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Analyze the following 8051 assembly code and determine the final values of the Accumulator (A), Register B, and the Stack Pointer (SP) after complete execution. Assume the initial SP is 60H, A = 00H, and B = 00H. **(7 Marks)**

```assembly
MOV  A,    #0F0H
MOV  R0,   #0AH
PUSH ACC
PUSH 00H
MOV  A,    #55H
MOV  R0,   #66H
POP  B
POP  ACC
```

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Stack Organization and Overflow:**

The 8051 stack resides in the **Internal RAM (IRAM)**, specifically in the lower 128 bytes (00H to 7FH). The Stack Pointer (SP, SFR 81H) is an 8-bit register that addresses the top of the stack. The stack grows **upward**; every PUSH increments the SP and stores 1 byte of data, and every POP reads 1 byte and decrements the SP.

The IRAM is partitioned as follows:
- **00H–07H:** Register Bank 0
- **08H–0FH:** Register Bank 1
- **10H–17H:** Register Bank 2
- **18H–1FH:** Register Bank 3
- **20H–2FH:** Bit-Addressable Area
- **30H–7FH:** General Purpose RAM (Scratchpad)

A **Stack Overflow** occurs when a series of PUSH operations (e.g., from deeply nested subroutine calls or recursive interrupts) increment the SP past 7FH (127 decimal). Because the SP is only 8 bits wide, the next increment causes it to **wrap around to 00H**, silently overwriting Register Bank 0, the stack itself, or the bit-addressable memory. Consequences include:
- **Corruption of the return address**, causing the program to jump to arbitrary or illegal memory locations.
- **Corruption of the PSW** (if pushed), which flips the active register bank, causing "ghost" variable updates.
- **System lockup, watchdog timer reset, or complete firmware failure** in safety-critical applications.

**(b) Step-by-Step Execution Trace:**

| Step | Instruction | A | R0 | B | SP | RAM 61H | RAM 62H |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Init | (Initial values) | 00H | 00H | 00H | 60H | xx | xx |
| 1 | `MOV A, #0F0H` | **0F0H** | 00H | 00H | 60H | xx | xx |
| 2 | `MOV R0, #0AH` | 0F0H | **0AH** | 00H | 60H | xx | xx |
| 3 | `PUSH ACC` | 0F0H | 0AH | 00H | **61H** | **0F0H** | xx |
| 4 | `PUSH 00H` | 0F0H | 0AH | 00H | **62H** | 0F0H | **0AH** |
| 5 | `MOV A, #55H` | **55H** | 0AH | 00H | 62H | 0F0H | 0AH |
| 6 | `MOV R0, #66H` | 55H | **66H** | 00H | 62H | 0F0H | 0AH |
| 7 | `POP B` | 55H | 66H | **0AH** | **61H** | 0F0H | 0AH |
| 8 | `POP ACC` | **0F0H** | 66H | 0AH | **60H** | 0F0H | 0AH |

**Final Answers:**
- **A = 0F0H**
- **B = 0AH**
- **SP = 60H** (fully restored to its initial value, proving the LIFO property).

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary Exam]**

**(a)** Explain in detail how the 8051 uses the stack to handle subroutine calls (`LCALL`) and interrupts. Illustrate with a timing diagram or step sequence showing how the 16-bit Program Counter is pushed onto the 8-bit stack. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A programmer initializes the Stack Pointer to 70H. Subsequently, two LCALL instructions are executed consecutively, followed by two RET instructions. Determine the final value of the SP and the exact contents of the RAM locations 70H, 71H, 72H, and 73H, assuming the 16-bit return address pushed by the first LCALL is **1234H** and by the second LCALL is **5678H**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Subroutine and Interrupt Stack Handling:**

When the 8051 executes an `LCALL address16` instruction, the CPU automatically saves the 16-bit Program Counter (the address of the instruction immediately following LCALL) onto the stack. Because the 8051 stack operates on 8-bit bytes, the 16-bit PC is split into two 8-bit halves and pushed sequentially:

- **Step 1:** SP is incremented by 1. The **low byte** of the PC (bits 0–7) is written to the new SP location.
- **Step 2:** SP is incremented by 1 again. The **high byte** of the PC (bits 8–15) is written to the new SP location.
- **Step 3:** The PC is loaded with the 16-bit subroutine address, and execution jumps.

When the corresponding `RET` instruction is executed at the end of the subroutine, the reverse process occurs:
- **Step 1:** The high byte is read from the current SP location into PC_high.
- **Step 2:** SP is decremented by 1.
- **Step 3:** The low byte is read into PC_low.
- **Step 4:** SP is decremented by 1, and execution resumes at the reconstructed 16-bit address.

**Interrupt Handling:** Upon detecting a hardware interrupt, the 8051 hardware automatically pushes the current PC (the address where execution was paused) onto the stack using the exact same two-step mechanism. Additionally, the current Program Status Word (PSW) may be pushed by user code at the start of the ISR to preserve the register bank selection and flag states.

**(b) Numerical Trace:**

Initial state: $\text{SP} = 70\text{H}$

**First `LCALL` (Return Address 1234H):**
- $\text{SP} \rightarrow 71\text{H}$; $\text{RAM}_{71\text{H}} = 34\text{H}$ (low byte pushed first)
- $\text{SP} \rightarrow 72\text{H}$; $\text{RAM}_{72\text{H}} = 12\text{H}$ (high byte pushed second)

**Second `LCALL` (Return Address 5678H):**
- $\text{SP} \rightarrow 73\text{H}$; $\text{RAM}_{73\text{H}} = 78\text{H}$
- $\text{SP} \rightarrow 74\text{H}$; $\text{RAM}_{74\text{H}} = 56\text{H}$

**First `RET` (Returns from 5678H):**
- Read high byte: $\text{PC\_high} = \text{RAM}_{74\text{H}} = 56\text{H}$; $\text{SP} \rightarrow 73\text{H}$
- Read low byte: $\text{PC\_low} = \text{RAM}_{73\text{H}} = 78\text{H}$; $\text{SP} \rightarrow 72\text{H}$
- PC reconstructed as 5678H.

**Second `RET` (Returns from 1234H):**
- Read high byte: $\text{PC\_high} = \text{RAM}_{72\text{H}} = 12\text{H}$; $\text{SP} \rightarrow 71\text{H}$
- Read low byte: $\text{PC\_low} = \text{RAM}_{71\text{H}} = 34\text{H}$; $\text{SP} \rightarrow 70\text{H}$
- PC reconstructed as 1234H.

**Final Answers:**
- **SP = 70H** (perfectly restored)
- **RAM 70H = xxH** (untouched)
- **RAM 71H = 34H** (low byte of first LCALL)
- **RAM 72H = 12H** (high byte of first LCALL)
- **RAM 73H = 78H** (low byte of second LCALL)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **The PUSH/POP Order Trap (Most Common, -2 Marks):** Students often write that PUSH writes first and then increments, or that POP decrements first and then reads. **This is wrong.** The 8051 hardware strictly follows: PUSH = increment-then-store; POP = read-then-decrement. Any deviation in the order will cost you full marks.
> 2. **The LCALL Byte Order Trap (-2 Marks):** When a 16-bit return address is pushed, the **low byte is pushed FIRST (at the lower address)**, and the high byte is pushed SECOND (at the higher address). Do not write them in the reverse order; the RET instruction relies on this exact sequence.
> 3. **The Default SP Trap (-1 Mark):** Never answer that the default stack pointer is 00H. It is **07H**, and the very first push therefore writes to address 08H. Examiners will deduct marks if you confuse the default value with the first PUSH address.
> 4. **The "Stack in External RAM" Misconception (-1 Mark):** The 8-bit SP cannot point to external data memory. Do not claim the stack can grow into XRAM. The stack is strictly confined to the lower 128 bytes of Internal RAM (00H–7FH).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** The 8051 stack is a LIFO data structure in Internal RAM, managed by the 8-bit Stack Pointer (SFR **81H**).
- **Default SP after RESET:** **07H**, which is **risky**; always reinitialize to **60H or higher**.
- **PUSH Operation:** Increment SP first, then write data to the new SP address.
- **POP Operation:** Read data from the current SP address first, then decrement SP.
- **Stack Growth Direction:** **Upward** (towards 7FH). This is rare; most modern microcontrollers grow downward.
- **LCALL / Interrupt Stack Effect:** The 16-bit return address is pushed in **two bytes** (low byte first, then high byte), consuming **2 bytes of stack space per call**.
- **RET Effect:** Reads the high byte first, then the low byte, then decrements SP twice.
- **Stack Overflow:** Occurs when SP > 7FH. The next PUSH wraps SP to 00H, corrupting Register Bank 0 and the bit-addressable area.
- **PUSH/POP Operands:** Must be a **direct internal RAM address** (e.g., `PUSH ACC`, `PUSH 00H`, `PUSH PSW`). You cannot PUSH an immediate value directly (e.g., `PUSH #55H` is illegal in 8051 assembly).
- **Best Practice:** Reserve the upper region of IRAM (e.g., 60H–7FH) for the stack to prevent collisions with register banks and bit-addressable memory.
- **Symmetry Check:** A balanced sequence of PUSH and POP instructions will always return the SP to its original value — a useful property for debugging embedded firmware.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Addressing Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Addressing Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **Intel 8051 microcontroller architecture**, an **Addressing Mode** is a formalized method or rule that specifies *how* the operand(s) of a machine instruction are referenced, located, and accessed by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) during the fetch-decode-execute cycle. It defines the pathway through which the instruction obtains its required data (operand) from memory, registers, or the instruction code itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "Addressing modes describe the formats by which the processor accesses data stored in memory or registers to execute an instruction. They determine the method used to locate the operand(s) referenced by an instruction."

The 8051 microcontroller supports **five (5) fundamental addressing modes**, each optimized for specific instruction scenarios:

| # | Addressing Mode | Mnemonic Notation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Immediate | `#data` |
| 2 | Register | `Rn` |
| 3 | Direct | `direct address` |
| 4 | Register Indirect | `@Rn` |
| 5 | Indexed | `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC` |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍕

Imagine you are a **pizza delivery boy** and your manager hands you a sealed envelope containing an order. The "address" of the customer can be communicated to you in **five different ways** — analogous to the five addressing modes of the 8051:

- **Immediate Addressing** → *"Deliver 2 pizzas"* — The data (`2`) is **already in the envelope** with the instruction.
- **Register Addressing** → *"Deliver to locker #5"* — You look in a specific **on-body pocket** (one of 8 register banks R0–R7).
- **Direct Addressing** → *"Deliver to Flat No. 30, ABC Apartments"* — The exact **memory address** is given in the instruction.
- **Register Indirect Addressing** → *"Look in locker #5, the number written there is the flat number to deliver to"* — A register **contains a pointer** to the actual address.
- **Indexed Addressing** → *"Start at base address 2000, and add 15 to reach the target"* — Used for **lookup tables** where the offset is in the accumulator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study addressing modes?**
> 1. Determines **instruction length** (1, 2, or 3 bytes).
> 2. Affects **execution time** (machine cycles).
> 3. Optimizes **program memory usage** in embedded C/Assembly.
> 4. Critical for **pointer-based operations** in C compilers (e.g., Keil µVision IDE).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **8051 Program Memory Size** = **64 KB** (0000H to FFFFH)
> - **8051 Internal RAM (idata)** = **128 bytes** (00H to 7FH)
> - **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** = **128 bytes** (80H to FFH)
> - **Register Banks** = **4 banks** × **8 registers** (R0–R7) = **32 bytes** (00H–1FH)
> - **Bit-addressable RAM** = **16 bytes** (20H–2FH) → **128 addressable bits**
> - **DPTR (Data Pointer)** = **16-bit register** (DPH + DPL) → can address up to **64 KB** of external data memory.
> - **PC (Program Counter)** = **16-bit** register (used in indexed addressing with `@A+PC`).
> - **Accumulator (A or Acc)** = **8-bit** primary register for arithmetic/logic operations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Addressing Modes — Structured Breakdown

### 🔹 Mode 1: Immediate Addressing (`#data`)

**Definition:** The operand (data) is a **constant value** that is part of the instruction itself, immediately following the opcode byte.

**Operational Logic:**
- Step 1: CPU fetches the **opcode** from program memory.
- Step 2: The next byte(s) fetched IS the actual data — no further memory access required.
- Step 3: Data is loaded directly into the destination register.

**Example:**
```assembly
MOV A, #25H     ; Load the constant 0x25 into Accumulator
MOV DPTR, #2000H ; Load 16-bit constant 0x2000 into DPTR
```

**Why it matters:** Used to initialize registers, set constant values, and load lookup table base addresses. **No memory variable required** — efficient for constants.

---

### 🔹 Mode 2: Register Addressing (`Rn`)

**Definition:** The operand resides in one of the **eight working registers R0–R7** of the currently selected register bank.

**Operational Logic:**
- Step 1: Opcode specifies which register (`000` for R0 to `111` for R7).
- Step 2: The 3 LSBs of the opcode byte act as the register selector.
- Step 3: CPU accesses the register directly in the **Register Bank** of internal RAM (selected via PSW bits RS0, RS1).

**Example:**
```assembly
MOV A, R0       ; Copy content of R0 into A
ADD A, R1       ; A = A + R1
```

**Bank Selection via PSW:**
- RS1=0, RS0=0 → **Bank 0** (00H–07H)
- RS1=0, RS0=1 → **Bank 1** (08H–0FH)
- RS1=1, RS0=0 → **Bank 2** (10H–17H)
- RS1=1, RS0=1 → **Bank 3** (18H–1FH)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why only R0–R7?** The 8051 opcode uses 3 bits (encoded within the instruction byte) to identify the register. Thus, only 8 registers can be directly addressed.

---

### 🔹 Mode 3: Direct Addressing (`direct`)

**Definition:** The instruction contains the **8-bit direct address** of the operand in internal RAM or SFR space.

**Operational Logic:**
- Step 1: CPU fetches opcode (1 byte).
- Step 2: CPU fetches the **address byte** (the second byte of the instruction).
- Step 3: CPU uses this address to access internal RAM (00H–7FH) or SFR (80H–FFH).
- Step 4: Data is transferred between A and the addressed location.

**Example:**
```assembly
MOV A, 30H      ; Copy byte at internal RAM location 30H into A
MOV 30H, A      ; Copy A into location 30H
MOV P1, #0FFH   ; Write to Port 1 SFR (address 90H)
```

**SFR Address Map (Key Registers):**
- Accumulator → **E0H**
- B Register → **F0H**
- Port 0 → **80H**
- Port 1 → **90H**
- Port 2 → **A0H**
- Port 3 → **B0H**
- SP → **81H**
- PSW → **D0H**
- DPTR → **82H (DPL), 83H (DPH)**

---

### 🔹 Mode 4: Register Indirect Addressing (`@Rn`)

**Definition:** The address of the operand is **contained in a register** (R0 or R1, or the 16-bit Stack Pointer SP for some cases). The `@` symbol denotes "contents of" — i.e., the register holds a pointer.

**Operational Logic:**
- Step 1: The chosen register (R0 or R1) holds the **target memory address**.
- Step 2: CPU reads the register → uses the value AS an address → fetches the operand from that address.
- Step 3: For external data memory access, the 16-bit DPTR is used with `MOVX`.

**Example:**
```assembly
MOV R0, #40H    ; R0 = 40H (pointer to RAM location)
MOV A, @R0      ; A = [40H] — read from address pointed by R0
MOV @R0, A      ; [40H] = A — write to address pointed by R0
```

**Critical Use Cases:**
- **Array traversal:** Increment R0 to scan through a table.
- **Stack operations** (PUSH/POP internally use SP).
- **External memory access** (MOVX uses @R0/@R1/@DPTR).

---

### 🔹 Mode 5: Indexed Addressing (`@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC`)

**Definition:** The effective address is computed as the **sum of a base address** (held in DPTR or PC) **plus the offset** stored in the Accumulator (A). Used almost exclusively for **lookup table access** with `MOVC` (Move Code).

**Operational Logic:**
- Step 1: Load DPTR (or use current PC) with the base address of a table.
- Step 2: Load the index/offset value into A.
- Step 3: CPU computes effective address = `DPTR + A` (or `PC + A`).
- Step 4: Reads the byte at that computed address from program memory.

**Example:**
```assembly
MOV DPTR, #0400H  ; Base address of LUT in program memory
MOV A, #05H       ; Index (offset)
MOVC A, @A+DPTR   ; A = [0400H + 05H] = byte at 0405H
```

> [!NOTE]
> The `@A+PC` form uses the **current PC value** as the base — useful when the LUT is located **right after** the MOVC instruction. `PC` automatically points to the next instruction.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📊

### Table A: Addressing Mode Comparison Matrix

| S.No | Mode | Operand Location | Instr. Bytes | Cycles | Symbol | Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Immediate | In instruction (code) | 2 / 3 | 1 | `#data` | `MOV A, #25H` |
| 2 | Register | R0–R7 (current bank) | 1 | 1 | `Rn` | `MOV A, R0` |
| 3 | Direct | Internal RAM / SFR | 2 / 3 | 1 / 2 | `direct` | `MOV A, 30H` |
| 4 | Register Indirect | @R0 or @R1 or @DPTR | 1 / 2 | 1 / 2 | `@Rn` | `MOV A, @R0` |
| 5 | Indexed | @A+DPTR or @A+PC | 1 | 2 | `@A+DPTR` | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` |

### Table B: Effective Address (EA) Formulas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Effective Address (EA)** = the actual physical memory location from which the operand is fetched or to which it is written.

| Mode | Effective Address Equation |
|---|---|
| Immediate | **EA = PC** (operand IS the instruction byte) |
| Register | **EA = Register Bank Base + 3-bit reg code** |
| Direct | **EA = 8-bit address field** (from instruction) |
| Register Indirect | **EA = content of R0 or R1 or DPTR** |
| Indexed (DPTR) | **EA = DPTR + A** (16-bit addition) |
| Indexed (PC) | **EA = PC + A** (current PC value + offset) |

### Table C: Memory Access Mapping Summary

> **Note:** In the following notation, `(x)` denotes the *contents of* memory/register `x`, and `$\vert$` denotes logical separation of alternatives.

| Mode | Where Data Comes From | Can Access |
|---|---|---|
| Immediate | Inside instruction | Constants only |
| Register | R0–R7 | Active register bank only |
| Direct | Byte 2 of instruction | Internal RAM 00H–7FH, SFR 80H–FFH |
| Register Indirect | Address in R0, R1, SP, DPTR | Internal RAM 00H–7FH, External RAM (via MOVX) |
| Indexed | Sum of A and DPTR/PC | Program memory (ROM) only — via MOVC |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility 🏭

- **Embedded firmware development** (washing machines, automotive ECUs, IoT sensors): Selecting the right addressing mode reduces code size and execution time.
- **Compiler optimization**: Keil C51 uses register and direct addressing for internal variables, register indirect for pointers, and indexed for switch-case LUTs.
- **Real-time systems**: Time-critical loops prefer **1-byte / 1-cycle** instructions (register, register indirect) to meet tight deadlines.
- **Memory-mapped I/O**: Direct addressing is the *only* way to access SFRs like P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, SCON.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Problem 1 — Opcode Encoding Derivation for Register Addressing

**Problem:** Derive the machine code for the instruction `MOV A, R3` in 8051.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The 8051 instruction set uses an **8-bit opcode** for register-addressing MOV instructions. The format is:

$$
\text{Opcode Byte} = 1110\,1\,rrr
$$

Where:
- The upper 5 bits `11101` = the operation code for "MOV A, Rn".
- The lower 3 bits `rrr` = the 3-bit binary code of the source register (R0–R7).

**Encoding table for `rrr`:**

| Register | 3-bit Code (rrr) |
|---|---|
| R0 | 000 |
| R1 | 001 |
| R2 | 010 |
| R3 | 011 |
| R4 | 100 |
| R5 | 101 |
| R6 | 110 |
| R7 | 111 |

**For R3:**

$$
rrr = 011
$$

**Substituting into the opcode template:**

$$
\text{Opcode} = 1110\,1\,011 = 11101011_2
$$

**Converting to hexadecimal:**

$$
11101011_2 = EB_H
$$

**Final Machine Code:** `EB` (1 byte, 1 machine cycle).

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using logical shift:** Group as `1110 1011`. `1110` = E, `1011` = B. Hence `EBH`. ✓

---

## 3.2 Analytical Problem 2 — Effective Address Calculation for Indexed Addressing

**Problem:** Given the 8051 instructions below, determine the effective address fetched by `MOVC` and the value loaded into A.

```assembly
ORG 0000H
    MOV DPTR, #0400H
    MOV A, #03H
    MOVC A, @A+DPTR
    SJMP $
ORG 0400H
    DB 10H, 20H, 30H, 40H, 50H, 60H, 70H, 80H
END
```

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Initialize DPTR.**

The instruction `MOV DPTR, #0400H` loads the 16-bit constant `0400H` into the DPTR register.

$$
\text{DPTR} = 0400H = 0000\,0100\,0000\,0000_2
$$

**Step 2 — Load offset into A.**

The instruction `MOV A, #03H` loads the value `03H` into the accumulator.

$$
A = 03H = 0000\,0011_2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Effective Address (EA).**

By definition of indexed addressing:

$$
EA = DPTR + A
$$

$$
EA = 0400H + 0003H
$$

Performing 16-bit hexadecimal addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
  & \;\; 0400H \\
+ & \;\;\; 0003H \\
\hline
  & \;\; 0403H
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
EA = 0403H
$$

**Step 4 — Fetch the operand from program memory.**

At program memory address `0400H`, the `DB` directive stores the following byte sequence:

| Address | 0400H | 0401H | 0402H | **0403H** | 0404H | 0405H | 0406H | 0407H |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Data | 10H | 20H | 30H | **40H** | 50H | 60H | 70H | 80H |

The CPU fetches the byte at address `0403H`, which is `40H`.

**Step 5 — Load A.**

$$
A \leftarrow 40H
$$

**Final Result:** After execution, **A = 40H**, and program memory at `0403H` is unchanged.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Problem 3 — Instruction Length Derivation for MOV Variants

**Problem:** Determine the number of bytes, machine cycles, and time taken (assuming an 11.0592 MHz crystal) for the following MOV instructions.

**Instruction 1:** `MOV A, #25H` (Immediate)

- **Opcode byte:** `74H`
- **Data byte:** `25H`
- **Total bytes:** 2
- **Machine cycles:** 1
- **Time calculation:**

$$
f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}
$$

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{exec} = 1 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 1.085\,\mu s
$$

**Instruction 2:** `MOV A, 30H` (Direct)

- **Opcode byte:** `E5H`
- **Address byte:** `30H`
- **Total bytes:** 2
- **Machine cycles:** 1
- **Time:** `1.085 µs`

**Instruction 3:** `MOV A, @R0` (Register Indirect)

- **Opcode byte:** `E2H` (assuming R0)
- **Total bytes:** 1
- **Machine cycles:** 1
- **Time:** `1.085 µs`

**Instruction 4:** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (Indexed)

- **Opcode byte:** `E4H`
- **Total bytes:** 1
- **Machine cycles:** 2
- **Time calculation:**

$$
T_{exec} = 2 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 2.17\,\mu s
$$

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Speed vs. Memory Trade-off

| Mode | Speed (cycles) | Code Size (bytes) | Flexibility | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Register | **1** | **1** | Low (R0–R7 only) | Inner loops, counters |
| Immediate | 1 | 2 | Low (constant only) | Init values, masks |
| Direct | 1 | 2 | High (full RAM/SFR) | SFR access, globals |
| Register Indirect | 1 | 1 | **High** (pointer) | Arrays, stacks, strings |
| Indexed | **2** | 1 | **Highest** (LUT) | Lookup tables, ASCII tables |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** For time-critical code, **register and register indirect** are fastest. For data-intensive code (e.g., large lookup tables), **indexed** is most powerful. For SFR access, **direct** is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — 8051 Addressing Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeProg["PROGRAM MEMORY 64KB ROM"] --> nodeOpcode["OPCODE FETCH"]
    nodeOpcode --> nodeDecode["INSTRUCTION DECODE"]
    nodeDecode --> nodeModeSel{"ADDRESSING MODE SELECTOR"}
    
    nodeModeSel -->|Immediate| nodeImm["Operand = Instruction Byte"]
    nodeModeSel -->|Register| nodeReg["Operand in R0-R7 of Active Bank"]
    nodeModeSel -->|Direct| nodeDir["Operand at 8-bit Address in RAM or SFR"]
    nodeModeSel -->|Reg Indirect| nodeRegInd["Address in R0 or R1 or DPTR"]
    nodeModeSel -->|Indexed| nodeIndex["Address = DPTR plus A OR PC plus A"]
    
    nodeReg --> nodeBankSel["PSW bits RS0 RS1 select Bank 0-3"]
    nodeDir --> nodeRamSfr["Internal RAM 00H-7FH and SFR 80H-FFH"]
    nodeRegInd --> nodeRamSfr
    nodeRegInd --> nodeExtRam["External RAM via MOVX"]
    nodeIndex --> nodeCodeMem["Program Memory via MOVC"]
    
    nodeImm --> nodeALU["ALU PROCESSING UNIT"]
    nodeBankSel --> nodeALU
    nodeRamSfr --> nodeALU
    nodeExtRam --> nodeALU
    nodeCodeMem --> nodeALU
    nodeALU --> nodeAcc["RESULT TO ACCUMULATOR A OR DESTINATION"]
```

---

## 4.2 Data Flow Diagram — Indexed Addressing (`MOVC A, @A+DPTR`)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["ACCUMULATOR A 8-bit OFFSET"] --> nodeAdd
    nodeDPTR["DPTR 16-bit BASE ADDRESS"] --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd["16-bit ADDER EA = A plus DPTR"] --> nodeEA
    nodeEA["EFFECTIVE ADDRESS 16-bit"] --> nodeProgMem
    nodeProgMem["PROGRAM MEMORY LOOKUP TABLE"] --> nodeData
    nodeData["FETCHED BYTE"] --> nodeALoad["A REGISTER UPDATED"]
```

---

## 4.3 Addressing Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart["OPERAND LOCATION?"] --> nodeQ1{"Is the operand a constant in the instruction?"}
    nodeQ1 -->|YES| nodeImmediate["MODE 1 IMMEDIATE: Use #data"]
    nodeQ1 -->|NO| nodeQ2{"Is the operand in R0-R7?"}
    nodeQ2 -->|YES| nodeRegister["MODE 2 REGISTER: Use Rn"]
    nodeQ2 -->|NO| nodeQ3{"Is the address given directly in instruction?"}
    nodeQ3 -->|YES| nodeDirect["MODE 3 DIRECT: Use direct address"]
    nodeQ3 -->|NO| nodeQ4{"Is the address inside R0 or R1?"}
    nodeQ4 -->|YES| nodeRegInd["MODE 4 REGISTER INDIRECT: Use @Rn"]
    nodeQ4 -->|NO| nodeQ5{"Need LUT access in code memory?"}
    nodeQ5 -->|YES| nodeIndexed["MODE 5 INDEXED: Use @A+DPTR or @A+PC"]
```

---

## 4.4 Register Bank Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodePSW["PSW REGISTER"] --> nodeRS
    nodeRS["RS1 RS0 BITS"] --> nodeBankSel
    nodeBankSel{"Bank Selector"}
    nodeBankSel -->|RS1=0 RS0=0| nodeB0["BANK 0: R0-R7 at 00H-07H"]
    nodeBankSel -->|RS1=0 RS0=1| nodeB1["BANK 1: R0-R7 at 08H-0FH"]
    nodeBankSel -->|RS1=1 RS0=0| nodeB2["BANK 2: R0-R7 at 10H-17H"]
    nodeBankSel -->|RS1=1 RS0=1| nodeB3["BANK 3: R0-R7 at 18H-1FH"]
    nodeB0 --> nodeCPU["CPU ACCESS"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeCPU
    nodeB2 --> nodeCPU
    nodeB3 --> nodeCPU
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 🅰️ PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. Define addressing mode. List the addressing modes supported by the 8051 microcontroller.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
