**Definition (1 mark):** An addressing mode is a method by which an instruction specifies its operand(s) — i.e., the location of the data on which the instruction operates.

**List (2 marks):** The 8051 microcontroller supports **five** addressing modes:

1. **Immediate Addressing** — e.g., `MOV A, #25H`
2. **Register Addressing** — e.g., `MOV A, R0`
3. **Direct Addressing** — e.g., `MOV A, 30H`
4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — e.g., `MOV A, @R0`
5. **Indexed Addressing** — e.g., `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between direct addressing and register indirect addressing modes of 8051.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Direct Addressing | Register Indirect Addressing |
|---|---|---|
| Address source | Instruction itself contains the 8-bit address | Address is *inside* R0, R1, or DPTR |
| Notation | `MOV A, 30H` | `MOV A, @R0` |
| Memory accessed | Internal RAM + SFR | Internal RAM + External RAM (with MOVX) |
| Instruction size | 2–3 bytes | 1 byte |
| Pointer role | No pointer register | R0/R1/DPTR acts as a pointer |
| Use case | Accessing SFRs, fixed locations | Array traversal, dynamic memory |

---

## 🅱️ PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks):** Explain the five addressing modes of the 8051 microcontroller with suitable examples. Mention the machine code and number of bytes for each mode. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

#### **Part (a) — Explain Immediate, Register, and Direct Addressing modes with examples. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**(i) Immediate Addressing (2 marks):** The operand is a constant that immediately follows the opcode. Used for initialization.

Example: `MOV A, #25H`
- Opcode byte: `74H`
- Data byte: `25H`
- Total bytes: **2 bytes**
- Machine cycles: **1 cycle**

**(ii) Register Addressing (2 marks):** Operand is in one of the 8 working registers (R0–R7) of the active register bank. Most compact and fastest mode.

Example: `MOV A, R5`
- Opcode byte: `EDH` (1110 1101₂)
- Total bytes: **1 byte**
- Machine cycles: **1 cycle**

**(iii) Direct Addressing (3 marks):** The 8-bit address of the operand is given explicitly in the instruction. Can access internal RAM (00H–7FH) and SFRs (80H–FFH).

Example 1: `MOV A, 30H` — reads from internal RAM.
- Opcode: `E5H`, Address: `30H`
- Total bytes: **2 bytes**

Example 2: `MOV P1, #0FFH` — writes to Port 1 SFR (address `90H`).
- Opcode: `75H`, Address: `90H`, Data: `FFH`
- Total bytes: **3 bytes**

---

#### **Part (b) — Explain Register Indirect and Indexed Addressing modes with examples and a neat diagram. (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**(i) Register Indirect Addressing (3 marks):** The address of the operand is contained in a register (R0, R1, or DPTR). The `@` symbol denotes "content of address pointed by."

Example: `MOV A, @R0`
- If R0 = `40H`, then A ← [40H]
- Opcode: `E2H`
- Total bytes: **1 byte**

**Diagram — Register Indirect:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeR0["R0 = 40H"] --> nodeMem["INTERNAL RAM LOCATION 40H"]
    nodeMem --> nodeData["DATA AT 40H = 5AH"]
    nodeData --> nodeLoad["LOADED INTO A"]
    nodeLoad --> nodeAcc["A = 5AH"]
```

**Use case:** Iterating through arrays.

---

**(ii) Indexed Addressing (4 marks):** Effective Address = Sum of base address (DPTR or PC) and offset (Accumulator). Used with `MOVC` to read from program memory (lookup tables).

**Equation:**

$$
EA = DPTR + A
$$

**Example:**

```assembly
MOV DPTR, #0400H
MOV A, #02H
MOVC A, @A+DPTR
```

- EA = `0400H` + `02H` = `0402H`
- A ← content of program memory at `0402H`

**Diagram — Indexed Addressing:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeD["DPTR = 0400H"] --> nodeSum["16-BIT ADDER"]
    nodeAcc["A = 02H"] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeEA["EA = 0402H"]
    nodeEA --> nodeLUT["PROGRAM MEMORY LUT"]
    nodeLUT --> nodeVal["BYTE AT 0402H = 30H"]
    nodeVal --> nodeFinal["A = 30H"]
```

**Use case:** ASCII conversion, square-wave LUT for DAC, sine-wave generation.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** What is an addressing mode? Explain the significance of indexed addressing mode in 8051. Write a program to find the sum of first 10 natural numbers stored in an array using indexed addressing. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — Concept of Addressing Mode and Significance of Indexed Mode (7 marks)**

**Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):** An addressing mode specifies the rule by which the operand(s) of an instruction are accessed. The 8051 supports five addressing modes: Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, and Indexed.

**Significance of Indexed Addressing Mode (5 marks):**

1. **Lookup Tables (LUT):** Used to read constant data (sine values, ASCII codes, 7-segment codes) from program memory efficiently.
2. **Equation for Effective Address:**

$$
EA = \text{DPTR} + A \quad \text{or} \quad EA = \text{PC} + A
$$

3. **No data RAM consumption:** LUTs are stored in ROM (program memory), saving precious internal RAM.
4. **Fast table access:** A 2-cycle instruction replaces a long switch-case or if-else ladder.
5. **Real-time waveform generation:** Used in DDS (Direct Digital Synthesis), PWM generation, and motor control.
6. **Only way to read program memory:** The `MOVC` instruction (where C = Code) **must** use indexed addressing.

**Difference between `@A+DPTR` and `@A+PC`:**

| Feature | `@A+DPTR` | `@A+PC` |
|---|---|---|
| Base | 16-bit DPTR (user-controlled) | 16-bit PC (auto-incremented) |
| Flexibility | **High** (DPTR can be set to any address) | **Low** (PC is fixed at next instruction) |
| Table location | Anywhere in 64 KB ROM | Must be **right after** the MOVC instruction |
| Use | Jump tables, large LUTs | Small, position-independent LUTs |

---

#### **Part (b) — Assembly Program Using Indexed Addressing (7 marks)**

**Problem:** Sum of first 10 natural numbers (01H to 0AH = 55H) stored in an array in program memory.

**Solution Program:**

```assembly
ORG 0000H
    LJMP MAIN
ORG 0030H
MAIN:
    MOV DPTR, #TABLE       ; DPTR = address of array
    MOV R0, #00H           ; Index = 0
    MOV R1, #0AH           ; Counter = 10
    MOV A, #00H            ; Accumulator = 0 (sum)

LOOP:
    MOVC A, @A+DPTR        ; Read byte at DPTR + A
    ADD A, R2              ; Sum = Sum + A
    MOV R2, A              ; Store partial sum
    INC DPTR               ; Move pointer to next byte
    DJNZ R1, LOOP          ; Decrement counter, loop if not zero
    SJMP $                 ; Stop

ORG 0500H
TABLE: DB 01H, 02H, 03H, 04H, 05H
       DB 06H, 07H, 08H, 09H, 0AH
END
```

**Explanation (step by step):**

1. **Initialization:** DPTR ← `0500H` (base of array). R0 ← 0 (offset). R1 ← `0AH` (loop counter). R2 ← 0 (sum register). A ← 0.
2. **Loop Iteration:** For each iteration, `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` reads the array element. The result is added to R2.
3. **Pointer update:** `INC DPTR` advances the base pointer to the next array element.
4. **Loop control:** `DJNZ R1, LOOP` decrements R1; loops until R1 = 0 (10 iterations).
5. **Result:** After the loop, **R2 = 55H = 85** (decimal sum of 1+2+…+10).
6. **Verification:** Sum formula: $n(n+1)/2 = 10(11)/2 = 55$. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing R0 with R2 in indexed addressing:** Only R0 and R1 (and DPTR for MOVX) can be used in register indirect addressing — NOT R2, R3, etc. Many students incorrectly write `MOV A, @R2`.
> 2. **Using MOVC with `@A+R0`:** The instruction `MOVC` REQUIRES either `@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC`. Using `@A+R0` is invalid and results in an **assembly error**.
> 3. **Forgetting to reset A:** In `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`, the offset must be in A. If A is not preloaded, garbage value causes wrong address.
> 4. **Wrong register bank:** Instructions like `MOV A, R0` refer to the *currently selected* bank — using wrong RS0/RS1 in PSW leads to wrong data.
> 5. **Mixing MOVX and MOVC:** MOVX accesses external DATA memory; MOVC accesses CODE memory. Students often confuse the two.
> 6. **Not initializing DPTR:** Without setting DPTR to the LUT base, indexed addressing fetches from address `0000H` (start of ROM) by default.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

✅ **The 8051 supports 5 addressing modes** — Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed.

✅ **Immediate (`#data`)** — Data is part of instruction; 2/3 bytes; used for constants.

✅ **Register (`Rn`)** — Operands in R0–R7 of active bank; **1 byte / 1 cycle** (fastest & smallest); bank selected via PSW (RS0, RS1).

✅ **Direct (`direct`)** — 8-bit address in instruction; only mode for SFR access; 2/3 bytes; 1 cycle.

✅ **Register Indirect (`@Rn`)** — R0/R1/DPTR holds a pointer; **1 byte** for internal RAM, **1 byte + 2 cycles** for external RAM via MOVX.

✅ **Indexed (`@A+DPTR` or `@A+PC`)** — Effective address = base + offset; used with **MOVC** for program memory LUTs; 2 cycles.

✅ **Instruction length formula:** `1 byte (opcode) + 0/1/2 bytes (operand)` depending on mode.

✅ **Execution time formula:** $T_{exec} = \text{Machine Cycles} \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$.

✅ **Effective Address formulas:**
- Direct: `EA = address byte`
- Reg Indirect: `EA = (R0) or (R1) or (DPTR)`
- Indexed: `EA = DPTR + A` or `EA = PC + A`

✅ **SFR Access** = always via **direct addressing** (addresses 80H–FFH).

✅ **Lookup tables** in program memory = always accessed via **indexed addressing** with `MOVC`.

✅ **Arrays in internal RAM** = traversed via **register indirect** (R0/R1 as pointer + INC).

✅ **KTU weightage tip:** Addressing modes is a **guaranteed 7–14 mark question** in Module 1 — every ESE/IA paper has at least one full question or sub-part on this topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Port Organization and Dual Operations of 8051 Ports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Port Organization and Dual Operations of 8051 Ports # 🏛️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Port Organization of 8051** refers to the internal hardware architecture and pin-out structure of the four 8-bit bidirectional Input/Output (I/O) ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) present on the 8051 microcontroller. Each port is mapped into the **Special Function Register (SFR)** memory space and is bit-addressable, allowing the CPU to communicate with external peripheral devices or to function as an alternate bus/control interface for external memory interfacing. **Dual Operations of 8051 Ports** describe the multiplexed functional role each port plays, where a port can act either as a **general-purpose I/O port** or as a **dedicated alternate function pin** (such as address bus, data bus, control signals, or peripheral interface). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Exact Wording Expected in Exams):** > *"The 8051 microcontroller has four 8-bit I/O ports designated P0, P1, P2, and P3. Port 0 is a bidirectional open-drain port, while Ports 1, 2, and 3 are quasi-bidirectional with internal pull-up resistors. Ports 0, 2, and 3 have dual functions, allowing them to serve as address/data bus lines or alternate function signals when external memory is accessed."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition 🎯 Imagine a **large corporate office building** with four entrances (P0, P1, P2, P3): | Port | Real-World Analogy | Function | |------|-------------------|----------| | **P0** | The Main Lobby (no security gate — open entry/exit) | Used for both visitors and as a freight elevator (carrying address + data). Needs an **external security guard** (pull-up resistor). | | **P1** | A Standard Office Door (has internal latch) | Used **only for general entry** — no alternate job. | | **P2** | A Door That Also Serves as a VIP Address Board | When special events happen, this door displays the floor number (A8–A15). | | **P3** | A Multi-Tool Swiss Army Knife Door | Each of the 8 doors has a **secondary purpose** (telephone line, fire alarm, timer inputs, etc.). | > [!NOTE] > **The Key Intuition:** The 8051 was designed to save silicon area. Instead of providing 32 separate dedicated pins for I/O, address bus, data bus, and control signals, the designers made the pins **multiplexed** — meaning they can do "two jobs" depending on whether the chip is talking to internal registers or external memory. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Number of I/O Ports:** **4** (P0, P1, P2, P3) - **Port Width:** **8 bits** each (total **32 I/O lines**) - **Port 0 Internal Pull-up:** **None** (requires external **10 kΩ** pull-up resistors) - **SFR Address of P0:** **0x80** (bit-addressable from 0x80 to 0x87) - **SFR Address of P1:** **0x90** (bit-addressable from 0x90 to 0x97) - **SFR Address of P2:** **0xA0** (bit-addressable from 0xA0 to 0xA7) - **SFR Address of P3:** **0xB0** (bit-addressable from 0xB0 to 0xB7) - **External Memory Addressing Range (combined P0 + P2):** **64 KB** (since 16-bit address formed by A0–A7 from P0 and A8–A15 from P2) > [!TIP] > **Memorization Trick (For Exams):** P0 → 0x80, P1 → 0x90, P2 → 0xA0, P3 → 0xB0. Notice the pattern — last nibble is 0, 0, 0, 0 and the previous nibble is 8, 9, A, B in hexadecimal! 🚀 # 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Internal Bit Structure of Each Port Pin Every bit of every port in 8051 is composed of the following internal hardware blocks: 1. **D-Latch (1-bit D-type flip-flop):** Stores the value written to the port. 2. **Output Buffer (Driver):** Drives the pin HIGH or LOW. 3. **Input Buffer:** Reads the current logic state of the pin. 4. **Read Latch vs. Read Pin Logic:** Uses MUX to select whether to read the latch output (for read-modify-write) or the actual pin state. 5. **Pull-up FET (Internal FET Transistor):** Provides weak internal pull-up (except P0). ## 2.2 Port 0 — Detailed Analysis (Pins 32–39) - **SFR Address:** 0x80 - **Function:** Can act as **General Purpose I/O** OR as **Multiplexed Address/Data Bus (AD0–AD7)**. - **Configuration:** **Open-drain** (no internal pull-up). - **Why?** When used as an output, P0 has open-drain transistors, meaning it can only actively pull the pin LOW. To get a HIGH output, an **external 10 kΩ pull-up resistor** to Vcc (typically +5V) is mandatory. - **When does dual function activate?** When accessing **external memory** (program memory or data memory outside the chip). ### 2.2.1 Dual Function of P0: | Mode | Trigger | Function | |------|---------|----------| | I/O Mode | Reset default / no external memory access | Reads/Writes data via SFR P0 | | Bus Mode | ALE signal + external memory access (EA = 0 or beyond internal ROM) | Outputs low-byte address (A0–A7) first, then switches to data (D0–D7) | > [!NOTE] > The demultiplexing is achieved using the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal and an external latch like **74HC373 (Octal D-type transparent latch)** or **74LS573**. ## 2.3 Port 1 — Detailed Analysis (Pins 1–8) - **SFR Address:** 0x90 - **Function:** **General Purpose I/O only** — no alternate function. - **Configuration:** **Quasi-bidirectional** with internal pull-up (~30–40 kΩ weak pull-up). - **Structure:** Simplest of all ports — has D-latch, output driver, input buffer, and read pin/latch logic, but **no control signal multiplexer** and **no alternate function logic**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Asked Point:** *"Why is P1 called a general-purpose I/O port while P0, P2, P3 are not?"* > **Answer:** P1 lacks the additional multiplexer and alternate function control logic present in P0, P2, and P3. Its sole purpose is to act as a simple I/O port. ## 2.4 Port 2 — Detailed Analysis (Pins 21–28) - **SFR Address:** 0xA0 - **Function:** **General Purpose I/O** OR **High-byte Address Bus (A8–A15)**. - **Configuration:** **Quasi-bidirectional** with internal pull-up. - **Internal Structure:** Similar to P1 but with an additional **MUX** controlled by an internal signal. When the CPU accesses external memory, the MUX selects the high-byte of the address; otherwise, it selects the latch output. ### 2.4.1 Dual Function of P2: | Mode | Trigger | Function | |------|---------|----------| | I/O Mode | No external memory / SFR access | Normal I/O | | Bus Mode | External memory access | Outputs A8–A15 (upper 8 bits of 16-bit address) | ## 2.5 Port 3 — Detailed Analysis (Pins 10–17) - **SFR Address:** 0xB0 - **Function:** **General Purpose I/O** OR **Alternate Function** (most multi-functional port). - **Configuration:** **Quasi-bidirectional** with internal pull-up. ### 2.5.1 Dual Function of P3 (The Most Asked Topic in KTU!): | Pin | Bit | Alternate Function | Purpose | |-----|-----|-------------------|---------| | P3.0 | 0 | **RxD** | Serial Data Input (Receiver) | | P3.1 | 1 | **TxD** | Serial Data Output (Transmitter) | | P3.2 | 2 | **INT0** | External Interrupt 0 (Active Low) | | P3.3 | 3 | **INT1** | External Interrupt 1 (Active Low) | | P3.4 | 4 | **T0** | Timer/Counter 0 External Input | | P3.5 | 5 | **T1** | Timer/Counter 1 External Input | | P3.6 | 6 | **WR** | External Data Memory Write Strobe (Active Low) | | P3.7 | 7 | **RD** | External Data Memory Read Strobe (Active Low) | > [!TIP] > **Memory Mnemonic for P3 Alternate Functions (Row-wise):** > **R**xD, **T**xD, **I**NT0, **I**NT1, **T**0, **T**1, **W**R, **R**D > Phrase: **"Real Time Is Important To Two Weird Routines"** — Sounds silly, but exam recall is instant! 🧠 ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 | **Parameter** | **Port 0** | **Port 1** | **Port 2** | **Port 3** | |---------------|------------|------------|------------|------------| | SFR Address (Hex) | 0x80 | 0x90 | 0xA0 | 0xB0 | | Pin Numbers (DIP-40) | 32–39 | 1–8 | 21–28 | 10–17 | | Bit-Addressable? | Yes (0x80–0x87) | Yes (0x90–0x97) | Yes (0xA0–0xA7) | Yes (0xB0–0xB7) | | Internal Pull-up | **No** | Yes (~40 kΩ) | Yes (~40 kΩ) | Yes (~40 kΩ) | | Quasi-bidirectional? | No (Open Drain) | Yes | Yes | Yes | | Alternate Function | AD0–AD7 | **None** | A8–A15 | RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD | | External Pull-up Req. | **Yes (10 kΩ)** | No | No | No | ### 2.6.1 Key Address Calculation Formulae: **External Memory Address Formation:** $$\text{16-bit Address} = (\text{P2 contents}) \times 256 + (\text{P0 contents})$$ $$\text{16-bit Address} = (A_{15}...A_{8}) \times 2^{8} + (A_{7}...A_{0})$$ **Total Addressable External Memory:** $$\text{Memory Size} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes} = 65536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > In **embedded systems design**, this dual operation is used to interface: > - **LCD displays** (8-bit data mode uses P0; 4-bit mode uses P0.0–P0.3). > - **External EPROM/ROM** chips (P0 + P2 + PSEN). > - **External RAM** chips (P0 + P2 + ALE + RD/WR). > - **Serial Communication** (P3.0/P3.1 with UART). > - **Motor Control Interrupts** (P3.2/P3.3 for emergency stops). # 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: External Memory Address Formation Using P0 and P2 The 8051 uses a **Harvard architecture** with separate address spaces for code (16-bit via PC) and external data memory. The 16-bit address is formed by concatenating the contents of P2 (high byte) and P0 (low byte). ### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Define the contents of the two ports symbolically. - Let $\text{P2} = H$ (high byte, 8-bit value from 0x00 to 0xFF) - Let $\text{P0} = L$ (low byte, 8-bit value from 0x00 to 0xFF) **Step 2:** Express the 16-bit physical address. $$\text{Address}_{16\text{-bit}} = (H \ll 8) \vert L$$ **Step 3:** Since shifting left by 8 bits is equivalent to multiplication by 256: $$\text{Address}_{16\text{-bit}} = H \times 2^{8} + L$$ $$\text{Address}_{16\text{-bit}} = 256 \cdot H + L$$ **Step 4:** Substitute the bit ranges. $H \in [0, 255]$ and $L \in [0, 255]$. **Step 5:** Calculate the maximum addressable range. $$\text{Address}_{max} = 255 \times 256 + 255 = 65280 + 255 = 65535$$ **Step 6:** Convert to standard units. $$\text{Address Range} = 0 \text{ to } 65535 = 65536 \text{ locations} = 64 \text{ KB}$$ > [!NOTE] > This derivation is **exam-critical**. KTU frequently asks: *"How much external memory can be interfaced using P0 and P2?"* The exact derivation above should be reproduced in the answer script. ## 3.2 Worked Example: Finding the External Address **Problem:** P2 contains 0x1F and P0 contains 0xA5. Find the 16-bit physical address to be accessed. ### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1:** Convert hexadecimal to decimal for calculation. $$H = 0x1F = (1 \times 16^{1}) + (15 \times 16^{0}) = 16 + 15 = 31$$ $$L = 0xA5 = (10 \times 16^{1}) + (5 \times 16^{0}) = 160 + 5 = 165$$ **Step 2:** Apply the formula. $$\text{Address} = 256 \times 31 + 165$$ **Step 3:** Perform the multiplication. $$256 \times 31 = 256 \times 30 + 256 \times 1 = 7680 + 256 = 7936$$ **Step 4:** Add the low byte. $$\text{Address} = 7936 + 165 = 8101$$ **Step 5:** Convert the result back to hexadecimal for the address bus representation. $$8101 \div 16 = 506 \text{ remainder } 5 \quad (\text{LSB} = 0x5)$$ $$506 \div 16 = 31 \text{ remainder } 10 \quad (\text{Next nibble} = 0xA)$$ $$31 \div 16 = 1 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad (\text{Next nibble} = 0xF)$$ $$1 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 1 \quad (\text{MSB} = 0x1)$$ $$\text{Address} = 0x1FA5$$ **Step 6:** Verification — Check that the high byte is 0x1F and low byte is 0xA5. $$\text{High Byte} = \text{Address} \gg 8 = 0x1FA5 \gg 8 = 0x1F \checkmark$$ $$\text{Low Byte} = \text{Address} \ \& \ 0xFF = 0x1FA5 \ \& \ 0x00FF = 0xA5 \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The result confirms:** P2 → A15..A8 = 0x1F, P0 → A7..A0 = 0xA5, forming the 16-bit address 0x1FA5. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pull-up Resistor Analysis | **Configuration** | **P0 Output Stage** | **P1/P2/P3 Output Stage** | |-------------------|---------------------|---------------------------| | Writing Logic 0 | Pull-down FET ON → Pin = 0V (LOW) | Pull-down FET ON → Pin = 0V (LOW) | | Writing Logic 1 | Both FETs OFF → Pin is **floating** (needs external pull-up) | Pull-up FET ON (weak) → Pin ≈ 5V (HIGH) | | Driving Capability LOW | **Strong** (sink current up to ~3.2 mA per pin) | Strong (sink current up to ~3.2 mA per pin) | | Driving Capability HIGH | **Very weak** (open drain — depends on external resistor) | **Weak** (internal pull-up ~40 kΩ) | ### 3.3.1 Derivation of External Pull-up Resistance Value The 8051 datasheet specifies that when P0 acts as an address/data bus, the internal pull-up is engaged through a special bus-mode pull-up FET that is stronger (yet still weaker than a push-pull configuration). **For external pull-up calculation:** $$R_{pull-up} = \frac{V_{cc} - V_{OH(min)}}{I_{OH(total)}}$$ Where: - $V_{cc} = +5\text{ V}$ - $V_{OH(min)} = 2.4\text{ V}$ (TTL-compatible HIGH minimum) - $I_{OH(total)} = \text{Sum of currents from all P0 pins going HIGH}$ **Standard Recommendation (used in 8051 development boards):** $$R_{pull-up} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (a 10-kilohm resistor pack is typical)}$$ # 🎨 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: 8051 Port Organization The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the internal architecture of a single bit of each port, showing the differences between P0 (open-drain with alternate function), P1 (simple I/O), P2 (I/O with address bus), and P3 (I/O with alternate functions). ```mermaid graph TD CPU["8051 CPU Core"] --> Dbus["Internal Data Bus"] Dbus --> SFR["Special Function Register Space"] SFR --> P0Reg["P0 Register (SFR 0x80)"] SFR --> P1Reg["P1 Register (SFR 0x90)"] SFR --> P2Reg["P2 Register (SFR 0xA0)"] SFR --> P3Reg["P3 Register (SFR 0xB0)"] P0Reg --> P0Latch["P0 D-Latch"] P0Latch --> P0Mux{"MUX: Internal Addr/Data Bus
or Latch Output"} P0Mux --> P0Driver["P0 Open-Drain Output Driver
NO Internal Pull-up"] P0Driver --> P0Pin["P0.0 - P0.7
Pins 32-39
Acts as AD0-AD7
when external memory is accessed"] P1Reg --> P1Latch["P1 D-Latch"] P1Latch --> P1Driver["P1 Quasi-Bidirectional Driver
WITH Internal Pull-up"] P1Driver --> P1Pin["P1.0 - P1.7
Pins 1-8
General Purpose I/O Only"] P2Reg --> P2Latch["P2 D-Latch"] P2Latch --> P2Mux{"MUX: High Address Byte A8-A15
or Latch Output"} P2Mux --> P2Driver["P2 Quasi-Bidirectional Driver
WITH Internal Pull-up"] P2Driver --> P2Pin["P2.0 - P2.7
Pins 21-28
Acts as A8-A15
when external memory is accessed"] P3Reg --> P3Latch["P3 D-Latch"] P3Latch --> P3Mux{"MUX: Alternate Function Signal
or Latch Output"} P3Mux --> P3Driver["P3 Quasi-Bidirectional Driver
WITH Internal Pull-up"] P3Driver --> P3Pin["P3.0 RxD, P3.1 TxD, P3.2 INT0,
P3.3 INT1, P3.4 T0, P3.5 T1,
P3.6 WR, P3.7 RD
Pins 10-17"] P0Pin --> ExtMem["External Memory / Peripherals
Max 64 KB Addressable Space"] P1Pin --> GPIO["General I/O Devices
LEDs, Switches, Relays"] P2Pin --> ExtMem P3Pin --> Alternate["Alternate Function Devices
UART, Timers, Interrupts, Memory R/W"] style CPU fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style SFR fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P0Driver fill:#991b1b,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P1Driver fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P2Driver fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P3Driver fill:#14532d,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000 style P0Pin fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000,stroke:#000000 style P1Pin fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000,stroke:#000000 style P2Pin fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000,stroke:#000000 style P3Pin fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000,stroke:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Schematic: 8051 Port 3 Alternate Function Mapping This diagram explicitly maps every bit of P3 to its alternate function, which is the most heavily tested concept in KTU examinations. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph "Port 3 Register (SFR 0xB0)" P3Bit0["P3.0 (Bit 0)"] P3Bit1["P3.1 (Bit 1)"] P3Bit2["P3.2 (Bit 2)"] P3Bit3["P3.3 (Bit 3)"] P3Bit4["P3.4 (Bit 4)"] P3Bit5["P3.5 (Bit 5)"] P3Bit6["P3.6 (Bit 6)"] P3Bit7["P3.7 (Bit 7)"] end P3Bit0 --> RxD["RxD
Serial Input
UART Receiver"] P3Bit1 --> TxD["TxD
Serial Output
UART Transmitter"] P3Bit2 --> INT0["INT0
External Interrupt 0
Active LOW"] P3Bit3 --> INT1["INT1
External Interrupt 1
Active LOW"] P3Bit4 --> T0["T0
Timer 0 External Input
Pin"] P3Bit5 --> T1["T1
Timer 1 External Input
Pin"] P3Bit6 --> WRbar["WR-bar
External Data Memory
Write Strobe"] P3Bit7 --> RDbar["RD-bar
External Data Memory
Read Strobe"] style P3Bit0 fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style P3Bit1 fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style P3Bit2 fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style P3Bit3 fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style P3Bit4 fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style P3Bit5 fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style P3Bit6 fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style P3Bit7 fill:#0c4a6e,color:#ffffff style RxD fill:#9a3412,color:#ffffff style TxD fill:#9a3412,color:#ffffff style INT0 fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff style INT1 fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff style T0 fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff style T1 fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff style WRbar fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style RDbar fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Functional Flow: P0 Demultiplexing with External Latch This diagram explains how the multiplexed AD0–AD7 signals from P0 are separated into proper address and data lines using an external 74HC373 latch. ```mermaid graph LR CPU["8051 CPU
Issues 16-bit Address"] --> AddrLatch["Internal Address Latch"] AddrLatch --> P0Emit["P0 emits Low-Byte Address
A0-A7 on AD0-AD7"] P0Emit --> LatchIC["External 74HC373
Octal Transparent Latch"] ALE["ALE Signal
(Address Latch Enable)"] --> LatchIC LatchIC --> AOutput["Latched Address A0-A7
to External Memory"] P0Emit --> DataBus["P0 then switches to
Data Mode D0-D7"] DataBus --> MemoryIO["Read/Write Data
with External Memory"] AOutput --> MemoryIO P2Path["P2 emits A8-A15
directly (no latch needed)"] --> AHigh["High Address A8-A15"] AHigh --> MemoryIO MemoryIO --> Result["External Memory Access
Complete"] style CPU fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff style ALE fill:#b91c1c,color:#ffffff style LatchIC fill:#166534,color:#ffffff style MemoryIO fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style Result fill:#fbbf24,color:#000000 ``` # 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) — Short Answer Type ### Question 1: Define Port Organization of 8051. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Model Answer:** The 8051 microcontroller has four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports: P0, P1, P2, and P3. Each port is mapped into the SFR (Special Function Register) memory space and is bit-addressable. - **P0 (SFR 0x80):** Open-drain bidirectional, no internal pull-up. Acts as **AD0–AD7** (multiplexed address/data bus) when external memory is accessed. - **P1 (SFR 0x90):** Quasi-bidirectional with internal pull-up. **General-purpose I/O only** with no alternate function. - **P2 (SFR 0xA0):** Quasi-bidirectional with internal pull-up. Acts as **A8–A15** (high-byte address bus) during external memory access. - **P3 (SFR 0xB0):** Quasi-bidirectional with internal pull-up. Has **alternate functions** (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR-bar, RD-bar) for serial I/O, interrupts, timers, and memory control. These four ports together provide **32 I/O lines** and can interface up to **64 KB of external memory** through the P0/P2 dual bus operation. --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Quasi-Bidirectional and Open-Drain Ports. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** | **Parameter** | **Open-Drain Port (P0)** | **Quasi-Bidirectional Port (P1, P2, P3)** | |---------------|--------------------------|--------------------------------------------| | Internal Pull-up | **Absent** | **Present** (~30–40 kΩ weak pull-up FET) | | External Pull-up Required | **Yes (10 kΩ typical)** | No | | Output HIGH Strength | Weak (depends on external resistor) | Weak (only via internal pull-up) | | Output LOW Strength | Strong (active pull-down) | Strong (active pull-down) | | Use Case | Address/Data bus, multiplexed signals | General I/O, address high byte, alternate functions | | Configuration | True open-drain transistor | Modified quasi-bidirectional (weak HIGH + strong LOW) | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Full Descriptive Type ### 🅰️ Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024] **Q: (a)** Explain the internal structure of **Port 0** of the 8051 microcontroller with a neat block diagram. Why is it called an open-drain port? Discuss its dual functions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the role of **P2 and P3** in external memory interfacing and alternate function operations. List the alternate functions of all eight pins of Port 3. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A: ### Part (a) — Port 0 Internal Structure and Dual Function [7 Marks] **Introduction (1 Mark):** Port 0 (Pins 32–39, SFR 0x80) is an 8-bit bidirectional I/O port of the 8051 microcontroller. It is the most complex of all four ports because it serves a dual purpose — both as a general-purpose I/O port and as the multiplexed low-byte address/data bus (AD0–AD7). **Internal Structure (3 Marks):** Each bit of Port 0 consists of: 1. A **D-latch** (1-bit flip-flop) to store the data written to the port pin. 2. A **control MUX** that selects between the latch output (I/O mode) and the internal address/data bus (external memory mode). 3. **Two output transistors** (an active pull-down FET and an open-drain pull-up path). 4. An **input buffer** for reading the pin state. 5. **Read-latch and Read-pin logic** controlled by the CPU's "Read" signal. **Why Open-Drain? (1 Mark):** Port 0 is called an **open-drain port** because its output stage lacks a true push-pull configuration. The pull-up transistor is **NOT a continuous active device** — it is engaged only during external memory access (as a bus driver). When used as a simple I/O port, writing a '1' to P0 turns **OFF** both output transistors, leaving the pin in a **high-impedance (floating) state**. Therefore, external pull-up resistors (typically 10 kΩ to Vcc) are mandatory for proper logic HIGH output. **Dual Function of P0 (2 Marks):** - **Mode 1 — General Purpose I/O:** By default, after reset, P0 acts as a general-purpose I/O port. To use it as input, the port must first be written with 1's (to disable the pull-down transistor). - **Mode 2 — Address/Data Bus (AD0–AD7):** When the CPU executes a `MOVX` instruction or fetches external program memory, P0 first outputs the **low-byte address (A0–A7)** during the first half of the memory cycle (when ALE = 1), and then switches to **data (D0–D7)** during the second half (when ALE = 0). The address is latched externally using the **ALE** signal and a **74HC373** latch. --- ### Part (b) — P2 and P3 Roles and Alternate Functions [7 Marks] **P2 in External Memory Interfacing (2 Marks):** Port 2 (SFR 0xA0, Pins 21–28) plays a critical role in 16-bit external memory addressing. Whenever the 8051 accesses external memory (code or data beyond 64 KB), P2 automatically outputs the **high byte of the 16-bit address (A8–A15)**. Internally, a multiplexer selects between the P2 latch contents (I/O mode) and the high-byte address from the internal address bus (external memory mode). The P2 outputs remain stable throughout the external memory cycle, unlike P0 which multiplexes address and data. When not accessing external memory, P2 acts as a quasi-bidirectional general-purpose I/O port with internal pull-up resistors. **P3 Alternate Functions (5 Marks):** Port 3 (SFR 0xB0, Pins 10–17) has dual functionality. Each pin has an alternate function activated by the corresponding internal peripheral or control signal. The alternate functions are: | **Pin** | **Alternate Function** | **Description** | |---------|------------------------|-----------------| | P3.0 | **RxD** | Serial Data Input (UART Receiver) | | P3.1 | **TxD** | Serial Data Output (UART Transmitter) | | P3.2 | **INT0** | External Interrupt 0 (Active LOW, edge/level triggered) | | P3.3 | **INT1** | External Interrupt 1 (Active LOW) | | P3.4 | **T0** | Timer/Counter 0 External Input | | P3.5 | **T1** | Timer/Counter 1 External Input | | P3.6 | **WR-bar** | External Data Memory Write Strobe (Active LOW) | | P3.7 | **RD-bar** | External Data Memory Read Strobe (Active LOW) | For any of these alternate functions to be activated, the corresponding P3 bit latch must be set to '1'. When the alternate function is active, the pin behaves according to the peripheral (e.g., RxD becomes an input regardless of P3.0 latch value), and writing to P3.0 may interfere with the operation. --- ### 🅱️ Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **Q: (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **demultiplexing of Address/Data bus** of the 8051. Why is ALE required? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the concept of **bit-addressability of 8051 ports** with examples. Calculate the 16-bit external memory address when P2 = 0x2A and P0 = 0xC4. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B: ### Part (a) — Address/Data Demultiplexing [7 Marks] **Why Demultiplexing is Needed (2 Marks):** The 8051 has only 40 pins and must support both an 8-bit data bus and a 16-bit address bus for external memory interfacing. To save pins, Intel multiplexed the lower 8 bits of the address with the 8-bit data bus onto **Port 0 (AD0–AD7)**. During the first half of a memory cycle, P0 carries the low byte of the address; during the second half, P0 carries data. This is called **multiplexing**. However, external memory devices (like ROM/RAM chips) require a **stable address** throughout the entire read/write cycle. Hence, the low-byte address must be **latched (demultiplexed)** from the multiplexed AD0–AD7 lines using an external latch and the **ALE** signal. **Working with Diagram (3 Marks):** The 8051 outputs a pulse on the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** pin during the first half-cycle. This pulse is used to **enable a transparent latch** (typically **74HC373** or **74LS573**). The latch captures the address present on AD0–AD7. When ALE goes LOW, the latch **freezes** the address and continues to output it stably to the memory chip, while P0 transitions to data mode for the read/write operation. **Steps:** 1. CPU places 16-bit address on the bus. 2. P0 outputs low-byte address A0–A7; P2 outputs high-byte A8–A15. 3. ALE goes HIGH → external latch (74HC373) is transparent → passes address. 4. ALE goes LOW → latch holds the address → memory chip sees stable address. 5. P0 switches to data mode → reads/writes D0–D7. 6. RD-bar or WR-bar goes LOW to complete the memory access. **Role of ALE (2 Marks):** **ALE = Address Latch Enable** is a control signal that tells the external latch when to capture the address from the multiplexed bus. It is a HIGH-going pulse generated by the 8051 at the start of every external memory access. ALE also provides a clock output at **1/6th of the oscillator frequency** (for external synchronization). --- ### Part (b) — Bit-Addressability and Address Calculation [7 Marks] **Bit-Addressability Concept (3 Marks):** The 8051 allows the programmer to access **individual bits of a port** using bit-manipulation instructions. The SFR addresses 0x80, 0x90, 0xA0, and 0xB0 are bit-addressable, meaning each of the 8 bits of P0, P1, P2, and P3 can be set, cleared, or read independently. The bit addresses are: - **P0 bits:** 0x80, 0x81, 0x82, ..., 0x87 (corresponding to P0.0, P0.1, ..., P0.7) - **P1 bits:** 0x90, 0x91, 0x92, ..., 0x97 - **P2 bits:** 0xA0, 0xA1, 0xA2, ..., 0xA7 - **P3 bits:** 0xB0, 0xB1, 0xB2, ..., 0xB7 **Example:** `SETB P1.0` sets only P1.0 HIGH without affecting other P1 bits. This is useful for controlling individual LEDs, motors, or sensors. **Address Calculation (4 Marks):** Given: P2 = 0x2A, P0 = 0xC4 **Step 1:** Use the address formation formula. $$\text{Address}_{16} = 256 \times \text{P2} + \text{P0}$$ **Step 2:** Convert hex to decimal. $$\text{P2} = 0x2A = 2 \times 16 + 10 = 42$$ $$\text{P0} = 0xC4 = 12 \times 16 + 4 = 196$$ **Step 3:** Substitute into the formula. $$\text{Address} = 256 \times 42 + 196 = 10752 + 196 = 10948$$ **Step 4:** Convert to hexadecimal. $$10948 \div 16 = 684 \text{ remainder } 4$$ $$684 \div 16 = 42 \text{ remainder } 12 \quad (0xC)$$ $$42 \div 16 = 2 \text{ remainder } 10 \quad (0xA)$$ $$2 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 2$$ $$\text{Address} = 0x2AC4$$ **Step 5:** Verification. $$\text{High Byte} = 0x2AC4 \gg 8 = 0x2A = \text{P2} \checkmark$$ $$\text{Low Byte} = 0x2AC4 \ \& \ 0xFF = 0xC4 = \text{P0} \checkmark$$ **The external memory address accessed is 0x2AC4 (10948 in decimal).** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **🚨 Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting external pull-up for P0:** When asked to interface an LED to P0, students often forget the 10 kΩ pull-up resistor. Without it, P0 will NOT produce a stable logic HIGH. **(-2 marks typical)** > 2. **Confusing active-low signals:** WR-bar and RD-bar are **active LOW**. Forgetting to mention this explicitly costs marks. > 3. **Wrong SFR addresses:** Writing P0 = 0x81, P1 = 0x91, etc. The correct addresses are 0x80, 0x90, 0xA0, 0xB0. **(-1 mark per wrong address)** > 4. **Missing the ALE explanation:** Many students answer demultiplexing without explaining **why** ALE is needed. Always state: "ALE is used to latch the low-byte address using an external 74HC373 latch." > 5. **P1 has NO alternate function:** Students often wrongly mention alternate functions for P1. P1 is strictly general-purpose I/O. > 6. **Bit-addressing syntax:** Use `P1.0` (capital P, decimal point), not `P1_0` or `P10`. Syntax matters in KTU valuation. > 7. **Not mentioning P3 latch must be 1 for alternate function:** When using RxD/TxD/INT0 etc., the corresponding P3 bit latch MUST be set to 1. **(-2 marks if missed)** --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋 > [!NOTE] > **🚀 Rapid-Revision Checklist for Last-Minute Exam Prep:** - ✅ **8051 has 4 ports:** P0, P1, P2, P3 — each is **8 bits wide** (32 I/O lines total). - ✅ **SFR Addresses:** P0 = **0x80**, P1 = **0x90**, P2 = **0xA0**, P3 = **0xB0** (all bit-addressable). - ✅ **P0 is OPEN-DRAIN:** Requires **external 10 kΩ pull-up** resistors; carries **AD0–AD7** during external memory access. - ✅ **P1 is general-purpose I/O only** — no alternate function, no multiplexer. - ✅ **P2 is quasi-bidirectional:** Carries **A8–A15** during external memory access. - ✅ **P3 is the multi-function port:** RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR-bar, RD-bar. - ✅ **P3 alternate function mnemonic:** *"Real Time Is Important To Two Weird Routines"*. - ✅ **External memory address formula:** $\text{Address} = 256 \times \text{P2} + \text{P0}$ (16-bit address = 64 KB max). - ✅ **ALE signal + 74HC373 latch** are required to demultiplex P0's AD0–AD7 lines. - ✅ **P3.6 (WR-bar) and P3.7 (RD-bar) are active LOW** — used to interface external RAM. - ✅ **Bit-addressing example:** `SETB P1.0`, `CLR P3.7`, `MOV C, P3.2` (read INT0). - ✅ **Quasi-bidirectional = weak internal pull-up + strong pull-down** (no external resistor needed for P1/P2/P3). - ✅ **Reading a port pin:** Use the actual **physical pin** (e.g., `MOV A, P1`). Use the **latch** for read-modify-write to avoid wrong pin-state interpretation.

topics.title: Instruction Format
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Format

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Instruction Format** in a microcontroller is the standardized binary layout or encoding scheme that defines how a single machine instruction is represented in program memory. Every instruction issued to the CPU (Central Processing Unit) is fundamentally a binary word composed of two principal fields: the **Operation Code (Opcode)** and the **Operand Field(s)**.

The *Opcode* specifies **what operation** the microcontroller must perform (e.g., addition, data transfer, logical AND), while the *Operand field* specifies **where the data resides or where the result should go** (i.e., registers, memory addresses, or immediate constants).

In the context of the 8051 microcontroller family — the canonical architecture studied under **PCBMT402** — instructions can be **1-byte, 2-byte, or 3-byte** long, depending on the operation and addressing mode used. The Program Counter (PC) automatically increments by the corresponding byte length after every fetch cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Instruction Format dictates the **Instruction Cycle Timing**, **Program Memory Footprint**, and **Opcode Decoding Complexity** of every line of assembly code you write. KTU examiners frequently test the relationship between instruction size, machine cycles, and addressing modes.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a microcontroller instruction as a **postal courier slip** 📬:

- **Opcode** → The **verb** (the action: "DELIVER", "PICKUP", "RETURN")
- **Operand** → The **address & item description** (the details: "to House 42, Blue Package")

Just as a postal service cannot dispatch a courier without knowing both the action and the destination, the microcontroller CPU cannot execute any operation without an opcode (action) and operand (target data). The *format* is the standard template ensuring every "slip" is universally readable by the decoder hardware.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value (8051) | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Program Memory Word Size | **8 bits (1 byte)** | Smallest addressable program unit |
| Maximum Opcode Size | **8 bits (256 primary opcodes)** | Direct decoding capability |
| Instruction Length Range | **1 byte to 3 bytes** | Variable-length CISC architecture |
| Maximum Direct Address Range | **00H – FFH (256 bytes)** | Internal RAM / SFR limit |

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 employs a **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** philosophy with variable-length instructions, in contrast to RISC architectures (like ARM) which mandate fixed 32-bit instruction words.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Machine Instruction

Every 8051 instruction, regardless of length, follows a universal template containing:

1. **Operation Code (Opcode) Field** — Identifies the operation (always present, typically the first byte).
2. **Operand Field(s)** — Identifies the source/destination of data (registers, memory, immediate data, or I/O ports).

The CPU's **Instruction Decoder** is hard-wired to interpret this binary template, activating the appropriate internal circuitry (ALU, register bank, bus controller).

## 2.2 The Three Instruction Format Categories

### A. 1-Byte Instruction Format
Contains **only the opcode**; the operation and operand information are *implicit* in the opcode itself (typically involves accumulator `A` or specific registers).

$$
\underbrace{[\text{Opcode (8 bits)}]}_{1 \text{ byte total}}
$$

**Examples:** `CLR A`, `CPL A`, `INC A`, `NOP`, `RET`, `RETI`

### B. 2-Byte Instruction Format
Contains **opcode + 8-bit operand** (immediate data or direct register address).

$$
\underbrace{[\text{Opcode (8 bits)}]}_{Byte\ 1} \quad \underbrace{[\text{Operand/Immediate data (8 bits)}]}_{Byte\ 2}
$$

**Examples:** `MOV A, #25H`, `MOV R0, #50H`, `ADD A, #10H`, `ANL A, #0FH`

### C. 3-Byte Instruction Format
Contains **opcode + operand address/data + another operand/data** (typical for direct memory operations).

$$
\underbrace{[\text{Opcode (8 bits)}]}_{Byte\ 1} \quad \underbrace{[\text{Operand 1 (8 bits)}]}_{Byte\ 2} \quad \underbrace{[\text{Operand 2 (8 bits)}]}_{Byte\ 3}
$$

**Examples:** `MOV 30H, #0FH`, `MOV DPTR, #2000H`, `LJUMP 1234H`, `CJNE A, #45H, label`

## 2.3 Why Instruction Size Varies — The Engineering Rationale

- **Code Density Optimization:** Common operations (like accumulator increment) consume only 1 byte, conserving precious on-chip ROM.
- **Hardware Simplicity:** A 1-byte opcode is decoded in a single fetch cycle.
- **Flexibility:** Larger address spaces (16-bit addresses like `LJMP`) require 3 bytes.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| Total Program Memory Used | $\Sigma (\text{Bytes per instruction})$ | Bytes |
| PC Increment per Instruction | $PC \leftarrow PC + N$ | $N \in \{1, 2, 3\}$ |
| Machine Cycles (1-byte) | $1$ | Machine Cycle |
| Machine Cycles (2-byte) | $1$ | Machine Cycle (8051) |
| Machine Cycles (3-byte) | $2$ | Machine Cycle (8051) |
| Crystal Frequency | $f_{osc}$ | **11.0592 MHz** (standard) |
| State Time | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{osc}}$ | Seconds |
| Machine Cycle Duration | $T_{mc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}} = 12T$ | **1.085 µs** (at 11.0592 MHz) |
| Execution Time | $T_{exec} = N_{mc} \times T_{mc}$ | Seconds |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** Think **"1-1-2"** — 1-byte takes **1** machine cycle, 2-byte takes **1** machine cycle, 3-byte takes **2** machine cycles. This is a high-frequency KTU exam question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In embedded systems design, mastering instruction format enables engineers to:

- **Optimize Firmware Size:** Choose shorter instruction formats for tight memory budgets (e.g., IoT sensor nodes with 4 KB ROM).
- **Predict Execution Timing:** Critical for real-time control loops (motor control, ABS braking) where deterministic timing is non-negotiable.
- **Reverse Engineer Binary Code:** Essential for cybersecurity, malware analysis, and legacy system maintenance.
- **Code Hand-Assembly:** Allows manual conversion of mnemonics to machine code during bootloaders or interrupt vector programming.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decoding Common 8051 Instructions

Below is an exhaustive analytical matrix showing how the three instruction formats are laid out in program memory, with the corresponding assembly mnemonics, hex encoding, and the byte-by-byte fetch sequence.

### Worked Example 1: 1-Byte Instruction (Format A)

**Assembly:** `CLR A` (Clear Accumulator — set A = 00H)

| Step | Memory Address | Content (Hex) | Content (Binary) | Field Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0000H | E4H | `1110 0100` | Opcode (CLR A) |

**Derivation Logic:**
$$
\text{Opcode Table Lookup: } \text{CLR A} \rightarrow \text{Opcode} = \text{E4H}
$$

The CPU fetches 1 byte from code memory at address `0000H`. Since the operand (Accumulator `A`) is *implicit*, no second byte is fetched. The PC increments by 1 → `PC = 0001H`.

**Machine Cycles Consumed:** 1  
**Execution Time:** $1 \times 1.085\ \mu s = 1.085\ \mu s$

---

### Worked Example 2: 2-Byte Instruction (Format B)

**Assembly:** `MOV A, #25H` (Load immediate value 25H into accumulator)

| Step | Memory Address | Content (Hex) | Content (Binary) | Field Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0000H | 74H | `0111 0100` | Opcode (MOV A, #data) |
| 2 | 0001H | 25H | `0010 0101` | Immediate Data (25H) |

**Derivation Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Opcode Table:} \quad \text{MOV A, \#data} &\rightarrow \text{Opcode} = \text{74H} \\
\text{Immediate Operand:} \quad \text{User-specified} &\rightarrow \text{25H} \\
\hline
\text{Final Encoded Word:} \quad [74H] &\ [25H]
\end{aligned}
$$

The CPU fetches byte 1 (opcode `74H`) from `0000H`, recognizes it as a "load immediate to A" instruction, fetches byte 2 (data `25H`) from `0001H`, transfers it to the accumulator. The PC ends at `0002H`.

**Machine Cycles Consumed:** 1  
**Execution Time:** $1 \times 1.085\ \mu s = 1.085\ \mu s$

---

### Worked Example 3: 3-Byte Instruction (Format C)

**Assembly:** `MOV 30H, #0FH` (Move immediate 0FH to internal RAM at address 30H)

| Step | Memory Address | Content (Hex) | Content (Binary) | Field Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0000H | 75H | `0111 0101` | Opcode (MOV direct, #data) |
| 2 | 0001H | 30H | `0011 0000` | Direct Address (30H) |
| 3 | 0002H | 0FH | `0000 1111` | Immediate Data (0FH) |

**Derivation Logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Opcode Table:} \quad \text{MOV direct, \#data} &\rightarrow \text{Opcode} = \text{75H} \\
\text{Direct Address Operand:} \quad \text{Target RAM location} &\rightarrow \text{30H} \\
\text{Immediate Data Operand:} \quad \text{Value to be stored} &\rightarrow \text{0FH} \\
\hline
\text{Final Encoded Word:} \quad [75H] &\ [30H] \ [0FH]
\end{aligned}
$$

The CPU executes a 3-phase fetch:
1. Fetches opcode `75H` at `0000H`.
2. Fetches target address `30H` at `0001H`.
3. Fetches immediate data `0FH` at `0002H`.

The value `0FH` is then written to internal RAM location `30H`. The PC ends at `0003H`.

**Machine Cycles Consumed:** 2  
**Execution Time:** $2 \times 1.085\ \mu s = 2.17\ \mu s$

---

## 3.2 Derived Analytical Relationship

For any 8051 program, the total **Program Memory Footprint (PMF)** is:

$$
PMF = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i
$$

where $L_i$ is the byte length of the $i^{th}$ instruction and $n$ is the total instruction count.

The total **Execution Time (T_exec)** is:

$$
T_{exec} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (M_i \times T_{mc})
$$

where $M_i$ is the machine cycle count for instruction $i$ (1 for 1-byte/2-byte, 2 for 3-byte in 8051), and $T_{mc}$ is the machine cycle period.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off Insight:** 3-byte instructions are powerful (full addressing) but consume more ROM and twice the execution time of 1/2-byte instructions. Optimal firmware minimizes 3-byte instructions in time-critical loops.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of an 8051 Instruction Word

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the high-level functional architecture showing how a generic 8051 instruction is structured, fetched, and decoded within the CPU pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PgmMem["Program Memory ROM 0000H-FFFFH"] --> FetchUnit["Instruction Fetch Unit"]
    FetchUnit --> Dec1{"Check Byte 1 Opcode Field"}
    Dec1 -- "E4H / 04H / 00H etc" --> OneByte["1-Byte Instruction Path"]
    Dec1 -- "74H / E8H / 28H etc" --> TwoByte["2-Byte Instruction Path"]
    Dec1 -- "75H / 90H / B4H etc" --> ThreeByte["3-Byte Instruction Path"]
    OneByte --> Exec1["Execute Implicit Operand Operation"]
    TwoByte --> Fetch2["Fetch Byte 2 Immediate or Register Data"]
    Fetch2 --> Exec2["Execute Operation with Operand"]
    ThreeByte --> Fetch3a["Fetch Byte 2 Address Field"]
    Fetch3a --> Fetch3b["Fetch Byte 3 Data or Low Address"]
    Fetch3b --> Exec3["Execute Full 3-Byte Operation"]
    Exec1 --> RegBank["Update Registers or Accumulator"]
    Exec2 --> RegBank
    Exec3 --> RegBank
    RegBank --> PCInc["Increment Program Counter by 1 2 or 3"]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Bit-Level Layout of the Three Instruction Formats

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OneByteFormat["1-Byte Instruction Format"]
        OB1["Bit7 Bit6 Bit5 Bit4 Bit3 Bit2 Bit1 Bit0"] --> OB2["Opcode Only Implicit Operand"]
    end
    subgraph TwoByteFormat["2-Byte Instruction Format"]
        TB1["Byte1 Bit7..Bit0"] --> TB2["Opcode Field"]
        TB2 --> TB3["Byte2 Bit7..Bit0"]
        TB3 --> TB4["Immediate Data or Register Address"]
    end
    subgraph ThreeByteFormat["3-Byte Instruction Format"]
        ThB1["Byte1 Bit7..Bit0"] --> ThB2["Opcode Field"]
        ThB2 --> ThB3["Byte2 Bit7..Bit0"]
        ThB3 --> ThB4["Address or Operand 1"]
        ThB4 --> ThB5["Byte3 Bit7..Bit0"]
        ThB5 --> ThB6["Immediate Data or Operand 2"]
    end
```

## 4.3 CPU Instruction Execution Flow (Fetch-Decode-Execute Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["CPU Reset or Wake Up"]) --> Fetch["Fetch Instruction Byte at PC"]
    Fetch --> Decode{"Decode Opcode to Determine Format"}
    Decode -- "Format A 1-Byte" --> ExA["Execute Implicit Operation in 1 Machine Cycle"]
    Decode -- "Format B 2-Byte" --> FetchB["Fetch Next Byte Operand from PC Plus 1"]
    FetchB --> ExB["Execute Operation in 1 Machine Cycle"]
    Decode -- "Format C 3-Byte" --> FetchC1["Fetch Second Byte at PC Plus 1"]
    FetchC1 --> FetchC2["Fetch Third Byte at PC Plus 2"]
    FetchC2 --> ExC["Execute Operation in 2 Machine Cycles"]
    ExA --> Upd["Update PC by Instruction Length"]
    ExB --> Upd
    ExC --> Upd
    Upd --> Loop{"More Instructions"}
    Loop -- "Yes" --> Fetch
    Loop -- "No" --> Halt(["HALT or IDLE State"])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is meant by the instruction format of a microcontroller? Differentiate between 1-byte, 2-byte, and 3-byte instructions with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (1 Mark):** Instruction format refers to the binary layout of a machine instruction, consisting of an *operation code (opcode)* field and one or more *operand* fields. It defines how the CPU decodes and executes the instruction.
- **1-Byte (1 Mark):** Contains only the opcode; operand is implicit. Example: `CLR A` (opcode E4H).
- **2-Byte (0.5 Mark):** Opcode + 8-bit operand. Example: `MOV A, #25H` (74H, 25H).
- **3-Byte (0.5 Mark):** Opcode + 16-bit operand. Example: `MOV 30H, #0FH` (75H, 30H, 0FH).

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of the opcode in an instruction format. Why is the 8051 called a CISC architecture?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Opcode Role (2 Marks):** The opcode (operation code) is the binary pattern in the first byte of an instruction that tells the CPU's instruction decoder *what operation* to perform (arithmetic, logical, data transfer, branching). It activates specific internal circuitry such as the ALU, register bank, or bus controller.
- **CISC Justification (1 Mark):** The 8051 is called a CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer) because it supports **variable-length instructions** (1, 2, or 3 bytes), a large instruction set (~111 opcodes), and multiple addressing modes, providing rich programming flexibility at the cost of decoder complexity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 1 Choice]`**

#### (a) Explain the various instruction formats of the 8051 microcontroller in detail. Show the byte-level encoding for the instructions `CLR A`, `MOV A, #25H`, and `MOV 30H, #0FH`. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of Instruction Format (2 Marks):**
The 8051 uses a variable-length instruction set with three formats: 1-byte, 2-byte, and 3-byte. The opcode field always occupies the first byte. Operand fields may follow based on addressing mode.

**Step 2 — 1-Byte Format (1 Mark):**
`CLR A` → Opcode: `E4H`. Stored at memory address `0000H` as a single byte. Operand (Accumulator A) is implicit. PC = 0001H after execution.

**Step 3 — 2-Byte Format (2 Marks):**
`MOV A, #25H` → First byte: opcode `74H` (MOV A, #data). Second byte: immediate data `25H`. PC = 0002H after execution. Takes 1 machine cycle.

**Step 4 — 3-Byte Format (2 Marks):**
`MOV 30H, #0FH` → First byte: opcode `75H` (MOV direct, #data). Second byte: direct address `30H`. Third byte: immediate data `0FH`. PC = 0003H after execution. Takes 2 machine cycles.

#### (b) A program segment in 8051 assembly consists of the following instructions: `MOV A, #45H`, `ADD A, #32H`, `MOV 40H, A`, `NOP`. Calculate the total program memory consumed and the total execution time, assuming a crystal frequency of 11.0592 MHz. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Instruction Lengths (2 Marks):**

| Instruction | Type | Bytes | Machine Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|
| `MOV A, #45H` | 2-byte | 2 | 1 |
| `ADD A, #32H` | 2-byte | 2 | 1 |
| `MOV 40H, A` | 2-byte | 2 | 1 |
| `NOP` | 1-byte | 1 | 1 |

**Step 2 — Calculate Total Program Memory (1 Mark):**

$$
PMF = 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 = 7 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Total Machine Cycles (1 Mark):**

$$
N_{mc} = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4 \text{ machine cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Machine Cycle Time (1 Mark):**

$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\ \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Total Execution Time (2 Marks):**

$$
T_{exec} = N_{mc} \times T_{mc} = 4 \times 1.085\ \mu s = 4.34\ \mu s
$$

**Final Answer:** Total program memory = **7 bytes**; Total execution time = **4.34 µs**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 1 Choice]`**

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the Fetch-Decode-Execute cycle of an 8051 microcontroller. How does the instruction format influence the number of fetch cycles? (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fetch-Decode-Execute Concept (2 Marks):**
The 8051 CPU processes every instruction through a 3-stage pipeline:
1. **Fetch:** The byte at the address pointed to by the Program Counter (PC) is read from program ROM and loaded into the Instruction Register (IR).
2. **Decode:** The opcode is interpreted by the instruction decoder, which generates control signals.
3. **Execute:** The relevant functional units (ALU, registers) perform the operation.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram Explanation (2 Marks):**

```
[Program ROM] → [PC] → [Instruction Register] → [Instruction Decoder] → [Control Unit] → [ALU / Registers / SFRs]
```

**Step 3 — Effect of Instruction Format on Fetch Cycles (3 Marks):**
- **1-byte instructions** require only **1 fetch cycle** (e.g., `NOP`, `CLR A`). The opcode alone contains all necessary information.
- **2-byte instructions** require **2 fetch cycles** within 1 machine cycle (e.g., `MOV A, #data`). The 8051 fetches both bytes back-to-back using its 16-bit program bus.
- **3-byte instructions** require **3 fetch cycles** spread over **2 machine cycles** (e.g., `MOV direct, #data`). The extra machine cycle accommodates the third byte fetch and data write to internal RAM.

#### (b) Differentiate clearly between machine cycle, state, and instruction cycle. Given that the 8051 uses a 12 MHz crystal, compute the time required to execute a 3-byte instruction. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Terms (3 Marks):**

- **State (T):** The basic time unit equal to one period of the oscillator. $T = 1/f_{osc}$.
- **Machine Cycle:** A unit of 12 oscillator states. Used by the 8051 for all timing operations.
- **Instruction Cycle:** The total time required to fetch, decode, and execute one complete instruction. Comprises one or more machine cycles.

**Step 2 — State Time Calculation (1 Mark):**

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{12 \times 10^6} = 0.0833\ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Machine Cycle Time (1 Mark):**

$$
T_{mc} = 12 \times T = 12 \times 0.0833\ \mu s = 1\ \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Time for 3-Byte Instruction (2 Marks):**
A 3-byte 8051 instruction requires **2 machine cycles**.

$$
T_{exec} = 2 \times T_{mc} = 2 \times 1\ \mu s = 2\ \mu s
$$

**Final Answer:** Time required to execute a 3-byte instruction = **2 µs**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — DO NOT LOSE MARKS HERE:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting that 8051 uses a **12-clock-per-machine-cycle** architecture (not 4 like many other controllers). Using 4 instead of 12 in $T_{mc} = 12/f_{osc}$ will lose 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Confusing **byte count** with **machine cycle count**. A 2-byte instruction takes 1 machine cycle, not 2!
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Writing `MOV` opcode as `70H` instead of `74H` (for MOV A,#data) or `75H` (for MOV direct,#data). Always verify opcode against the standard 8051 instruction set table.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting the **# symbol** for immediate addressing. `MOV A, 25H` means *move from address 25H*, while `MOV A, #25H` means *load value 25H*. This distinction is a favorite KTU question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Three Formats:** 8051 supports **1-byte, 2-byte, and 3-byte** instruction formats.
- 📌 **Opcode is Mandatory:** Every instruction begins with an 8-bit opcode; operands are optional/implicit based on format.
- 📌 **CISC Architecture:** Variable-length instructions classify 8051 as CISC.
- 📌 **Memory Rule "1-1-2":** 1-byte = 1 MC, 2-byte = 1 MC, 3-byte = 2 machine cycles.
- 📌 **Crystal Frequency:** Standard KTU numerical problems use $f_{osc} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$, giving $T_{mc} = 1.085\ \mu s$.
- 📌 **Master Opcode Table:** Memorize these 5 high-frequency opcodes:
  - `E4H` → CLR A
  - `74H` → MOV A, #data
  - `75H` → MOV direct, #data
  - `04H` → INC A
  - `00H` → NOP
- 📌 **PC Behavior:** PC auto-increments by instruction length (1, 2, or 3) after every fetch.
- 📌 **Implicit vs. Explicit Operands:** 1-byte instructions always have implicit operands (e.g., Accumulator A).
- 📌 **Hex Notation:** Always use suffixes `H` for hex, `B` for binary, `D` (or none) for decimal in assembly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Counter and Timer Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Counter and Timer Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **8051 Microcontroller** integrates two on-chip **16-bit Timer/Counter registers** — **Timer 0** and **Timer 1** — that can be programmed to operate either as **timers** (counting internal machine cycles derived from the oscillator) or as **counters** (counting external events applied on pins **P3.4 (T0)** and **P3.5 (T1)**). Each timer/counter consists of two 8-bit registers: the **High byte (THx)** and the **Low byte (TLx)**, which together form a 16-bit up-counter that increments on every clock pulse and sets the **Timer Overflow Flag (TFx)** when it rolls over from its maximum value (`FFFFH` or `FFH` depending on the mode) back to `00H`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Counter/Timer block is one of the **most repeated, high-weightage topics** in PCBMT402. Students are expected to clearly distinguish between *Timer Mode* and *Counter Mode*, identify the four operating modes, and perform **delay calculations** using the formula involving the oscillator frequency and the TH/TL preload values.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sand Timer

Imagine two sand timers on a table:
- The first sand timer is connected to a **mechanical clock** that drops one grain of sand every tick. You know exactly *how much time* has passed (Timer mode).
- The second sand timer is connected to a **door sensor**, and every time someone walks in, a grain falls. You are counting *events*, not seconds (Counter mode).

Both timers are **identical devices** — the only thing that changes is **what triggers the count**. The 8051's Timer/Counter works exactly the same way: the hardware is the same; only the **clock source** is selected via the **`C/T̄`** bit in the **TMOD** register.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

> [!NOTE]
> - **Machine Cycle (MC):** One machine cycle = **12 oscillator periods**. For an 11.0592 MHz crystal, one MC = 12 / 11.0592 MHz ≈ **1.085 µs**.
> - **Prescaler:** The internal divide-by-12 hardware that converts the oscillator frequency into the timer tick rate.
> - **Preload Value:** A starting value loaded into THx/TLx so that the timer overflows after a *desired* number of counts.
> - **Auto-Reload:** A feature in Mode 2 where THx holds a constant value and is automatically copied to TLx upon overflow.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Maximum 16-bit count (Mode 1): $2^{16} = 65536$
- Maximum 8-bit count (Mode 2): $2^{8} = 256$
- Oscillator-to-Timer division factor: **12**
- Standard KTU/Academic crystal: **11.0592 MHz** (used because it yields exact baud rate timings for serial communication)
- 8051 alternative crystals: 12 MHz, 16 MHz, 24 MHz
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The TMOD Register — Mode Configuration

The **Timer Mode (TMOD)** register is an **8-bit, non-bit-addressable** SFR located at address **`89H`**. It is **not** bit-addressable, so the entire byte must be loaded as a single value (e.g., `MOV TMOD, #01H`).

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Symbol | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** | **GATE** | **C/T̄** | **M1** | **M0** |
| Applies to | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 1 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 | Timer 0 |

- **GATE:** Software (0) or Hardware (1) triggering control. When GATE = 1, timer runs only when `INTx` pin is HIGH **and** `TRx` = 1.
- **C/T̄:** Clock select. `0` = Timer mode (internal oscillator ÷ 12). `1` = Counter mode (external pin T0/T1).
- **M1, M0:** Mode select bits (00, 01, 10, 11 → Modes 0, 1, 2, 3).

### 2.2 The TCON Register — Control & Flags

The **Timer Control (TCON)** register is an **8-bit, bit-addressable** SFR at address **`88H`**. Upper nibble handles timer control; lower nibble handles external interrupts.

| Bit | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Address | `8FH` | `8EH` | `8DH` | `8CH` | `8BH` | `8AH` | `89H` | `88H` |

- **TRx (Timer Run):** `1` = Start timer; `0` = Stop timer. Used in instructions like `SETB TR0` and `CLR TR0`.
- **TFx (Timer Flag):** `1` = Timer has overflowed; must be cleared by software.

### 2.3 The Four Operating Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mode 1 (16-bit)** and **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)** are the **most exam-relevant** modes in KTU.

#### Mode 0 — 13-Bit Timer
- Uses only **13 bits** (5 bits of TLx + 8 bits of THx). A legacy mode compatible with the 8048.
- Rarely used in modern 8051 designs.

#### Mode 1 — 16-Bit Timer
- Uses all 16 bits (THx as high byte, TLx as low byte).
- Counts from the preload value up to $2^{16} = 65536$.
- On overflow, **TFx = 1** and the timer resets to `0000H`. The user must **re-preload** THx/TLx to repeat a delay.
- This is the **standard mode** for generating custom delays.

#### Mode 2 — 8-Bit Auto-Reload
- TLx is the actual 8-bit counter; THx holds the **reload value**.
- On overflow, TLx resets to the value stored in THx **automatically** — no software reload required.
- Ideal for **baud-rate generation** for the serial port and for **periodic interrupts**.

#### Mode 3 — Split Timer
- Timer 0 is split into two independent 8-bit timers (TL0 and TH0).
- Timer 1 **stops** in this mode.
- Rare in textbook problems.

### 2.4 Timer vs Counter — The Critical Distinction

| Parameter | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **C/T̄ bit in TMOD** | `0` | `1` |
| **Clock Source** | Internal oscillator (÷ 12) | External pin (P3.4 for T0, P3.5 for T1) |
| **Trigger** | Automatic, machine-cycle based | Each external pulse edge |
| **Application** | Delay generation, periodic interrupts | Event counting (e.g., product count, RPM measurement) |
| **Pin used** | None | T0 (P3.4), T1 (P3.5) |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these four equations — they cover ~80% of KTU timer problems.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | $f_{osc}$ in Hz |
| 2 | Timer tick frequency | $f_{timer} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | Hz | Inverse of $T_{MC}$ |
| 3 | Total counts to overflow | $N = 65536 - \text{Preload}$ (Mode 1) | counts | Use 256 instead of 65536 for Mode 2 |
| 4 | Generated delay | $t_{delay} = N \times T_{MC}$ | seconds | Multiply by $10^6$ for µs |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Forgetting the divide-by-12 prescaler. Students often write $T_{MC} = 1/f_{osc}$ — this is **wrong**. The 8051 needs 12 oscillator cycles for one machine cycle.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Industrial conveyor control** — Counter counts bottles passing a sensor.
- **Traffic light controller** — Timer 0 generates the green-light duration; Timer 1 generates the yellow blink.
- **Digital stopwatch** — Timer 0 in Mode 1 with interrupt-on-overflow counts milliseconds.
- **UART baud rate generation** — Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)** generates exact baud rates.
- **Servo motor PWM** — Timer overflows trigger an ISR that toggles the PWM pin.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Calculating the Machine Cycle Period

**Given:** $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$

The 8051 architecture divides the oscillator frequency by **12** to produce the internal machine cycle clock. Therefore, the time taken for **one machine cycle** is:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}
$$

$$
T_{MC} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Divide the period by $10^6$ to express in microseconds. This is the *fundamental unit of time* for every delay calculation in 8051.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Computing the Preload Value for a Given Delay

**Problem:** Generate a delay of exactly **50 ms** using Timer 0, Mode 1, with $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$.

**Step 1 — Compute machine cycle period** (already derived above):

$$
T_{MC} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required number of timer counts:**

The relationship between delay, count, and machine cycle is:

$$
t_{delay} = N \times T_{MC}
$$

Rearranging for $N$:

$$
N = \frac{t_{delay}}{T_{MC}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
N = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
N = 46082.949 \approx 46083 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the 16-bit preload value (Mode 1):**

In Mode 1, the timer counts from the preload value up to $2^{16} = 65536$ before overflowing. Hence:

$$
\text{Preload} = 65536 - N
$$

$$
\text{Preload} = 65536 - 46083
$$

$$
\text{Preload} = 19453
$$

**Step 4 — Split the 16-bit preload into TH0 and TL0:**

Convert $19453$ to hexadecimal:

$$
19453 \div 16 = 1215 \text{ remainder } 13 \quad (D)
$$

$$
1215 \div 16 = 75 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad (F)
$$

$$
75 \div 16 = 4 \text{ remainder } 11 \quad (B)
$$

$$
4 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 4
$$

Reading remainders bottom-up: $\text{Preload}_{HEX} = 4BFD_{H}$

Therefore:

$$
TH0 = 4BH, \quad TL0 = FDH
$$

**Step 5 — Final 8051 Assembly Code:**

```assembly
        MOV TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1, Timer mode, software start
AGAIN:  MOV TH0, #4BH      ; Load high byte of preload
        MOV TL0, #0FDH     ; Load low byte of preload
        SETB TR0           ; Start Timer 0
HERE:   JNB TF0, HERE      ; Wait until TF0 = 1 (overflow)
        CLR TR0            ; Stop Timer 0
        CLR TF0            ; Clear overflow flag
        SJMP AGAIN         ; Repeat for next 50 ms
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Actual delay = $46083 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 50000.055 \, \mu s \approx 50$ ms. The rounding error is the unavoidable quantization error of 1.085 µs per count.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mode 2 (Auto-Reload) Baud-Rate Calculation

**Problem:** Calculate the reload value for Timer 1, Mode 2, to generate a **9600 baud** rate with $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$.

**Step 1 — Use the standard 8051 baud-rate formula:**

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - X)}
$$

where:
- $SMOD = 0$ (standard, no double-rate)
- $X$ = reload value (TH1)

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for $X$:**

$$
9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \times (256 - X)}
$$

$$
9600 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times (256 - X)}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $(256 - X)$:**

$$
256 - X = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{384 \times 9600}
$$

$$
256 - X = \frac{11059200}{3686400}
$$

$$
256 - X = 3
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $X$:**

$$
X = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0FD_{H}
$$

Therefore $TH1 = TL1 = FDH$.

> **Conversion Logic:** The 11.0592 MHz crystal was specifically chosen by Intel so that $256 - X$ divides evenly into the baud rate — eliminating the timing drift seen with 12 MHz crystals.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Counter Mode Frequency of an Event Source

**Problem:** A motor encoder sends 100 pulses per revolution. Timer 0 is configured in **Counter Mode, Mode 1**. After 1 minute, the timer has overflowed exactly 12 times. Find the **RPM** of the motor.

**Step 1 — Total pulses counted in 1 minute:**

In Mode 1, the counter overflows after 65536 counts. With 12 overflows:

$$
\text{Total Pulses} = 12 \times 65536 = 786432 \text{ pulses}
$$

**Step 2 — Revolutions per minute:**

$$
\text{RPM} = \frac{\text{Total Pulses}}{\text{Pulses per Revolution}}
$$

$$
\text{RPM} = \frac{786432}{100}
$$

$$
\text{RPM} = 7864.32
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The counter increments once per *external event edge*, not per machine cycle. The divide-by-12 prescaler is **bypassed** in Counter Mode.

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Mode to Use?

| Use Case | Recommended Mode | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| One-shot delay (LED toggle) | Mode 1 | 16-bit range gives long delays without interrupts |
| Periodic task every 1 ms | Mode 2 | Auto-reload removes ISR overhead of reloading TH/TL |
| UART serial communication | Mode 2 (Timer 1) | Standard for baud-rate generation |
| Counting external pulses | Counter Mode, Mode 1 | Need maximum count range (65536) |
| Generating PWM manually | Mode 1 with ISR | Full 16-bit range for duty-cycle resolution |
| High-frequency event counting | Counter Mode, Mode 2 | Quick auto-reset between events |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Internal Timer/Counter Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the data path inside the 8051 for Timer 0 in Mode 1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oscillator f_osc] -->|12 MHz, 11.0592 MHz, 16 MHz| B(Divide by 12 Prescaler)
    B --> C{CT bar bit in TMOD}
    C -->|CT = 0 Timer Mode| D[Internal Machine Cycle Clock]
    C -->|CT = 1 Counter Mode| E[External Pin P3.4 T0 or P3.5 T1]
    D --> F[Increment Logic]
    E --> F
    F --> G[TLx 8-bit Low Register]
    G -->|carry overflow| H[THx 8-bit High Register]
    H --> I{TFx Overflow Detect}
    I -->|set to 1| J[CPU Interrupt or Polling]
    I -->|Mode 2 only| K[Auto Reload from THx to TLx]
    K --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **Mode 2**, the dashed loop labelled *Auto Reload from THx to TLx* is the only additional pathway activated. In Modes 0, 1, 3, this loop is disabled.

### 4.2 Flowchart — Programmatic Steps to Generate a Delay

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Configure Timer] --> B[Load TMOD with mode and CT selection]
    B --> C[Load THx with high byte of preload]
    C --> D[Load TLx with low byte of preload]
    D --> E[Set TRx = 1 to start timer]
    E --> F{Is TFx = 1?}
    F -->|No, still counting| F
    F -->|Yes, overflow occurred| G[Clear TRx = 0 to stop timer]
    G --> H[Clear TFx = 0 for next iteration]
    H --> I{Repeat delay?}
    I -->|Yes| C
    I -->|No| J[End: Timer disabled, CPU continues]
```

### 4.3 Control Signal Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[TMOD Register 89H] --> B[GATE bit]
    A --> C[CT bar bit]
    A --> D[M1 M0 mode bits]
    B --> E[Software vs Hardware Trigger Logic]
    C --> F[Select Internal or External Clock]
    D --> G[Mode 0, 1, 2, or 3]
    E --> H[Final Enable Signal to Counter]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[TRx in TCON Register 88H]
    I -->|TRx = 1| J[Counter Increments]
    I -->|TRx = 0| K[Counter Frozen]
    J --> L{TFx overflow?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Set TFx to 1, request interrupt if enabled]
    L -->|No| J
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Timer/Counter Pin Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P3[Port P3 Alternate Functions] --> T0[P3.4 T0 Counter Input]
    P3 --> T1[P3.5 T1 Counter Input]
    P3 --> INT0[P3.2 INT0 External Interrupt 0]
    P3 --> INT1[P3.3 INT1 External Interrupt 1]
    P3 --> TXD[P3.1 TXD Serial Transmit]
    P3 --> RXD[P3.0 RXD Serial Receive]
    T0 --> T0B[Timer 0 Counter Mode Source]
    T1 --> T1B[Timer 1 Counter Mode Source]
    INT0 --> G0[GATE = 1 Control of Timer 0]
    INT1 --> G1[GATE = 1 Control of Timer 1]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Explain the function of the TMOD register in the 8051 microcontroller. List the four timer modes with their counting ranges.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **TMOD** (Timer Mode) register is an 8-bit SFR at address `89H`, used to configure the operating mode of Timer 0 and Timer 1. It is **not bit-addressable** and must be loaded as a complete byte. Each timer occupies 4 bits of TMOD: **GATE, C/T̄, M1, M0**.

The four modes are:
- **Mode 0 (M1 M0 = 00):** 13-bit timer (legacy 8048 mode).
- **Mode 1 (M1 M0 = 01):** **16-bit timer**, range **0 to 65535** ($2^{16} = 65536$ counts).
- **Mode 2 (M1 M0 = 10):** **8-bit auto-reload**, range **0 to 255** with automatic THx → TLx reload.
- **Mode 3 (M1 M0 = 11):** Split timer mode (Timer 0 only).

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's tip:** Most students lose the third mark by omitting the *count ranges* or by stating Mode 0 is "5-bit" instead of "13-bit".

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between Timer Mode and Counter Mode of the 8051. Mention the relevant pin numbers.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **C/T̄ bit** | `0` | `1` |
| **Clock Source** | Internal oscillator divided by 12 | External event on T0/T1 pin |
| **Pin used** | None (internal clock) | **P3.4 (T0)** and **P3.5 (T1)** |
| **Typical use** | Time delay generation | Counting external events |
