| **Tick rate** | $f_{osc} / 12$ | Frequency of external pulses |

The hardware registers (THx, TLx, TMOD, TCON) are identical in both modes — only the clock source selection changes.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of the 8051 Timer/Counter in **Mode 1 (16-bit)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **TMOD** and **TCON** registers. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Write an 8051 assembly language program to generate a delay of **exactly 30 ms** using Timer 0 in Mode 1. Show all calculations for TH0 and TL0. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

In Mode 1, the 16-bit Timer 0 uses the full pair **TH0-TL0** to count from the preload value up to `FFFFH`, then rolls over to `0000H` and sets **TF0 = 1**.

- **TMOD (address 89H):** Loaded with `01H` to select Timer 0, Mode 1, software trigger, timer mode. The upper nibble (Timer 1) is kept at `0`.
- **TCON (address 88H):** Bit-addressable. The instruction `SETB TR0` starts the timer. The CPU polls `JNB TF0, HERE` or uses an interrupt to detect overflow.

**Block Diagram Description (refer to Section 4.1):**
The 11.0592 MHz oscillator feeds a divide-by-12 prescaler, producing a 921.6 kHz machine cycle clock. This clock increments TL0. When TL0 rolls over from `FFH` to `00H`, the carry is passed to TH0. When TH0 also overflows, **TF0 = 1** and the timer stops incrementing (TR0 still equals 1; user must clear TF0 and reload).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Machine cycle period:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2:** Required counts:

$$
N = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 27649 \text{ counts}
$$

**Step 3:** Preload value:

$$
\text{Preload} = 65536 - 27649 = 37887
$$

**Step 4:** Convert 37887 to hex by repeated division by 16:

- $37887 \div 16 = 2367$ remainder **15 (F)**
- $2367 \div 16 = 147$ remainder **15 (F)**
- $147 \div 16 = 9$ remainder **3**
- $9 \div 16 = 0$ remainder **9**

Therefore: $\text{Preload} = 93FF_{H}$, so **TH0 = 93H** and **TL0 = FFH**.

**Step 5:** Assembly Program:

```assembly
        MOV TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1
BACK:   MOV TH0, #93H      ; Load high byte
        MOV TL0, #0FFH     ; Load low byte
        SETB TR0           ; Start timer
HERE:   JNB TF0, HERE      ; Wait for overflow
        CLR TR0            ; Stop timer
        CLR TF0            ; Clear flag
        SJMP BACK          ; Repeat
```

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Auto-Reload** feature of Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (8-bit)**. Why is this mode preferred for **baud-rate generation** in serial communication? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stepper motor is driven by counting pulses from an external sensor. The sensor emits **500 pulses per revolution**. Timer 0 is configured in **Counter Mode, Mode 1**. If the counter overflows **25 times in 10 seconds**, calculate the **RPM** of the motor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

In **Mode 2**, TL1 acts as the 8-bit counter, while **TH1 holds the reload value**. The mechanism is:

1. TH1 is loaded once with the desired reload value (e.g., `FDH` for 9600 baud).
2. The user copies TH1 to TL1 once: `MOV TL1, #FDH`.
3. Timer is started with `SETB TR1`.
4. TL1 increments on every tick. When it overflows from `FFH` → `00H`:
   - The hardware **automatically copies TH1 → TL1** (this is the auto-reload).
   - **TF1 is set to 1** (triggering the serial port's baud-rate generator).
5. No software reloading is needed in the ISR.

**Why preferred for baud-rate generation:**
- The serial port's internal shift register requires a **stable, periodic overflow tick**.
- Auto-reload guarantees the period is **identical for every overflow**, eliminating jitter caused by software reload latency.
- The 11.0592 MHz crystal allows $256 - X$ to be an exact integer, so the generated baud rate is **error-free**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Total pulses counted in 10 seconds:**

In Mode 1, each overflow represents 65536 counts. With 25 overflows:

$$
\text{Total Pulses} = 25 \times 65536 = 1638400
$$

**Step 2 — Revolutions in 10 seconds:**

$$
\text{Revs}_{10s} = \frac{1638400}{500} = 3276.8
$$

**Step 3 — Revolutions per minute (RPM):**

$$
\text{RPM} = \frac{3276.8 \times 60}{10} = 19660.8 \text{ RPM}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Since 10 seconds = 1/6 of a minute, multiply revolutions-per-10-seconds by 6, or equivalently, multiply by 60/10.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones — Avoid These Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting the divide-by-12 prescaler** — Using $1/f_{osc}$ instead of $12/f_{osc}$ for $T_{MC}$ is the **#1 error** in KTU scripts. This alone can wipe out **3 to 4 marks**.
> 2. **Using the wrong maximum count** — Mode 1 max is **65536**; Mode 2 max is **256**. Mixing them up gives incorrect preloads.
> 3. **Not clearing TF0** — Many students set `CLR TR0` but forget `CLR TF0`. The next loop iteration will detect the stale flag and exit instantly.
> 4. **TMOD loading error** — Writing `MOV TMOD, #01H` configures only Timer 0. If both timers are used, the upper nibble must also be set (e.g., `MOV TMOD, #11H` for both in Mode 1).
> 5. **Confusing Counter with Timer clock** — In Counter Mode, the prescaler is **bypassed**; the counter increments on **every external pulse edge**, not every machine cycle.
> 6. **Hex-to-decimal conversion slip** — Always show the hex conversion steps. Examiners award method marks even if the final byte is one off due to a single arithmetic slip.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Counter and Timer Operation**

- ✅ The 8051 has **two 16-bit Timer/Counters** (Timer 0 and Timer 1), each split into an 8-bit **THx** and **TLx**.
- ✅ **TMOD (89H)** is *not* bit-addressable; load the whole byte (e.g., `MOV TMOD, #01H`).
- ✅ **TCON (88H)** *is* bit-addressable; key bits are **TRx (start/stop)** and **TFx (overflow flag)**.
- ✅ **C/T̄ = 0** selects Timer Mode (internal clock); **C/T̄ = 1** selects Counter Mode (external pulses on P3.4 / P3.5).
- ✅ **Mode 1 (16-bit)**: Range 0 to 65535. **Software reload required** after every overflow.
- ✅ **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)**: THx holds the reload value; copied to TLx on overflow. **Used for UART baud rates**.
- ✅ **Machine Cycle Period**: $T_{MC} = 12 / f_{osc}$. For 11.0592 MHz, $T_{MC} = 1.085 \, \mu s$.
- ✅ **Delay Formula**: $t_{delay} = (65536 - \text{Preload}) \times T_{MC}$ for Mode 1.
- ✅ **Preload Formula**: $\text{Preload} = 65536 - (t_{delay} / T_{MC})$ for Mode 1.
- ✅ **GATE = 1** enables hardware control via the `INTx` pin — used for pulse-width measurement.
- ✅ **Counter Mode** bypasses the divide-by-12 prescaler; every external pulse edge increments the counter.
- ✅ **Standard crystal: 11.0592 MHz** — chosen because it produces exact integer baud rates with Mode 2 reloads.
- ✅ Standard 8051 timer code structure: Configure TMOD → Load THx → Load TLx → `SETB TRx` → Poll `JNB TFx` → `CLR TRx` → `CLR TFx`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Organization of 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory Organization of 8051

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Memory Organization of the 8051 Microcontroller** refers to the systematic, Harvard-style segregation of the addressable storage resources into two physically and logically distinct memory spaces: the **Program Memory (Code Memory)** and the **Data Memory (XData + IDATA)**. The 8051 possesses a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)** allowing it to address up to **64 KB (65,536 bytes)** of program memory, and a separate **16-bit Data Pointer (DPTR)** for addressing up to **64 KB of external data memory**, alongside **128 bytes of on-chip Internal RAM (IRAM)** and a **Special Function Register (SFR) space** occupying the upper **128 bytes (0x80–0xFF)** of the direct addressing region.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 uses a **Modified Harvard Architecture** — meaning program and data memory are accessed via *separate* address buses and signals (`PSEN` for program, `RD/WR` for data), unlike the Von Neumann architecture used in 8085 which multiplexes them.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the 8051's memory as a **two-storey office building with separate elevators**:
- **Ground Floor (Program Memory):** This is the **"Instruction Library"** where the executable code (your .hex file) is permanently stored. You go there via the `PSEN` (Program Store Enable) elevator. It is **read-only** during normal execution.
- **First Floor (Data Memory):** This is the **"Working Desk"** where the microcontroller stores *variables*, *scratchpad values*, and *control registers*. You go there via `RD/WR` (Read/Write) elevators.
- **Reception Desk (SFRs):** Specific lockers at the front of the first floor that control hardware peripherals (P0, P1, P2, P3, TCON, TMOD, SCON, etc.). You cannot store normal variables here — these are special-purpose.

Why two separate memories? Because while the CPU is *fetching* the next instruction from Program Memory, it can simultaneously *read/write* a variable from Data Memory. This is called **pipelining**, and it roughly **doubles the execution throughput** compared to a single-bus design.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Program Memory Space | **64 KB** | Addressed by 16-bit PC (0x0000–0xFFFF) |
| External Data Memory | **64 KB** | Addressed by 16-bit DPTR |
| Internal RAM (IDATA) | **128 bytes** | 0x00–0x7F |
| SFR Space | **128 bytes** | 0x80–0xFF |
| Total On-chip RAM | **256 bytes** | Unified address space 0x00–0xFF |
| Register Banks | **4 banks × 8 registers** | R0–R7, switched via PSW.3 & PSW.4 |
| Bit-addressable RAM | **16 bytes** | 0x20–0x2F (128 addressable bits) |
| Stack (on-chip) | Grows **upward** | 8-bit SP (default 0x07) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Harvard Architecture:** A computer architecture where program instructions and data are stored in *separate* memory spaces and accessed via *separate* buses. The 8051 is a textbook example.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Von Neumann Architecture:** A design where program and data share a *single* memory and bus. Used in 8085, 8086, and most modern PCs. Single bus = bottleneck, but flexible.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Memory Spaces — Operational Logic

The 8051 has **four distinct physical memory regions**, but they collapse into **two logical addressing spaces** depending on which bus the CPU uses:

**Step 1 — Program Memory Access (Code Fetch):**
- Signal used: **`PSEN̄` (Program Store Enable, active low)**
- Read-only during normal execution (writeable only during programming of flash/EPROM variants).
- 16-bit address supplied by the **Program Counter (PC)**.
- Reset vector resides at **0x0000**. External interrupt vectors are at **0x0003, 0x000B, 0x0013, 0x001B, 0x0023**.

**Step 2 — External Data Memory Access (XDATA):**
- Signal used: **`RD̄` and `WR̄`** (Read/Write, active low)
- 16-bit address supplied by **DPTR (Data Pointer)** or via `@Ri` (R0/R1) for MOVX paged access.
- Maximum size: **64 KB** (0x0000–0xFFFF), separate from code memory.
- Accessed via instructions: `MOVX A,@DPTR`, `MOVX @DPTR,A`, `MOVX A,@Ri`, `MOVX @Ri,A`.

**Step 3 — Internal Data Memory (Lower 128 bytes, 0x00–0x7F):**
- Accessed using **direct** or **indirect** addressing.
- Divided into three sub-regions:
  * **Register Banks (RB0–RB3):** 32 bytes (0x00–0x1F), 8 banks of R0–R7 (only 1 active at a time).
  * **Bit-Addressable Area:** 16 bytes (0x20–0x2F) = 128 individual addressable bits.
  * **General-Purpose RAM (Scratchpad):** 80 bytes (0x30–0x7F).

**Step 4 — Special Function Register Space (Upper 128 bytes, 0x80–0xFF):**
- Accessed using **direct addressing only** (the SFRs are not present in indirect addressing space of the 128-byte standard 8051).
- 21 of these 128 bytes are actually implemented; the rest are reserved/undefined.
- All ports, timers, serial port, interrupt, and power control registers live here.

### 2.2 The Register Banks — Why They Matter

The 8051 dedicates **32 bytes (0x00–0x1F)** to **four register banks**, each containing registers `R0` through `R7`. The active bank is selected by bits **RS0 (PSW.3)** and **RS1 (PSW.4)** in the **Program Status Word (PSW)**.

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** (default) | 0x00–0x07 |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 0x08–0x0F |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 0x10–0x17 |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 0x18–0x1F |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Register banks enable **fast context switching** in real-time systems. A high-priority interrupt service routine (ISR) can switch to Bank 1, so it uses a *different set of R0–R7* without saving the main program's registers. This is critical in real-time control systems such as automotive ECUs and industrial PLCs.

### 2.3 The Bit-Addressable Memory (0x20–0x2F)

This 16-byte region is a **special engineering feature** of the 8051. The CPU can address **each individual bit** within it using direct bit addressing (e.g., `SETB 0x20`, `CLR 0x2F.7`).

- 16 bytes × 8 bits/byte = **128 individually addressable bits** (bit addresses 0x00–0x7F).
- Bits 0x80–0xFF of the bit-addressable space map into the **bit-addressable SFRs** (ACC, B, PSW, TCON, SCON, P0, P1, P2, P3, IE, IP).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Bit Addressing?** It enables single-instruction single-bit operations (SETB, CLR, ANL, ORL) — essential for **I/O pin manipulation** and **flag handling** without disturbing the rest of the byte.

### 2.4 SFR Memory Map (Key Registers You MUST Know for KTU)

| SFR | Address | Bit-Addressable? | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P0** | 0x80 | Yes | Port 0 latch |
| **SP** | 0x81 | No | Stack Pointer (default 0x07) |
| **DPL** | 0x82 | No | Data Pointer Low byte |
| **DPH** | 0x83 | No | Data Pointer High byte |
| **PCON** | 0x87 | No | Power Control |
| **TCON** | 0x88 | Yes | Timer/Counter Control |
| **TMOD** | 0x89 | No | Timer/Counter Mode |
| **TL0** | 0x8A | No | Timer 0 Low byte |
| **TL1** | 0x8B | No | Timer 1 Low byte |
| **TH0** | 0x8C | No | Timer 0 High byte |
| **TH1** | 0x8D | No | Timer 1 High byte |
| **P1** | 0x90 | Yes | Port 1 latch |
| **SCON** | 0x98 | Yes | Serial Control |
| **SBUF** | 0x99 | No | Serial Data Buffer |
| **P2** | 0xA0 | Yes | Port 2 latch |
| **IE** | 0xA8 | Yes | Interrupt Enable |
| **P3** | 0xB0 | Yes | Port 3 latch |
| **IP** | 0xB8 | Yes | Interrupt Priority |
| **PSW** | 0xD0 | Yes | Program Status Word |
| **ACC** | 0xE0 | Yes | Accumulator |
| **B** | 0xF0 | Yes | B register (for MUL/DIV) |

### 2.5 The Stack Pointer (SP)

- 8-bit register located at SFR address **0x81**.
- On reset, SP is initialized to **0x07**, meaning the *first* PUSH will store data at 0x08.
- **Stack grows upward** in internal RAM (toward higher addresses). This is opposite to the 8085, which grows downward.
- Always incremented *before* a PUSH, decremented *after* a POP.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Fact:** Stack overflow into Register Bank 1 is a **classic bug** in 8051 assembly programs. Always initialize `SP` to **0x60 or 0x70** to relocate the stack into the upper general-purpose RAM region and protect your register banks.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula & Boundary Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Boundary | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Program Memory Size | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Bytes (64 KB) |
| External Data Memory Size | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Bytes (64 KB) |
| Internal RAM Size | $2^{7} = 128$ | Bytes (0x00–0x7F) |
| SFR Space Size | $2^{7} = 128$ | Bytes (0x80–0xFF) |
| Bit-addressable RAM bits | $16 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 128$ | Bits (0x00–0x7F bit-addr) |
| Bit-addressable SFR bits | 11 SFRs × 8 = 88 | Bits (0x80–0xF7 of bit-addr space) |
| Register Banks | $4 \times 8 \text{ registers}$ | R0–R7 per bank |
| Address range of RB0 | `0x00`–`0x07` | 8 bytes |
| Address range of RB3 | `0x18`–`0x1F` | 8 bytes |
| Stack direction | Upward (`SP` increments) | Toward higher addresses |
| Default SP after reset | `0x07` | First PUSH writes to 0x08 |
| Reset vector address | `0x0000` | 16-bit PC value |
| Highest external int vector | `0x0023` | UART interrupt |
| Number of implemented SFRs | **21** | Out of 128 possible |
| Total on-chip RAM (incl. SFR) | 128 + 128 = **256 bytes** | Shared address space 0x00–0xFF |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Harvard split of the 8051 is exploited in **embedded automotive systems**, **smart sensor nodes**, and **industrial IoT**. The deterministic, separate program fetch means a flash memory fault cannot corrupt your data memory, and the bit-addressable space allows single-pin control without complex bitwise operations — saving program memory in cost-sensitive 8-bit designs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Addressable Program Memory

The 8051's Program Counter (PC) is a 16-bit register, meaning it can hold values from `0x0000` to `0xFFFF` in hexadecimal.

**Step 1.** Identify the number of unique bit patterns for a 16-bit counter:

$$
N_{\text{patterns}} = 2^{n}, \quad \text{where } n = 16
$$

**Step 2.** Substitute the value:

$$
N_{\text{patterns}} = 2^{16}
$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate the power:

$$
2^{16} = 2^{10} \times 2^{6} = 1024 \times 64 = 65{,}536
$$

**Step 4.** Convert to KB:

$$
\frac{65{,}536 \text{ bytes}}{1024 \text{ bytes/KB}} = 64 \text{ KB}
$$

**Conclusion:** Maximum program memory = **64 KB**, occupying address range `0x0000` to `0xFFFF`.

> **Conversion Logic:** The formula $2^n$ arises because each bit can take 2 values (0 or 1), and with 16 independent bits we have $2 \times 2 \times \dots \times 2 = 2^{16}$ unique combinations, each corresponding to one addressable byte.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Bit-Addressable Bits in the 0x20–0x2F Region

**Step 1.** Identify the number of bytes in the bit-addressable region:

$$
N_{\text{bytes}} = (0x2F - 0x20 + 1) = 0x10 = 16 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 2.** Each byte contains 8 bits:

$$
N_{\text{bits/byte}} = 8
$$

**Step 3.** Total individual addressable bits:

$$
N_{\text{bits}} = N_{\text{bytes}} \times N_{\text{bits/byte}} = 16 \times 8
$$

$$
N_{\text{bits}} = 128
$$

**Conclusion:** 128 individually addressable bits exist in the 0x20–0x2F region, with bit addresses 0x00 through 0x7F.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stack Pointer Behavior (Mathematical Modeling)

The Stack Pointer (SP) is an 8-bit register (SFR 0x81), thus it can hold values from `0x00` to `0xFF`.

**Step 1.** Define the SP update rule for PUSH:

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 1
$$

**Step 2.** The byte to be pushed is then stored at:

$$
\text{Address}_{\text{store}} = \text{SP}_{\text{new}}
$$

**Step 3.** For POP, the rule is:

$$
\text{Data}_{\text{read}} = \text{Contents at } \text{SP}_{\text{old}}
$$

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 1
$$

**Step 4.** Initial state after reset:

$$
\text{SP}_{\text{initial}} = 0x07
$$

**Step 5.** First PUSH operation (e.g., `PUSH ACC`):

$$
\text{SP} = 0x07 + 1 = 0x08 \implies \text{Store ACC at address } 0x08
$$

**Step 6.** Second PUSH operation:

$$
\text{SP} = 0x08 + 1 = 0x09 \implies \text{Store next value at } 0x09
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** This **post-increment-on-write** behavior means the SP always points to the *last used* stack location (not the next free one, as in 8085). The stack grows toward higher addresses — opposite of 8085.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Address Calculation for MOVX Instruction

The instruction `MOVX A,@DPTR` fetches data from external memory using the 16-bit DPTR.

**Step 1.** DPTR is formed by concatenating DPH (SFR 0x83) and DPL (SFR 0x82):

$$
\text{DPTR} = (\text{DPH} \ll 8) \mid \text{DPL}
$$

**Step 2.** Example: If `DPH = 0x30` and `DPL = 0x50`:

$$
\text{DPTR} = (0x30 \times 256) + 0x50
$$

$$
\text{DPTR} = 12288 + 80 = 12368 \text{ (decimal)} = 0x3050 \text{ (hex)}
$$

**Step 3.** The CPU places `0x3050` on the 16-bit address bus and asserts `RD̄`, reading the byte at that external memory location into the accumulator.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Each Memory Region is Addressed

| Memory Region | Size | Address Range | Allowed Addressing Modes | Access Signal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Program Memory (internal) | 4 KB (typical) | 0x0000–0x0FFF | PC-relative, absolute, indexed | `PSEN̄` |
| Program Memory (external) | Up to 64 KB | 0x0000–0xFFFF | PC-relative, absolute, indexed | `PSEN̄` |
| Internal RAM (lower) | 128 B | 0x00–0x7F | Direct, indirect, bit | `RD̄/WR̄` (on-chip) |
| Internal RAM (SFR) | 128 B (21 impl.) | 0x80–0xFF | Direct, bit (11 SFRs) | `RD̄/WR̄` (on-chip) |
| External Data Memory | Up to 64 KB | 0x0000–0xFFFF | MOVX only (@DPTR, @Ri) | `RD̄/WR̄` (external) |

### 3.6 Memory Map — Visual ASCII Layout

```
            +----------------------+ 0xFFFF  ┐
            |   EXTERNAL PROGRAM   |         │
            |   MEMORY (up to 64K) |         │  CODE SPACE
            |   (Read via PSEN)    |         │  (64 KB max)
            |                      |         │
            |     ...              |         │
            +----------------------+ 0x0000  ┘
            
            +----------------------+ 0xFFFF  ┐
            |  SFR SPACE (128 B)   | 0x80    │  UPPER INTERNAL
            |  (21 implemented)    |         │  (special regs only)
            +----------------------+ 0x7F    │
            |  General Purpose RAM |         │
            |  (80 B)              | 0x30    │  LOWER INTERNAL
            +----------------------+ 0x2F    │  DATA MEMORY
            |  Bit-Addressable     |         │  (128 B)
            |  (16 B = 128 bits)   | 0x20    │
            +----------------------+ 0x1F    │
            |  Register Bank 3     |         │
            |  Register Bank 2     |         │
            |  Register Bank 1     | 0x08    │
            |  Register Bank 0     | 0x00    ┘
            +----------------------+

            +----------------------+ 0xFFFF  ┐
            |  EXTERNAL DATA MEM   |         │  XDATA SPACE
            |  (up to 64 KB)       |         │  (MOVX only)
            |  (Read/Write via     |         │
            |   RD/WR)             |         │
            |     ...              |         │
            +----------------------+ 0x0000  ┘
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of 8051 Memory Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpuBlock[CPU CORE\nPC, ACC, B, PSW]:::core
    progMem[PROGRAM MEMORY\n64 KB max\n0x0000 - 0xFFFF]:::code
    intRamLow[INTERNAL RAM LOWER\n128 bytes\n0x00 - 0x7F]:::data
    sfrBlock[INTERNAL RAM SFR\n128 bytes\n0x80 - 0xFF]:::sfr
    extData[EXTERNAL DATA MEMORY\n64 KB max\n0x0000 - 0xFFFF]:::data
    psenSig[PSEN-bar Signal\nProgram Fetch]:::signal
    rdSig[RD-bar Signal]:::signal
    wrSig[WR-bar Signal]:::signal
    pcBus[16-bit PC Bus]:::bus
    dptrBus[16-bit DPTR Bus]:::bus
    addrBus[16-bit Address Bus\nDirect/Indirect]:::bus
    dplReg[DPL 0x82]:::reg
    dphReg[DPH 0x83]:::reg
    spReg[SP 0x81]:::reg
    port0[P0 0x80]:::reg
    port1[P1 0x90]:::reg
    port2[P2 0xA0]:::reg
    port3[P3 0xB0]:::reg
    tconReg[TCON 0x88]:::reg
    sconReg[SCON 0x98]:::reg
    ieReg[IE 0xA8]:::reg

    cpuBlock --> pcBus
    cpuBlock --> dptrBus
    cpuBlock --> addrBus
    pcBus --> psenSig
    psenSig --> progMem
    dptrBus --> dplReg
    dptrBus --> dphReg
    addrBus --> intRamLow
    addrBus --> sfrBlock
    sfrBlock --> spReg
    sfrBlock --> port0
    sfrBlock --> port1
    sfrBlock --> port2
    sfrBlock --> port3
    sfrBlock --> tconReg
    sfrBlock --> sconReg
    sfrBlock --> ieReg
    dptrBus --> extData
    extData --> rdSig
    extData --> wrSig

    classDef core fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#fbbf24,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef code fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#fed7aa,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef data fill:#064e3b,stroke:#a7f3d0,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef sfr fill:#581c87,stroke:#e9d5ff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef signal fill:#991b1b,stroke:#fecaca,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef bus fill:#374151,stroke:#fde68a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef reg fill:#0c4a6e,stroke:#bae6fd,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Internal RAM Sub-Region Layout (Lower 128 Bytes)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    topBlock[0x7F - General Purpose RAM - 80 bytes\nScratchpad for variables]:::gpram
    bitBlock[0x20 - 0x2F - Bit-Addressable Area\n16 bytes = 128 bits]:::bitaddr
    rb3[0x18 - 0x1F - Register Bank 3\nR0 to R7]:::rb3
    rb2[0x10 - 0x17 - Register Bank 2\nR0 to R7]:::rb2
    rb1[0x08 - 0x0F - Register Bank 1\nR0 to R7]:::rb1
    rb0[0x00 - 0x07 - Register Bank 0\nDefault after reset]:::rb0

    classDef gpram fill:#065f46,stroke:#6ee7b7,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

```

### 4.3 Memory Access Routing Flowchart (PSEN vs RD/WR)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[CPU Issues Memory Address]:::start
    isCode{Is it a Code Fetch?}:::decision
    isXdata{Is it MOVX instruction?}:::decision
    intRamRoute[Use Direct or Indirect Addressing\nLower 128 bytes + SFR]:::intram
    sfrRoute[Direct addressing only\n0x80 to 0xFF]:::sfr
    psenRoute[Assert PSEN-bar\nRead from Code Memory]:::psen
    movxRoute[Assert RD-bar or WR-bar\nAccess External Data Memory]:::movx

    start --> isCode
    isCode -- Yes --> psenRoute
    isCode -- No --> isXdata
    isXdata -- Yes --> movxRoute
    isXdata -- No --> intRamRoute
    intRamRoute -- If address >= 0x80 --> sfrRoute
    intRamRoute -- If address < 0x80 --> intRamRoute

```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Harvard and Von Neumann architecture. Which one does the 8051 use?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Harvard | Von Neumann |
|---|---|---|
| Memory spaces | Separate for code & data | Single shared memory |
| Buses | Separate instruction & data buses | Single common bus |
| Simultaneous access | Yes (instruction fetch + data R/W) | No (must alternate) |
| Speed | Faster (parallel access) | Slower (bus contention) |
| Flexibility | Less flexible | More flexible |
| Example | **8051, PIC, ARM Cortex-M (Harvard variant)** | 8085, 8086, modern PCs |

**Conclusion:** The 8051 uses a **Modified Harvard Architecture** with separate program and data memory spaces accessed via separate control signals (`PSEN̄` and `RD̄/WR̄`).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the four distinct memory regions of the 8051 microcontroller with their respective sizes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051 has four physically distinct memory regions:

1. **Program Memory (Code ROM):** Up to **64 KB**, addressed by the 16-bit PC, accessed via `PSEN̄` signal.
2. **Internal Data Memory (Lower 128 bytes):** From `0x00` to `0x7F`, used for register banks, bit-addressable area, and general-purpose RAM.
3. **Special Function Registers (Upper 128 bytes):** From `0x80` to `0xFF`, with 21 registers implemented for ports, timers, serial, and interrupt control.
4. **External Data Memory (XDATA):** Up to **64 KB**, addressed by 16-bit DPTR, accessed via `MOVX` instructions and `RD̄/WR̄` signals.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks Total)

---

**Q. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal RAM organization of the 8051 microcontroller** in detail. Discuss the role of register banks, the bit-addressable area, and the general-purpose RAM region. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Special Function Register (SFR) space** of the 8051. List any **ten important SFRs** with their addresses and functions. How is the SFR space distinguished from the lower 128 bytes of internal RAM? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.(a):

The internal RAM of the 8051 occupies the **lower 128 bytes** (addresses `0x00` to `0x7F`) of the on-chip data memory. It is divided into three distinct sub-regions:

**1. Register Bank Region (0x00 to 0x1F) — 32 bytes**

This region contains **four register banks**, each with 8 registers named R0 through R7. Only one bank is active at a time, controlled by bits RS0 (PSW.3) and RS1 (PSW.4):

- **Bank 0** (default after reset): 0x00–0x07
- **Bank 1:** 0x08–0x0F
- **Bank 2:** 0x10–0x17
- **Bank 3:** 0x18–0x1F

**2. Bit-Addressable Region (0x20 to 0x2F) — 16 bytes**

This region enables **single-bit operations** on any of its 128 bits. The bit addresses range from 0x00 to 0x7F. Instructions like `SETB 0x20`, `CLR 0x2F.7`, and `JB 0x20, label` can be used.

**3. General-Purpose RAM (0x30 to 0x7F) — 80 bytes**

This is the **scratchpad area** used by the programmer to store variables, arrays, and stack. By convention, the stack is initialized to a value in this region (e.g., `MOV SP, #0x60`) to avoid conflict with the register banks.

**Diagram (ASCII):**
```
0x7F  +----------------------+
       |   General Purpose     |
       |   RAM (80 bytes)      |
0x30  +----------------------+
       |   Bit-Addressable     |
       |   (16 bytes)          |
0x20  +----------------------+
       |   Register Bank 3     | 0x18-0x1F
       |   Register Bank 2     | 0x10-0x17
       |   Register Bank 1     | 0x08-0x0F
       |   Register Bank 0     | 0x00-0x07
0x00  +----------------------+
```

**Role of Each Region:**
- **Register banks** provide fast context switching — only 1-cycle access, no address decoding.
- **Bit-addressable area** allows efficient single-bit control (e.g., toggle one I/O pin).
- **General-purpose RAM** holds the stack, arrays, and local variables.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.(b):

The **SFR space** occupies the upper 128 bytes of the on-chip 256-byte data memory (`0x80` to `0xFF`). Out of 128 possible SFR addresses, only **21 registers are physically implemented**; the rest are reserved.

**Key Distinguishing Features:**

| Feature | Lower 128 bytes (IRAM) | Upper 128 bytes (SFR) |
|---|---|---|
| Address range | 0x00–0x7F | 0x80–0xFF |
| Indirect addressing (`@R0`, `@R1`) | Allowed | NOT allowed (in basic 8051) |
| Direct addressing | Allowed | Allowed |
| Bit addressing | 0x20–0x2F only | 11 specific SFRs (ACC, B, PSW, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, P0–P3) |
| Purpose | User data, registers, stack | Hardware control registers |

**Ten Important SFRs:**

| SFR | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|
| P0 | 0x80 | Port 0 I/O latch |
| SP | 0x81 | Stack Pointer |
| DPL | 0x82 | Data Pointer Low byte |
| DPH | 0x83 | Data Pointer High byte |
| TCON | 0x88 | Timer/Counter Control and Flags |
| TMOD | 0x89 | Timer/Counter Mode selection |
| P1 | 0x90 | Port 1 I/O latch |
| SCON | 0x98 | Serial Port Control |
| P2 | 0xA0 | Port 2 I/O latch |
| IE | 0xA8 | Interrupt Enable register |
| P3 | 0xB0 | Port 3 I/O latch |
| PSW | 0xD0 | Program Status Word (flags, RS bits) |
| ACC | 0xE0 | Accumulator |
| B | 0xF0 | B register for MUL/DIV |

**Distinguishing Mechanism:** The CPU decodes the 8-bit address. If A7 = 0, it accesses the lower 128 bytes; if A7 = 1, it accesses the SFR space. This is why **MOV A,@R0 cannot access SFRs** in the standard 8051 — it implicitly targets the lower 128 bytes.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks Total)

---

**Q. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Explain the **Program Memory and External Data Memory organization** of the 8051. How are they accessed differently? What are the reset and interrupt vector addresses? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a diagram, describe the **bit-addressable memory of the 8051** in detail. Explain how bit addresses 0x00 to 0x7F map to bytes 0x20 to 0x2F and how bit addresses 0x80 to 0xFF map into the SFR space. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.(a):

**Program Memory (Code Memory):**

The 8051's program memory is a **64 KB address space** (`0x0000` to `0xFFFF`) accessed via a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)**. The CPU fetches each opcode by placing the PC value on the address bus and asserting the **`PSEN̄` (Program Store Enable)** signal. This memory is **read-only** during normal execution.

- **Internal Program Memory:** Variants like 8051 have 4 KB on-chip ROM, 8751 has 4 KB UV-EPROM, AT89C51 has 4 KB Flash. Beyond this, external ROM can be connected (up to 64 KB total).
- **Reset Vector:** `0x0000` — the CPU always starts execution here after power-on or reset.
- **Interrupt Vectors:**
  - External Interrupt 0 (INT0): `0x0003`
  - Timer 0 Overflow: `0x000B`
  - External Interrupt 1 (INT1): `0x0013`
  - Timer 1 Overflow: `0x001B`
  - Serial Port (UART): `0x0023`

**External Data Memory (XDATA):**

The external data memory is also up to **64 KB** but is **completely separate** from program memory. It is accessed using the `MOVX` instruction family, with the address provided by **DPTR** or by **R0/R1** (for paged access of 256 bytes). Access signals are `RD̄` (read) and `WR̄` (write).

**Differences in Access:**

| Aspect | Program Memory | External Data Memory |
|---|---|---|
| Control signal | `PSEN̄` | `RD̄` / `WR̄` |
| Address source | PC | DPTR (or R0/R1) |
| Access instructions | All fetch ops | `MOVX` only |
| Read/Write | Read-only normally | Read/Write |
| Maximum size | 64 KB | 64 KB |
| Address space | Code space (separate) | Data space (separate) |

---

#### Model Solution for Q.(b):

The 8051 supports **direct bit addressing** on 256 individual bits (addresses 0x00 to 0xFF). These split into two groups:

**Group 1: Bits in Internal RAM (0x20–0x2F) → Bit Addresses 0x00–0x7F**

The 16 bytes from 0x20 to 0x2F are bit-addressable. The mapping follows this rule:

$$
\text{Bit address} = (n - 0x20) \times 8 + b
$$

where $n$ is the byte address (0x20 to 0x2F) and $b$ is the bit position (0 to 7).

**Example:** Byte `0x22`, bit 3:

$$
\text{Bit address} = (0x22 - 0x20) \times 8 + 3 = 2 \times 8 + 3 = 19 = 0x13
$$

So `0x13` (bit address) refers to byte `0x22`, bit 3.

**Group 2: Bits in SFR Space → Bit Addresses 0x80–0xFF**

Eleven SFRs are bit-addressable, occupying 11 × 8 = 88 bits. The bit address mapping is:

$$
\text{Bit address} = (\text{SFR address} - 0x80) \times 8 + b
$$

**Example:** SFR ACC at address `0xE0`, bit 5:

$$
\text{Bit address} = (0xE0 - 0x80) \times 8 + 5 = 0x60 \times 8 + 5 = 768 + 5 = 773 = 0x305
$$

Wait — that exceeds 0xFF. The actual 8051 convention is simpler: **bit addresses 0x80 to 0xF7** map directly to certain SFRs using the rule:

$$
\text{Bit address} = \text{SFR address} + b
$$

**Example:** ACC (0xE0), bit 5:

$$
\text{Bit address} = 0xE0 + 5 = 0xE5
$$

**Diagram of Bit-Address Space:**

```
0xFF  +-----+
       | P3  | 0xB0-0xB7
0xF7  +-----+
       | IP  | 0xB8-0xBF
0xBF  +-----+
       | IE  | 0xA8-0xAF
0xAF  +-----+
       | P2  | 0xA0-0xA7
0xA7  +-----+
       | SCON| 0x98-0x9F
0x9F  +-----+
       | P1  | 0x90-0x97
0x97  +-----+
       | TCON| 0x88-0x8F
0x8F  +-----+
       | P0  | 0x80-0x87
0x80  +-----+
       | 0x2F bit 7  | 0x7F
       | ...          | ...
       | 0x20 bit 0  | 0x00
0x00  +-----+
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing bit addresses and byte addresses:** Students often write `MOV A, 0x20` thinking it moves the byte, but they meant the bit. Use `SETB`/`CLR`/`JB` for bit operations.
> 2. **SFRs not accessible via indirect addressing:** Writing `MOV A, @R0` where R0=0xE0 (ACC address) will **NOT** read ACC — it will read IRAM byte 0xE0 (which doesn't exist). Only **direct addressing** can access SFRs.
> 3. **Stack pointer default confusion:** Many students think the stack starts at 0x07. Actually, **0x07 is the SP value**; the first PUSH writes to **0x08** (SP increments first).
> 4. **Register bank switch forgetting:** Changing PSW bits RS0/RS1 changes R0–R7 mappings — your variables in R0 are no longer where you think they are.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The 8051 uses a **Modified Harvard Architecture** with separate code and data memory spaces.
- ✅ **Program Memory** is up to **64 KB**, accessed via the 16-bit PC and the `PSEN̄` signal.
- ✅ **Reset vector** is at **`0x0000`**. Interrupt vectors start at `0x0003` and are spaced 8 bytes apart.
- ✅ **External Data Memory (XDATA)** is up to **64 KB**, accessed via `MOVX` instructions and `RD̄/WR̄` signals.
- ✅ The **DPTR (DPH+DPL)** is a 16-bit address register used for MOVX access: `DPTR = (DPH << 8) | DPL`.
- ✅ **Internal RAM** has 128 bytes (`0x00` to `0x7F`) divided into:
  * **Register Banks** (4 × 8 bytes, `0x00`–`0x1F`), selected by PSW.3 and PSW.4.
  * **Bit-Addressable Area** (16 bytes, `0x20`–`0x2F`) = 128 individual bits.
  * **General-Purpose RAM** (80 bytes, `0x30`–`0x7F`) — used for stack and variables.
- ✅ **SFR space** occupies the upper 128 bytes (`0x80`–`0xFF`) with **21 implemented registers**; only direct addressing (and bit addressing for 11 SFRs) is permitted.
- ✅ **Stack grows upward** (SP increments on PUSH). Default SP after reset is `0x07` → first PUSH writes to `0x08`.
- ✅ All four 8051 ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) are bit-addressable SFRs at `0x80`, `0x90`, `0xA0`, `0xB0`.
- ✅ The `PSW` register at `0xD0` contains the carry, auxiliary carry, parity, overflow, and register-bank-select flags.
- ✅ The `ACC` (`0xE0`) and `B` (`0xF0`) are both bit-addressable; `B` is used by MUL and DIV instructions.
- ✅ For MOVX paged access, use `MOVX A,@Ri` (i = 0 or 1) for 256-byte pages; use `MOVX A,@DPTR` for full 64 KB access.
- ✅ **Key constant to memorize:** $2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64 \text{ KB}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Transfer Instructions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Data Transfer Instructions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Transfer Instructions** in the **Intel 8051 microcontroller** are a class of instructions that **copy** data from a **source operand** to a **destination operand** without altering the source. These instructions form the foundational block of any 8051 assembly program and are responsible for moving 8-bit or 16-bit data between the **Accumulator (A)**, **General Purpose Registers (R0–R7)**, **Data Pointer (DPTR)**, **Program Counter (PC)**, **Internal RAM (00H–7FH)**, **Special Function Registers (SFRs, 80H–FFH)**, and **External Data Memory (0000H–FFFFH)**.

The eight core data transfer mnemonics studied under KTU PCBMT402 Module 2 are:
1. `MOV` (Move)
2. `MOVC` (Move Code)
3. `MOVX` (Move External)
4. `PUSH` (Push onto Stack)
5. `POP` (Pop from Stack)
6. `XCH` (Exchange)
7. `XCHD` (Exchange Digit)
8. `SWAP` (Swap Nibbles inside Accumulator)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Post Office & Warehouse:**
> Think of the 8051's memory as a **massive postal warehouse** with thousands of labeled lockers (addresses).
> - **`MOV` is a clerk who copies a parcel** from locker A to locker B. The original parcel in A still exists; only a duplicate is placed in B.
> - **`XCH` is a swap exchange** — two parcels at lockers A and B are physically exchanged in a single handshake.
> - **`PUSH`/`POP`** are like a **spring-loaded inbox tray on a desk** — PUSH slides a parcel onto the top, POP takes the most recent one off. Last-In, First-Out (LIFO).
> - **`MOVC`** is a **read-only reference librarian** who can only fetch (read) books from a protected archive (Program Memory) and never write to it.
> - **`MOVX`** is a **special courier with a passkey** who can access a secured external warehouse (External Data Memory).

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Internal RAM size:** **128 Bytes** (00H–7FH)
- **SFR space size:** **128 Bytes** (80H–FFH), partially populated
- **External Data Memory addressable:** **64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH) using 16-bit DPTR
- **Program (Code) Memory addressable:** **64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH)
- **Stack Pointer default reset value:** **07H** (grows upward)
- **DPTR width:** **16 bits** (composed of DPH + DPL)
- **Data width transferred:** **8 bits** for most ops, **16 bits** for DPTR load

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Data Transfer Operations

The 8051 data transfer instruction set can be classified into **three behavioral categories** that align directly with the **three addressable memory spaces** of the Harvard-architecture 8051.

### Category A — Internal Data Movement (`MOV` family)
Operates entirely within the **Internal RAM and SFR region**. Most flexible category.

- **Syntax:** `MOV <dest>, <src>`
- **Size:** 1, 2, or 3 bytes per instruction
- **Flag impact:** **No flags are affected** (PSW bits unchanged)
- **Allowed operands:** A, Rn (R0–R7), direct address, @Ri (indirect), immediate #data, DPTR

### Category B — Code Memory Read (`MOVC` family)
- **Read-only** access to **Program Memory** (ROM/Flash).
- **Variants:**
  * `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → Indexes with 16-bit base
  * `MOVC A, @A+PC` → Indexes with 16-bit base (PC pre-incremented)
- Used for **Look-Up Tables (LUTs)** and **character/font generation**.

### Category C — External Data Access (`MOVX` family)
- The only instructions that touch **External Data Memory**.
- Requires port lines **P0** (multiplexed A0–A7 / D0–D7) and **P2** (A8–A15).
- **Variants:**
  * `MOVX @DPTR, A` (write)
  * `MOVX A, @DPTR` (read)
  * `MOVX @Ri, A` and `MOVX A, @Ri` (8-bit page address)

### Category D — Stack Operations (`PUSH` / `POP`)
- Direct addressing only (the operand must be a direct address or SFR name).
- **PUSH** increments SP first, then writes.
- **POP** reads first, then decrements SP.
- Used for **register bank switching** and **context save/restore** during interrupts/subroutines.

### Category E — Exchange Operations (`XCH` / `XCHD` / `SWAP`)
- Atomic byte/digit swaps — no temporary register needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam:** `MOV` operates within internal data space only. If the problem says *"fetch from external RAM"* or *"read program memory"*, the answer is `MOVX` or `MOVC` respectively, **never** plain `MOV`.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates every key formula, addressing range, and operand rule tested in KTU PCBMT402 Module 2.

| # | Instruction | Syntax Template | Addressing Mode | Bytes | Cycles | Flags Affected |
|---|------------|----------------|------------------|-------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | MOV A, Rn | `MOV A, R0..R7` | Register | 1 | 12 | None |
| 2 | MOV A, direct | `MOV A, 30H` | Direct | 2 | 12 | None |
| 3 | MOV A, @Ri | `MOV A, @R0` | Register Indirect | 1 | 12 | None |
| 4 | MOV A, #data | `MOV A, #25H` | Immediate | 2 | 12 | None |
| 5 | MOV Rn, A | `MOV R3, A` | Register | 1 | 12 | None |
| 6 | MOV Rn, direct | `MOV R2, 40H` | Direct | 2 | 24 | None |
| 7 | MOV Rn, #data | `MOV R1, #0FH` | Immediate | 2 | 12 | None |
| 8 | MOV direct, A | `MOV 30H, A` | Direct | 2 | 12 | None |
| 9 | MOV direct, Rn | `MOV 30H, R4` | Direct | 2 | 24 | None |
| 10 | MOV direct1, direct2 | `MOV 20H, 30H` | Direct-to-Direct | 3 | 24 | None |
| 11 | MOV direct, @Ri | `MOV 30H, @R0` | Indirect | 2 | 24 | None |
| 12 | MOV direct, #data | `MOV P1, #55H` | Immediate | 3 | 24 | None |
| 13 | MOV DPTR, #data16 | `MOV DPTR, #2000H` | Immediate 16-bit | 3 | 24 | None |
| 14 | MOVC A, @A+DPTR | LUT fetch via DPTR | Indexed | 1 | 24 | None |
| 15 | MOVC A, @A+PC | LUT fetch via PC | Indexed | 1 | 24 | None |
| 16 | MOVX A, @DPTR | Read ext RAM via DPTR | External Indirect | 1 | 24 | None |
| 17 | MOVX @DPTR, A | Write ext RAM via DPTR | External Indirect | 1 | 24 | None |
| 18 | MOVX A, @Ri | Read ext RAM (8-bit) | External Indirect | 1 | 24 | None |
| 19 | MOVX @Ri, A | Write ext RAM (8-bit) | External Indirect | 1 | 24 | None |
| 20 | PUSH direct | `PUSH 30H` | Direct | 2 | 24 | None |
| 21 | POP direct | `POP 30H` | Direct | 2 | 24 | None |
| 22 | XCH A, Rn | `XCH A, R3` | Register | 1 | 12 | None |
| 23 | XCH A, direct | `XCH A, 30H` | Direct | 2 | 12 | None |
| 24 | XCH A, @Ri | `XCH A, @R0` | Indirect | 1 | 12 | None |
| 25 | XCHD A, @Ri | `XCHD A, @R0` | Indirect (low nibble) | 1 | 12 | None |
| 26 | SWAP A | `SWAP A` | Implicit | 1 | 12 | None |

**Time Calculation Formula (used for delay loops in KTU problems):**

$$
T_{\text{instruction}} = \text{Number of cycles} \times T_{\text{machine}} = \text{Cycles} \times \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}
$$

**Machine Cycle Relation:**

$$
T_{\text{machine}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}
$$

**Real-World Utility:**
- **MOV/MOVX/MOVC** form the backbone of every embedded firmware — sensor reading, LCD driving, EEPROM access, ADC interfacing.
- **PUSH/POP** are essential for **interrupt service routines (ISRs)** where registers must be saved atomically.
- **XCH/SWAP** are heavily used in **BCD arithmetic** and **hex-to-ASCII conversions** for displays.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis — The `MOV` Family

### 3.1.1 Internal Working of `MOV A, direct`

**Instruction:** `MOV A, 30H`
**Opcode byte:** `E5 30` (2-byte instruction)
**Bytes consumed from PC:** 2

**Step-by-step execution trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{MCU fetches opcode E5 from Program Memory at PC} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1 \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{MCU fetches direct address 30H from next program byte} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 1 \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{ALU generates effective Internal RAM address = 30H} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Data bus reads the byte stored at Internal RAM[30H]} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{Accumulator} \leftarrow \text{Internal RAM[30H]} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Program Counter} \leftarrow \text{PC} \quad \text{(moves to next instruction)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **source location (30H) is NOT cleared**. Only the Accumulator is overwritten. This is the universal "copy, not move" rule.

### 3.1.2 Working of `MOV DPTR, #data16`

This is the **only 3-byte immediate data instruction** in the 8051 data transfer set.

**Instruction:** `MOV DPTR, #2500H`
**Encoding:** `90 25 00`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Fetch opcode 90H (MOV DPTR)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Fetch immediate high byte = 25H} \rightarrow \text{DPH} \leftarrow 25\text{H} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Fetch immediate low byte = 00H} \rightarrow \text{DPL} \leftarrow 00\text{H} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{DPTR} = \text{DPH:DPL} = 25\text{H:00H} = 2500\text{H} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 3
\end{aligned}
$$

This sets up DPTR to point at external data memory address 2500H for subsequent `MOVX` operations.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis — The `PUSH` and `POP` Mechanism

The 8051 stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses). This is the **opposite** of the 8085/8086 microprocessors.

### 3.2.1 PUSH Operation: `PUSH direct`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1 \quad \text{(increment SP first)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Read the byte at the specified direct address} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Internal RAM[SP]} \leftarrow \text{byte read} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 POP Operation: `POP direct`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Read the byte at Internal RAM[SP]} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Load the specified direct address with the byte read} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} - 1 \quad \text{(decrement SP last)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{PC} \leftarrow \text{PC} + 2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Trace — PUSH/POP on Register Bank 0

Assume **initial state:** SP = 07H, Accumulator = AAH, R0 = 55H, R1 = 66H.

| Step | Instruction | SP (after) | RAM[08H] | RAM[09H] | A | R0 | R1 |
|------|------------|------------|----------|----------|-----|-----|-----|
| 1 | Initial | 07H | xx | xx | AAH | 55H | 66H |
| 2 | `PUSH ACC` | 08H | AAH | xx | AAH | 55H | 66H |
| 3 | `PUSH 00H` (R0 addr) | 09H | AAH | 55H | AAH | 55H | 66H |
| 4 | `MOV A, #00H` | 09H | AAH | 55H | 00H | 55H | 66H |
| 5 | `MOV R0, #FFH` | 09H | AAH | 55H | 00H | FFH | 66H |
| 6 | `POP 00H` | 08H | AAH | 55H | 00H | 55H | 66H |
| 7 | `POP ACC` | 07H | AAH | 55H | AAH | 55H | 66H |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack is LIFO:** Item pushed last is popped first. Mismatched PUSH/POP order is a **frequent KTU mistake** that the examiner penalises under "logical error" deduction.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis — `MOVC` (Look-Up Table)

**Use Case:** Convert a hex digit (0–F) stored in A to its ASCII equivalent.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source code:} \quad & \\
& \text{MOV DPTR, \#TABLE} \quad ; \text{DPTR} \leftarrow \text{base address of LUT} \\
& \text{MOVC A, @A+DPTR} \quad ; \text{A} \leftarrow \text{code memory[DPTR + A]} \\
& \\
\text{Suppose A} & = 0\text{BH (i.e., 11 decimal, hex digit 'B')} \\
\text{DPTR} & = 0200\text{H (LUT start address)} \\
\text{Then effective address} & = \text{DPTR} + \text{A} = 0200\text{H} + 000\text{BH} = 020\text{BH} \\
\text{Contents at 020BH} & = 42\text{H (ASCII of 'B')} \\
\text{Final A} & = 42\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The ASCII codes for '0'..'9' are 30H..39H and for 'A'..'F' are 41H..46H. The table must be pre-stored in **Program Memory** at known addresses.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis — `XCH` and `XCHD`

### 3.4.1 XCH (Full Byte Exchange)

**Instruction:** `XCH A, R0`
**Initial:** A = 3FH, R0 = 42H
**After:** A = 42H, R0 = 3FH

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Read A} \rightarrow \text{TEMP} \leftarrow \text{A} = 3\text{FH} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Read R0} \rightarrow \text{A} \leftarrow \text{R0} = 42\text{H} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{R0} \leftarrow \text{TEMP} = 3\text{FH}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.2 XCHD (Lower Nibble Exchange)

**Instruction:** `XCHD A, @R0`
**Initial:** A = 3FH, Internal RAM[R0] = 52H
**Result:** A = 32H, Internal RAM[R0] = 5FH

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Upper nibble of A} & = 3 \quad \text{(unchanged)} \\
\text{Lower nibble of A} & = \text{F} \quad \text{(swapped)} \\
\text{Upper nibble of [R0]} & = 5 \quad \text{(unchanged)} \\
\text{Lower nibble of [R0]} & = 2 \quad \text{(swapped)} \\
& \\
\text{New A} & = 32\text{H} \\
\text{New [R0]} & = 5\text{FH}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4.3 SWAP (Nibble Swap within A)

**Instruction:** `SWAP A`
**Initial:** A = A5H
**After:** A = 5AH

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Before: } & \text{A} = 1010\;0101\text{B} = \text{A5H} \\
\text{After: } & \text{A} = 0101\;1010\text{B} = 5\text{AH}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Allowed vs. Forbidden Operands

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU topic**. The examiner frequently tests whether the student knows which operand combinations are illegal.

| Instruction | A | Rn | direct | @Ri | #data | #data16 | DPTR |
|-------------|---|----|----|------|-------|---------|------|
| **MOV dest, src** | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ (use DPTR) | ✗ |
| **MOV DPTR, #** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ only | ✗ |
| **MOVX A, @** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (Ri) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (DPTR) |
| **MOVX @, A** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (Ri) | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (DPTR) |
| **MOVC A, @** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ (DPTR/PC) |
| **PUSH / POP** | ✓ (ACC) | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **XCH A, src** | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **XCHD A, src** | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| **SWAP** | ✓ only | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — 8051 Data Transfer Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Memory 64KB] -->|MOVC A, @A+DPTR| B[Accumulator A]
    A2[Program Memory 64KB] -->|MOVC A, @A+PC| B
    B -->|MOVX DPTR, A| C[External Data Memory 64KB]
    C -->|MOVX A, DPTR| B
    D[Internal RAM 00H-7FH] -->|MOV direct| B
    B -->|MOV direct| D
    D -->|MOV @Ri| E[Indirect Addressing R0/R1]
    E --> D
    F[SFR Region 80H-FFH] -->|MOV direct| B
    B -->|MOV direct| F
    G[Stack Internal RAM] -->|PUSH direct| D
    D -->|POP direct| G
    H[Register Banks R0-R7] -->|MOV Rn| B
    B -->|MOV Rn| H
    I[DPTR DPH+DPL] -->|MOVC / MOVX base| B
    B -->|MOV DPTR 16 bit| I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** `MOV` arrows form a closed loop among Internal RAM, SFR, Registers, and Accumulator. `MOVC` is a **one-way arrow** from Program Memory to A (read-only). `MOVX` creates the only bridge to **External Data Memory**.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — MOVC Indexed Addressing Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Fetch Opcode MOVC] --> step2[Read Accumulator value]
    step2 --> step3[Read DPTR or PC value]
    step3 --> step4[ALU Adds A to DPTR or PC]
    step4 --> step5[Form 16-bit effective address]
    step5 --> step6[Access Program Memory byte]
    step6 --> step7[Load result into Accumulator]
    step7 --> step8[Increment PC to next instruction]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — PUSH/POP Stack Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pStart[Execute PUSH direct] --> p1[SP = SP + 1]
    p1 --> p2[Read byte at direct address]
    p2 --> p3[Write byte to RAM at new SP]
    p3 --> pEnd[Instruction complete]
    popStart[Execute POP direct] --> pop1[Read byte at RAM SP]
    pop1 --> pop2[Write byte to direct address]
    pop2 --> pop3[SP = SP - 1]
    pop3 --> popEnd[Instruction complete]
```

## 4.4 Classification Tree — Full Data Transfer Family

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[Data Transfer Instructions]
    root --> m1[MOV Family - Internal]
    root --> m2[MOVC Family - Code Memory]
    root --> m3[MOVX Family - External RAM]
    root --> m4[Stack Family - PUSH POP]
    root --> m5[Exchange Family - XCH XCHD SWAP]
    m1 --> m1a[Register to Register]
    m1 --> m1b[Immediate to Register]
    m1 --> m1c[Direct to Direct]
    m1 --> m1d[Indirect to A]
    m1 --> m1e[DPTR Load 16 bit]
    m2 --> m2a[DPTR Indexed]
    m2 --> m2b[PC Indexed]
    m3 --> m3a[DPTR Indirect]
    m3 --> m3b[R0 or R1 Indirect 8 bit]
    m4 --> m4a[PUSH direct]
    m4 --> m4b[POP direct]
    m5 --> m5a[Full Byte XCH]
    m5 --> m5b[Low Nibble XCHD]
    m5 --> m5c[SWAP A]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the instructions `MOV`, `MOVC`, and `MOVX` in the 8051 microcontroller. State the addressing mode used by each and the memory space accessed.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `MOV` | `MOVC` | `MOVX` |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory space | Internal RAM & SFR (00H–FFH) | Program Memory (Code ROM) | External Data Memory |
| Direction | Read/Write (Copy) | Read-only (Copy) | Read/Write (Copy) |
| Addressing | Register, Direct, Indirect, Immediate | Indexed (@A+DPTR, @A+PC) | Indirect (@DPTR, @Ri) |
| Flag effect | None | None | None |
| Bus signals | Internal only | PSEN-bar activated | RD-bar / WR-bar activated |

`MOV` is used for internal data shuffling. `MOVC` is used to fetch constants or look-up tables from code memory. `MOVX` is used to access external RAM chips.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the working of the `PUSH` and `POP` instructions in the 8051. How does the Stack Pointer (SP) behave during each operation?**

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 stack grows **upward** in Internal RAM starting from address 08H by default (SP resets to 07H).

- **`PUSH direct`** performs two operations: (1) `SP = SP + 1`, then (2) writes the byte from the direct address into Internal RAM at the new SP location.
- **`POP direct`** performs: (1) reads the byte at `RAM[SP]`, (2) stores it into the specified direct address, (3) `SP = SP − 1`.

The stack is LIFO. PUSH and POP must be paired in reverse order to preserve data. SP must be initialized to a safe value (e.g., 30H or 60H) before use to avoid overlap with register banks 0–3.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting that `MOV` to `Rn` from `#data` is 2 bytes/1 cycle, but `MOV direct, Rn` is 2 bytes/2 cycles.** The cycle count differs!
> 2. **Wrong operand combinations** (e.g., writing `MOV DPTR, A` — illegal!). Always verify using the addressing-mode matrix in Section 2.2.
> 3. **Confusing MOVC and MOVX.** MOVC reads from code memory; MOVX reads from external data memory. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks per such error.
> 4. **Incorrect stack order in PUSH/POP.** PUSH increments SP first; POP decrements SP last. Reversing this is a common error.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention that no flags are affected** by data transfer instructions. Examiners often allocate 1 mark for this statement.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain any **eight addressing modes** supported by the 8051 microcontroller with one example instruction for each. State the symbol used to denote each mode. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Write an 8051 ALP to copy a block of 10 bytes of data starting from internal RAM location 30H to external RAM location starting at 2000H. Use appropriate data transfer instructions. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The 8051 supports **eight addressing modes**:

1. **Immediate Addressing** — Operand is a constant. Symbol: `#`
   * Example: `MOV A, #25H`

2. **Register Addressing** — Operand is one of R0–R7. Symbol: `Rn`
   * Example: `MOV A, R3`

3. **Direct Addressing** — Operand is the 8-bit address of an Internal RAM or SFR location. Symbol: `direct`
   * Example: `MOV A, 30H`

4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — Address is in R0 or R1. Symbol: `@Ri`
   * Example: `MOV A, @R0`

5. **Indexed Addressing** — Used with DPTR or PC. Symbol: `@A+DPTR`, `@A+PC`
   * Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`

6. **Implied (Inherent) Addressing** — Operand is implicit (e.g., A, DPTR). Symbol: none
   * Example: `SWAP A`, `DA A`

7. **Relative Addressing** — Used by branch instructions. Symbol: `rel`
   * Example: `SJMP LABEL`

8. **Absolute Addressing** — Used by `AJMP` and `ACALL`. Symbol: `addr11`
   * Example: `AJMP LABEL`

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Algorithm:**
1. Load source pointer in R0.
2. Load DPTR with destination address 2000H.
3. Loop 10 times: read from internal RAM, write to external RAM.

**Assembly Language Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV R0, #30H        ; R0 points to source in internal RAM
        MOV DPTR, #2000H    ; DPTR points to destination in ext RAM
        MOV R2, #0AH        ; Counter = 10

LOOP:   MOV A, @R0          ; A = Internal RAM[R0]
        MOVX @DPTR, A       ; External RAM[DPTR] = A
        INC R0              ; Increment source pointer
        INC DPTR            ; Increment destination pointer
        DJNZ R2, LOOP       ; Decrement counter, loop if not zero
        END
```

**Memory trace (first iteration only):**

| Step | R0 | DPTR | A | Internal RAM[30H] | External RAM[2000H] |
|------|----|------|---|-------------------|---------------------|
| Init | 30H | 2000H | xx | (assume 5AH) | xx |
| Step 1 | 30H | 2000H | 5AH | 5AH | xx |
| Step 2 | 30H | 2000H | 5AH | 5AH | 5AH |
| Step 3 | 31H | 2001H | 5AH | 5AH | 5AH |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The counter `R2` is decremented using `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero), which itself is **not** a data transfer instruction but is the standard KTU-approved loop control.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe the working of the instructions `XCH`, `XCHD`, and `SWAP` with suitable examples. Show their effect on the Accumulator and the addressed memory. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Design an 8051 ALP to read a hex digit (0–F) from internal RAM location 40H, convert it to its ASCII equivalent using a Look-Up Table stored in code memory, and store the result back in location 41H. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. XCH A, byte** — Full 8-bit byte exchange between A and the operand.

- **Example:** If `A = 3FH` and `R0 = 52H`, then executing `XCH A, R0` results in `A = 52H` and `R0 = 3FH`.
- **Use case:** Useful in sorting and swapping algorithms.
- **Bytes/Cycles:** 1 byte / 12 cycles.

**2. XCHD A, @Ri** — Exchange only the **lower 4 bits (nibble)** between A and the internal RAM byte pointed by R0 or R1.

- **Example:** If `A = 3FH` and `Internal RAM[R0] = 52H`, then executing `XCHD A, @R0` results in `A = 32H` and `Internal RAM[R0] = 5FH`.
- **Use case:** BCD digit manipulations, hex-to-BCD conversions.
- **Bytes/Cycles:** 1 byte / 12 cycles.

**3. SWAP A** — Exchange the upper and lower 4-bit nibbles inside the Accumulator itself.

- **Example:** If `A = A5H = 10100101B`, then `SWAP A` produces `A = 5AH = 01011010B`.
- **Bytes/Cycles:** 1 byte / 12 cycles.

**Comparison Table:**

| Instruction | Bits Exchanged | Operand Required | Memory Affected |
|---|---|---|---|
| `XCH A, Rn` | 8 bits | R0–R7 | R0–R7 |
| `XCH A, direct` | 8 bits | Direct address | Internal RAM/SFR |
| `XCH A, @Ri` | 8 bits | R0 or R1 | Internal RAM |
| `XCHD A, @Ri` | 4 bits (low) | R0 or R1 | Internal RAM |
| `SWAP A` | 4 bits ↔ 4 bits | None (A only) | A only |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Algorithm:**
1. Read the hex digit from RAM[40H] into A.
2. Use `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` to fetch the ASCII code from the LUT in program memory.
3. Store the ASCII result in RAM[41H].

**Assembly Language Program:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV A, 40H          ; A = hex digit (0 to F)
        MOV DPTR, #ASCII_TBL ; DPTR = base of LUT
        MOVC A, @A+DPTR     ; A = ASCII code from LUT
        MOV 41H, A          ; Store result in RAM[41H]
        END

; Look-Up Table stored in Program Memory
        ORG 0200H
ASCII_TBL:
        DB  '0','1','2','3','4','5','6','7'
        DB  '8','9','A','B','C','D','E','F'
```

**Numerical trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Assume RAM[40H]} & = 0\text{BH (hex digit 'B')} \\
\text{A after MOV A, 40H} & = 0\text{BH} \\
\text{DPTR} & = 0200\text{H} \\
\text{Effective address} & = \text{A} + \text{DPTR} = 000\text{BH} + 0200\text{H} = 020\text{BH} \\
\text{ROM[020BH]} & = 42\text{H (ASCII for 'B')} \\
\text{A after MOVC} & = 42\text{H} \\
\text{RAM[41H]} & = 42\text{H} \quad \text{(final ASCII character 'B')}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the use of `MOVC` (not `MOVX`) because we are reading from **Code Memory (ROM)**, not External RAM. The table begins at the program memory address pointed to by the label `ASCII_TBL`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU PCBMT402 Module 2 Data Transfer Instructions**

- **MOV** operates ONLY on the **internal 8-bit data space** (Internal RAM 00H–7FH and SFR 80H–FFH).
- **MOVC** accesses **Program Memory (Code ROM)** and is **read-only** for the Accumulator.
- **MOVX** accesses **External Data Memory** (up to 64 KB) and uses either **DPTR (16-bit)** or **R0/R1 (8-bit)** as the address pointer.
- **MOV DPTR, #data16** is the **only 16-bit data load** instruction in the 8051.
- **PUSH** increments SP first, then writes. **POP** reads first, then decrements SP. This is opposite to the 8085 stack behavior.
- The 8051 stack is **LIFO** and grows **upward** in Internal RAM.
- **`XCH`** performs full 8-bit exchange; **`XCHD`** performs only the **lower nibble** exchange; **`SWAP`** exchanges the two nibbles **inside the Accumulator**.
- **No data transfer instruction affects the PSW flags** (Carry, Auxiliary Carry, Overflow, Parity are all unchanged).
- Machine cycle duration: $T_{\text{machine}} = 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$. With $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592\,\text{MHz}$, $T_{\text{machine}} = 1.085\,\mu s$.
- `MOV A, Rn` is 1 byte/1 machine cycle (12 oscillator cycles). `MOV direct, Rn` is 2 bytes/2 machine cycles (24 oscillator cycles).
- **MOVX** activates the external memory control signals **RD-bar** and **WR-bar**; **MOVC** activates **PSEN-bar**. The bus signals differ.
- Allowed addressing modes for `MOV`: Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Immediate. **Not allowed:** Indexed.
- For `MOVX A, @Ri` and `MOVX @Ri, A`, R0/R1 hold the **lower 8 bits** of the address; the upper 8 bits come from **port P2** (latched).
- PUSH/POP operate only on **direct** addresses. You cannot PUSH/POP using `@Ri` or `#data`.
- The `SWAP` instruction is the fastest way to multiply or divide an 8-bit unsigned number by 16 (shift left or right by 4 bits).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to 8051 Instruction Set — The Data Manipulation Family

The **8051 microcontroller** executes instructions belonging to five primary functional groups: Data Transfer, Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean (Bit-level), and Program Branching. This module focuses on the **Arithmetic, Logical, Compare, and Rotate** family, which together form the **ALU-centric** instruction category of the MCS-51 architecture. Every instruction in this family operates on the **8-bit Accumulator (A)** or on **internal RAM (data memory) registers** (R0–R7), and most of them modify the **Program Status Word (PSW)** flags.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme — PCBMT402, Module 2):**
> *"8051 Instruction Set — Arithmetic Instructions, Logical Instructions, Compare (Branch) Instructions, and Rotate Instructions and their addressing modes."*

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definitions

### **Arithmetic Instructions**
A set of **machine-level opcodes** that perform binary addition, subtraction with borrow, increment, decrement, multiplication, division, and **Binary-Coded Decimal (BCD) correction** on data stored in registers or memory. The principal mnemonic group is: `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `INC`, `DEC`, `MUL AB`, `DIV AB`, and `DA A`.

### **Logical Instructions**
Bitwise Boolean operations executed at the **gate level** inside the ALU. These include `ANL` (AND), `ORL` (OR), `XRL` (XOR), `CLR A` (clear), `CPL A` (complement), and the rotate family. Logical instructions never propagate a carry/borrow between bytes; they work in strict isolation on individual bits.

### **Compare Instructions**
Conditional branch instructions that **test a relationship** between two operands *without* destroying either operand. The only true compare opcode in the 8051 is `CJNE` (Compare and Jump if Not Equal), while `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) is a loop-control cousin.

### **Rotate Instructions**
A specialized sub-category of logical instructions that **circularly shift the bits** of the accumulator left or right, with or without the **Carry flag (CY)**. The instruction set includes `RL A`, `RLC A`, `RR A`, `RRC A`, and `SWAP A`.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop Toolbox"

Imagine the **Accumulator (A)** as a **workbench** in a carpentry workshop.

| Workshop Object | 8051 Equivalent | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Workbench | Accumulator (A) | The "home base" where most operations begin and end |
| Carpenters (Saw, Hammer, Drill) | ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) | The actual physical engines that cut, join, or split bits |
| Tool Wall (Bit Selectors) | Addressing Modes | Decide *which* piece of wood to fetch from storage |
| Quality Inspection Stamp | PSW Flags (CY, AC, OV, P) | The supervisor's tick mark after every ALU job |
| Conveyor Belt (Rotate) | Rotate Instructions | The bit "shuffle" that moves pieces around on the belt |
| Quality Comparator | CJNE / DJNZ | The "are these two pieces identical?" inspector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Arithmetic and Logical instructions *modify data*; Compare and Rotate instructions *inspect or rearrange* data. The PSW flags are the only way the CPU "remembers" the result of a previous operation.

## 1.4 The 8051 PSW (Program Status Word) — The Flag Register

The PSW is an **8-bit, bit-addressable** register located at internal RAM address **D0H**.

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
|-----|--------|---------|----------|
| PSW.7 | **CY** | D7H | Carry Flag — Set on unsigned overflow/underflow |
| PSW.6 | **AC** | D6H | Auxiliary Carry — Carry between bit 3 and bit 4 (for BCD) |
| PSW.5 | **F0** | D5H | User-defined Flag 0 |
| PSW.4 | **RS1** | D4H | Register Bank Select 1 |
| PSW.3 | **RS0** | D3H | Register Bank Select 0 |
| PSW.2 | **OV** | D2H | Overflow Flag — Set on signed overflow |
| PSW.1 | — | D1H | Reserved (undefined) |
| PSW.0 | **P** | D0H | Parity Flag — Set if odd number of 1s in A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Register Bank Selection (RS1:RS0):**
> - **00** → Bank 0 (00H–07H) — *Default after reset*
> - **01** → Bank 1 (08H–0FH)
> - **10** → Bank 2 (10H–17H)
> - **11** → Bank 3 (18H–1FH)

The total bit-width of the 8051 ALU is **8 bits**, meaning a single arithmetic operation spans exactly **2⁸ = 256 discrete values** (00H to FFH).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Arithmetic Instructions — The Complete Group

### 2.1.1 ADD A, <src> — Addition without Carry

Performs the binary sum:

$$A \leftarrow A + \text{src}$$

The `<src>` operand may be a register (Rn), direct address, @Ri (indirect), or **immediate data (#data)**. The result overwrites the **Accumulator**.

**Flag Effects:**
- **CY** is set if a carry-out from bit 7 occurs (i.e., unsigned overflow).
- **AC** is set if a carry-out from bit 3 to bit 4 occurs.
- **OV** is set if there is a carry into bit 7 **and** no carry out (i.e., a C6 or C7 exclusive case for signed overflow).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is OV set this way?** For two 8-bit signed numbers, the sign bit is bit 7. If the sign bits of both operands are the same but the sign of the result differs, overflow has occurred. Mathematically, OV = C7 ⊕ C6 (carry-in XOR carry-out of the MSB).

### 2.1.2 ADDC A, <src> — Addition with Carry

$$A \leftarrow A + \text{src} + CY$$

Used to implement **multi-byte arithmetic** (16-bit, 24-bit, 32-bit addition). After the LSB addition via `ADD`, any carry generated is propagated into the next-byte addition via `ADDC`.

### 2.1.3 SUBB A, <src> — Subtract with Borrow

$$A \leftarrow A - \text{src} - CY$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 8051 has **no plain SUB instruction**; even for a first subtraction, you must clear the carry flag using `CLR C` (opcode C3H) beforehand. This is one of the most-tested KTU pitfalls.

**Flag Effects:**
- **CY** = 1 if a borrow is required (unsigned underflow).
- **AC** = 1 if a borrow from bit 4 occurs.
- **OV** = 1 if signed subtraction overflows.

### 2.1.4 INC <byte> — Increment by 1

$$\text{byte} \leftarrow \text{byte} + 1$$

Operates on A, Rn, direct address, or @Ri. **Does NOT affect any flag** — a unique property of the 8051's INC instruction.

### 2.1.5 DEC <byte> — Decrement by 1

$$\text{byte} \leftarrow \text{byte} - 1$$

Same operand rules as INC. **Does NOT affect any flag.**

### 2.1.6 MUL AB — Unsigned Multiply

$$A_{7:0} \leftarrow \text{low byte of } (A \times B)$$
$$B_{7:0} \leftarrow \text{high byte of } (A \times B)$$

The 16-bit result is split: low byte → A, high byte → B. **CY is always cleared.** **OV is set if the high byte (B) is non-zero** (i.e., result > 255).

### 2.1.7 DIV AB — Unsigned Divide

$$A \leftarrow \text{quotient of } (A \div B)$$
$$B \leftarrow \text{remainder of } (A \div B)$$

If B = 0 before execution, the values of A and B are **undefined** and **OV is set**. Otherwise, CY and OV are both cleared.

### 2.1.8 DA A — Decimal Adjust Accumulator

Used **only after an ADD or ADDC** to convert the binary result into proper **BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal)** format. It uses the CY and AC flags to decide whether to add 0x00, 0x06, 0x60, or 0x66.

## 2.2 Logical Instructions

### 2.2.1 ANL <dest>, <src> — Logical AND

$$\text{Result} = \text{dest} \text{ AND } \text{src} \rightarrow \text{dest}$$

Common uses: **masking bits** (clearing specific bits), testing individual bits, isolating nibbles.

### 2.2.2 ORL <dest>, <src> — Logical OR

$$\text{Result} = \text{dest} \text{ OR } \text{src} \rightarrow \text{dest}$$

Common uses: **setting specific bits** to logic 1, combining register contents.

### 2.2.3 XRL <dest>, <src> — Logical XOR

$$\text{Result} = \text{dest} \text{ XOR } \text{src} \rightarrow \text{dest}$$

Common uses: **toggling bits**, fast zero test, complementary arithmetic.

### 2.2.4 CLR A and CPL A

- `CLR A` — Clears A to 0x00.
- `CPL A` — Bitwise complement (1's complement) of A.

> [!NOTE]
> `CPL` complements every bit, while `CPL C` complements only the carry flag. Distinguish them carefully in KTU exams.

## 2.3 Compare Instructions

### 2.3.1 CJNE <dest>, <src>, <rel>

Compares the magnitude of the two operands by performing an **internal subtraction** (dest − src) *without* storing the result. The **CY flag is the only indicator** of the comparison outcome.

**Flag Logic (CRITICAL for KTU):**
- If `dest ≥ src` → **CY = 0** (no borrow needed).
- If `dest < src` → **CY = 1** (borrow needed).

If `dest ≠ src`, the program branches to the relative address; otherwise execution falls through.

### 2.3.2 DJNZ <byte>, <rel>

Decrement the byte and jump if it is **not zero**. Primarily used to construct **counted loops**.

> Both `CJNE` and `DJNZ` modify the **Program Counter (PC)** and are therefore classified as **branch (control transfer)** instructions in the broader sense.

## 2.4 Rotate Instructions

### 2.4.1 RL A — Rotate Accumulator Left (within A)

Each bit shifts left by one position. **Bit 7 wraps around to Bit 0.** The Carry flag is **NOT involved**.

$$A_{n+1} \leftarrow A_n \text{ for } n=0..6, \quad A_0 \leftarrow A_7$$

### 2.4.2 RLC A — Rotate Left through Carry

A 9-bit rotation including CY.

$$A_{n+1} \leftarrow A_n, \quad CY \leftarrow A_7, \quad A_0 \leftarrow CY_{\text{old}}$$

### 2.4.3 RR A and RRC A — Right Rotations

Mirror image of RL/RLC, shifting toward the LSB direction.

### 2.4.4 SWAP A — Swap Nibbles

Exchanges the upper and lower 4 bits of the accumulator in a single cycle.

$$A_{7:4} \leftrightarrow A_{3:0}$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Instruction Group | Mnemonic | Operation | Bytes | Cycles | Flags Affected | Allowed Addressing Modes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic** | `ADD A, src` | A = A + src | 1–2 | 1–2 | CY, AC, OV, P | R, Dir, @R, Imm |
| | `ADDC A, src` | A = A + src + CY | 1–2 | 1–2 | CY, AC, OV, P | R, Dir, @R, Imm |
| | `SUBB A, src` | A = A − src − CY | 1–2 | 1–2 | CY, AC, OV, P | R, Dir, @R, Imm |
| | `INC byte` | byte = byte + 1 | 1–2 | 1–2 | None | A, R, Dir, @R |
| | `DEC byte` | byte = byte − 1 | 1–2 | 1–2 | None | A, R, Dir, @R |
| | `MUL AB` | B:A = A × B | 1 | 4 | CY=0, OV | A & B only |
| | `DIV AB` | A = A/B, B = A%B | 1 | 4 | CY=0, OV | A & B only |
| | `DA A` | BCD adjust | 1 | 1 | CY | A only |
| **Logical** | `ANL d, s` | d = d AND s | 2–3 | 1–2 | P | A, Dir |
| | `ORL d, s` | d = d OR s | 2–3 | 1–2 | P | A, Dir |
| | `XRL d, s` | d = d XOR s | 2–3 | 1–2 | P | A, Dir |
| | `CLR A` | A = 0x00 | 1 | 1 | P | A only |
| | `CPL A` | A = ~A | 1 | 1 | None | A only |
| **Compare** | `CJNE d, s, rel` | Branch if d ≠ s | 3 | 2 | CY | A, R, @R vs Imm; Dir vs Imm |
| | `DJNZ byte, rel` | Decrement & branch if ≠ 0 | 2–3 | 2 | None | R, Dir |
| **Rotate** | `RL A` | Rotate left (8-bit) | 1 | 1 | None | A only |
| | `RLC A` | Rotate left (9-bit w/ CY) | 1 | 1 | CY, P | A only |
| | `RR A` | Rotate right (8-bit) | 1 | 1 | None | A only |
| | `RRC A` | Rotate right (9-bit) | 1 | 1 | CY, P | A only |
| | `SWAP A` | Swap nibbles | 1 | 1 | None | A only |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Machine Cycles per Instruction** is critical for KTU "Time Delay Calculation" problems. The canonical 8051 oscillator frequency is **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz**, giving a **machine cycle of 1.085 µs** per cycle. (Sometimes **12 MHz** is used, giving **1 µs/cycle**.)

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Industrial Counters** — `DJNZ` constructs a software "down-counter" to time relay closures.
2. **Hex-to-BCD Display Drivers** — `DIV AB` and `DA A` cooperate to display numeric readings on 7-segment displays.
3. **CRC and Checksum Computation** — `XRL` and rotate-with-carry operations implement polynomial division in software.
4. **PID Control Loops** — `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` are used in fixed-point arithmetic for embedded motor controllers.
5. **Digital Filtering** — `RLC A` and `RRC A` are used in shifting accumulators to implement bit-serial signal processing.
