# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Manual Execution of `ADD A, R2`

**Problem:** Given `A = 6CH` and `R2 = F4H` (both in Bank 0), determine the result and all affected PSW flags after executing `ADD A, R2`.

### Solution — Bit-by-Bit Binary Addition

First, convert the hex values into binary.

$$A = 6\text{C}_{16} = 0110\,1100_2$$
$$R2 = \text{F4}_{16} = 1111\,0100_2$$

Now perform the bit-by-bit binary sum:

$$\begin{aligned}
&0110\,1100 & (A = 6\text{CH}) \\
+&1111\,0100 & (R2 = \text{F4H}) \\
\hline
&1\,0110\,0000 & (\text{Result} = 160\text{H}, \text{Accumulator gets 60H, CY = 1})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 1 — Identify the Accumulator result:**
$$A = 60\text{H} = 0110\,0000_2$$

**Step 2 — Determine CY (Carry out of bit 7):**
Since the sum of the two MSBs (1 + 1) generated a 9th bit, $\text{CY} = 1$.

**Step 3 — Determine AC (Auxiliary Carry between bit 3 and bit 4):**
Look at bits 3 and 4 of the addends:
- Bit 4 of A = 1, Bit 4 of R2 = 1 → Sum = 1 + 1 + 0 (no carry-in) → produces a carry to bit 5? No, but it produces a 0 with carry into bit 5.
- Bit 3 of A = 0, Bit 3 of R2 = 1 → Sum = 0 + 1 + 0 (carry-in from bit 3+1? Let's verify using the actual bits).

A simpler method: examine the lower nibble. The sum of the lower nibble of A (1100) + lower nibble of R2 (0100) = 1 0000, generating a carry into the upper nibble. Therefore $\text{AC} = 1$.

**Step 4 — Determine OV (Overflow):**
$$\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0$$
There is no signed overflow.

**Step 5 — Determine P (Parity):**
A = 60H = 0110 0000. Number of 1-bits = 2 (even). $\text{P} = 0$ (P=0 means even parity).

**Final PSW state:** $\text{CY}=1, \text{AC}=1, \text{OV}=0, \text{P}=0$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multi-Byte Addition with `ADDC`

**Problem:** Add the 16-bit number stored at internal RAM locations `30H` (low byte) and `31H` (high byte) to the 16-bit number at `32H` (low) and `33H` (high). Store the 16-bit result at `30H` and `31H`.

Assume initial values: (31H)(30H) = 1A2BH and (33H)(32H) = 3F5CH.

### Solution — Two-Step Assembly

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Add the LSBs (no carry-in from prior operation).} \\
&(30\text{H}) = 2\text{BH}, (32\text{H}) = 5\text{CH} \\
&\text{ADD A, 32H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = 2\text{B} + 5\text{C} = 87\text{H}, \text{CY} = 0 \\
&(30\text{H}) \leftarrow A = 87\text{H}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 2: Add the MSBs WITH carry from Step 1.} \\
&(31\text{H}) = 1\text{AH}, (33\text{H}) = 3\text{FH} \\
&\text{ADDC A, 33H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = 1\text{A} + 3\text{F} + 0 = 59\text{H} \\
&(31\text{H}) \leftarrow A = 59\text{H}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final 16-bit Result:** 5987H. (1A2BH + 3F5CH = 5987H ✓ verified by hexadecimal arithmetic.)

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — The `SUBB` First-Time Borrow Pitfall

**Problem:** Compute `A − R1` where `A = 3EH` and `R1 = 2FH`. Determine the result and CY flag.

### Solution — Forgetting the Initial `CLR C`

If the student writes:

```
MOV A, #3EH
MOV R1, #2FH
SUBB A, R1   ; CY is in an UNKNOWN state from a previous instruction!
```

The result will be **wrong** because the 8051 always subtracts the **current CY** along with the operands.

**Correct procedure:**

```
CLR C        ; CY = 0   <-- MANDATORY
MOV A, #3EH
SUBB A, R1
```

$$\begin{aligned}
3\text{EH} - 2\text{FH} - 0 &= 0011\,1110_2 - 0010\,1111_2 \\
&= 0000\,1111_2 = 0\text{FH}
\end{aligned}$$

Since no borrow was needed, $\text{CY} = 0$. Result: **A = 0FH, CY = 0**.

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — `MUL AB` and the OV Flag

**Problem:** Given `A = F0H` and `B = 10H`, evaluate `MUL AB`.

### Solution

$$\begin{aligned}
A \times B &= \text{F0H} \times 10\text{H} \\
&= 240 \times 16 = 3840 \text{ (decimal)} \\
&= 0\text{F00H}
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:
- A = **00H** (low byte of result)
- B = **0FH** (high byte of result)
- **CY = 0** (always)
- **OV = 1** (because B ≠ 0, indicating result > 255)

> [!NOTE]
> If `OV = 1` after `MUL AB`, the result does not fit in A. The programmer must access B for the upper byte.

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — `CJNE` Branch Logic

**Problem:** Analyze the following snippet and determine the program flow:

```
MOV A, #55H
MOV R3, #55H
CLR C
CJNE A, R3, NOT_EQUAL
   ; (equal block)
SJMP END
NOT_EQUAL:
   ; (not equal block)
END:
```

### Solution

The internal subtraction is `A − R3 = 55H − 55H = 00H`. Since the result is zero, the **destination operand equals the source operand**, so the jump **is not taken**. Execution falls through to the "equal block."

**Sub-question (b):** What if R3 = 56H instead?

The internal subtraction is `55H − 56H` — a borrow is needed, so **CY = 1**. The operands are unequal, so the branch **is taken**, and CY = 1 can be inspected later by the programmer to deduce that `A < R3`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CJNE Golden Rule:** A common KTU trick is to follow `CJNE` with `JC` or `JNC`. After a `CJNE`:
> - If `CY = 1` → the **first operand < second operand**.
> - If `CY = 0` → the **first operand ≥ second operand**.

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — `RLC A` 9-Bit Rotation

**Problem:** Given `A = A5H` and `CY = 1`, trace the execution of `RLC A`.

### Solution

Initial state:
- A = 1010 0101
- CY = **1** (treated as bit 8 in a 9-bit barrel)

$$\text{9-bit barrel} = \mathbf{1}\,1010\,0101$$

After RLC, the bits shift left, with the LSB receiving the old CY and the MSB becoming the new CY:

$$\text{New barrel} = 1010\,0101\,\mathbf{1}$$

Therefore:
- **A = 0101 0011 = 53H** (the upper 8 bits of the shifted barrel)
- **CY = 1** (the bit that was shifted out from the MSB)

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Decision Guide: Which Instruction to Use?

| Engineering Task | Recommended Instruction | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Add two numbers without carry | `ADD A, src` | Simplest and fastest |
| Add two 16-bit numbers | `ADD` + `ADDC` chain | CY propagation handles multi-byte |
| Subtract with prior context | `SUBB A, src` | Reuses prior CY for multi-precision |
| Set bit 5 of a port | `ORL P1, #00100000B` | OR preserves other bits |
| Clear bit 3 of a port | `ANL P1, #11110111B` | AND masks out the target bit |
| Toggle bit 7 | `XRL P1, #10000000B` | XOR flips the target bit |
| Compare two values and branch | `CJNE A, src, label` | Standard idiom |
| Execute N times | `DJNZ Rn, label` | Loop counter, no flags affected |
| Multiply 8-bit values | `MUL AB` | Hardware multiply, 4 cycles |
| Software shift left by 1 | `RL A` (multiply by 2) | No carry involvement |
| Software shift left, preserving bit 7 | `RLC A` (multi-byte shift) | CY is the spillover |
| Test if A = 0 | `XRL A, #0` then `JZ` | XOR with self clears A but sets P |
| Build 1 µs time delay | `NOP` (12 MHz crystal) | 1 machine cycle = 1 µs |
| Convert binary to BCD | `DIV AB` by 10 iteratively | Quotient/remainder extraction |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Instruction-Family Classification Flowchart

The diagram below classifies the **ALU-centric** instruction family and shows the typical flag-update paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ALU-Centric Instruction Family"]:::root --> B["Arithmetic Group"]
    A --> C["Logical Group"]
    A --> D["Compare Group"]
    A --> E["Rotate Group"]

    B --> B1["ADD / ADDC"]
    B --> B2["SUBB"]
    B --> B3["INC / DEC"]
    B --> B4["MUL AB / DIV AB"]
    B --> B5["DA A"]

    C --> C1["ANL (AND)"]
    C --> C2["ORL (OR)"]
    C --> C3["XRL (XOR)"]
    C --> C4["CLR A / CPL A"]

    D --> D1["CJNE dest, src, rel"]
    D --> D2["DJNZ byte, rel"]

    E --> E1["RL A / RR A"]
    E --> E2["RLC A / RRC A"]
    E --> E3["SWAP A"]

    B1 --> F1["Updates CY, AC, OV, P"]
    B2 --> F1
    B4 --> F1
    B5 --> F1
    C1 --> F2["Updates P only"]
    C2 --> F2
    C3 --> F2
    C4 --> F2
    E1 --> F3["No flag changes"]
    E3 --> F3
    E2 --> F4["Updates CY and P"]
    D1 --> F5["Updates CY only"]
    D2 --> F6["No flag changes"]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a5f,stroke:#1e3a5f,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.2 Accumulator-Centric Execution Pipeline (ALU Functional Architecture)

This block-level functional architecture shows how an `ADD A, R2` instruction flows through the 8051 hardware.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PC["Program Counter PC"]:::reg --> IR["Instruction Register IR"]:::reg
    IR --> DEC["Instruction Decoder / Timing"]:::ctrl
    DEC --> ADG["Address Generation Unit"]:::ctrl
    DEC --> ALU["ALU 8-bit Adder/Subtractor"]:::alu
    ADG --> R2["Register Bank R0-R7 (selects R2)"]:::reg
    R2 --> ALU
    A["Accumulator A"]:::reg --> ALU
    ALU --> A
    ALU --> PSW["PSW Register CY AC OV P"]:::reg
    PSW --> DEC
    DEC --> PC

    classDef reg fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#000000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000000
    classDef alu fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Compare Instruction Decision Flow

The `CJNE` instruction is best understood as a state machine that examines the relationship between two operands.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> FetchOp
    FetchOp: Fetch A and src operands
    FetchOp --> InternalSubtract
    InternalSubtract: Perform internal A - src (result discarded)
    InternalSubtract --> CheckEqual
    CheckEqual: A equals src?
    CheckEqual --> FallThrough: YES - continue sequentially
    CheckEqual --> CheckCY: NO - examine CY
    CheckCY: Borrow needed?
    CheckCY --> SetCY: YES - CY = 1 (A less than src)
    CheckCY --> ClearCY: NO - CY = 0 (A greater than src)
    SetCY --> Branch
    ClearCY --> Branch
    Branch: PC = PC + rel offset
    FallThrough --> [*]
    Branch --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Rotation Visualization — `RLC A` as a 9-Bit Barrel

The following state machine illustrates the 9-bit rotation including the carry flag. The bits move left, the MSB spills into CY, and the old CY enters the LSB.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CY["CY_bit 8"]:::cy --> A7["A_bit 7"]:::a7
    A7 --> A6["A_bit 6"]
    A6 --> A5["A_bit 5"]
    A5 --> A4["A_bit 4"]
    A4 --> A3["A_bit 3"]
    A3 --> A2["A_bit 2"]
    A2 --> A1["A_bit 1"]
    A1 --> A0["A_bit 0"]
    A0 -.->|"new CY = old A7"| CY
    A7 -.->|"new A0 = old CY"| A0

    classDef cy fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef a7 fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,color:#000000
```
