## 1.2 What is an ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)?

An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** performs the inverse operation. It samples a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog signal and produces a discrete $n$-bit binary codeword proportional to the input voltage. The KTU syllabus focuses on the **ADC0804** (8-bit, successive approximation) and the **ADC0808/0809** (8-bit, 8-channel mux + SAR).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** An ADC is a mixed-signal device that converts a sampled analog input voltage $V_{in}$ into a digital output word $D$ such that $D = \text{round}\left( \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n \right)$, where $V_{ref}$ is the reference voltage and $n$ is the resolution.

### Intuitive Analogy — The Ruler in the Dark

Picture trying to measure the length of a book in a dark room using only your hand-span. Your hand-span has a fixed width (the **LSB / step size**). You can only report lengths in multiples of your hand-span — never more precisely. An ADC behaves the same way: the analog voltage is "rounded" to the nearest **quantum step** of size $\Delta V = V_{ref}/2^n$. A finer ruler (more bits) gives a more accurate measurement.

---

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Must-Know for KTU)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Range |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Resolution (Step Size / LSB) | $\Delta V$ | **Vref / 2ⁿ** (e.g., 19.53 mV for 8-bit, 5 V) |
| Full-Scale Range | FSR | **Vref · (2ⁿ − 1) / 2ⁿ** |
| Quantization Error | $Q_e$ | **± ½ LSB** |
| Conversion Time | $T_{conv}$ | **100 µs** (ADC0804) |
| Settling Time | $T_{set}$ | **150 ns** (DAC0808) |
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | **≥ 2 · f_max** (Nyquist Criterion) |
| Operating Voltage | $V_{CC}$ | **+5 V DC** (single supply) |
| Reference Voltage | $V_{ref}$ | **+10 V** (DAC0808 typical) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always state the LSB value in **mV** explicitly when solving numericals. Writing "$5/256$ V" is acceptable; writing "$0.0195$ V" is even better. Examiners reward unit-aware answers.

---

## 1.4 Why are ADC/DAC Interfacing Critical?

Microcontrollers like the **8051** operate purely in the digital domain — they understand only HIGH (1) and LOW (0). However, the real world is overwhelmingly analog: temperature, pressure, light, sound, vibration, and humidity are all continuous physical quantities. **Interfacing** is the engineering discipline that bridges this divide, allowing the 8051 to:

1. **Sense** the real world (via sensors + ADC).
2. **Actuate** the real world (via DAC + drivers).
3. **Generate** arbitrary analog waveforms (sine, triangular, square, staircase) for test equipment, audio synthesis, and motor control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Successive Approximation ADC (ADC0804)

The ADC0804 uses a **Successive Approximation Register (SAR)** algorithm. The conversion proceeds in exactly $n$ clock cycles (8 cycles for 8-bit resolution), comparing the input $V_{in}$ against an internal DAC's output.

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow (8-bit example)

1. **START Pulse:** A HIGH-to-LOW transition on the $\overline{WR}$ pin begins a conversion. The SAR register is **cleared to 0x00**.
2. **MSB Trial:** The SAR sets bit 7 (MSB) to 1, producing a trial codeword of `1000 0000` (128). The internal 8-bit DAC converts this to a trial voltage $V_{DAC} = 128 \times \Delta V$.
3. **Comparison:** A precision comparator evaluates $\text{sign}(V_{in} - V_{DAC})$.
    - **If $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$:** The bit is **kept at 1** (the input lies above the trial midpoint).
    - **If $V_{in} < V_{DAC}$:** The bit is **cleared to 0** (the input lies below).
4. **Next Bit:** The SAR moves to bit 6, sets it to 1, and repeats the comparison.
5. **Iteration:** After 8 such trials (one per bit), the SAR holds the **final digital equivalent** of $V_{in}$.
6. **EOC Pulse:** The $\overline{INTR}$ pin goes LOW to signal **End of Conversion**. The host reads the data by pulling $\overline{RD}$ LOW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SAR is widely used:** It offers an excellent **speed–resolution trade-off** — $n$ clock cycles per conversion — making it ideal for general-purpose 8051 data-acquisition systems. Flash ADCs are faster but expensive; dual-slope ADCs are slower but more accurate.

---

## 2.2 Operational Breakdown — DAC0808 (8-bit Current-Steering DAC)

The DAC0808 uses a **bipolar current-steering architecture** with an internal R-2R ladder network. It has **8 current sources**, each binary-weighted ($I, 2I, 4I, \ldots, 128I$). Bits set to "1" switch their corresponding current source to the $\overline{I_{out}}$ pin; bits set to "0" switch to the $I_{out}$ pin.

### Why an External Op-Amp is Required

The DAC0808 outputs a **current**, not a voltage. A **current-to-voltage converter** built around an op-amp (LF351 or LM741) is mandatory. The output voltage is given by:

$$V_{out} = -I_{out} \cdot R_f$$

where $R_f$ is the feedback resistor (typically **5 kΩ**, sometimes integrated into the DAC package as $R_{fb}$). The negative sign indicates **phase inversion** by the inverting amplifier configuration.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Revision-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING RULE:** Vertical bars $\vert$ are used here instead of the markdown-reserved pipe symbol $\mid$ to preserve table integrity.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Step size (LSB) | DAC/ADC resolution |
| 2 | $V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{2^n - 1}{2^n}$ | Full-scale output | Max DAC swing |
| 3 | $V_{out} = D \cdot \Delta V$ | Output for input $D$ | Linear DAC response |
| 4 | $I_{out} = I_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{D}{2^n}$ | DAC0808 current eq. | Current output |
| 5 | $V_{out} = -I_{out} \cdot R_f$ | I-to-V converter | Op-amp interface |
| 6 | $D = \text{round}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n\right)$ | ADC digital code | ADC0804 output |
| 7 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist criterion | Sampling theorem |
| 8 | $Q_e = \pm \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \Delta V$ | Quantization error | ADC accuracy |
| 9 | $I_{ref} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{R_{14}}$ | Reference current | DAC0808 biasing |
| 10 | $V_{out}(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi f t)$ | Sine waveform | Look-up table gen. |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive ECUs:** ADC reads throttle-position sensor; DAC drives the dashboard gauge.
- **Medical Instrumentation:** ECG/EEG machines use ADCs to digitize bio-signals.
- **Industrial Process Control:** PLCs use ADC/DAC to regulate temperature, flow, and pressure in PID loops.
- **Audio Equipment:** CD players (DAC) and digital microphones (ADC).
- **Function Generators:** Waveform synthesis using DAC + microcontroller look-up tables (a frequent KTU lab exercise).
- **Motor Speed Control:** DAC generates 0–10 V analog reference for VFD drives.

---

## 2.5 The Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon)

For an analog signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$ to be **faithfully reconstructed** from its samples, the sampling rate $f_s$ must satisfy:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

If this condition is violated, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency content folds back into the low-frequency band, corrupting the signal. A low-pass **anti-aliasing filter** is therefore placed before the ADC input.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — LSB and Full-Scale Voltage of an 8-bit DAC

**Given:** $V_{ref} = +5.12$ V, $n = 8$ bits, DAC linear response.

**Step 1 — Identify the resolution formula.**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$\Delta V = \frac{5.12 \text{ V}}{2^8} = \frac{5.12}{256} \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator–denominator pair.**

$$\Delta V = 0.020 \text{ V} = 20 \text{ mV}$$

> This is the **voltage step (LSB)** for every increment in the digital input codeword.

**Step 4 — Derive the full-scale output.**

The maximum digital code is $D_{max} = 2^n - 1 = 255$. Hence:

$$V_{FS} = D_{max} \cdot \Delta V = 255 \times 0.020 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify.**

$$V_{FS} = 5.10 \text{ V} = \frac{255}{256} \cdot V_{ref}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** $V_{FS}$ is always **slightly less** than $V_{ref}$ because the digital code never reaches $2^n$. Choosing $V_{ref} = 5.12$ V (instead of 5.00 V) yields a "clean" 20 mV LSB — a classic KTU exam technique to simplify binary-to-decimal conversion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DAC0808 Output Voltage with Op-Amp

**Given:** $V_{ref} = +10$ V, $R_{14} = 5$ kΩ, $R_f = 5$ kΩ, digital input $D = 1000 0000_2$.

**Step 1 — Compute the reference current $I_{ref}$.**

$$I_{ref} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R_{14}} = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{5 \text{ k}\Omega} = 2 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 2 — Convert digital input to decimal.**

$$D = 1000\,0000_2 = 1 \times 2^7 + 0 \times 2^6 + \cdots + 0 \times 2^0 = 128$$

**Step 3 — Apply the DAC0808 current-equation.**

$$I_{out} = I_{ref} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n} = 2 \text{ mA} \cdot \frac{128}{256} = 1.0 \text{ mA}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the op-amp I-to-V transfer function.**

$$V_{out} = -I_{out} \cdot R_f = -1.0 \text{ mA} \times 5 \text{ k}\Omega = -5.0 \text{ V}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sine Wave Look-Up Table Generation

To generate a sine wave digitally using the 8051 + DAC0808, we pre-compute a **look-up table** of digital codes corresponding to evenly-spaced samples along the sine curve.

**Given:** $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $n = 8$ bits, **36 samples per cycle** (a popular KTU exam value).

**Step 1 — State the mathematical model of a sampled sine wave.**

$$V(t) = V_{offset} + A \cdot \sin\!\left(2\pi \cdot \frac{k}{N}\right), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, N-1$$

where $N$ = samples per cycle, $A$ = amplitude, $V_{offset}$ = DC bias.

**Step 2 — Choose parameters for an 8-bit unipolar DAC.**

For the DAC0808 to swing from 0 V to $V_{ref}$, the output must be biased at $V_{ref}/2$ with amplitude $V_{ref}/2$:

$$V_{offset} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2} = 2.5 \text{ V}, \quad A = \frac{V_{ref}}{2} = 2.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Map voltage to digital code (0–255).**

$$D(k) = \text{round}\!\left[\frac{V_{ref}}{2} + \frac{V_{ref}}{2} \cdot \sin\!\left(2\pi \cdot \frac{k}{36}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate for $k = 0$.**

$$\sin(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; D(0) = \text{round}(2.5 + 0) = 3 \quad (\text{ideal mid-scale} = 127.5)$$

Wait — this is for a **unipolar DAC** biased at 2.5 V. For a true 0–5 V swing, we use the full 0–255 range:

$$D(k) = \text{round}\!\left[127.5 + 127.5 \cdot \sin\!\left(2\pi \cdot \frac{k}{36}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Compute sample-by-sample values.**

| $k$ | Angle (°) | $\sin(\theta)$ | $127.5 + 127.5\sin(\theta)$ | $D(k)$ (hex) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | 0.0000 | 127.50 | 7F |
| 1 | 10 | 0.1736 | 149.64 | 95 |
| 2 | 20 | 0.3420 | 171.10 | AA |
| 3 | 30 | 0.5000 | 191.25 | BF |
| 4 | 40 | 0.6428 | 209.46 | D1 |
| 5 | 50 | 0.7660 | 225.17 | E1 |
| 6 | 60 | 0.8660 | 237.93 | ED |
| 7 | 70 | 0.9397 | 247.32 | F7 |
| 8 | 80 | 0.9848 | 253.06 | FD |
| 9 | 90 | 1.0000 | 255.00 | FF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Shortcut:** Pre-compute only the first quadrant values, then **mirror** them for quadrants II, III, and IV (with appropriate sign inversion). This halves the table size — a frequently tested KTU exam optimization.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Triangular Wave Counter Sequence

A triangular wave is generated by **counting up** from 0 to 255, then **counting down** from 254 back to 0. The total count sequence per cycle is **510 steps**.

**Step 1 — Define the ramp direction.**

$$\text{If count} \leq 255: \text{ count++} \quad \text{(rising ramp)}$$

$$\text{If count} > 255: \text{ count{--}} \quad \text{(falling ramp)}$$

**Step 2 — Express the period.**

For a clock period $T_{clk}$ of the 8051's instruction cycle (1.085 µs at 11.0592 MHz), one full triangular cycle is:

$$T_{tri} = 510 \times T_{clk} = 510 \times 1.085 \,\mu\text{s} \approx 553.35 \,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the frequency.**

$$f_{tri} = \frac{1}{T_{tri}} \approx 1.807 \text{ kHz}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Staircase Wave Generation

**Step 1 — Divide the full-scale range into $M$ equal steps.**

$$\text{Step height} = \frac{256}{M} \quad \text{(digital codes)}$$

**Step 2 — For $M = 8$ equal steps (popular KTU problem):**

$$\text{Step height} = \frac{256}{8} = 32$$

**Step 3 — Generate the sequence.**

$$D = 0, 32, 64, 96, 128, 160, 192, 224, \text{then reset to 0}$$

In **hexadecimal**: 00, 20, 40, 60, 80, A0, C0, E0, 00.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A staircase wave is a **quantized ramp** — it visualizes the discrete nature of DAC output and is often used in KTU labs to demonstrate the LSB step graphically on an oscilloscope.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — ADC0804 Conversion Time

**Given:** ADC0804 uses an external RC oscillator with $R = 10$ kΩ, $C = 150$ pF.

**Step 1 — Compute the clock frequency.**

$$f_{CLK} = \frac{1}{1.1 \cdot R \cdot C} = \frac{1}{1.1 \times 10{,}000 \times 150 \times 10^{-12}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the denominator.**

$$1.1 \times 10{,}000 \times 150 \times 10^{-12} = 1.65 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the frequency.**

$$f_{CLK} \approx 606 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Compute conversion time for 8 bits.**

$$T_{conv} = \frac{n + 1}{f_{CLK}} = \frac{9}{606 \text{ kHz}} \approx 14.85 \,\mu\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Always include the $+1$ extra clock cycle needed to flag the End-of-Conversion (EOC) interrupt. Many students lose marks by using $n$ instead of $n+1$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ADC0804 Interfaced with 8051 Microcontroller

```mermaid
graph LR
    mcu[8051 MCU] --> p1[PORT 1 DATA INPUT]
    mcu --> p36[P3.6 WR]
    mcu --> p37[P3.7 RD]
    mcu --> p35[P3.5 INTR]
    p36 --> wrpin[ADC0804 WR BAR]
    p37 --> rdpin[ADC0804 RD BAR]
    p35 --> intrpin[ADC0804 INTR BAR]
    analog[ANALOG INPUT VIN] --> adcin[ADC0804 VIN PLUS]
    vref5[VREF PLUS 5V] --> vref2[ADC0804 VREF BY 2]
    rc[RC NETWORK 10K AND 150PF] --> clkpin[ADC0804 CLK R AND CLK IN]
    p1 --> adcout[ADC0804 DB0 TO DB7]
    adcin --> vss1[AGND]
    clkpin --> vss2[DGND]
    vref2 --> vss3[DGND]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The 8051 writes a START pulse on $\overline{WR}$, polls the $\overline{INTR}$ line to detect End-of-Conversion, then reads the 8-bit data from Port 1. The $V_{ref}/2$ pin is driven directly from a stable +5 V reference, giving a full-scale input range of **0–5 V** (since the internal divider halves it).

---

## 4.2 DAC0808 Interfaced with 8051 with Op-Amp Output Stage

```mermaid
graph LR
    port1[8051 PORT 1 P1.0 TO P1.7] --> dac[DAC0808 A1 TO A8]
    vrefp[VREF PLUS 10V] --> dacrefp[DAC0808 VREF PLUS PIN 14]
    vrefn[VEE MINUS 10V] --> dacrefn[DAC0808 VEE PIN 3]
    daciout[DAC0808 IOUT] --> opinv[OP AMP LF351 INVERTING INPUT]
    dacgnd[DAC0808 GND] --> opgnd[OP AMP NON INVERTING INPUT GROUNDED]
    rfb[EXTERNAL RFB 5K] --> opfb[FEEDBACK FROM OUTPUT TO INVERTING INPUT]
    opinv --> opamp[LF351 OP AMP]
    opamp --> vout[VOUT TO OSCILLOSCOPE]
    vcc[VCC PLUS 5V] --> dacvcc[DAC0808 VCC PIN 13]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin-out must-knows for DAC0808:**
> - Pin 14 ($V_{ref+}$) — Reference voltage input.
> - Pin 15 ($V_{ref-}$, often tied to $-10$ V or GND) — Negative reference.
> - Pin 4 ($I_{out}$) — Current output (sum of weighted currents).
> - Pin 13 ($V_{CC}$, $+5$ V) — Logic supply.
> - Pin 3 ($V_{EE}$, typically $-10$ V to $-15$ V) — Substrate bias.

---

## 4.3 Successive Approximation ADC — Internal Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    vin[VIN ANALOG INPUT] --> comp[COMPARATOR PLUS]
    vref[VREF] --> intdac[INTERNAL DAC]
    intdac --> comp
    comp --> sar[SUCCESSIVE APPROXIMATION REGISTER SAR]
    sar --> latch[OUTPUT LATCH]
    latch --> dout[8 BIT DIGITAL OUTPUT DB0 TO DB7]
    clk[CLOCK INPUT] --> sar
    start[START PULSE WR] --> sar
    sar --> eoc[EOC INTR BAR]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the SAR loop:** The clock drives the SAR bit-by-bit. The internal DAC reconstructs an analog trial voltage from the SAR's current codeword. The comparator decides whether the input lies above or below this trial value, and the SAR retains or discards the bit accordingly.

---

## 4.4 R-2R Ladder Network — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Ladder
    b7[D7 MSB] --> r7[SWITCH S7]
    b6[D6] --> r6[SWITCH S6]
    b5[D5] --> r5[SWITCH S5]
    b4[D4] --> r4[SWITCH S4]
    b3[D3] --> r3[SWITCH S3]
    b2[D2] --> r2[SWITCH S2]
    b1[D1] --> r1[SWITCH S1]
    b0[D0 LSB] --> r0[SWITCH S0]
    r7 --> node7[NODE 2R TO GND OR Iout]
    r6 --> node6[NODE 2R]
    r5 --> node5[NODE 2R]
    r4 --> node4[NODE 2R]
    r3 --> node3[NODE 2R]
    r2 --> node2[NODE 2R]
    r1 --> node1[NODE 2R]
    r0 --> node0[NODE 2R AT IOUT]
    end
    vref[VREF] --> topnode[TOP OF LADDER]
    topnode --> node7
    topnode --> ioutterminal[IOUT TERMINAL]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why R-2R and not weighted resistors?** In a **weighted-resistor DAC**, the MSB uses a resistor of value $R$ and the LSB uses $2^{n-1} \cdot R$. For an 8-bit DAC, the LSB resistor would be $128R$ — extremely difficult to fabricate accurately on a single IC die. The **R-2R ladder** uses only **two resistor values** ($R$ and $2R$), making it economical and precise in monolithic form.

---

## 4.5 Sampling and Quantization — Conceptual Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    cont[CONTINUOUS ANALOG SIGNAL] --> sampler[SAMPLE AND HOLD CIRCUIT]
    clk[CLOCK FS] --> sampler
    sampler --> quant[QUANTIZER]
    quant --> coder[ENCODER]
    coder --> bits[8 BIT PARALLEL OUTPUT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample-and-Hold function:** A switch + capacitor combination holds the analog voltage constant during the conversion process. This is essential because the ADC requires finite time to perform the SAR trials; without the hold, the input could drift mid-conversion, producing incorrect bits.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Define the term *resolution* of a DAC. An 8-bit DAC has a reference voltage of 10 V. Calculate its resolution and full-scale output voltage.**

#### Model Answer:

**Definition:** Resolution (also called step size or LSB) of a DAC is the smallest change in the analog output corresponding to a 1-LSB change in the digital input codeword.

**Formula:**

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^8} = \frac{10}{256} = 39.0625 \text{ mV}$$

**Full-Scale Output:**

$$V_{FS} = (2^8 - 1) \times \Delta V = 255 \times 39.0625 \text{ mV} = 9.96 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners expect the units (mV) explicitly. Always state $V_{FS} \approx 9.96$ V, not just 255 × LSB.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**What is quantization error? Explain with reference to an 8-bit ADC having $V_{ref} = 5$ V.**

#### Model Answer:

**Definition:** Quantization error is the inherent difference between the actual (continuous) analog input voltage and its discretized (rounded) digital representation at the ADC output.

**Mathematical Form:**

$$Q_e = \pm \frac{1}{2} \cdot \Delta V = \pm \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$$

**Numerical Value:**

$$Q_e = \pm \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{5}{256} = \pm 9.765 \text{ mV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Expectation:** State that quantization error is **inherent** and **unavoidable** — it can only be reduced by increasing $n$ (more bits) or by using techniques like oversampling and dithering, not by calibration.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** with internal choice. The typical split is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, allowing a student to attempt one full question or mix sub-parts across alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 2]

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the interfacing of **ADC0804** with the 8051 microcontroller. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution:

**1. Functional Overview:** The ADC0804 is an 8-bit successive approximation ADC with a built-in clock generator, sample-and-hold, and tri-state output latches. It interfaces to the 8051 via three control lines ($\overline{WR}, \overline{RD}, \overline{INTR}$) and an 8-bit parallel data bus.

**2. Pin Description:**

- **$\overline{CS}$ (Pin 1):** Active-low chip select. Tie LOW permanently in single-chip mode.
- **$\overline{RD}$ (Pin 2):** Active-low read enable. A LOW here latches the converted data onto DB0–DB7.
- **$\overline{WR}$ (Pin 3):** Active-low write/start. A HIGH-to-LOW edge initiates a new conversion.
- **CLK IN (Pin 4) & CLK R (Pin 19):** External RC components set the conversion clock (~606 kHz with $R=10$ kΩ, $C=150$ pF).
- **$\overline{INTR}$ (Pin 5):** End-of-Conversion interrupt (active LOW). Goes LOW when conversion completes.
- **$V_{in}(+), V_{in}(-)$ (Pins 6, 7):** Differential analog inputs. $V_{in}(-)$ is tied to AGND for single-ended mode.
- **DB0–DB7 (Pins 11–18):** Tri-state 8-bit data outputs.
- **AGND, DGND (Pins 8, 10):** Analog and digital grounds — must be tied together at one point.
- **$V_{ref}/2$ (Pin 9):** Reference voltage input. The internal reference is $2 \times V_{ref}/2$. With $V_{ref}/2 = 2.5$ V, full-scale input = 5 V.
- **$V_{CC}$ (Pin 20):** +5 V digital supply.

**3. Interfacing Block Diagram & Signal Mapping:**

| 8051 Pin | ADC0804 Pin | Function |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| P1.0–P1.7 | DB0–DB7 | 8-bit data bus (input to 8051) |
| P3.6 | $\overline{WR}$ | Start conversion pulse |
| P3.7 | $\overline{RD}$ | Read converted data |
| P3.5 | $\overline{INTR}$ | End-of-Conversion status |

**4. Assembly Program (8051):**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV DPTR, #0FE00H       ; ADC0804 base address (external memory)
        MOV A, #00H             ; Dummy write to start conversion
        MOVX @DPTR, A
WAIT:   JB P3.5, WAIT           ; Poll INTR (P3.5); loop if HIGH (not done)
        MOVX A, @DPTR           ; Read converted digital byte
        MOV P0, A               ; Display on Port 0 (e.g., LEDs)
        SJMP START              ; Repeat
        END
```

---

#### (b) Explain the working of **Successive Approximation ADC** with a suitable diagram. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution:

**1. Principle:** The SAR ADC uses a **binary-search algorithm** to identify the digital equivalent of the analog input. It performs a sequence of $n$ comparisons, each narrowing the search range by half.

**2. Internal Blocks:**

- **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Captures $V_{in}$ and holds it constant during conversion.
- **Comparator:** A high-gain op-amp comparing $V_{in}$ with the internal DAC's output $V_{DAC}$.
- **Internal DAC:** Generates $V_{DAC}$ from the SAR's current codeword.
- **SAR (Successive Approximation Register):** An 8-bit shift register + output latch that holds the trial codeword.
- **Control Logic & Clock:** Sequencer that drives the SAR bit-by-bit.

**3. Conversion Algorithm (8-bit example with $V_{in} = 3.2$ V, $V_{ref} = 5$ V, $\Delta V = 19.53$ mV):**

| Step | Trial Code | $V_{DAC}$ | $V_{in} > V_{DAC}$? | Bit Decision |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 1000 0000 | 2.500 V | Yes | Keep MSB = 1 |
| 2 | 1100 0000 | 3.125 V | Yes | Keep bit 6 = 1 |
| 3 | 1110 0000 | 3.438 V | No | Clear bit 5 = 0 |
| 4 | 1101 0000 | 3.281 V | No | Clear bit 4 = 0 |
| 5 | 1100 1000 | 3.203 V | No | Clear bit 3 = 0 |
| 6 | 1100 0100 | 3.164 V | Yes | Keep bit 2 = 1 |
| 7 | 1100 0110 | 3.184 V | Yes | Keep bit 1 = 1 |
| 8 | 1100 0111 | 3.193 V | Yes | Keep bit 0 = 1 |

**Final digital code:** `1100 0111` = $0xC7$ = 199. Verification: $199 \times 19.53$ mV = 3.187 V $\approx$ 3.2 V. ✓

**4. Conversion Time:** $T_{conv} = (n+1) \cdot T_{CLK}$. For $n = 8$ and $f_{CLK} = 606$ kHz, $T_{conv} \approx 14.85 \,\mu$s.

**5. Advantages:** Fixed conversion time independent of input; excellent speed-resolution trade-off; widely used in 8051 data-acquisition systems.

**6. Disadvantages:** Requires a high-quality internal DAC; speed limited by DAC settling time.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Module 2]

#### (a) With neat diagram, explain the interfacing of **DAC0808** with the 8051 microcontroller. Write an 8051 assembly program to generate a **triangular wave**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution:

**1. DAC0808 Pin Description (Key Pins):**

- **A1–A8 (Pins 5–12):** 8-bit digital data inputs from 8051 Port 1.
- **$V_{ref+}$ (Pin 14):** Positive reference voltage (typically +10 V).
- **$V_{ref-}$ (Pin 15):** Negative reference (often grounded or tied to $-5$ V).
- **$I_{out}$ (Pin 4):** Current output — sum of all weighted current sources corresponding to set bits.
- **$V_{CC}$ (Pin 13):** +5 V digital supply.
- **$V_{EE}$ (Pin 3):** Negative supply (typically $-10$ V to $-15$ V).
- **$R_{14}$ (Pin 16):** Reference-current-setting resistor; $I_{ref} = V_{ref+} / R_{14}$.

**2. Interfacing Circuit:**

- 8051 **Port 1 (P1.0–P1.7)** → DAC0808 **A1–A8** (digital data).
- DAC0808 **$I_{out}$** → Op-amp **LF351 inverting input**.
- Op-amp output = $-I_{out} \cdot R_f$ where $R_f$ = 5 kΩ.
- $V_{ref+} = +10$ V through $R_{14} = 5$ kΩ ⇒ $I_{ref} = 2$ mA.
- Op-amp non-inverting input → GND.

**3. 8051 Assembly Program for Triangular Wave:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV DPTR, #0FE00H       ; DAC0808 mapped to external memory
AGAIN:  MOV R0, #00H            ; Start count from 0
UP:     MOV A, R0
        MOVX @DPTR, A           ; Send digital value to DAC
        INC R0
        CJNE R0, #0FFH, UP      ; Continue up-count until 255
DOWN:   MOV A, R0
        MOVX @DPTR, A           ; Send to DAC
        DEC R0
        CJNE R0, #00H, DOWN     ; Continue down-count until 1
        SJMP AGAIN              ; Repeat cycle
        END
```

**4. Waveform Analysis:** The output oscillates linearly from 0 V → 5 V (rising ramp) and 5 V → 0 V (falling ramp). With a 11.0592 MHz crystal, each count takes ~1.085 µs, so one full triangle cycle of 510 counts occupies ~553 µs, giving a frequency of approximately **1.807 kHz**.

---

#### (b) Explain the **R-2R Ladder Network** used in DACs. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution:

**1. Disadvantage of Weighted-Resistor DAC:** In a weighted-resistor DAC, the resistor values are $R, 2R, 4R, \ldots, 2^{n-1}R$. For an 8-bit DAC, the MSB resistor is $R$ (e.g., 10 kΩ) while the LSB resistor is $128R = 1.28$ MΩ. Fabricating such a wide range of values on a single IC die with **matched temperature coefficients** is extremely difficult and expensive.

**2. R-2R Ladder Architecture:** The R-2R ladder uses only **two resistor values** — $R$ and $2R$ — repeated in a regular geometric pattern. This is far easier to fabricate, and the resistors can be **laser-trimmed** for high precision.

**3. Operating Principle:**

- Each bit $D_i$ controls a switch that routes the corresponding $2R$ branch either to **GND** (if $D_i = 0$) or to the **virtual ground of the op-amp summing junction** (if $D_i = 1$).
- Due to the ladder's symmetry, each successive bit contributes **half** the current of the previous bit — the binary weighting is achieved by the network's intrinsic voltage division.
- The output current at the summing junction is:

$$I_{out} = \frac{V_{ref}}{R} \cdot \left( \frac{D_7}{2} + \frac{D_6}{4} + \frac{D_5}{8} + \cdots + \frac{D_0}{256} \right) = \frac{V_{ref}}{R} \cdot \frac{D}{2^n}$$

**4. Advantages:**

- Only two resistor values (manufacturing simplicity, low cost).
- Excellent matching → high linearity (typical INL < 0.5 LSB).
- Easily scalable to higher resolutions (10, 12, 16 bits).
- Lower glitch energy at output compared to weighted-resistor designs.

**5. Disadvantages:**

- Requires an external op-amp (cannot directly drive a low-impedance load).
- Output is current-mode → a current-to-voltage conversion stage is always needed.

**6. Real-World Use:** The R-2R ladder is the **industry standard** for integrated DACs (DAC0808, DAC0832, AD7524, etc.) and is also used inside most SAR ADCs as the internal reference DAC.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $V_{ref}/2$ pin of ADC0804:** Many students apply the full $V_{ref}$ directly. Remember: the ADC0804's internal reference is **twice** the voltage on Pin 9, so $V_{ref}/2 = 2.5$ V gives a 5 V full-scale range.
> 2. **Sign error in DAC0808 output:** The op-amp is in the **inverting** configuration. The output is **negative** of the expected polarity. Use a second inverting stage or offset the reference to get a positive output.
> 3. **Confusing $V_{ref+}$ and $V_{EE}$ on DAC0808:** $V_{ref+}$ is the *reference* (often +10 V), while $V_{EE}$ is the *substrate bias* (often $-10$ V to $-15$ V). Swapping these destroys the chip.
> 4. **Sample-and-Hold omission:** In ADC interfacing, students often forget to specify the S/H time. Always mention that the input must remain stable for at least one conversion cycle.
> 5. **Quantization error sign:** $Q_e = \pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB, not just $+\frac{1}{2}$ LSB. The $\pm$ is critical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 2: ADC and DAC Interfacing**

### DAC0808 — Must-Know
- 8-bit current-output DAC using **R-2R ladder** architecture.
- Reference current $I_{ref} = V_{ref+} / R_{14}$, typically 2 mA.
- **External op-amp mandatory** for I-to-V conversion: $V_{out} = -I_{out} \cdot R_f$.
- Settling time: **150 ns**; supply: $V_{CC} = +5$ V, $V_{EE} = -10$ V to $-15$ V.
- Used for waveform generation (triangular, staircase, sine via look-up table).

### ADC0804 — Must-Know
- 8-bit **successive approximation** ADC.
- Internal clock from RC network: $f_{CLK} = 1 / (1.1 R C)$.
- Conversion time: $T_{conv} = (n+1) / f_{CLK}$ ≈ **14.85 µs** at 606 kHz.
- Control pins: $\overline{CS}, \overline{RD}, \overline{WR}, \overline{INTR}$.
- $V_{ref}/2$ pin sets full-scale: apply 2.5 V for 0–5 V input range.

### General Theory — Must-Know
- **Resolution (LSB):** $\Delta V = V_{ref} / 2^n$.
- **Full-Scale Output:** $V_{FS} = V_{ref} \cdot (2^n - 1) / 2^n$.
- **Quantization Error:** $Q_e = \pm \frac{1}{2}$ LSB (inherent, unavoidable).
- **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ (otherwise aliasing occurs).
- **Anti-aliasing filter** must precede every real-world ADC.

### 8051 Interfacing — Must-Know
- Data bus: **Port 1** (8 bits) for both ADC and DAC.
- ADC handshake: $\overline{WR}$ (P3.6) starts, $\overline{INTR}$ (P3.5) flags EOC, $\overline{RD}$ (P3.7) reads.
- DAC update: write new codeword to external memory address using `MOVX @DPTR, A`.
- Triangular wave = count up (0→255) then count down (255→0) = 510 steps per cycle.
- Staircase wave = increment by $256/M$ for $M$ steps then reset.
- Sine wave = 8051 reads from ROM look-up table and writes to DAC at fixed intervals.

### Conversion Pitfalls — Must-Avoid
- ❌ Forgetting the $+1$ in $T_{conv} = (n+1) T_{CLK}$.
- ❌ Using $V_{ref}$ directly on ADC0804 Pin 9 (use $V_{ref}/2$).
- ❌ Confusing $I_{out}$ polarity on DAC0808.
- ❌ Skipping the op-amp when discussing DAC0808 output.
- ❌ Sampling below Nyquist rate (aliasing).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PIC Microcontrollers: Overview of PIC 16 Series
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PIC Microcontrollers: Overview of PIC 16 Series

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**PIC (Peripheral Interface Controller)** is a family of **Harvard-architecture**, **RISC-based** microcontrollers manufactured by **Microchip Technology**. The **PIC 16 Series** specifically refers to the **mid-range 8-bit microcontroller family** featuring 14-bit instruction word length, designed for embedded control applications requiring moderate computational power, low power consumption, and cost-effective peripheral integration.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The PIC 16 family occupies the middle tier in the PIC hierarchy — positioned above the **PIC 10/12** (baseline, 12-bit instructions) and below the **PIC 17/18** (high-performance, 16-bit instructions). This positions the PIC 16 as the **workhorse of industrial and consumer embedded design**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of the PIC 16 microcontroller as a **"Swiss Army Knife for Embedded Systems"**:

- The **CPU core** is like the knife's handle — a compact, reliable center.
- The **on-chip peripherals** (timers, ADC, USART, CCP modules) are the fold-out tools — each designed for one specific job.
- The **Harvard architecture** is like having **two separate workbenches** — one for fetching instructions and another for fetching data — so both can happen **simultaneously without collision**.

Just as a Swiss Army Knife is preferred by field engineers for its **versatility, portability, and battery efficiency**, the PIC 16 is favored in real-world products like washing machines, automotive dashboards, and IoT sensor nodes.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Classification Constants

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Data Bus Width** | **8 bits** |
| **Instruction Word Length** | **14 bits** (Mid-Range) |
| **Architecture** | **Harvard (Separate Program & Data Buses)** |
| **Instruction Set** | **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** |
| **Instruction Count** | **35 instructions** (standard) |
| **Operating Voltage** | **2.0 V to 5.5 V** |
| **Clock Speed** | **DC to 20 MHz** |
| **ROM (Program Memory)** | **Up to 8 K × 14** |
| **RAM (Data Memory)** | **Up to 368 × 8 bytes** |
| **EEPROM (Data)** | **Up to 256 × 8 bytes** |
| **I/O Pins** | **Up to 33** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Exams:** The PIC 16 series uses **14-bit wide program memory** but fetches data from an **8-bit wide data memory**. This asymmetry is a classic exam trap — always specify the bus width when asked.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 PIC Family Classification Tree

The PIC family is broadly classified into four tiers based on instruction word length and performance:

```
PIC Family
├── Baseline (PIC 10 / PIC 12) → 12-bit instructions, 33 instructions
├── Mid-Range  (PIC 16)        → 14-bit instructions, 35 instructions  ← FOCUS
├── Enhanced Mid-Range (PIC 16F1xxx) → 14-bit instructions, 49 instructions
└── High-Performance (PIC 17 / PIC 18) → 16-bit instructions, 58 / 75 instructions
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mid-Range PIC 16** vs **Enhanced Mid-Range PIC 16F1xxx**: The enhanced variants (like PIC 16F877A used in KTU labs) add **linear addressing of data memory**, **interrupt priority levels**, and **larger instruction set** — but the bus width remains **14-bit** and the core is still **8-bit RISC**.

### 2.2 Harvard Architecture — The 'Why' and 'How'

The PIC 16 implements a **modified Harvard architecture** with two physically separate memory spaces:

| Memory Type | Bus Width | Addressable Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Program Memory (Flash/ROM)** | **14 bits** | Up to 8K × 14 | Stores opcodes and immediate data |
| **Data Memory (RAM)** | **8 bits** | Up to 512 × 8 | Stores variables, SFRs, GPRs |
| **Data EEPROM** | **8 bits** | Up to 256 × 8 | Non-volatile user data storage |

**Why two buses?**
- Allows **simultaneous instruction fetch and data access** → **pipeline execution**.
- Different bus widths (14-bit for instructions, 8-bit for data) → **optimized silicon area** and **reduced cost**.
- The **14-bit opcode can embed a literal operand directly**, reducing instruction count for constant operations.

### 2.3 RISC Properties in PIC 16

The PIC 16 mid-range implements core RISC principles:

- **Single-cycle instruction execution** (except branches) → **4 clock cycles per instruction** using a **4:1 internal clock divider**.
- **Fixed instruction length** (14 bits) → simplifies the instruction decoder.
- **Load/Store architecture** — most operations work on the **W (Working) register**.
- **35 single-word instructions** — easier to learn and decode.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Engineering Relationships

> [!NOTE]
> **Clock-to-Instruction Timing Formula** (High-Yield for KTU):

$$T_{instruction} = 4 \times T_{osc} = \frac{4}{f_{osc}}$$

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction Cycle Time** | $T_{inst} = 4 / f_{osc}$ | seconds |
| **Instruction Throughput** | $\text{MIPS} = f_{osc} / 4$ | Million Instructions Per Second |
| **Maximum MIPS (PIC 16)** | $\text{MIPS}_{max} = 20\text{ MHz} / 4$ | **5 MIPS** |
| **Timer Overflow Period** | $T_{overflow} = 4 \times (Prescaler) \times (256 - TMR0) / f_{osc}$ | seconds |
| **ADC Conversion Time** | $T_{ADC} = T_{AD} \times 12$ where $T_{AD} = T_{osc} \times (\text{ADCS} \mid 2)$ | seconds |
| **Baud Rate (USART)** | $\text{Baud} = f_{osc} / (16 \times (SPBRG + 1))$ | bits/second |
| **Watchdog Timer Period** | $T_{WDT} = 18\text{ ms (typ)} \times \text{Postscaler}$ | milliseconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **MIPS = f_osc / 4** relationship is why PIC 16 at 20 MHz delivers **5 MIPS** — a critical benchmark when comparing it with 8051 (which at 20 MHz gives ~1 MIPS due to 12-clock cycles per instruction). This makes PIC 16 **~5× faster per MHz** for most operations.

### 2.5 PIC 16 CPU Core — Register Architecture

The CPU uses a **register-based architecture** centered on these key registers:

| Register | Size | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **W (Working Register)** | 8-bit | Primary accumulator for ALU operations |
| **PC (Program Counter)** | 13-bit | Addresses up to 8K × 14 of program memory |
| **STATUS Register** | 8-bit | Holds flags: Z, DC, C, IRP, RP0, RP1 |
| **FSR (File Select Register)** | 8-bit | Indirect data memory addressing pointer |
| **PCLATH** | 8-bit | Upper 5 bits of PC for computed GOTO/CALL |
| **INTCON** | 8-bit | Global and peripheral interrupt control |
| **Stack (Hardware)** | 13-bit × 8 levels | Hardware return address stack for CALL/RETURN |

> [!TIP]
> The **STATUS register's RP0 and RP1 bits** perform **bank switching** in the data memory — this is unique to PIC and is a **frequently tested concept** in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Instruction Cycle Time vs. Oscillator Frequency

**Problem:** A PIC 16F877A operates with an external crystal of $f_{osc} = 20 \text{ MHz}$. Determine the instruction cycle time, instruction throughput, and time taken to execute 1000 instructions.

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship between oscillator and instruction cycle.**

The PIC 16 mid-range divides the input oscillator frequency by **4 internally** to produce the instruction clock. This is called the **4T oscillator scheme**.

$$f_{instruction\_clock} = \frac{f_{osc}}{4}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given oscillator frequency.**

$$f_{instruction\_clock} = \frac{20 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}}{4} = 5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} = 5 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the instruction cycle time $T_{instruction}$.**

$$T_{instruction} = \frac{1}{f_{instruction\_clock}} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}}$$

$$T_{instruction} = 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 200 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 4 — Determine instruction throughput in MIPS.**

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{osc}}{4} = \frac{20 \text{ MHz}}{4} = 5 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate total time to execute 1000 instructions.**

$$T_{total} = N \times T_{instruction} = 1000 \times 200 \text{ ns} = 200{,}000 \text{ ns} = 200 \text{ \mu s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** PIC 16 at 20 MHz executes **1 instruction every 200 ns**, achieving a throughput of **5 MIPS**. This 4:1 clock division is the foundation of all PIC timing calculations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: USART Baud Rate Generator Value (BRG)

**Problem:** For a PIC 16F877A with $f_{osc} = 20 \text{ MHz}$, calculate the SPBRG value to generate an **asynchronous baud rate of 9600 bps** in **low-speed (BRGH = 0)** mode.

**Step 1 — Recall the baud rate formula for asynchronous low-speed mode (BRGH = 0).**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{osc}}{64 \times (\text{SPBRG} + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for SPBRG.**

$$\text{SPBRG} + 1 = \frac{f_{osc}}{64 \times \text{Baud Rate}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known values.**

$$\text{SPBRG} + 1 = \frac{20 \times 10^{6}}{64 \times 9600}$$

$$\text{SPBRG} + 1 = \frac{20 \times 10^{6}}{614{,}400}$$

**Step 4 — Perform the division.**

$$\text{SPBRG} + 1 = 32.552 \ldots$$

**Step 5 — Take the integer floor (SPBRG is an 8-bit integer register).**

$$\text{SPBRG} + 1 = 32 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SPBRG} = 31$$

**Step 6 — Compute the actual achieved baud rate for error analysis.**

$$\text{Baud}_{actual} = \frac{20 \times 10^{6}}{64 \times (31 + 1)} = \frac{20 \times 10^{6}}{2048}$$

$$\text{Baud}_{actual} = 9765.625 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the percentage error.**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{\text{Baud}_{actual} - \text{Baud}_{desired}}{\text{Baud}_{desired}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Error} = \frac{9765.625 - 9600}{9600} \times 100\% = 1.725\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict:** Since the error is **less than 2%**, SPBRG = 31 is **acceptable for reliable asynchronous communication**. KTU expects students to verify baud rate error thresholds.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Timer0 Overflow Delay Calculation

**Problem:** Using Timer0 in PIC 16F877A with $f_{osc} = 4 \text{ MHz}$, **prescaler = 1:256**, and **TMR0 initial value = 100**, calculate the time to overflow.

**Step 1 — Recall the Timer0 overflow formula.**

$$T_{overflow} = 4 \times T_{osc} \times \text{Prescaler} \times (256 - TMR0_{initial})$$

**Step 2 — Compute the oscillator period.**

$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{4 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 0.25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 0.25 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the instruction cycle period.**

$$T_{instruction} = 4 \times T_{osc} = 4 \times 0.25 \text{ \mu s} = 1 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the count delta to overflow.**

$$(256 - TMR0_{initial}) = 256 - 100 = 156 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the formula with prescaler = 256.**

$$T_{overflow} = 4 \times 0.25 \text{ \mu s} \times 256 \times 156$$

$$T_{overflow} = 1 \text{ \mu s} \times 256 \times 156$$

$$T_{overflow} = 1 \text{ \mu s} \times 39{,}936 = 39.936 \text{ ms}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Students often forget the **factor of 4** in the timer formula (because Timer0 is clocked by the **instruction clock**, not the oscillator). This is a **guaranteed 1-mark trap** in KTU exams.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PIC 16 vs 8051

| Feature | PIC 16F877A | 8051 (Intel) |
|---|---|---|
| Architecture | **Harvard** | **Modified Harvard (Von Neumann-like)** |
| Instruction Set | **RISC** | **CISC** |
| Instruction Length | **14-bit (variable within 14 bits)** | **8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit (variable)** |
| Instructions per Cycle | **1 (most)** | **~12 oscillator cycles per instruction** |
| MIPS @ 20 MHz | **5 MIPS** | **~1.66 MIPS** |
| Working Register | **W (single accumulator)** | **A + B + R0–R7 + SFRs** |
| Stack | **Hardware, 8 levels** | **Software stack in RAM** |
| Power Consumption | **Lower (CMOS)** | **Higher** |
| Data EEPROM | **On-chip** | **External (usually)** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PIC 16 Series Family Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["PROGRAM MEMORY\nFlash 8K x 14 bits\n14-bit Bus"]
    nodeB["DATA MEMORY\nRAM 368 x 8 bytes\n8-bit Bus"]
    nodeC["DATA EEPROM\n256 x 8 bytes\nNon-Volatile"]
    nodeD["ALU 8-bit\nArithmetic Logic Unit"]
    nodeE["W REGISTER\nWorking Accumulator\n8-bit"]
    nodeF["STATUS REGISTER\nFlags Z, C, DC, IRP, RP0, RP1"]
    nodeG["PROGRAM COUNTER PC\n13-bit Up to 8K"]
    nodeH["HARDWARE STACK\n8 Levels x 13-bit"]
    nodeI["INSTRUCTION DECODER\nAND CONTROL UNIT"]
    nodeJ["PERIPHERAL MODULES\nTimers, ADC, USART, CCP, SSP"]
    nodeK["OSCILLATOR\nDC to 20 MHz\n4T Internal Clock"]
    nodeL["INTERRUPT CONTROLLER\nINTCON, PIE, PIR"]

    nodeA --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeD
    nodeD <--> nodeE
    nodeD <--> nodeF
    nodeB <--> nodeD
    nodeC <--> nodeB
    nodeI --> nodeG
    nodeI --> nodeH
    nodeK --> nodeI
    nodeL --> nodeI
    nodeD --> nodeJ
```

### 4.2 PIC 16 Program Execution Pipeline Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA["Cycle T1\nFetch Instruction\nfrom Program Memory\n14-bit bus"]
    stepB["Cycle T2\nDecode Instruction\nby Control Unit"]
    stepC["Cycle T3\nRead Data Operand\nfrom Data Memory\n8-bit bus if needed"]
    stepD["Cycle T4\nExecute in ALU\nWrite Result to W\nor Data Memory"]
    stepE["Next Instruction T1\nFetch OVERLAPS with\nExecute of previous"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE -.-> stepA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipeline Insight:** The fetch of instruction N+1 **overlaps** with the execution of instruction N. This is why most PIC 16 instructions appear to execute in **one instruction cycle** (200 ns at 20 MHz), even though the internal pipeline takes 4 oscillator cycles.

### 4.3 PIC 16 Data Memory Bank Switching Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeBank0["BANK 0\nAddress 00h to 7Fh\nRP1=0, RP0=0"]
    nodeBank1["BANK 1\nAddress 80h to FFh\nRP1=0, RP0=1"]
    nodeBank2["BANK 2\nAddress 100h to 17Fh\nRP1=1, RP0=0"]
    nodeBank3["BANK 3\nAddress 180h to 1FFh\nRP1=1, RP0=1"]
    nodeGPR["GPR General Purpose\nRegisters in each bank\nVariable size"]
    nodeSFR["SFR Special Function\nRegisters mapped in\nBank 0 and Bank 1"]
    nodeStatus["STATUS Register\nControls RP0, RP1\nBits 5 and 6"]

    nodeStatus --> nodeBank0
    nodeStatus --> nodeBank1
    nodeStatus --> nodeBank2
    nodeStatus --> nodeBank3
    nodeBank0 --> nodeGPR
    nodeBank1 --> nodeGPR
    nodeBank2 --> nodeGPR
    nodeBank3 --> nodeGPR
    nodeBank0 --> nodeSFR
    nodeBank1 --> nodeSFR
```

### 4.4 Instruction Format and W-Register Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeFmt["14-bit Instruction Word\nPIC 16 Mid-Range"]
    nodeOp["Opcode Field\n6 or 7 bits"]
    nodeDest["Destination bit d\n0 = Result to W\n1 = Result to File Register"]
    nodeLit["Literal Field k\n8 bits for immediate values"]
    nodeFile["File Address f\n7 bits for register address"]
    nodeBit["Bit Address b\n3 bits for bit operations"]

    nodeFmt --> nodeOp
    nodeFmt --> nodeDest
    nodeFmt --> nodeLit
    nodeFmt --> nodeFile
    nodeFmt --> nodeBit
```

> [!TIP]
> **Decoding Insight:** A typical byte-oriented instruction like `ADDWF f, d` is encoded as: **7-bit opcode (000111)** + **7-bit file address (f)** + **1-bit destination (d)**. This compact encoding is why the 14-bit word holds more functionality than an 8-bit 8051 instruction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List any three features of the PIC 16 series microcontroller that distinguish it from the 8051 family.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Harvard Architecture with Separate Buses:** PIC 16 uses a 14-bit program bus and an 8-bit data bus, allowing simultaneous instruction fetch and data access, whereas 8051 uses a unified memory space (modified Harvard with Von Neumann characteristics).

2. **RISC Instruction Set:** PIC 16 uses a streamlined 35-instruction RISC set with most instructions executing in a single instruction cycle (4 oscillator cycles), giving **5 MIPS at 20 MHz**. In contrast, 8051 is CISC-based with 12 oscillator cycles per machine cycle, yielding only ~1.66 MIPS at 20 MHz.

3. **On-chip Data EEPROM and Flash Program Memory:** PIC 16 features integrated non-volatile EEPROM (e.g., 256 bytes in PIC 16F877A) for persistent user data, eliminating the need for external storage. The 8051 traditionally requires external program/data memory.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Explain the significance of the W register in PIC 16 architecture. Why is it sometimes called the "accumulator"?**

**Model Answer:**

The **W (Working) register** is an 8-bit special-purpose register that serves as the **implicit accumulator** for all arithmetic and logical operations in the PIC 16 ALU. It is called the accumulator because:

- It is the **default source operand** for most ALU operations (e.g., `ADDWF f, d`, `ANDWF f, d`, `XORWF f, d`).
- The **destination bit `d`** in these instructions determines whether the result is stored back in **W (d = 0)** or in the **file register (d = 1)**.
- Since the PIC 16 has **only one working register** (unlike 8051 which has A and B), all two-operand operations must use W as one of the operands.

**Example:** `ADDWF 0x20, 0` → adds the contents of register 0x20 to W, and stores the sum in W.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse W with the File Register. Remember — **W is NOT memory-mapped**. It is a dedicated internal register accessed only by ALU instructions. It cannot be directly written to RAM.

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks):**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted to 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the PIC 16 series microcontroller**. Clearly mark the program bus, data bus, ALU, W register, STATUS register, Program Counter, hardware stack, and the oscillator. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PIC 16F877A operates at $f_{osc} = 10 \text{ MHz}$. Compute:
1. The instruction cycle time.
2. The maximum achievable MIPS throughput.
3. The time required to execute 500 machine instructions.
4. The Timer0 overflow delay when prescaler = 1:128 and TMR0 is loaded with 50. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The PIC 16 architecture is a **modified Harvard design** with three distinct memory spaces and an 8-bit RISC CPU core. Refer to the architecture diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. The components work as follows:

- **Program Memory (Flash):** 14-bit wide, up to 8K words, accessed via the **14-bit program bus** driven by the **13-bit Program Counter (PC)**. The address from PC is latched into **PCLATH** for indirect jumps and table reads.

- **Data Memory (RAM):** 8-bit wide, organized into **four banks** of 128 bytes each, selected via the **RP0 and RP1** bits in the **STATUS register**. The lower half of each bank contains **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** for peripherals; the upper half contains **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** for user data.

- **ALU and W Register:** The 8-bit ALU performs arithmetic (+, −, INC, DEC), logic (AND, OR, XOR, COM), and shift (RRF, RLF) operations. One operand is **always the W register**; the other is a file register or a literal. The **STATUS register** captures the result flags: **Z (Zero)**, **C (Carry)**, **DC (Digit Carry)**, and bank-select bits **IRP, RP0, RP1**.

- **Hardware Stack:** An **8-level × 13-bit** on-chip stack stores return addresses during `CALL` instructions. It is **not user-accessible** as memory — it is managed automatically by the CPU.

- **Oscillator and Instruction Decoder:** The external crystal (or internal RC) oscillator is divided by 4 internally to generate the **Q1–Q4 quarter-cycle clocks**. These clocks drive the **fetch, decode, read, and execute** phases of the instruction pipeline. Instructions like `CALL`, `GOTO`, and `RETURN` take **2 instruction cycles** (8 oscillator cycles) due to pipeline flush.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Part (1) — Instruction cycle time:**

$$T_{instruction} = \frac{4}{f_{osc}} = \frac{4}{10 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 400 \text{ ns}$$

**Part (2) — Maximum MIPS throughput:**

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{osc}}{4} = \frac{10 \text{ MHz}}{4} = 2.5 \text{ MIPS}$$

**Part (3) — Time to execute 500 instructions:**

$$T_{total} = N \times T_{instruction} = 500 \times 400 \text{ ns} = 200{,}000 \text{ ns} = 200 \text{ \mu s}$$

**Part (4) — Timer0 overflow delay with prescaler 1:128 and TMR0 = 50:**

$$T_{overflow} = 4 \times T_{osc} \times \text{Prescaler} \times (256 - TMR0_{initial})$$

$$T_{overflow} = 4 \times \frac{1}{10 \times 10^{6}} \times 128 \times (256 - 50)$$

$$T_{overflow} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 128 \times 206$$

$$T_{overflow} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 26{,}368$$

$$T_{overflow} = 10{,}547.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 10.5472 \text{ ms}$$

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare the **Harvard architecture of PIC 16** with the **Von Neumann architecture** used in early microprocessors. Mention the advantages of PIC 16's architecture for real-time embedded applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a 4 MHz crystal oscillator connected to a PIC 16F877A, configure the **USART in asynchronous low-speed mode (BRGH = 0)** to achieve a baud rate as close to **1200 bps** as possible. Show all calculation steps and compute the actual baud rate error percentage. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

| Feature | Von Neumann (e.g., 8085) | Harvard (PIC 16) |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Single shared memory for code and data | Separate program memory (Flash) and data memory (RAM) |
| **Buses** | One common bus for both fetch and data | Separate 14-bit program bus + 8-bit data bus |
| **Bottleneck** | Von Neumann bottleneck — cannot fetch instruction while accessing data | Eliminated — fetch and data access can occur simultaneously |
| **Instruction Width** | Typically 8/16/24 bits, variable | Fixed 14 bits in PIC 16 |
| **Pipelining** | Limited by shared bus | Pipelined — fetch(N+1) overlaps execute(N) |
| **Speed** | Slower per cycle | Faster — 1 instruction per cycle for most ops |
| **Cost & Silicon** | Cheaper, smaller | Slightly higher silicon area |

**Advantages of Harvard in real-time embedded systems:**

1. **Deterministic execution time:** Most instructions take exactly 1 cycle → predictable timing for control loops, motor control, and communication protocols.
2. **Higher throughput:** Pipelined fetch/execute delivers 5 MIPS @ 20 MHz.
3. **Simultaneous access:** The CPU can read a sensor via ADC while fetching the next instruction from flash, ensuring no missed samples in real-time systems.
4. **Lower power:** Smaller word widths (14-bit) and pipelined design reduce dynamic switching power.

**Disadvantages (for completeness):**
- Code cannot be executed from data memory (no self-modifying code).
- More complex bus structure → slightly more silicon area.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Recall the formula for asynchronous low-speed (BRGH = 0) baud rate:**

$$\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{osc}}{64 \times (\text{SPBRG} + 1)}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to solve for SPBRG:**

$$\text{SPBRG} + 1 = \frac{f_{osc}}{64 \times \text{Baud Rate}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{64 \times 1200}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator and denominator:**

$$\text{SPBRG} + 1 = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{76{,}800} = 52.0833...$$

**Step 4 — Take the integer floor value:**

$$\text{SPBRG} + 1 = 52 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SPBRG} = 51$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the actual achieved baud rate:**

$$\text{Baud}_{actual} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{64 \times (51 + 1)} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{64 \times 52} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{3328}$$

$$\text{Baud}_{actual} = 1201.92 \text{ bps}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the percentage error:**

$$\text{Error} = \frac{\text{Baud}_{actual} - \text{Baud}_{desired}}{\text{Baud}_{desired}} \times 100\% = \frac{1201.92 - 1200}{1200} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Error} = \frac{1.92}{1200} \times 100\% = 0.16\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** With **SPBRG = 51**, the actual baud rate is **1201.92 bps**, yielding an error of **0.16%**, which is well within the **±2%** UART error tolerance. This makes the configuration suitable for reliable serial communication.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PIC 16 Architecture Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing bus widths:** Writing "PIC 16 has 8-bit instruction width" instead of **14-bit**. KTU deducts 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 4** in timer and instruction cycle formulas — results in answers off by 4×.
> 3. **MIPS computation:** Students often write "MIPS = f_osc" instead of **f_osc / 4** — this is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Bank switching confusion:** Forgetting to set **RP0 = 1** before accessing TRISx registers in Bank 1. Always mention bank selection in code-based questions.
> 5. **Baud rate error:** Skipping the error percentage calculation. Even if SPBRG is correct, KTU awards full marks **only if the error is verified**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PIC 16 Series Overview:**

- **Family Position:** Mid-Range PIC — 14-bit instructions, 35-instruction RISC set, 8-bit ALU.
- **Architecture:** Modified Harvard with **14-bit program bus** and **8-bit data bus**.
- **Clocking:** 4T internal clock → **1 instruction cycle = 4 oscillator periods**.
- **MIPS formula:** $\text{MIPS} = f_{osc} / 4$ → at 20 MHz gives **5 MIPS**.
- **Program Memory:** 8K × 14 bits, addressed by 13-bit PC, accessed via PCLATH for computed jumps.
- **Data Memory:** 4 banks × 128 bytes, selected by STATUS register's **RP0, RP1** bits.
- **W Register:** 8-bit implicit accumulator — **not memory-mapped**; cannot be read/written via file instructions.
- **STATUS Register:** Holds Z, DC, C, IRP, RP0, RP1 — the **control center** for flags and bank selection.
- **Hardware Stack:** 8 levels × 13-bit — auto-managed, no PUSH/POP instructions available.
- **Timer0:** 8-bit timer, clocked by instruction cycle, with programmable prescaler (up to 1:256).
- **On-chip Peripherals:** ADC (10-bit), USART, CCP, SSP, parallel ports (PORTA–E).
- **Voltage Range:** 2.0 V to 5.5 V → suitable for battery-powered applications.
- **Key Advantage over 8051:** ~3–5× faster per MHz, on-chip EEPROM, lower power, deterministic timing.
- **Common Variants Studied at KTU:** **PIC 16F84A**, **PIC 16F877A**, **PIC 16F887** (enhanced mid-range with more peripherals).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture of PIC16F877A
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Architecture of PIC16F877A

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **PIC16F877A — The Industry Workhorse of KTU Labs**
> The **PIC16F877A** is an 8-bit, high-performance, flash-based RISC microcontroller manufactured by *Microchip Technology*. It is built on **Harvard architecture** (separate program and data buses), executes one instruction per clock cycle (except branches), and is the most extensively tested and documented device in the PIC16 family — making it the default teaching platform in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCBMT402*.

It belongs to the **PIC16F87XA mid-range family** and offers a balanced combination of:

| Parameter | Specification |
|---|---|
| Program Memory (Flash) | **14.3 KB** (8192 × 14-bit words) |
| Data Memory (RAM) | **368 Bytes** |
| EEPROM Data Memory | **256 Bytes** |
| I/O Pins | **33 digital** (5 ports: A, B, C, D, E) |
| Instruction Set | **35 single-word instructions** |
| Operating Frequency | **DC – 20 MHz** |
| Instruction Cycle | **200 ns @ 20 MHz** (1/4 of oscillator) |
| ADC | **10-bit, 8 channels** |
| Timers | 3 (Timer0, Timer1, Timer2) |
| Communication | **UART, SPI, I²C (MSSP)** |
| Operating Voltage | **2.0 V – 5.5 V** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Hot Spot:** Out of all topics in Module 3, the *block diagram of PIC16F877A* and *pin description* appear in nearly every question paper (typical marks: 7 or 14). Memorize the port pinouts, alternate functions, and oscillator pins (OSC1/OSC2).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Building" View

Think of the PIC16F877A as a **highly efficient single-room office building** (the *CPU core* is the manager):

- **The Filing Cabinet (Program Memory / Flash)** — Stores *permanent* instructions (the SOPs of the office). Can only be re-written using a programmer.
- **The Whiteboard (Data Memory / RAM)** — Temporary scratchpad. Cleared when the power goes off.
- **The Manager's Desk (ALU + W register)** — Where all calculations actually happen. The `W` (working) register is the manager's pen — every computation passes through it.
- **The Receptionist (I/O Ports)** — The face of the office. Five reception desks: **Port A, B, C, D, E**. Visitors (sensors, LEDs) come and go through these.
- **The PA System (Interrupts)** — An external event (like a fire alarm) that *immediately* diverts the manager's attention, regardless of the current task.
- **The Clock on the Wall (Oscillator)** — Sets the rhythm. The whole office ticks at this rate. The PIC's instruction cycle is **¼ of the clock period** (4 clock pulses = 1 instruction).

> [!TIP]
> **Why "16F" and not "16C"?** The "F" stands for **Flash memory** (re-programmable, electrically erasable). "C" versions used EPROM (needed UV light to erase). This is also why KTU labs only use 'F' series — students can re-flash a chip hundreds of times.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Harvard vs. Von-Neumann — The Heart of the PIC

The PIC16F877A uses a **modified Harvard architecture** with separate buses for instructions and data.

| Feature | Harvard (PIC) | Von-Neumann (8085) |
|---|---|---|
| Buses | **2 separate** (Program + Data) | 1 shared |
| Instruction Width | Fixed (14-bit) | Variable (8/16/24 bit) |
| Fetch + Execute | **Simultaneous (pipelined)** | Sequential |
| Speed | Faster | Slower |
| Complexity | Higher pin count / cost | Lower |

> [!NOTE]
> The **two-stage pipeline** of PIC16F877A allows it to *fetch* the next instruction while *executing* the current one. This is why one instruction completes every clock cycle (after the first 2 cycles of startup latency).

## 2.2 CPU Core — The Brain of PIC16F877A

The CPU consists of:

1. **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** — 8-bit adder, subtractor, logic gates, and bit-manipulation unit.
2. **Working Register (`W`)** — A single 8-bit register that acts as the **implicit operand** for nearly all ALU operations.
3. **Status Register (`STATUS`)** — Contains the flags that reflect the result of the last ALU operation:
   - `C` (Carry)
   - `DC` (Digit Carry — for BCD)
   - `Z` (Zero)
   - `RP0`, `RP1` (Register Bank Select bits)
   - `IRP` (Indirect RAM bank select)
4. **Program Counter (PC)** — **13-bit wide**; can address up to **8K × 14-bit** program memory locations (0x0000 – 0x1FFF).
5. **Stack (8 levels × 13-bit)** — Hardware stack for subroutine CALL/RETURN. **Not user-accessible in software** (no PUSH/POP instructions).

## 2.3 Memory Organization — The Two Worlds

The PIC16F877A has **three physically separate memory spaces**:

### A) Program Memory (Flash) — 14-bit wide
- Capacity: **8K × 14 = 14,336 bytes** of instruction storage.
- On reset, PC = **0x0000** (Reset vector).
- Interrupt vector is at **0x0004**.
- 14-bit word width because all instructions fit in one 14-bit word → true single-word, single-cycle RISC.

### B) Data Memory (RAM) — 8-bit wide
- Total: **368 bytes** (0x00 – 0x1FF) split into:
  - **General Purpose Registers (GPR)** — 96 bytes
  - **Special Function Registers (SFR)** — control/status
- Divided into **4 banks** (Bank 0–3), selected via `RP1:RP0` bits in the STATUS register.

| `RP1` | `RP0` | Bank | Address Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | 0x00 – 0x7F |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | 0x80 – 0xFF |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | 0x100 – 0x17F |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | 0x180 – 0x1FF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Killer Concept:** To access a register in Bank 1 (e.g., `TRISB`), the program must explicitly set `RP0 = 1` using `BSF STATUS, RP0`. The compiler (XC8) usually hides this, but in KTU exam answers, you **must show the bank-select logic** to get full marks.

### C) EEPROM Data Memory — 256 bytes
- Non-volatile, byte-addressable, readable/writable during normal program execution.
- Access via `EECON1`, `EECON2`, `EEDATA`, `EEADR` registers.

## 2.4 I/O Ports — The Five Windows

| Port | Pins | Direction Register | Other Features |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PORTA** | 6 (RA0–RA5) | TRISA | RA0–RA3 analog-capable; RA4 is *open-drain* (no output driver) |
| **PORTB** | 8 (RB0–RB7) | TRISB | RB0 = external interrupt (INT); RB4–RB7 = interrupt-on-change |
| **PORTC** | 8 (RC0–RC7) | TRISC | Used for UART, SPI, I²C, CCP |
| **PORTD** | 8 (RD0–RD7) | TRISD | Parallel Slave Port (PSP) |
| **PORTE** | 3 (RE0–RE2) | TRISE | Analog inputs (AN5–AN7); control pins for PSP |

**Rule of the PIC GPIO:** `TRISx = 1` → Input; `TRISx = 0` → Output. (Counter-intuitive — *"1 is in, 0 is out"*).

## 2.5 Oscillator & Instruction Timing

The PIC16F877A divides the external oscillator frequency by **4** internally to produce the instruction clock.

$$T_{instruction} = \frac{4}{f_{osc}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why divide by 4?** This is a fundamental design choice in mid-range PICs. It allows the internal pipelines to be clocked at a slower, more reliable rate even if the crystal is fast (e.g., a 20 MHz crystal gives a 5 MHz instruction clock → 200 ns per instruction).

## 2.6 Reset Circuit

The PIC16F877A has multiple reset sources:
1. **Power-on Reset (POR)** — Automatic on Vdd rise.
2. **MCLR reset** — Manual reset pin (active LOW).
3. **Watchdog Timer (WDT) reset** — When WDT overflows and is enabled.
4. **Brown-out Reset (BOR)** — When Vdd drops below threshold.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{inst} = \dfrac{4}{f_{osc}}$ | Instruction cycle time | seconds |
| 2 | $N_{inst/sec} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{4}$ | Instructions per second | IPS |
| 3 | $T_{delay} = (Count + 2) \times T_{inst} \times Prescaler$ | Timer0 delay (with prescaler) | seconds |
| 4 | $f_{timer} = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{4 \times Prescaler}$ | Timer0 clock frequency | Hz |
| 5 | $T_{overflow} = \dfrac{4 \times 256 \times Prescaler}{f_{osc}}$ | Timer0 overflow time (8-bit) | seconds |
| 6 | $ADC_{result} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 1024$ | 10-bit ADC output | decimal |
| 7 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{ADC_{result} \times V_{ref}}{1024}$ | Reconstructed input voltage | Volts |
| 8 | $BR = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \times (SPBRG + 1)}$ | UART baud rate (async) | bps |
| 9 | $SPBRG = \dfrac{f_{osc}}{16 \times BR} - 1$ | UART baud-rate generator value | integer |
| 10 | $T_{WDT} = \dfrac{4 \times 2^{18} \times Prescaler}{f_{osc}}$ | Watchdog timer overflow | seconds |

> [!WARNING]
> **Always show units in the final answer.** Many KTU toppers lose 1–2 marks per paper simply because they wrote $5 \times 10^{-5}$ without writing **seconds**. Examiners *will* deduct for this.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Calculating Timer Delay

**Problem (Typical KTU 14-mark sub-part):**
Using PIC16F877A with a **20 MHz crystal** and a **1:256 prescaler**, calculate the delay generated by a single overflow of **Timer0** (8-bit mode).

### Step 1 — Identify the formula

The Timer0 overflow time in 8-bit mode is:

$$T_{overflow} = \frac{4 \times 2^{8} \times Prescaler}{f_{osc}}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the given values

- $f_{osc} = 20\,\text{MHz} = 20 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$
- $Prescaler = 256$
- Timer0 is 8-bit → it overflows when it counts from $0$ to $2^{8} = 256$

$$T_{overflow} = \frac{4 \times 256 \times 256}{20 \times 10^{6}}$$

### Step 3 — Simplify the numerator

$$4 \times 256 \times 256 = 4 \times 65536 = 262144$$

### Step 4 — Final division

$$T_{overflow} = \frac{262144}{20 \times 10^{6}} = 13.1072 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{T_{overflow} = 13.1072\,\text{ms}}$$

### Step 5 — Verification of order of magnitude
Sanity check: With a 20 MHz crystal, one instruction cycle is 200 ns. To overflow the 8-bit timer with a 1:256 prescaler requires $256 \times 256 = 65536$ instruction cycles.
$65536 \times 200 \times 10^{-9} = 13.107 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}$ ✓ (matches).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — ADC Reconstruction

**Problem:** The ADC of PIC16F877A reads a value of `0x1F4` (hex) with $V_{ref+} = 5.0\,\text{V}$ and $V_{ref-} = 0\,\text{V}$. Find the input voltage.

### Step 1 — Convert hex to decimal

$$0x1F4 = 1 \times 16^{2} + 15 \times 16^{1} + 4 \times 16^{0}$$
$$= 256 + 240 + 4 = 500$$

### Step 2 — Apply the ADC formula

$$V_{in} = \frac{ADC_{result} \times V_{ref}}{1024} = \frac{500 \times 5.0}{1024}$$

### Step 3 — Final result

$$V_{in} = \frac{2500}{1024} = 2.4414\,\text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{in} \approx 2.44\,\text{V}}$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — UART Baud-Rate Calculation

**Problem:** Compute the value of `SPBRG` for a baud rate of **9600 bps** with a **4 MHz crystal**.

### Step 1 — Use the formula

$$SPBRG = \frac{f_{osc}}{16 \times BR} - 1$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$SPBRG = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times 9600} - 1$$

### Step 3 — Simplify

$$SPBRG = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{153600} - 1 = 25.04 - 1 = 24.04$$

### Step 4 — Round to the nearest integer

$$\boxed{SPBRG = 24}$$

### Step 5 — Calculate the actual achieved baud rate

$$BR_{actual} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{16 \times (24 + 1)} = \frac{4 \times 10^{6}}{400} = 10000\,\text{bps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Reality Check:** The error in the above is $(10000 - 9600)/9600 = 4.16\%$. For UART, an error **< 2%** is desirable. With a 4 MHz crystal, **9600 bps is not ideal** — use a 3.6864 MHz or 11.0592 MHz crystal for clean integer values.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Special Function Registers (SFRs) by Bank

| SFR | Bank | Address | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `PORTA` | 0 | 0x05 | Port A latch | High |
| `PORTB` | 0 | 0x06 | Port B latch | High |
| `TRISA` | 1 | 0x05 | Port A direction | High |
| `TRISB` | 1 | 0x06 | Port B direction | High |
| `STATUS` | All | 0x03 | ALU flags & bank select | **Very High** |
| `INTCON` | All | 0x0B | Interrupt control | High |
| `OPTION_REG` | 1 | 0x81 | Timer0 config | High |
| `TMR0` | 0 | 0x01 | Timer0 counter | High |
| `ADCON0` | 1 | 0x1F | ADC control | Medium |
| `ADCON1` | 1 | 0x9F | ADC pin config | Medium |
| `TXREG` | 0 | 0x19 | UART transmit | Medium |
| `RCREG` | 0 | 0x1A | UART receive | Medium |
| `SPBRG` | 1 | 0x99 | UART baud rate | Medium |
| `EECON1` | 3 | 0x18C | EEPROM control | Low |
| `PCLATH` | All | 0x0A | Upper 5 bits of PC | Low |
| `CMCON` | 1 | 0x9C | Comparator config | Low |

---
