---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**List any four salient features of PIC16F877A.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **RISC architecture** with only 35 single-word instructions, each executing in one instruction cycle.
2. **14.3 KB of in-circuit serial programming (ICSP) flash program memory** with 8K × 14-bit width.
3. **368 bytes of data RAM** and **256 bytes of EEPROM** data memory.
4. **10-bit, 8-channel Analog-to-Digital Converter** with internal sample-and-hold.
5. Three timers, two CCP modules, USART, SPI, and I²C peripherals.
*(Any 4 = 3 marks; 1 mark lost if numbering not done)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Differentiate between Harvard and Von-Neumann architecture.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Harvard | Von-Neumann |
|---|---|---|
| Buses | Separate code & data buses | Single shared bus |
| Speed | Faster (parallel fetch) | Slower (sequential) |
| Power | Higher | Lower |
| Reliability | Robust (data can't corrupt code) | Vulnerable to bus contention |
| Example | PIC16F877A | Intel 8085 |

*(3 marks for a clean table with at least 3 valid differences.)*

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **Instructions (KTU ESE):** *Answer any ONE full question from this module. Each has two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**(a)** Draw the architectural block diagram of PIC16F877A and explain the function of the **ALU**, **W register**, **STATUS register**, and **Program Counter**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **Data Memory organization** of PIC16F877A in detail. Discuss the role of **STATUS register bits RP0 and RP1** in bank selection. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

The PIC16F877A is a **Harvard-architecture** 8-bit RISC microcontroller. Its block diagram is shown in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**1. ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) — 2 marks**
- The 8-bit ALU performs arithmetic (ADD, SUB, INC, DEC), logical (AND, OR, XOR, COM), and bit-manipulation (BCF, BSF) operations.
- It accepts one operand from the `W` register and the other from any file register, then stores the result back.

**2. W (Working) Register — 1 mark**
- An 8-bit register that is the **implicit operand** for nearly all instructions.
- It is **not a memory-mapped SFR**; it exists inside the CPU core and is accessed as a special operand.

**3. STATUS Register — 2 marks**
- Address `0x03`, accessible from **all banks**.
- Contains: `IRP` (bit 7), `RP1:RP0` (bits 6–5) for bank select, `Z` (bit 2) Zero flag, `DC` (bit 1) Digit Carry, `C` (bit 0) Carry.

**4. Program Counter (PC) — 2 marks**
- **13-bit** wide → can address $2^{13} = 8192$ locations in program memory.
- On reset, PC = `0x0000` (Reset vector); interrupt vector is at `0x0004`.
- The upper 5 bits of PC are buffered in `PCLATH` (0x0A) for computed GOTOs and CALLs.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**1. Memory Map (3 marks)**
The 368-byte data memory is split into four banks as shown in **Section 4.2**:

- Each bank is 128 bytes.
- The first 32 locations of Bank 0 and the entire Bank 1 are SFRs.
- Banks 2 and 3 are GPRs that mirror Banks 0 and 1.

**2. Bank Selection Logic (3 marks)**

To read/write an SFR, the correct bank must be selected via `RP1:RP0` in the STATUS register:

- `BSF STATUS, RP0` → switches to **Bank 1** (for TRISx, OPTION_REG, etc.).
- `BCF STATUS, RP0` → switches to **Bank 0** (for PORTx, TMR0, etc.).
- Example: To make `RB0` an output:
  ```assembly
  BSF  STATUS, RP0    ; Select Bank 1
  BCF  TRISB, 0       ; Set RB0 as output
  BCF  STATUS, RP0    ; Switch back to Bank 0
  ```

**3. Common GPR locations (1 mark)**
GPR starts at `0x20` in Bank 0 and is mirrored from `0xA0` to `0xFF` in Bank 1.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Pattern]**

**(a)** List the **features** of PIC16F877A. Explain the function of the **MCLR**, **OSC1/OSC2**, and **PORTA pins RA0–RA5** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a **20 MHz crystal** and **Prescaler = 64**, calculate the **Timer0 overflow time** in 8-bit mode. If we want a delay of **exactly 50 ms**, how many timer overflows are required? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to (a):

**1. Features (3 marks)** — Same as Part A Q1 above.

**2. MCLR Pin (Pin 1) — 1 mark**
- **Master Clear Reset** input. Active LOW.
- A logic low applied to MCLR forces the device into reset.
- Internally pulled high by a weak pull-up. A 10 kΩ pull-up + push-button to ground forms the standard manual reset circuit.

**3. OSC1 (Pin 13) and OSC2 (Pin 14) — 2 marks**
- These form the **oscillator input/output** pair.
- In crystal mode: connect a quartz crystal (e.g., 20 MHz) between OSC1 and OSC2, with two 22 pF capacitors to ground.
- In RC mode: a resistor + capacitor on OSC1 provides a cheaper (but less accurate) clock.
- The internal clock is then divided by 4 to form the instruction clock.

**4. PORTA — RA0 to RA5 (2 marks)**
- 6-bit bidirectional port, register `PORTA` (0x05) and `TRISA` (0x85).
- **RA0, RA1, RA2, RA3, RA5** can be configured as **analog inputs** (AN0–AN4) using the `ADCON1` register.
- **RA4** is an **open-drain output** (no internal pull-up; needs an external pull-up if used as output) and shares the pin with `T0CKI` (Timer0 clock input).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**1. Timer0 Overflow Calculation — 4 marks**

Formula: $T_{overflow} = \dfrac{4 \times 2^{8} \times Prescaler}{f_{osc}}$

$$T_{overflow} = \frac{4 \times 256 \times 64}{20 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{65536}{20 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\boxed{T_{overflow} = 3.2768 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s} = 3.2768\,\text{ms}}$$

**2. Number of overflows for 50 ms — 3 marks**

$$N = \frac{T_{required}}{T_{overflow}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{3.2768 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$N = 15.258 \approx 16\,\text{overflows (rounded up)}$$

> **Verification:** $16 \times 3.2768\,\text{ms} = 52.43\,\text{ms}$ (slightly over 50 ms, which is acceptable since the next instruction can fine-tune using a preload value of `TMR0`).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Marks Are Lost**
> 1. **Forgetting to set `RP0`** before writing to `TRISx` is the #1 reason students' assembly programs don't work. In exam answers, **always** show the bank-select sequence.
> 2. **Confusing PORTA pins with analog capability.** Only RA0–RA3 and RA5 are analog by default — RA4 is digital + open-drain.
> 3. **Forgetting the /4 divider** when computing the instruction cycle time. Examiners love to test this with a "20 MHz crystal, find instruction cycle" question.
> 4. **Not converting hex to decimal** in ADC problems. Writing `0x1F4` without expanding it loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Ignoring prescaler** in timer calculations — it must always be included for higher crystal frequencies.

---

## 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print and pin to your wall):**

- [ ] PIC16F877A = **8-bit RISC, Harvard architecture, 14-bit instruction word, 35 instructions, single-cycle execution**.
- [ ] **Flash:** 8K × 14 = 14.3 KB; **RAM:** 368 B; **EEPROM:** 256 B.
- [ ] **33 I/O pins** across 5 ports; total **40-pin DIP**.
- [ ] **TRISx = 1 → Input; TRISx = 0 → Output**.
- [ ] Bank selection: `RP1:RP0` bits in `STATUS` register.
- [ ] `STATUS` is at address `0x03` and is **accessible from all banks** (bank-independent).
- [ ] Reset vector: `0x0000`; Interrupt vector: `0x0004`.
- [ ] **Instruction cycle = 4 / $f_{osc}$** (always divide by 4).
- [ ] **10-bit ADC** → resolution = $V_{ref} / 1024$.
- [ ] **UART baud rate** $= f_{osc} / (16 \times (SPBRG + 1))$.
- [ ] **MCLR (Pin 1)** is active-LOW reset; needs 10 kΩ pull-up.
- [ ] **OSC1/OSC2** are oscillator pins; need 22 pF caps to ground.
- [ ] **RA4 is open-drain** — needs external pull-up for output.
- [ ] **RB0 = INT**, **RB4–RB7 = interrupt-on-change**.
- [ ] Timer0 is **8-bit**; Timer1 is **16-bit**; Timer2 is **8-bit with PR2 register**.
- [ ] Hardware stack is **8 levels deep**; no PUSH/POP, no overflow flag.
- [ ] Reading a register (PORT) reads the **pin state**; writing the **latch** (LAT) writes a value.
- [ ] Default oscillator on KTU trainer boards = **20 MHz crystal** = 5 MIPS = 200 ns/instruction.
- [ ] **Two-stage pipeline**: 1st cycle fetches, 2nd cycle executes; after that, 1 instruction per cycle.
- [ ] Brown-out reset (BOR) protects against low-Vdd lockups; enable via `PCON<SBOREN>`.
- [ ] Watchdog timer default enabled after reset — disable in software if not used (`WDTE = 0` in config bits).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin Description of the 8051 Microcontroller** refers to the systematic classification, electrical characterization, and functional mapping of the **40 pins** of the standard **8051 DIP (Dual In-line Package)** microcontroller. It documents the role of each pin in terms of power supply, clock input, I/O port lines, control signals, and alternate peripheral functions, forming the foundational hardware contract between the CPU core and the external world.

The 8051 is housed in a **40-pin DIP package**, with each pin serving a dedicated electrical or logical function. The pin description is the *physical interface blueprint* that engineers and programmers must master before designing any embedded system around the 8051.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Airport Terminal Building"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the 8051 chip as an international airport terminal building.**

Just like an airport has different types of doors and gates:
- **Arrival gates (Port 1)** – Dedicated entry doors for normal passenger data (general-purpose I/O).
- **Departure gates (Port 2, Port 0)** – Some double as boarding bridges that connect to *highways* (the external memory bus) for long-distance travel.
- **Service doors (Port 3)** – Special-purpose service entrances used by airline staff — cargo, maintenance, emergencies, etc. (alternate functions like RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD).
- **Control Tower (ALE, PSEN, EA, RST)** – Air traffic control signals that coordinate all ground movement and flight operations.
- **Power station and clock tower (Vcc, Vss, XTAL1, XTAL2)** – The electricity grid and the master timing clock that keep the entire airport synchronized.

A passenger (data byte) entering the terminal (CPU) must use the **correct gate (pin)** with the **correct ticket (logic level)** at the **correct time (clock cycle)**. Mis-routing leads to system failure — exactly as a wrongly assigned pin causes an embedded system to malfunction.

## 1.3 The 40-Pin Map at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard 8051 pin count = 40 pins**, organized as **4 ports × 8 bits = 32 I/O lines + 4 control pins + 2 power pins + 2 clock pins = 40 pins.**

| Pin Group | Pin Numbers | Quantity | Function Class |
|-----------|------------|----------|----------------|
| **Port 0 (P0.0 – P0.7)** | 32 – 39 | 8 | Multiplexed Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7) |
| **Port 1 (P1.0 – P1.7)** | 1 – 8 | 8 | General-purpose bidirectional I/O |
| **Port 2 (P2.0 – P2.7)** | 21 – 28 | 8 | High-byte address bus (A8–A15) |
| **Port 3 (P3.0 – P3.7)** | 10 – 17 | 8 | General I/O + alternate peripheral functions |
| **RST** | 9 | 1 | Reset input (active HIGH) |
| **XTAL1, XTAL2** | 19, 18 | 2 | Crystal oscillator connections |
| **Vss (GND)** | 20 | 1 | Ground reference (0 V) |
| **PSEN** | 29 | 1 | Program Store Enable (active LOW) |
| **ALE / PROG** | 30 | 1 | Address Latch Enable / EPROM program pulse |
| **EA / VPP** | 31 | 1 | External Access / EPROM programming voltage |
| **Vcc** | 40 | 1 | +5 V DC supply |

> [!NOTE]
> **Total pin count verification:** $4 \times 8 + 1 + 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 32 + 8 = 40$ pins. ✔ This matches the physical DIP package count.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Port-wise Pin Analysis (Why & How)

### 2.1.1 Port 1 (Pins 1 – 8) — "The Pure I/O Port"

- **Pins:** P1.0 → Pin 1, P1.1 → Pin 2, … , P1.7 → Pin 8
- **Function:** Pure, general-purpose, **bidirectional** I/O port. **No alternate functions.**
- **Internal structure:** Each pin has an internal pull-up resistor (≈ 30 kΩ to 40 kΩ), making it capable of driving logic HIGH without external resistors.
- **Quasi-bidirectional nature:** When written LOW, the internal pull-up is disabled and the pin actively sinks current. When written HIGH, the weak pull-up provides logic HIGH.
- **Why this design?** Port 1 is reserved for the cleanest, most predictable user I/O, freeing other ports for bus or alternate functions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Port 1 is the **only port without any alternate function**. This is a frequently asked 3-mark question.

### 2.1.2 Port 3 (Pins 10 – 17) — "The Alternate Function Port"

- **Pins:** P3.0 → Pin 10, P3.1 → Pin 11, … , P3.7 → Pin 17
- **Function:** Dual-role — general I/O **OR** dedicated alternate peripheral functions.
- **Internal structure:** Same quasi-bidirectional design as Port 1.

| Pin | Primary Name | Alternate Function | Purpose |
|-----|-------------|--------------------|---------|
| Pin 10 | P3.0 | **RxD** | Serial data **Receive** input |
| Pin 11 | P3.1 | **TxD** | Serial data **Transmit** output |
| Pin 12 | P3.2 | **INT0** | External **Interrupt 0** input (active LOW) |
| Pin 13 | P3.3 | **INT1** | External **Interrupt 1** input (active LOW) |
| Pin 14 | P3.4 | **T0** | **Timer 0** external input |
| Pin 15 | P3.5 | **T1** | **Timer 1** external input |
| Pin 16 | P3.6 | **WR** | External RAM **Write** strobe (active LOW) |
| Pin 17 | P3.7 | **RD** | External RAM **Read** strobe (active LOW) |

- **Why alternate functions?** Embedding peripheral signals on Port 3 keeps the package count at 40 while exposing UART, timers, interrupts, and bus control.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** When the corresponding SFR (Special Function Register) bit is set, the alternate function is activated; otherwise, the pin behaves as a normal I/O line. Example: setting SCON bit enables serial I/O on P3.0/P3.1.

### 2.1.3 Port 2 (Pins 21 – 28) — "The High-Address Bus"

- **Pins:** P2.0 → Pin 21, P2.1 → Pin 22, … , P2.7 → Pin 28
- **Function:** General-purpose I/O **OR** carries the **high-order address byte (A8 – A15)** when external memory is accessed.
- **Internal structure:** Quasi-bidirectional with internal pull-ups.
- **When used as address bus:** During MOVX or external code fetch, P2 holds the upper 8 bits of the 16-bit address.
- **Why this design?** Provides 64 KB addressing capability (A0–A15) by combining P0 (multiplexed) and P2 (high byte).

### 2.1.4 Port 0 (Pins 32 – 39) — "The Multiplexed Address/Data Bus"

- **Pins:** P0.0 → Pin 32, P0.1 → Pin 33, … , P0.7 → Pin 39
- **Function:** Most complex port — three roles:
  1. **General-purpose I/O** (when no external memory)
  2. **Multiplexed Address/Data bus (AD0 – AD7)** during external memory access
  3. **External pull-up resistors required** for I/O mode (no internal pull-up)
- **Internal structure:** **Open-drain** transistors — cannot drive HIGH without external 10 kΩ pull-ups.
- **Why open-drain?** Allows multiple devices to share the data bus without contention when properly externally pulled up.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** Port 0 is the **only port without internal pull-up resistors**. This is THE most-asked 8051 hardware trivia in KTU exams.

### 2.1.5 Control Pins

| Pin | Number | Name | Type | Active Level | Function |
|-----|--------|------|------|--------------|----------|
| **RST** | 9 | Reset | Input | **HIGH** | Resets CPU when held HIGH for ≥ 2 machine cycles |
| **ALE** | 30 | Address Latch Enable | Output | HIGH (pulse) | Latches low-byte address from P0 into external latch (74373) |
| **PSEN** | 29 | Program Store Enable | Output | **LOW** | Enables external code memory (ROM) read |
| **EA** | 31 | External Access | Input | **LOW** | Forces all code fetches from external ROM (overrides internal ROM) |
| **XTAL1** | 19 | Crystal Input 1 | Input | Analog | One terminal of crystal/oscillator |
| **XTAL2** | 18 | Crystal Output 2 | Output | Analog | Other terminal of crystal/oscillator |

### 2.1.6 Power Pins

- **Vcc (Pin 40):** **+5 V DC** supply.
- **Vss (Pin 20):** **0 V (Ground)** reference.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total pins in 8051 DIP | **40** | pins | Standard package |
| 2 | I/O ports | **4 (P0, P1, P2, P3)** | ports | Each 8-bit wide |
| 3 | Total I/O lines | $4 \times 8 = \mathbf{32}$ | lines | 32 of 40 pins |
| 4 | Crystal frequency range | $1\,\text{MHz} \le f_{\text{osc}} \le \mathbf{12\,MHz}$ (standard 8051) | MHz | Up to 24 MHz in some variants |
| 5 | Machine cycle period | $T_{\text{MC}} = \mathbf{12 \times T_{\text{osc}}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$ | seconds | 12 oscillator periods = 1 machine cycle |
| 6 | Reset pulse width | $t_{\text{RST}} \ge \mathbf{2}$ machine cycles | cycles | Must be HIGH for at least 2 MCs |
| 7 | Vcc supply | $\mathbf{+5\,\text{V}} \pm 10\%$ | V | TTL-compatible |
| 8 | Vih (min input HIGH) | $\mathbf{2.0\,V}$ | V | Standard TTL |
| 9 | Vil (max input LOW) | $\mathbf{0.8\,V}$ | V | Standard TTL |
| 10 | Port 0 pull-up (external) | $\mathbf{10\,k\Omega}$ (typical) | Ω | Required for I/O mode |
| 11 | Port 1/2/3 internal pull-up | $\approx \mathbf{30 \text{ to } 40\,k\Omega}$ | kΩ | Quasi-bidirectional |
| 12 | Address space (with external memory) | $\mathbf{2^{16} = 64\,KB}$ | bytes | 16-bit address bus (P0+P2) |
| 13 | ALE pulse rate | $\mathbf{2}$ pulses per machine cycle | pulses | Latches address byte |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Product Design:** The 40-pin DIP footprint is the universal reference for 8051-based hobbyist and industrial boards (e.g., 89C51, 89S52, P89V51RD2).
- **Bus Expansion:** The Port 0/Port 2 multiplexing scheme is replicated in **every modern microcontroller** (AVR, PIC, ARM) — understanding the 8051 bus structure teaches the universal concept of multiplexed address/data buses.
- **Interfacing Logic:** Knowing that PSEN, RD, WR are active LOW is essential for connecting external RAM (e.g., 6264) and ROM (e.g., 2764) chips.
- **Industrial Applications:** Used in washing machine controllers, elevator control panels, automotive dashboards, and instrumentation — all rely on the pinout knowledge to wire peripherals.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Machine Cycle and ALE Pulse Relationship

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency into manageable timing units. The derivation below shows the precise relationship between the crystal oscillator, machine cycles, states, and the ALE pulse — a guaranteed KTU 14-marker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Principle:** Every 8051 instruction executes in 1, 2, or 4 machine cycles. A machine cycle is the smallest unit of CPU action.

**Step 1: Define the oscillator period.**

$$T_{\text{osc}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{osc}}}$$

**Reasoning:** The on-chip oscillator (driven by a crystal across XTAL1–XTAL2) generates a base square wave. Each full cycle of this wave is one oscillator period $T_{\text{osc}}$.

**Step 2: Define the state (S).**

The 8051 internally divides $T_{\text{osc}}$ by 2 (using two clock phases, P1 and P2). One state equals 2 oscillator periods.

$$T_{\text{state}} = 2 \times T_{\text{osc}}$$

**Reasoning:** A "state" is the fundamental tick of the internal state machine. The 8051 uses 2-phase clocking to give setup and hold margins to internal flip-flops.

**Step 3: Define the machine cycle (MC).**

A machine cycle is fixed at 6 states (S1 through S6), each state being 2 oscillator periods.

$$T_{\text{MC}} = 6 \times T_{\text{state}} = 6 \times 2 \times T_{\text{osc}} = 12 \times T_{\text{osc}}$$

**Step 4: Express machine cycle in terms of crystal frequency.**

$$T_{\text{MC}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}$$

**Step 5: Count the ALE pulses per machine cycle.**

The Address Latch Enable signal goes HIGH twice per machine cycle — once during S1P2 → S2P1 and once during S4P2 → S5P1.

$$N_{\text{ALE}} = 2 \text{ pulses per machine cycle}$$

**Step 6: Compute the ALE pulse frequency.**

$$f_{\text{ALE}} = \frac{2}{T_{\text{MC}}} = \frac{2 \times f_{\text{osc}}}{12} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{6}$$

**Step 7: Numerical Example (KTU favourite).**

For a crystal of $f_{\text{osc}} = 12\,\text{MHz}$:

$$T_{\text{osc}} = \frac{1}{12 \times 10^6} = 83.33\,\text{ns}$$

$$T_{\text{MC}} = 12 \times 83.33\,\text{ns} = 1\,\mu\text{s}$$

$$f_{\text{ALE}} = \frac{12\,\text{MHz}}{6} = 2\,\text{MHz}$$

$$T_{\text{ALE pulse period}} = 0.5\,\mu\text{s} = 500\,\text{ns}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory hook:** For a **12 MHz crystal**, **1 machine cycle = 1 µs** exactly. This is why 12 MHz is the most common crystal in 8051 boards — timing calculations become trivial.

## 3.2 Derivation: Address Latching Process (Why ALE is needed)

**Step 1: The problem.**

Port 0 pins P0.0 – P0.7 must carry **two different signals** during one external memory access:
- First, the **low byte of the address (A0 – A7)**
- Then, the **data byte (D0 – D7)**

**Step 2: The solution.**

The 8051 uses **time-division multiplexing**. During the first half of the memory cycle, P0 carries A0–A7. During the second half, P0 carries D0–D7.

**Step 3: The role of ALE.**

The ALE signal acts as a **synchronization strobe** to an external latch (74HC373 or 74LS373). When ALE goes HIGH, the latch is transparent — the address flows through. When ALE goes LOW, the latch "freezes" the address, freeing P0 to switch to data mode.

**Step 4: The latching equation.**

Let $A(t)$ be the address on P0 and $D(t)$ the data on P0. The external latch output $A_{\text{latched}}$ satisfies:

$$
A_{\text{latched}} = 
\begin{cases}
A(t) & \text{when } \text{ALE} = 1 \text{ (transparent)} \\
A_{\text{previous}} & \text{when } \text{ALE} = 0 \text{ (hold)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5: The PSEN (Program Store Enable) timing.**

Once the address is latched, the 8051 drives **PSEN LOW** to enable the external code ROM. The ROM places the instruction byte on P0, and the 8051 reads it before the end of the machine cycle.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Alternate Function Activation

| SFR Bit Set? | Pin Behaviour | Control Register | Example |
|--------------|---------------|------------------|---------|
| SCON configured for serial | P3.0 = RxD, P3.1 = TxD | **SCON** | Serial communication |
| TCON IT0 = 1 | P3.2 = INT0 (edge-triggered) | **TCON** | External interrupt 0 |
| TCON IT1 = 1 | P3.3 = INT1 (edge-triggered) | **TCON** | External interrupt 1 |
| TMOD TR0 = 1 | P3.4 = T0 (counter input) | **TMOD, TCON** | External event counting |
| TMOD TR1 = 1 | P3.5 = T1 (counter input) | **TMOD, TCON** | External event counting |
| MOVX @DPTR,A | P3.6 = WR, P3.7 = RD active | (auto) | External RAM access |

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix of All Four Ports

| Feature | **Port 0** | **Port 1** | **Port 2** | **Port 3** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Pin numbers | 32 – 39 | 1 – 8 | 21 – 28 | 10 – 17 |
| Internal pull-up | **No (open drain)** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Quasi-bidirectional | No (true open drain) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Alternate function | Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7) | **None** | Address high byte (A8–A15) | RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD |
| External resistor needed | **Yes (10 kΩ pull-up)** | No | No | No |
| Driving capability | Higher sink current | Standard | Standard | Standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Comparison:** Port 0 = open drain (needs pull-up); Port 1 = pure I/O (no alternate); Port 2 = high address; Port 3 = alternate function.
