# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"List the four I/O ports of the 8051 microcontroller and mention one distinguishing feature of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051 has **four 8-bit I/O ports** designated **P0, P1, P2, and P3**.

| Port | Pin Nos. | Distinguishing Feature |
|------|----------|------------------------|
| **P0** | 32 – 39 | Open-drain; needs external 10 kΩ pull-up; doubles as multiplexed address/data bus (AD0–AD7) |
| **P1** | 1 – 8 | Pure general-purpose I/O; **no alternate function**; has internal pull-ups |
| **P2** | 21 – 28 | Carries high-byte address (A8–A15) during external memory access; has internal pull-ups |
| **P3** | 10 – 17 | Each pin has an alternate function (RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) |

**(3 marks: 1 mark for listing ports, 2 marks for distinguishing features.)**

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Explain the function of the ALE and PSEN pins of the 8051."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **ALE (Address Latch Enable, Pin 30):** It is an **output pin** that emits a HIGH pulse to latch the low-byte address (A0–A7) from the multiplexed Port 0 into an external latch (e.g., 74HC373). **Two ALE pulses occur per machine cycle**, freeing P0 to carry data afterward.

- **PSEN (Program Store Enable, Pin 29):** It is an **active-LOW output** that enables the **external code memory (ROM)** during a code-fetch operation. When PSEN goes LOW, the external ROM places the instruction byte on Port 0.

**(3 marks: 1.5 marks each for ALE and PSEN.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QN]**

**(a)** Draw the pin diagram of the 8051 microcontroller and explain the function of the following pins: **Vcc, Vss, XTAL1, XTAL2, RST, EA, ALE, PSEN**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the **Port 3 alternate functions** in detail. Why does the 8051 share these peripheral signals with the general-purpose I/O port? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) 8051 Pin Diagram & Key Pin Functions (7 marks):**

**Pin Diagram:**
*(Students should draw the 40-pin DIP top-view with the notch at the top, numbering pins 1–8 (P1), 9 (RST), 10–17 (P3), 18–19 (XTAL2, XTAL1), 20 (Vss), 21–28 (P2), 29 (PSEN), 30 (ALE), 31 (EA), 32–39 (P0), 40 (Vcc).)*

**Pin functions:**

- **Vcc (Pin 40):** +5 V DC supply input that powers the entire chip.
- **Vss (Pin 20):** Ground (0 V) reference for the chip.
- **XTAL1 (Pin 19) & XTAL2 (Pin 18):** Connect a quartz crystal (typically 11.0592 MHz or 12 MHz) with two 30 pF capacitors to ground to form the on-chip oscillator. The CPU derives its internal clock from this oscillator. XTAL1 is the input to the inverting amplifier; XTAL2 is the output.
- **RST (Pin 9):** A HIGH pulse of at least **2 machine cycles** resets the 8051: PC ← 0000H, all SFRs default, ports set to FFH. Internally, a capacitor-resistor network on power-up gives an automatic reset.
- **EA (Pin 31, External Access):** When tied **HIGH**, the 8051 fetches code from **internal ROM** (if present) up to 4 KB, then continues from external ROM. When tied **LOW**, all code fetches are from external ROM — this is how the ROM-less 8031 operates.
- **ALE (Pin 30, Address Latch Enable):** An output that emits a HIGH pulse to latch the low-byte address from Port 0's multiplexed bus into an external latch. Also used as the programming pulse input (PROG) during EPROM programming.
- **PSEN (Pin 29, Program Store Enable):** An active-LOW output that enables the external code ROM during instruction fetch. Goes LOW twice per machine cycle (during external code fetch).

**(7 marks: 1 mark for diagram, 6 marks for explanations.)**

**(b) Port 3 Alternate Functions (7 marks):**

| Pin | Alternate Name | Function | Activated By |
|-----|---------------|----------|--------------|
| P3.0 | **RxD** | Serial data input (UART receive) | SCON, REN bit |
| P3.1 | **TxD** | Serial data output (UART transmit) | SCON, TR1 for baud gen |
| P3.2 | **INT0** | External interrupt 0 (active LOW / edge) | TCON IT0, EX0 |
| P3.3 | **INT1** | External interrupt 1 (active LOW / edge) | TCON IT1, EX1 |
| P3.4 | **T0** | Timer 0 external count input | TMOD, TR0 |
| P3.5 | **T1** | Timer 1 external count input | TMOD, TR1 |
| P3.6 | **WR** | External RAM **write** strobe (active LOW) | MOVX @DPTR,A |
| P3.7 | **RD** | External RAM **read** strobe (active LOW) | MOVX A,@DPTR |

**Why share with Port 3?**

The 8051 is constrained to a 40-pin package to maintain DIP compatibility and cost-effectiveness. By reusing Port 3 pins for peripheral signals, the design avoids increasing the pin count. The user can still use any Port 3 pin as a normal I/O line when the alternate peripheral is not needed. The SFR bits act as software-controlled "switches" to enable the alternate function dynamically.

**(7 marks: 4 marks for the table, 3 marks for the rationale.)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QN]**

**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **internal structure of a Port 1 pin** and justify why it is called a **quasi-bidirectional** port. Compare it with the Port 0 structure. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 8051 system uses a **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Calculate: (i) Oscillator period, (ii) State period, (iii) Machine cycle period, (iv) ALE pulse frequency, (v) Time taken to execute a single-cycle instruction. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Port 1 Internal Structure & Quasi-Bidirectional Nature (7 marks):**

**Port 1 Pin Internal Circuit:**

A Port 1 bit consists of:
1. A **D-type latch** (1 bit of SFR P1) that stores the user's data.
2. A **field-effect transistor pull-up** to Vcc — but this is a *weak* pull-up (≈ 30–40 kΩ) that can only source a small current.
3. A **strong pull-down transistor** (FET driver) capable of sinking high current.
4. An **input buffer** that reads the pin's logic level.
5. A **read-latch** and **read-pin** multiplexer (controlled by the CPU's instruction).

**Quasi-bidirectional justification:**

The term **quasi-bidirectional** is used because:
- To make the pin HIGH, the internal weak pull-up is activated — the pin is **driven HIGH** but cannot source much current (limitation of quasi outputs).
- To make the pin LOW, the strong pull-down is activated — the pin is **actively driven LOW** and can sink ~20 mA.
- As an **input**, the pin must first be written '1' (so the pull-up is on) — otherwise the strong pull-down would override the external signal.
- This is "quasi" because the output structure is asymmetric (strong LOW, weak HIGH).

**Comparison with Port 0:**

| Aspect | Port 1 | Port 0 |
|---|---|---|
| Output structure | Quasi-bidirectional (weak pull-up + strong pull-down) | **True open-drain** (no pull-up, only pull-down) |
| Internal pull-up | Yes (~30–40 kΩ) | **No internal pull-up** |
| External pull-up required for I/O mode | No | **Yes (10 kΩ typical)** |
| External memory use | **No** | Yes — multiplexed AD0–AD7 |
| Driving capability HIGH | Weak | None without external pull-up |
| Driving capability LOW | Strong | Strong |

**(7 marks: 3 marks Port 1 structure, 2 marks quasi explanation, 2 marks Port 0 comparison.)**

**(b) Timing Calculations for f_osc = 11.0592 MHz (7 marks):**

**Given:** $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592\,\text{MHz} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$

**(i) Oscillator period:**

$$T_{\text{osc}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{osc}}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 90.42\,\text{ns}$$

**(ii) State period:**

$$T_{\text{state}} = 2 \times T_{\text{osc}} = 2 \times 90.42 = 180.85\,\text{ns}$$

**(iii) Machine cycle period:**

$$T_{\text{MC}} = 12 \times T_{\text{osc}} = 12 \times 90.42 = 1085.07\,\text{ns} \approx 1.085\,\mu\text{s}$$

**(iv) ALE pulse frequency:**

$$f_{\text{ALE}} = \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{6} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{6} = 1.8432\,\text{MHz}$$

**Note:** 1.8432 MHz is exactly **1/16 of 11.0592 MHz / 12** — this is why 11.0592 MHz is chosen: it produces standard baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400) cleanly for UART communication.

**(v) Time for single-cycle instruction:**

A single-cycle instruction (e.g., `INC A`, `CLR A`, `NOP`) executes in 1 machine cycle:

$$T_{\text{instruction}} = T_{\text{MC}} = 1.085\,\mu\text{s}$$

**(7 marks: 1.4 marks per sub-part.)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Port 0 needs external pull-up:** When asked "which port cannot drive HIGH without external resistor?", students often answer "Port 0 is open-drain" but forget to **mention the 10 kΩ pull-up** value. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing PSEN and RD:** PSEN is for **code memory** (ROM), RD is for **data memory** (RAM). Both are active LOW, but PSEN goes LOW during code fetch, RD goes LOW only during MOVX external data read. **Lose 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **ALE misconception:** Some students think ALE is HIGH continuously. **ALE is a periodic pulse, twice per machine cycle.** Marks lost if you don't state the frequency.
> 4. **EA pin confusion:** EA = **External Access**, not "Enable All". When LOW, the 8051 fetches ALL code from external ROM. When HIGH (with internal ROM chip), it fetches internal first then external above 4 KB boundary.
> 5. **RST active level:** Many write "RST is active LOW". **RST is active HIGH** — a HIGH pulse resets the chip.
> 6. **Port 3 alternate function omission:** Students often list RxD/TxD but forget INT0/INT1, T0/T1, WR, RD. **All 8 must be memorized.**
> 7. **Unit mistakes in timing calculations:** Mixing up ns, µs, ms is a guaranteed 0.5 mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin Description**

- **Total pins = 40**, **4 ports × 8 lines = 32 I/O pins**, plus 4 control + 2 clock + 2 power.
- **Port 0 (32–39):** Open-drain, no internal pull-up, multiplexed AD0–AD7, needs **10 kΩ external pull-up** for I/O.
- **Port 1 (1–8):** Pure general-purpose I/O, **no alternate function**, has internal pull-up.
- **Port 2 (21–28):** Carries high address A8–A15 during external memory access.
- **Port 3 (10–17):** Has 8 alternate functions: **RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD** — ALL active LOW except TxD, T0, T1 outputs.
- **RST (Pin 9):** **Active HIGH**, reset pulse ≥ 2 machine cycles.
- **XTAL1 (19) & XTAL2 (18):** Crystal connections, with two 30 pF capacitors to GND.
- **Vcc (40) = +5 V, Vss (20) = GND.**
- **PSEN (29):** Active LOW, enables **external code ROM** during instruction fetch.
- **ALE (30):** Output pulse (twice per machine cycle), latches low address from P0.
- **EA (31):** LOW = all code from external ROM; HIGH = internal ROM (if available) prioritized.
- **Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods = 6 states.**
- **For 12 MHz crystal:** $T_{\text{MC}} = 1\,\mu\text{s}$ exactly.
- **ALE frequency = $f_{\text{osc}} / 6$.**
- **For 11.0592 MHz crystal:** Generates exact standard UART baud rates (9600, 4800, 2400, 1200).
- **TTL levels:** $V_{IH} \ge 2.0\,\text{V}$, $V_{IL} \le 0.8\,\text{V}$.
- **Port 1, 2, 3:** Quasi-bidirectional with internal pull-up (~30–40 kΩ).
- **Why multiplexed bus?** Saves pins — 16-bit address + 8-bit data carried on just 8 Port 0 pins + 8 Port 2 pins = 16 pins total for full 64 KB addressing.
- **Address latching external chip:** **74HC373** (or 74LS373) transparent latch, controlled by ALE.
- **Total I/O + bus strategy:** 32 I/O lines + 16 address lines (shared) + 8 data lines (shared with low address) = efficient 40-pin packaging.

---

> **End of KTU Module 3 — Pin Description Notes**
> *Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme Aligned*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Memory Organization # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition — Memory Organization > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memory Organization** in the context of an embedded microcontroller refers to the systematic logical and physical arrangement, addressing scheme, partitioning, and access methodology of program code storage, data storage, and special-function registers within the device. In the **Intel 8051 family** (the reference architecture for PCBMT402), memory is physically separated into two distinct address spaces: **Program Memory (Code ROM)** and **Data Memory (RAM)**, following the **Modified Harvard Architecture** principles. The 8051 can address up to **64 KB** of program memory (0000H–FFFFH) and **128 bytes** of internal data memory (00H–7FH) along with **128 bytes** of Special Function Register (SFR) space (80H–FFH), with optional external data memory expansion up to 64 KB. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Two-Ward Hospital Imagine a large hospital building with **two completely separate wings**: - **Wing A (Program Memory / ROM)** — This is the **Operating Theater**. It only allows the **doctor (CPU)** to *read* instructions; no one is allowed to *write into it during surgery* (Read-Only during normal execution). The doctor follows a strict, pre-written surgical script (your compiled assembly code). - **Wing B (Data Memory / RAM)** — This is the **Patient Ward**. The doctor constantly **reads patient vitals and writes new medicine notes** (Read/Write). The patients can be moved in and out dynamically. - **The Reception Desk (SFR Area)** — A special, restricted zone within Wing B containing **critical monitoring equipment** (Timers, Serial Port, I/O Ports). Only the head doctor (CPU) with special keys (specific addresses) can access these. The **addresses** (room numbers) of these wings are **independent** — Room 0001 in Wing A and Room 0001 in Wing B are **completely different physical locations**. This is the essence of the Harvard separation in the 8051. ## 1.3 Key Terminology — Syllabus Highlights > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU 2024 Syllabus Terms (Must Memorize):** > - **Harvard Architecture** — Separate address and data buses for program and data memory. > - **Von Neumann Architecture** — Single shared bus for both program and data memory. > - **Program Memory (Code Memory)** — Non-volatile storage for executable instructions. > - **Data Memory** — Volatile read/write storage for variables, stack, and bit-addressable region. > - **SFR (Special Function Registers)** — Memory-mapped control registers for peripheral configuration. > - **Memory Map** — A diagrammatic representation of the address space allocation. > - **Stack Pointer (SP)** — An 8-bit register pointing to the top of the stack in internal RAM. > - **PSW (Program Status Word)** — A flag register carrying carry, auxiliary carry, and register bank selection bits. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics of the 8051 Memory System > [!TIP] > **Standard Numerical Boundaries (Bold for emphasis):** > - **Program Memory Space:** **64 KB** (Address range **0000H to FFFFH**) > - **Internal Data Memory (Lower 128 bytes):** **128 bytes** (Address range **00H to 7FH**) > - **SFR Space (Upper 128 bytes):** **128 bytes** (Address range **80H to FFH**) > - **External Data Memory Capacity:** **64 KB** (Address range **0000H to FFFFH**) > - **Total Addressable Space (logical):** **192 KB** combined > - **Stack Size (default):** **128 bytes** (limited to internal RAM, can be relocated) # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 8051 Memory Architecture — Three Logical Spaces The 8051 microcontroller implements a **Modified Harvard Architecture**, which provides **three distinct logical memory spaces** accessible through different instruction sets: ### A. Program Memory (Code Space) - **Physical Media:** Internal ROM/EPROM/Flash + Optional External ROM (up to 64 KB). - **Access Mechanism:** Read-only during normal execution via the **PSEN̄ (Program Store Enable)** signal. - **Width of Bus:** 16-bit address bus → 64 KB capability; 8-bit data bus. - **Key Addresses (board exam favorites):** - **0000H** — Reset vector (CPU fetches first instruction from here on power-up). - **0003H** — External Interrupt 0 (INT0) ISR vector. - **000BH** — Timer 0 overflow ISR vector. - **0013H** — External Interrupt 1 (INT1) ISR vector. - **001BH** — Timer 1 overflow ISR vector. - **0023H** — Serial Port ISR vector. ### B. Internal Data Memory (Lower 128 Bytes: 00H–7FH) This 128-byte region is divided into **three functional sub-regions**: | Address Range | Region | Size | Purpose | |---------------|--------|------|---------| | **00H – 1FH** | Register Banks (4 banks × 8 regs) | 32 bytes | R0–R7 across 4 selectable banks | | **20H – 2FH** | Bit-Addressable Area | 16 bytes | 128 individually addressable bits (00H–7FH of bit space) | | **30H – 7FH** | General Purpose RAM | 80 bytes | Scratchpad variables and stack | > [!NOTE] > **Register Bank Selection Logic:** > Bits **RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)** of the **Program Status Word** select the active bank. > - **00** = Bank 0 (addresses 00H–07H) — *default after reset* > - **01** = Bank 1 (addresses 08H–0FH) > - **10** = Bank 2 (addresses 10H–17H) > - **11** = Bank 3 (addresses 18H–1FH) ### C. Special Function Register (SFR) Space (80H–FFH) The upper 128 bytes share addresses with internal RAM (when viewed from 16-bit address perspective), but are accessed using a **different instruction mode (direct addressing only)**. Notable SFRs include: | SFR | Address | Function | |-----|---------|----------| | **ACC** | **E0H** | Accumulator (bit-addressable) | | **B** | **F0H** | B register for MUL/DIV (bit-addressable) | | **PSW** | **D0H** | Program Status Word (bit-addressable) | | **SP** | **81H** | Stack Pointer | | **DPTR (DPL/DPH)** | **82H/83H** | Data Pointer (16-bit address) | | **P0–P3** | **80H, 90H, A0H, B0H** | Port latches (bit-addressable) | | **IE** | **A8H** | Interrupt Enable (bit-addressable) | | **IP** | **B8H** | Interrupt Priority (bit-addressable) | | **TCON** | **88H** | Timer/Counter Control (bit-addressable) | | **TMOD** | **89H** | Timer/Counter Mode | | **SCON** | **98H** | Serial Control (bit-addressable) | | **SBUF** | **99H** | Serial Data Buffer | | **PCON** | **87H** | Power Control | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Only addresses ending in 0H or 8H in the SFR space are bit-addressable** — this gives exactly **16 bit-addressable SFRs** with **128 individually addressable bits**. ## 2.2 Harvard vs Von Neumann — Architectural Comparison > [!NOTE] > **Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU Essays):** | Parameter | Harvard | Von Neumann | Modified Harvard (8051) | |-----------|---------|-------------|-------------------------| | Memory spaces | Separate code & data | Unified single memory | Separate internal; unified external via MOVX | | Address bus | Two (16-bit each) | One shared | Two (code via PC, data via DPTR/Ri) | | Data bus | Two 8-bit | One 8-bit shared | Two 8-bit (internal) | | Instruction fetch | Simultaneous with data | Sequential (bottleneck) | Simultaneous internally | | Speed | Faster | Slower (Von Neumann bottleneck) | Fast for internal code | | 8051 example | Yes (internal) | No | Yes (this is the 8051 model) | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > **Memory Address Calculation Cheat Sheet:** | Quantity | Formula | Unit | |----------|---------|------| | Total Program Memory Locations | $2^n$ where $n$ = address bus width | Bytes | | 8051 Program Memory | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | Bytes (64 KB) | | 8051 Internal RAM | $2^7 = 128$ | Bytes | | 8051 SFR Space | $2^7 = 128$ | Bytes | | Bit-Addressable Bits in 20H–2FH | $16 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits} = 128$ | Bits | | Bit-Addressable SFR Bits | $16 \text{ SFRs} \times 8 \text{ bits} = 128$ | Bits | | Total Bit-Addressable Bits | $128 + 128 = 256$ | Bits | | Total Register Banks | $4 \text{ banks} \times 8 \text{ registers}$ | R0–R7 | | Maximum Stack Depth (default) | $128 \text{ bytes}$ | Bytes | | External Data Memory Max | $2^{16} = 64 \text{ KB}$ | Bytes | ## 2.4 Stack Memory Organization The 8051 stack is **8-bit wide** and **grows upward** (toward higher addresses). Key facts: - **SP is reset to 07H**, meaning the first PUSH places data at address 08H. - Stack can be **relocated** by writing to SP (e.g., `MOV SP, #0x5F` moves it to 5FH). - Stack resides **only in internal RAM** — there is no external stack memory in 8051. - On **CALL/LCALL/ACALL/Interrupt**, the 8051 auto-pushes the 16-bit PC (2 bytes). - The stack is used implicitly by **CALL, RET, RETI, PUSH, POP** instructions. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Stack is **NOT** a separate memory — it is a logical use of internal RAM. Examiners specifically test this in 2-mark questions. ## 2.5 External Memory Interfacing — Signal Lines To expand the 8051 beyond its internal memory, three control signals govern access: | Signal | Function | Active Level | |--------|----------|--------------| | **PSEN̄** (Program Store Enable) | Activates external **ROM** read | **Low** | | **RD̄** (Read, P3.7) | Activates external **RAM** read | **Low** | | **WR̄** (Write, P3.6) | Activates external **RAM** write | **Low** | | **ALE** (Address Latch Enable) | Demultiplexes address/data bus (low byte latched by 74LS373) | Toggle | | **EĀ** (External Access) | If **low** → all code fetched externally; If **high** → internal first | Configuration | > [!NOTE] > **Accessing External Memory in 8051 Assembly:** > - Use `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` or `MOVC A, @A+PC` for **external code memory reads** (uses PSEN̄). > - Use `MOVX A, @DPTR`, `MOVX @DPTR, A`, `MOVX A, @Ri`, or `MOVX @Ri, A` for **external data memory** (uses RD̄/WR̄). > - This naming convention — `MOVC` (Code) vs `MOVX` (eXternal data) — is a fundamental distinction in 8051 assembly language and a high-frequency KTU question. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Memory organization concepts are foundational for: - **Bare-metal embedded firmware development** — placing code in ROM, variables in RAM, stack in safe regions. - **RTOS kernel design** — understanding memory maps for memory protection and context switching. - **IoT edge device programming** — optimizing memory footprint on resource-constrained MCUs. - **Automotive ECU design** — ISO 26262 mandates deterministic memory access, requiring strict understanding of Harvard separation. - **Bootloader development** — exploiting reset vector and code-space remapping. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Total Bit-Addressable Memory Calculation The 8051's bit-addressable capability is a signature feature. Let us derive the total number of addressable bits rigorously. ### Step 1 — Identify Bit-Addressable Internal RAM Bytes The internal RAM region from addresses 20H to 2FH is reserved as bit-addressable. $$ \text{Number of bit-addressable bytes} = 2FH - 20H + 1H $$ We need to convert these hexadecimal values to decimal first to perform the addition: $$ 2FH = (2 \times 16) + 15 = 32 + 15 = 47_{10} $$ $$ 20H = (2 \times 16) + 0 = 32_{10} $$ Now perform the calculation in decimal: $$ \text{Number of bytes} = 47 - 32 + 1 = 16 \text{ bytes} $$ ### Step 2 — Calculate Total Addressable Bits in 20H–2FH Since each byte contains 8 bits: $$ \text{Bits in 20H–2FH} = 16 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} = 128 \text{ bits} $$ ### Step 3 — Bit Address Space in 20H–2FH The 128 bits are addressed from bit address **00H to 7FH**: $$ \text{Bit address range} = 00H \text{ to } 7FH $$ For example, bit address 00H corresponds to byte 20H bit D0, and bit address 7FH corresponds to byte 2FH bit D7. ### Step 4 — Calculate Bit-Addressable SFR Bits In the SFR space, only SFRs whose addresses end in either 0H or 8H are bit-addressable. The qualifying addresses are: $$ 80H, \; 88H, \; 90H, \; 98H, \; A0H, \; A8H, \; B0H, \; B8H, \; C0H^{*}, \; C8H^{*}, \; D0H, \; D8H, \; E0H, \; E8H, \; F0H, \; F8H $$ This gives a count of: $$ \text{Bit-addressable SFRs} = 16 $$ (For standard 8051, only 12 are actually implemented; the C0H, C8H, D8H, E8H, F8H regions are reserved/unused but still follow the address rule.) ### Step 5 — Calculate Bit-Addressable SFR Bits (Implemented) $$ \text{Bits in 12 implemented SFRs} = 12 \times 8 = 96 \text{ bits} $$ ### Step 6 — Total Bit-Addressable Memory in 8051 $$ \text{Total bit-addressable bits} = 128 \; (\text{from 20H–2FH}) + 96 \; (\text{from implemented SFRs}) $$ $$ \text{Total bit-addressable bits} = 224 \text{ bits} $$ > [!NOTE] > The maximum theoretical bit-addressable space is 256 bits (128 + 128), but only 224 are physically implemented in the standard 8051. The bit-addressing range reaches up to **7FH** for internal RAM and up to **FFH** for SFR bits in the bit-address space, but the implemented upper region has gaps. ## 3.2 Derivation: Memory Address Decoding for External ROM/RAM Interfacing When interfacing external memory, the address space must be non-overlapping. Let us design a 32 KB external ROM and 32 KB external RAM system. ### Step 1 — Define Memory Map For non-overlapping 32 KB blocks: - **External ROM:** 32 KB occupying 8000H to FFFFH (upper half of 64 KB space). - **External RAM:** 32 KB occupying 0000H to 7FFFH (lower half of 64 KB space). ### Step 2 — Determine the Decoder Input Lines The upper address lines determine the chip select. For 32 KB blocks, we need a decoder that activates when the upper 1 bit of the 16-bit address is at a specific level: - 32 KB block = $2^{15}$ bytes → 1 address line needed for block selection (A15). ### Step 3 — Generate ROM Chip Select For ROM at 8000H to FFFFH, A15 = 1. We use an **inverter** to generate ROM chip select from A15: $$ \overline{CS_{ROM}} = \overline{A15} \cdot \overline{PSEN} $$ This means the ROM is selected when A15 is HIGH (1) **and** PSEN is LOW (read from program memory). ### Step 4 — Generate RAM Chip Select For RAM at 0000H to 7FFFH, A15 = 0. The RAM chip select is simply A15 directly (no inverter needed): $$ \overline{CS_{RAM}} = A15 \cdot \overline{RD} \cdot \overline{WR} $$ ### Step 5 — Verify Non-Overlap For ROM access (A15 = 1): $\overline{CS_{RAM}}$ is HIGH (inactive). ✓ For RAM access (A15 = 0): $\overline{CS_{ROM}}$ is HIGH (inactive). ✓ Therefore, no conflict exists. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Memory Access Instructions in 8051 > [!IMPORTANT] > **The four memory access instruction groups (CRITICAL for 14-mark questions):** | Instruction | Memory Accessed | Addressing Mode | Signal Activated | |-------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------| | `MOV A, Rr` | Internal RAM (Register) | Register | None (internal) | | `MOV A, direct` | Internal RAM (00H–7FH) and SFRs (80H–FFH) | Direct | None (internal) | | `MOV A, @R0` / `@R1` | Internal RAM (00H–FFH) | Indirect | None (internal) | | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | External Code Memory | Indexed | **PSEN̄** | | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | External Data Memory | Indirect (16-bit) | **RD̄** | | `MOVX A, @R0` / `@R1` | External Data Memory | Indirect (8-bit, page-based) | **RD̄** | > [!WARNING] > **Common student error:** Using `MOV` instead of `MOVX` for external data memory access. The CPU will only access internal RAM if `MOV` is used — the external bus will **NOT** be activated. Examiners will deduct marks for this in assembly coding questions. ## 3.4 Numerical Problem: Calculating Stack Operation Effects **Problem:** Initial SP = 07H. Execute the following sequence: 1. `PUSH ACC` (ACC = 55H) 2. `PUSH PSW` (PSW = C0H) 3. `POP B` 4. `POP 30H` Find the final SP value, the contents of B, 30H, and the stack layout. ### Solution **Step 1 — Initial State:** SP = 07H. Stack is empty. **Step 2 — Execute PUSH ACC:** - SP increments: $SP = 07H + 01H = 08H$ - ACC (55H) is written to address 08H. - After: SP = 08H, [08H] = 55H **Step 3 — Execute PUSH PSW:** - SP increments: $SP = 08H + 01H = 09H$ - PSW (C0H) is written to address 09H. - After: SP = 09H, [09H] = C0H **Step 4 — Execute POP B:** - B receives content at SP: B = [09H] = C0H - SP decrements: $SP = 09H - 01H = 08H$ - After: SP = 08H, B = C0H, [09H] still = C0H (data not erased, only SP moved) **Step 5 — Execute POP 30H:** - 30H receives content at SP: [30H] = [08H] = 55H - SP decrements: $SP = 08H - 01H = 07H$ - After: SP = 07H, [30H] = 55H **Final State:** SP = 07H, B = C0H, [30H] = 55H, [08H] = 55H, [09H] = C0H. > [!NOTE] > **POP does NOT erase memory content** — it only moves the SP back. This is a frequently tested KTU concept worth 2–3 marks. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 8051 Memory Organization — Top-Level Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU[CPU Registers: A, B, SP, DPTR, PC] subgraph CODE["PROGRAM MEMORY (Code Space)"] ROM_INTERNAL["Internal ROM/Flash
4 KB
Addresses 0000H-0FFFH"] ROM_EXTERNAL["External ROM
Up to 60 KB
Addresses 1000H-FFFFH"] end subgraph DATA["DATA MEMORY SPACE"] subgraph LOWER["Lower 128 bytes (00H-7FH)"] BANK0["Bank 0: 00H-07H"] BANK1["Bank 1: 08H-0FH"] BANK2["Bank 2: 10H-17H"] BANK3["Bank 3: 18H-1FH"] BIT_AREA["Bit-Addressable: 20H-2FH
128 bits"] SCRATCH["Scratchpad RAM: 30H-7FH
80 bytes + Stack"] end subgraph UPPER["SFR Space (80H-FFH)"] SFR_BLOCK["SFRs: P0-P3, ACC, B, PSW,
SP, DPTR, TCON, TMOD,
SCON, IE, IP, PCON, etc."] end end EXT_RAM["External Data RAM
64 KB
0000H-FFFFH (via MOVX)"] CPU <--> CODE CPU <--> DATA CPU <--> EXT_RAM ``` ## 4.2 8051 Internal RAM Layout — Detailed Memory Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INT_RAM["INTERNAL RAM - 128 bytes + 128 bytes SFR"] ADDR_00["00H - 07H : Register Bank 0 (R0-R7)"] ADDR_08["08H - 0FH : Register Bank 1 (R0-R7)"] ADDR_10["10H - 17H : Register Bank 2 (R0-R7)"] ADDR_18["18H - 1FH : Register Bank 3 (R0-R7)"] ADDR_20["20H - 2FH : Bit-Addressable Area
16 bytes = 128 bits (00H-7FH bit addresses)"] ADDR_30["30H - 7FH : General Purpose RAM
80 bytes (Stack also resides here)"] end subgraph SFR_TOP["SFR SPACE - 80H-FFH"] SFR_80["80H P0 (bit-add) | 81H SP | 82H DPL | 83H DPH"] SFR_88["88H TCON | 89H TMOD | 8AH TL0 | 8BH TL1 | 8CH TH0 | 8DH TH1"] SFR_90["90H P1 (bit-add) | 98H SCON | 99H SBUF"] SFR_A0["A0H P2 (bit-add) | A8H IE | B0H P3 (bit-add) | B8H IP"] SFR_C0["C0H - FFH : Reserved/Extended SFRs"] SFR_E0["E0H ACC | F0H B | D0H PSW"] end INT_RAM --> SFR_TOP ``` ## 4.3 External Memory Interfacing Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR MPU["8051 Microcontroller"] ADDR_BUS["16-bit Address Bus
A0-A15"] DATA_BUS["8-bit Data Bus
P0.0-P0.7"] ALE["ALE Signal"] LATCH["74LS373
Address Latch"] PSEN["PSEN-bar Signal"] RD["RD-bar (P3.7)"] WR["WR-bar (P3.6)"] EXT_ROM["External ROM
(Program Memory)"] EXT_RAM["External RAM
(Data Memory)"] MPU --> ADDR_BUS MPU --> DATA_BUS MPU --> ALE ALE --> LATCH ADDR_BUS --> LATCH LATCH -->|"Low Byte A0-A7"| EXT_ROM LATCH -->|"Low Byte A0-A7"| EXT_RAM ADDR_BUS -->|"High Byte A8-A15"| EXT_ROM ADDR_BUS -->|"High Byte A8-A15"| EXT_RAM MPU --> PSEN PSEN -->|"ROM Read Enable"| EXT_ROM MPU --> RD RD -->|"RAM Read"| EXT_RAM MPU --> WR WR -->|"RAM Write"| EXT_RAM EXT_ROM --> DATA_BUS EXT_RAM --> DATA_BUS ``` ## 4.4 Stack Operation Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB INIT["Initialize: MOV SP, #5FH
SP = 5FH"] PUSH1["PUSH ACC
SP = 60H, Mem[60H] = ACC"] PUSH2["PUSH PSW
SP = 61H, Mem[61H] = PSW"] CALL["LCALL SUB_ROUTINE
SP = 63H, Mem[62H-63H] = PC (2 bytes)"] ROUTINE["Execute Subroutine"] POP_RET["RET Instruction
SP = 61H, PC restored from stack"] RESULT["Final SP = 5FH (returned to original)"] INIT --> PUSH1 PUSH1 --> PUSH2 PUSH2 --> CALL CALL --> ROUTINE ROUTINE --> POP_RET POP_RET --> RESULT ``` ## 4.5 Register Bank Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TB PSW_REG["PSW Register (D0H)"] RS1["RS1 (PSW.4)"] RS0["RS0 (PSW.3)"] SEL_DEC["Bank Selection Decoder"] BANK0["Bank 0: 00H-07H
RS1=0, RS0=0
(Default after reset)"] BANK1["Bank 1: 08H-0FH
RS1=0, RS0=1"] BANK2["Bank 2: 10H-17H
RS1=1, RS0=0"] BANK3["Bank 3: 18H-1FH
RS1=1, RS0=1"] PSW_REG --> RS1 PSW_REG --> RS0 RS1 --> SEL_DEC RS0 --> SEL_DEC SEL_DEC --> BANK0 SEL_DEC --> BANK1 SEL_DEC --> BANK2 SEL_DEC --> BANK3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Modified Harvard Architecture as applied to the 8051 microcontroller. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > The **Modified Harvard Architecture** is a memory organization scheme in which the microcontroller maintains **physically separate program and data memory spaces** with **independent address and data buses** for internal access (achieving simultaneous instruction fetch and data read/write — the Harvard advantage), while providing a **limited mechanism to access external data memory** using the same external bus. In the 8051: > - **Program Memory** uses the **16-bit PC** as the address register and is accessed via the **PSEN̄** signal. > - **Data Memory** uses **DPTR** or **R0/R1** as address registers and is accessed via **RD̄/WR̄** signals. > - The "modification" lies in allowing **MOVX instructions** to access external RAM, breaking pure Harvard separation for external expansion. > - This design provides **fast instruction execution** (1-machine-cycle dual fetch) while supporting **large external data storage** when needed. ### Question 2: List the four register banks of the 8051 and explain how they are selected. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** > [!TIP] > The 8051 has **4 register banks**, each containing **8 registers (R0–R7)**, occupying 32 bytes of internal RAM (addresses 00H–1FH). > - **Bank 0:** 00H–07H > - **Bank 1:** 08H–0FH > - **Bank 2:** 10H–17H > - **Bank 3:** 18H–1FH > > **Selection Method:** The active bank is determined by bits **RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)** of the **Program Status Word (PSW, address D0H)**: > - RS1=0, RS0=0 → Bank 0 (default after reset) > - RS1=0, RS0=1 → Bank 1 > - RS1=1, RS0=0 → Bank 2 > - RS1=1, RS0=1 → Bank 3 > > The instruction `SETB RS1` or `CLR RS0` style bit-manipulation instructions can switch the active bank. This feature facilitates **fast context switching** in interrupt service routines. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Draw and explain the internal RAM organization of the 8051 microcontroller with a neat memory map. Discuss the bit-addressable region and its significance. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the interfacing of external program memory (ROM) and external data memory (RAM) to the 8051. Mention the role of PSEN̄, ALE, and EĀ signals. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question A(a): **Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):** The 8051 internal RAM is 128 bytes (addresses 00H–7FH) plus 128 bytes of SFR space (80H–FFH), totaling 256 bytes of directly addressable memory. **Step 2 — Register Bank Region (2 marks):** The first 32 bytes (00H–1FH) are organized as 4 register banks of 8 registers each. Only one bank is active at a time, selected by RS0 and RS1 bits in PSW. **Step 3 — Bit-Addressable Region (2 marks):** The 16 bytes from 20H–2FH form the bit-addressable area, providing 128 individually addressable bits (bit addresses 00H–7FH). Significance: - Allows single-bit manipulation (SETB, CLR, ANL, ORL) without disturbing other bits in the byte. - Ideal for **flag bits, control signals, and boolean variables** in control applications. **Step 4 — General Purpose RAM (1 mark):** Bytes 30H–7FH (80 bytes) are used for variable storage, scratchpad operations, and **stack memory**. **Step 5 — SFR Space (1 mark):** The upper 128 bytes (80H–FFH) house the Special Function Registers, including port latches, timer registers, serial port, interrupt control, and accumulator. Addresses ending in 0H or 8H (16 SFRs) are bit-addressable. #### Model Solution for Question A(b): **Step 1 — System Block Diagram (3 marks):** The 8051's P0 port serves as the **multiplexed Address/Data bus (AD0–AD7)**. The P2 port provides the **upper address byte (A8–A15)**. **Step 2 — Role of ALE (1 mark):** The **Address Latch Enable (ALE)** signal is used to latch the lower address byte from P0 into an external latch (74LS373). A high-to-low transition of ALE causes the lower address to be captured and held on the address bus for the duration of the memory access. **Step 3 — Role of PSEN̄ (1.5 marks):** The **Program Store Enable (PSEN̄)** signal activates an **external ROM** when the CPU fetches instructions using the `MOVC` instruction. It is connected to the **Output Enable (OĒ)** input of the EPROM. PSEN̄ is **active LOW** and is generated only for external code fetches — not for data access. **Step 4 — Role of EĀ (1.5 marks):** The **External Access (EĀ)** pin configures the program memory source: - **EĀ = 1 (HIGH):** CPU first fetches from internal ROM (0000H–0FFFH for 4 KB 8051); then from external ROM above that range. - **EĀ = 0 (LOW):** All instruction fetches go to external ROM (used for 8031, which has no internal ROM). **Step 5 — Data Memory Interfacing Distinction (1 mark):** External RAM uses **RD̄ (P3.7)** for reads and **WR̄ (P3.6)** for writes, NOT PSEN̄. This separation prevents code and data collisions. The 8051 instructions `MOVX` activate these signals. --- ### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Compare Harvard and Von Neumann architectures with suitable diagrams. Highlight the architectural advantages of the 8051's memory organization. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the stack memory organization in 8051. Describe PUSH and POP operations with a numerical example showing the stack pointer movement. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Question B(a): **Step 1 — Harvard Architecture Definition (1 mark):** In **Harvard Architecture**, the program memory and data memory have **physically separate buses** — separate address buses, separate data buses, and separate control signals. This allows **simultaneous** instruction fetch and data access in a single clock cycle. **Step 2 — Von Neumann Architecture Definition (1 mark):** In **Von Neumann Architecture**, a **single memory space** holds both program and data, accessed via a **shared bus**. The CPU must fetch an instruction first, then access data, leading to the **Von Neumann bottleneck** in single-bus implementations. **Step 3 — Comparison Table (3 marks):** | Feature | Harvard | Von Neumann | |---------|---------|-------------| | Memory | Separate code & data | Unified single memory | | Buses | Two independent sets | One shared bus | | Speed | Faster (parallel access) | Slower (sequential) | | Cost | Higher (more pins) | Lower | | Flexibility | Lower (code separate) | Higher (code/data interchangeable) | **Step 4 — 8051 Architectural Advantages (2 marks):** - **Modified Harvard** combines Harvard's speed with Von Neumann's flexibility. - **Simultaneous internal fetch** via dual bus increases throughput. - **External MOVX instructions** allow large data memory access without affecting code execution speed. - **Reduced program/data interference** improves reliability in real-time systems. #### Model Solution for Question B(b): **Step 1 — Stack Definition (1 mark):** The **stack** is a LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) data structure in 8051 internal RAM used for **temporary storage of return addresses, intermediate data, and context preservation** during subroutine calls and interrupts. **Step 2 — Stack Pointer (1 mark):** The **SP (Stack Pointer)** is an **8-bit SFR at address 81H** that holds the address of the **top of the stack**. After reset, **SP = 07H**. **Step 3 — PUSH Operation (2 marks):** The `PUSH direct` instruction: 1. Increments SP by 1: $SP \leftarrow SP + 1$ 2. Copies the content of the direct address to the memory location pointed to by SP. **Step 4 — POP Operation (2 marks):** The `POP direct` instruction: 1. Copies content of memory at SP to the direct address. 2. Decrements SP by 1: $SP \leftarrow SP - 1$ **Step 5 — Numerical Example (1 mark):** If SP = 07H initially, and we execute `PUSH ACC` (ACC = 0A5H): - After execution: SP = 08H, Memory[08H] = 0A5H - On `POP B`: B = 0A5H, SP returns to 07H > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse PUSH/POP order:** PUSH increments SP FIRST, then stores. POP reads FIRST, then decrements SP. Reversing this loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Stack direction:** 8051 stack grows UPWARD (toward higher addresses), unlike many other architectures that grow downward. Examiners specifically test this misconception. > 3. **Stack limit:** Stack is limited to internal RAM (max 128 bytes). Attempting to overflow into SFR space will corrupt port latches and SFRs — a critical failure mode in real systems. > 4. **Register bank conflicts:** Stack starting at SP=07H overlaps Bank 1 (08H–0FH). Always relocate SP to 30H or higher in production code. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 High-Density Revision Checklist for Memory Organization:** ### 🔸 Architecture Fundamentals - 8051 uses **Modified Harvard Architecture** with separate code (16-bit PC addressing) and data (16-bit DPTR or 8-bit @R0/@R1 addressing) spaces. - **Program Memory** = 64 KB max (0000H–FFFFH); accessed via **MOVC** and **PSEN̄**. - **External Data Memory** = 64 KB max (0000H–FFFFH); accessed via **MOVX**, **RD̄**, **WR̄**. - **Internal Data Memory** = 128 bytes (00H–7FH) + 128 bytes SFR (80H–FFH). ### 🔸 Internal RAM Structure - **Register Banks (00H–1FH):** 4 banks × 8 registers; selected via PSW.3 (RS0) and PSW.4 (RS1). - **Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH):** 16 bytes → 128 individually addressable bits (00H–7FH). - **General Purpose RAM (30H–7FH):** 80 bytes for variables and stack. ### 🔸 SFR Space (80H–FFH) - 128-byte region, but only ~21 are implemented in standard 8051. - **16 SFRs** with addresses ending in 0H or 8H are bit-addressable. - Key SFRs: ACC (E0H), B (F0H), PSW (D0H), SP (81H), DPTR (82H/83H), P0–P3 (80H, 90H, A0H, B0H). ### 🔸 Reset and Interrupt Vectors - **0000H** → Reset (start of execution) - **0003H** → External Interrupt 0 (INT0) - **000BH** → Timer 0 Overflow - **0013H** → External Interrupt 1 (INT1) - **001BH** → Timer 1 Overflow - **0023H** → Serial Port Interrupt ### 🔸 Stack Behavior - **Stack Pointer (SP)** is 8-bit, reset to 07H. - Stack grows **UPWARD** (SP increments on PUSH, decrements on POP). - Stack resides **only in internal RAM**. - **POP does NOT clear memory** — only SP moves. - Best practice: Relocate SP to **0x5F or 0x60** at the start of every program. ### 🔸 Memory Access Instructions (Memorize) - `MOV` — Internal RAM and SFRs. - `MOVC` (with @A+DPTR or @A+PC) — External **C**ode memory. - `MOVX` (with @DPTR or @R0/R1) — External data memory (X for eXternal). - **Confusing MOVC with MOVX** is the #1 cause of assembly code bugs. ### 🔸 External Memory Signals - **PSEN̄** — Activates external **ROM** (code only). - **RD̄** (P3.7) — Activates external **RAM** read. - **WR̄** (P3.6) — Activates external **RAM** write. - **ALE** — Latches low-byte address from P0. - **EĀ** — Selects internal (HIGH) or external (LOW) program source. ### 🔸 Numerical Constants (Bold for Memory) - **64 KB** program memory, **64 KB** external data, **128 bytes** internal RAM, **128 bytes** SFR. - **128 bits** bit-addressable in 20H–2FH; **96 bits** in implemented bit-addressable SFRs. - **4 register banks** × **8 registers** = **32 bytes** R0–R7 storage. - **8 reset/interrupt vectors** at fixed code addresses (0000H–0023H). ### 🔸 Comparison Phrases for Essay Questions - "Harvard vs Von Neumann: speed vs flexibility" - "Internal vs External memory: bus separation for performance" - "SFR vs General Purpose RAM: control vs data" - "Stack vs Register Bank: dynamic vs static storage" - "MOVC vs MOVX: code-fetch vs data-fetch instruction differentiation" > [!TIP] > **🎯 Final Board Exam Tip:** When answering "Explain memory organization of 8051" questions, always structure your answer in this exact order for maximum marks: **(1) Define Modified Harvard, (2) Sketch the program memory map with vector addresses, (3) Sketch the internal RAM map with all four regions, (4) List key SFRs and their addresses, (5) Explain external memory interface with signals, (6) Brief note on stack and register banks.** This 6-step structure guarantees full marks alignment with KTU 2024 scheme marking schemes.

topics.title: Core and
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCBMT402 - MICROCONTROLLERS AND INTERFACING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Core and

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PCBMT402 – Microcontrollers and Interfacing (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 3)**, the topic **"Core and Programming Model"** of a microcontroller refers to the **central processing unit (CPU) architecture**, its **register set**, **operating modes**, **memory model**, and the **programmer-visible abstraction** that defines how software interacts with the underlying hardware.

For the **ARM Cortex-M processor family** (the industry-standard 32-bit RISC core used in modern microcontrollers like STM32, LPC1768, Tiva C, etc., and adopted in the KTU 2024 PCBMT402 syllabus), the **Core** represents the **ARMv7-M / ARMv6-M architecture** implementation. The **Programming Model** defines the complete set of registers, privilege levels, stack pointer options, and special-purpose hardware elements that a programmer must understand to write efficient firmware.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme)**
> The Core and Programming Model is the **foundational concept** on which all subsequent topics in Module 3 (interrupts, exceptions, NVIC, memory map, peripheral interfacing) depend. Questions worth **7 to 14 marks** appear almost every KTU examination cycle on this topic.

The **Core and Programming Model** encompasses:

1. **The Register Set** – including general-purpose registers, special-purpose registers, and the Program Status Register (PSR family).
2. **Operating Modes** – Thread Mode and Handler Mode.
3. **Privilege Levels** – Privileged and Unprivileged access.
4. **Stack Pointer Selection** – MSP (Main Stack Pointer) and PSP (Process Stack Pointer).
5. **Special Registers** – CONTROL, PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI.
6. **Memory Map Architecture** – predefined 4 GB address space.
7. **Bit-banding Mechanism** – atomic bit manipulation in peripheral and SRAM regions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Brain of the MCU

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Brain in an Office**

Imagine a microcontroller as a **smart office building**:
- The **Core** is the **CEO's brain** — it makes all the critical decisions and executes the commands.
- The **Register Set** is the **CEO's desk drawers** — small, ultra-fast storage spots where the CEO keeps the most important papers (data) needed *right now*.
- The **Stack Pointers (MSP/PSP)** are like **two in-trays** on the desk — one for routine paperwork (MSP) and one for special delegate work (PSP).
- The **Operating Modes (Thread/Handler)** are like **work shifts** — Thread mode is normal office hours, Handler mode is the emergency fire-alert response.
- The **Memory Map** is the **building's directory** — telling where the CEO can find every office (peripheral, SRAM, code, etc.).
- **Privilege Levels** are like **security clearances** — some staff can enter the server room (privileged), others cannot (unprivileged).

This analogy makes it intuitive: just as the CEO needs clear rules about *what* to do, *how* to access information, and *when* to delegate, the ARM Cortex-M Core has a **well-defined programming model** that programmers must respect.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **mandatory knowledge** for KTU examinations:

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Register Width | **32 bits** | All core registers are 32-bit wide |
| Address Bus Width | **32 bits** | Allows addressing up to $2^{32}$ locations |
| Total Addressable Memory | **4 GB** ($\text{4,294,967,296}$ bytes) | Linear memory map |
| Number of General Purpose Registers | **13** (R0–R12) | Plus 3 special-purpose (R13/SP, R14/LR, R15/PC) |
| Default Bit-band Region Size | **1 MB** | Maps to 32 MB alias region |
| Stack Alignment | **8-byte** (double-word) | Mandatory SP alignment on exception entry |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic to Remember Registers**: **"R0 to R12 are free workers, R13 holds plates (Stack), R14 holds return addresses (Link), R15 points to next instruction (Program Counter)"**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ARM Cortex-M Core — Architectural Breakdown

The **ARM Cortex-M core** is a **32-bit RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** processor designed specifically for **embedded microcontroller applications**. Unlike application processors (Cortex-A), the Cortex-M core is optimized for:
- Deterministic interrupt response
- Low power consumption
- Small silicon footprint
- High code density (Thumb-2 instruction set)

### 2.1.1 Core Architecture Pillars

1. **Harvard Bus Architecture** (in most implementations)
   - Separate instruction and data buses for **simultaneous fetch and read/write**.
   - Reduces the **Von-Neumann bottleneck**.

2. **Thumb-2 Instruction Set**
   - Mixes 16-bit and 32-bit instructions.
   - Provides **best of both worlds**: code density of Thumb, performance of ARM.

3. **Integrated NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller)**
   - Tightly coupled to the core.
   - Provides **hardware stacking** and **tail-chaining** for low-latency interrupts.

4. **Bit-Banding**
   - Allows **atomic** (single-instruction) bit manipulation.
   - Critical for hardware register access.

## 2.2 The Programming Model — Structured Analysis

The programming model of ARM Cortex-M consists of the following **programmer-visible elements**:

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

- **Step 1: General-Purpose Registers (R0–R12)**
  - 13 registers of 32-bit width.
  - Used for **data manipulation, address storage, and intermediate results**.
  - R0–R7 are **low registers** (accessible by all instructions).
  - R8–R12 are **high registers** (accessible only by 32-bit instructions in some implementations).

- **Step 2: Stack Pointer (R13 / SP)**
  - Two physical stack pointers exist: **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)** and **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)**.
  - **MSP** is the default stack used in **Handler Mode** and is always used in **Thread Mode on reset**.
  - **PSP** can be used in **Thread Mode** for user (unprivileged) application tasks — useful in **RTOS environments**.

- **Step 3: Link Register (R14 / LR)**
  - Stores the **return address** when a function call (`BL`, `BLX`) or exception entry occurs.
  - On exception entry, LR is loaded with a special value called **EXC_RETURN**, which tells the core *how* to return.

- **Step 4: Program Counter (R15 / PC)**
  - Always points to the **address of the next instruction to be executed** (+2 or +4 bytes depending on instruction size).

- **Step 5: Program Status Register (PSR) Family**
  - The PSR is **not a single register** but a **combination of three sub-registers**:
    - **APSR** (Application PSR) — flags from ALU operations: N, Z, C, V, Q.
    - **IPSR** (Interrupt PSR) — contains the number of the current exception (0 = Thread mode).
    - **EPSR** (Execution PSR) — contains the **T-bit** (Thumb state) and ICI/IT bits.

- **Step 6: PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI Registers**
  - These are **exception mask registers** for critical sections:
    - **PRIMASK** = 1 → blocks all exceptions except **NMI** and **HardFault**.
    - **FAULTMASK** = 1 → blocks all exceptions except **NMI**.
    - **BASEPRI** = $N$ → blocks all exceptions with priority $\geq N$.

- **Step 7: CONTROL Register**
  - A **special 8-bit register** (only bits 0, 1, 2 are defined) that controls:
    - **Bit 0 (nPRIV)**: Defines privilege level in Thread mode (0 = Privileged, 1 = Unprivileged).
    - **Bit 1 (SPSEL)**: Selects active stack pointer (0 = MSP, 1 = PSP).
    - **Bit 2 (FPCA)**: Indicates whether FP extension context is active (on Cortex-M4F with FPU).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Boundary Condition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addressable Memory | $M_{max} = 2^{32}$ | 32-bit address bus | Bytes (4 GB) |
| Bit-band Alias Address | $A_{alias} = A_{base} + 0x02000000 + 8 \cdot (A_{bitword} - A_{region})$ | $A_{region} \in [0x20000000, 0x200FFFFF]$ or $[0x40000000, 0x400FFFFF]$ | Bytes (32 MB alias) |
| Bit-band Bit Address | $A_{bit} = A_{bitword} - A_{region}$ | $0 \le A_{bit} \le 31$ | Bit index |
| Stack Alignment | $SP \equiv 0 \pmod 8$ | Required on exception entry | Bytes |
| EXC_RETURN Value (Handler→MSP) | $0xFFFFFFF1$ | Standard return | Hexadecimal |
| EXC_RETURN Value (Handler→PSP) | $0xFFFFFFFD$ | FPU not active | Hexadecimal |
| Exception Priority Encoding | $P_{effective} = P_{value} \ll$ (8 - $N_{bits}$) | Cortex-M3/M4: 3–8 bits | Implementation-defined |
| Number of Bit-band Bits per Alias Word | $\vert A_{bit} \vert_{max} = 31$ | 32 bits per 32-bit word | Bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip**: Always express the memory address in **hexadecimal** notation (e.g., `0x20000000`) in your answers, not decimal. The KTU 2024 marking scheme deducts marks for using decimal addresses.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **Core and Programming Model** is **not just an academic concept** — it is the foundation of every embedded system you will ever build:

- **Bare-Metal Firmware Development**: Understanding the register set is essential for writing **CMSIS (Cortex Microcontroller Software Interface Standard)** compliant code.
- **RTOS Design**: The dual stack pointer (MSP/PSP) enables **secure kernel-user separation** in real-time operating systems like FreeRTOS.
- **Debugging**: Knowledge of EPSR's T-bit helps debug **HardFaults** caused by accidental ARM-state jumps.
- **Power Optimization**: Setting PRIMASK during critical timing loops prevents interrupt jitter.
- **Safety-Critical Systems**: BASEPRI allows selective masking of interrupts based on priority — essential in **automotive (ISO 26262)** and **medical (IEC 62304)** applications.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Bit-Band Alias Address Calculation

The bit-band mechanism allows atomic access to a single bit in the **bit-band region** by writing to a corresponding **alias region** address. The relationship between the alias address and the actual bit-band address is given by:

$$
A_{alias} = A_{region\_base} + 0x02000000 + 8 \times (A_{bitword} - A_{region\_base})
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the bit-band region base address.**
For the **SRAM bit-band region** in a typical Cortex-M3/M4 MCU:
$$
A_{region\_base} = 0x20000000
$$
For the **Peripheral bit-band region**:
$$
A_{region\_base} = 0x40000000
$$

**Step 2: Identify the target bit-band word address.**
Suppose we want to access **bit 5** of the word located at address `0x20000100`. Then:
$$
A_{bitword} = 0x20000100
$$

**Step 3: Compute the offset of the bit-band word from the region base.**
$$
\Delta A_{word} = A_{bitword} - A_{region\_base}
$$

$$
\Delta A_{word} = 0x20000100 - 0x20000000
$$

$$
\Delta A_{word} = 0x00000100
$$

**Step 4: Multiply the word offset by 8 (since each 32-bit word is expanded to 8 addressable bytes in the alias region).**
$$
8 \times \Delta A_{word} = 8 \times 0x00000100
$$

$$
8 \times 0x00000100 = 0x00000800
$$

**Step 5: Add the bit offset (in bits) to the scaled word offset to get the byte offset within the alias region.**
The bit index is 5, so:
$$
A_{bit} = 5
$$

$$
A_{byte\_offset} = 8 \times \Delta A_{word} + A_{bit}
$$

$$
A_{byte\_offset} = 0x00000800 + 5
$$

$$
A_{byte\_offset} = 0x00000805
$$

**Step 6: Add the alias region base offset `0x02000000` and the bit-band region base.**
$$
A_{alias} = A_{region\_base} + 0x02000000 + A_{byte\_offset}
$$

$$
A_{alias} = 0x20000000 + 0x02000000 + 0x00000805
$$

$$
A_{alias} = 0x22000805
$$

**Final Result:**
$$
A_{alias} = 0x22000805
$$

Writing a `1` to address `0x22000805` will atomically set bit 5 of the word at `0x20000100`. Writing a `0` will atomically clear it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification with 1 MB region constraint**: The SRAM bit-band region spans `0x20000000` to `0x200FFFFF` (1 MB). The alias region spans `0x22000000` to `0x23FFFFFF` (32 MB). The ratio $32/1 = 32 = 2^5$ bits per word is consistent with 32-bit word size.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Total Number of Bit-Band Aliases

The total number of addressable bit-band aliases in the SRAM bit-band region is:

$$
N_{aliases} = \frac{\text{Size of bit-band region (in bytes)}}{\text{Size of one 32-bit word (in bytes)}} \times 32
$$

**Step 1: Identify the size of the bit-band region.**
$$
\text{Size}_{region} = 1 \, \text{MB} = 2^{20} \, \text{bytes}
$$

**Step 2: Number of 32-bit words in the region.**
$$
N_{words} = \frac{2^{20}}{4} = 2^{18} = 262144
$$

**Step 3: Number of bit-band aliases (one per bit per word).**
$$
N_{aliases} = N_{words} \times 32 = 2^{18} \times 2^5 = 2^{23}
$$

$$
N_{aliases} = 8388608 \approx 8.39 \, \text{million bits}
$$

**Step 4: Size of the alias region in bytes.**
$$
\text{Size}_{alias} = N_{aliases} \times 1 = 2^{23} \, \text{bytes} = 8 \, \text{MB}
$$

Wait — but the alias region is actually **32 MB**, not 8 MB. Let us correct this.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction**: The alias region has **32 MB** of address space because it contains **byte-addressable** aliases. Each 32-bit word is expanded to **32 byte-addressable locations** (not 1). So:
> $$\text{Size}_{alias} = N_{words} \times 32 \, \text{bytes} = 2^{18} \times 32 = 2^{18} \times 2^5 = 2^{23} \, \text{bytes} = 8 \, \text{MB}$$

Actually the bit-band alias region in ARM Cortex-M3 is **32 MB** for the **1 MB** bit-band region, because each bit is mapped to a **full 32-bit word** in the alias region (not 1 byte). Let me re-derive correctly:

**Corrected Derivation**:

Each bit in the bit-band region maps to a 32-bit word in the alias region. So:

$$
\text{Size}_{alias} = N_{words} \times 32 \times 4 \, \text{bytes} = 2^{18} \times 32 \times 4 = 2^{18} \times 2^7 = 2^{25} = 32 \, \text{MB}
$$

This is the **correct** derivation showing the 32 MB alias region.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Stack Memory Sizing for Nested Interrupts

To safely handle $N$ nested interrupts each requiring a maximum of $S$ bytes of stack, plus the main program requiring $M$ bytes:

$$
\text{Total Stack Size} \geq M + N \times S + \text{Margin}
$$

**Example**: For an application with $M = 256$ bytes main stack, $N = 8$ nested interrupts, each requiring $S = 64$ bytes (32-byte hardware stacking + 32-byte user context):

$$
\text{Stack} \geq 256 + 8 \times 64 + 64 \text{ (margin)}
$$

$$
\text{Stack} \geq 256 + 512 + 64
$$

$$
\text{Stack} \geq 832 \, \text{bytes}
$$

Recommended allocation: **1 KB** stack.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: MSP vs PSP

| Feature | MSP (Main Stack Pointer) | PSP (Process Stack Pointer) |
|---|---|---|
| **Used in Mode** | Thread Mode (default) & Handler Mode | Thread Mode (optional) |
| **Privilege** | Always accessible in Privileged mode | Used by both privileged and unprivileged |
| **Reset Value** | Loaded from vector table on reset | Undefined at reset |
| **Typical Use Case** | Kernel stack, interrupt stack, exception stack | User task stack in RTOS |
| **Selected by** | CONTROL bit 1 = 0 | CONTROL bit 1 = 1 |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | Medium |

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Cortex-M3 vs Cortex-M4

| Feature | Cortex-M3 | Cortex-M4 |
|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | ARMv7-M | ARMv7-M + ARMv7E-M |
| **DSP Extensions** | No | Yes (MAC, SIMD instructions) |
| **FPU** | No | Optional (Cortex-M4F variant) |
| **Instruction Set** | Thumb-2 | Thumb-2 + DSP extensions |
| **Cycle Count (typical)** | ~1.25 DMIPS/MHz | ~1.25 DMIPS/MHz |
| **Interrupt Latency** | 12 cycles | 12 cycles |
| **Bit-band** | Yes | Yes (most implementations) |

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram: Interrupt Stacking and EXC_RETURN

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application Code
    participant Core as Cortex-M Core
    participant Stack as Main Stack
    participant ISR as Interrupt Service Routine
    App->>Core: Execute instruction
    Core->>Core: Interrupt request received
    Core->>Stack: Push R0-R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR
    Core->>Core: LR = EXC_RETURN
    Core->>ISR: Jump to ISR vector
    ISR->>ISR: Execute handler code
    ISR->>Core: Return instruction
    Core->>Core: Read LR = EXC_RETURN
    Core->>Stack: Pop R0-R3, R12, LR, PC, xPSR
    Core->>App: Resume application
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define the ARM Cortex-M Programming Model. List the special-purpose registers available in it.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **ARM Cortex-M Programming Model** is the programmer-visible abstraction of the 32-bit RISC core that defines the register set, operating modes, privilege levels, and stack pointer selection mechanism used to write firmware for Cortex-M based microcontrollers.

The special-purpose registers are:
1. **R13 (SP)** — Stack Pointer (with MSP and PSP physical instances).
2. **R14 (LR)** — Link Register (stores return address or EXC_RETURN).
3. **R15 (PC)** — Program Counter.
4. **PSR family** — APSR, IPSR, EPSR.
5. **PRIMASK, FAULTMASK, BASEPRI** — Exception mask registers.
6. **CONTROL** — Controls privilege and stack pointer selection.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing registers)*

### Question 2
**Q: What is the bit-banding feature in ARM Cortex-M? Mention the two bit-band regions.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Bit-banding** is a memory-mapping feature in ARM Cortex-M3/M4 cores that allows **atomic** (single-instruction) read-modify-write operations on individual bits of memory-mapped peripheral and SRAM regions. It maps each bit in a 1 MB **bit-band region** to a full 32-bit word in a 32 MB **alias region**, enabling individual bit access without read-modify-write sequences.

The two bit-band regions are:
1. **SRAM bit-band region**: `0x20000000` to `0x200FFFFF` (1 MB) → Alias at `0x22000000` to `0x23FFFFFF` (32 MB).
2. **Peripheral bit-band region**: `0x40000000` to `0x400FFFFF` (1 MB) → Alias at `0x42000000` to `0x43FFFFFF` (32 MB).

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for regions)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**Q: (a)** Explain the complete ARM Cortex-M Programming Model with neat diagrams. Discuss the role of MSP and PSP in detail. **(7 Marks)**
**Q: (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the bit-banding mechanism in ARM Cortex-M3. Compute the alias address for setting bit 7 of the word at address `0x40005300`. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Model Solution for (a):

The **ARM Cortex-M Programming Model** consists of the following elements:

**1. General-Purpose Registers (R0–R12):**
- 13 registers of 32-bit width.
- R0–R7: Low registers, accessible by all 16-bit and 32-bit instructions.
- R8–R12: High registers, accessible only by 32-bit instructions.
- Used for data manipulation, address storage, and intermediate results.

**2. Stack Pointer (R13 / SP):**
- Banked into two physical registers: **MSP** and **PSP**.
- **MSP (Main Stack Pointer)**: Default stack pointer after reset. Used in Handler Mode and in Thread Mode for kernel/exception stacking. Always used in Handler Mode.
- **PSP (Process Stack Pointer)**: Used in Thread Mode only (when CONTROL[1] = 1). Used for user application stacks in RTOS environments.
- On reset, the MSP is loaded from the vector table (first word of the vector table), and the PSP is undefined.

**3. Link Register (R14 / LR):**
- Stores the return address after a `BL`/`BLX` instruction.
- On exception entry, LR is loaded with **EXC_RETURN** (e.g., `0xFFFFFFF1` for return to MSP).

**4. Program Counter (R15 / PC):**
- Points to the address of the next instruction to be fetched.
- On ARM, it reads as current instruction + 4; on Thumb, it reads as current instruction + 2.

**5. Program Status Registers (PSR Family):**
- **APSR**: Contains ALU flags (N, Z, C, V, Q).
- **IPSR**: Contains the exception number (0 = Thread mode).
- **EPSR**: Contains the T-bit (Thumb state indicator) and ICI/IT bits.

**6. Exception Mask Registers:**
- **PRIMASK**: 1-bit register; when set to 1, blocks all configurable exceptions.
- **FAULTMASK**: 1-bit register; when set to 1, blocks all exceptions except NMI.
- **BASEPRI**: 8-bit register; blocks all exceptions with priority numerically greater than or equal to its value.

**7. CONTROL Register:**
- Bit 0 (**nPRIV**): 0 = Privileged Thread mode, 1 = Unprivileged Thread mode.
- Bit 1 (**SPSEL**): 0 = MSP active, 1 = PSP active.
- Bit 2 (**FPCA**): FP context active (Cortex-M4F only).

**Role of MSP and PSP (2 Marks detailed):**

| Aspect | MSP | PSP |
|---|---|---|
| Default after reset | Yes | No |
| Used in Handler Mode | Yes (mandatory) | No |
| Used in Thread Mode | Yes (default) | Optional |
| Privilege requirement | None (always available) | Available in unprivileged mode |
| Typical use | Kernel, exceptions, OS | User tasks in RTOS |

**Diagram**: Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the complete register set architecture.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Bit-banding mechanism** *(refer to Section 4.4 for diagram)*.

The **bit-banding** feature maps each individual bit in the 1 MB bit-band region to a full 32-bit word in the 32 MB alias region. This provides atomic bit access.

**Address calculation formula:**
$$
A_{alias} = A_{region\_base} + 0x02000000 + 8 \times (A_{bitword} - A_{region\_base}) + \text{bit position}
$$

**Given:**
- $A_{bitword} = 0x40005300$
- Bit position = 7
- Peripheral bit-band region base: $A_{region\_base} = 0x40000000$

**Step 1: Compute the offset of the bit-band word from the region base.**
$$
\Delta A = 0x40005300 - 0x40000000 = 0x00005300
$$

**Step 2: Multiply the offset by 8 (each 32-bit word expands to 8 bytes in alias).**
$$
8 \times 0x00005300 = 0x00029800
$$

**Step 3: Add the bit position (7) to the scaled offset.**
$$
0x00029800 + 7 = 0x00029807
$$

**Step 4: Add the alias region base offset `0x02000000` to the bit-band base.**
$$
A_{alias} = 0x40000000 + 0x02000000 + 0x00029807
$$

$$
A_{alias} = 0x42029807
$$

**Final Answer:**
$$
\boxed{A_{alias} = 0x42029807}
$$

Writing a `1` to address `0x42029807` will atomically set bit 7 of the word at `0x40005300`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**Q: (a)** Explain the operating modes (Thread Mode and Handler Mode) and privilege levels (Privileged and Unprivileged) in ARM Cortex-M. How is mode switching achieved? **(7 Marks)**
**Q: (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the ARM Cortex-M memory map. What is the significance of the System Control Space at `0xE0000000`? List the important components in it. **(7 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Operating Modes (4 Marks):**

The ARM Cortex-M core operates in two distinct modes:

1. **Thread Mode:**
   - The **normal execution mode** where the main application code runs.
   - Entered automatically on reset.
   - Can be **Privileged** or **Unprivileged** depending on CONTROL[0] (nPRIV bit).
   - Uses either MSP or PSP depending on CONTROL[1] (SPSEL bit).

2. **Handler Mode:**
   - Entered whenever an **exception** (interrupt, fault, system call) occurs.
   - **Always Privileged**.
   - **Always uses MSP** as the stack pointer.
   - Exits via exception return mechanism (using EXC_RETURN value in LR).

**Privilege Levels (2 Marks):**

- **Privileged Access**: Full access to all memory regions and special registers. Software can configure NVIC, change CONTROL, and access bit-band regions.
- **Unprivileged Access**: Restricted access. Cannot access most NVIC registers, cannot change CONTROL (except via SVC), cannot access bit-band alias directly. Used to sandbox user code in RTOS.

**Mode Switching Mechanism (1 Mark):**

| From | To | Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Thread (Privileged) | Thread (Unprivileged) | Software writes `1` to CONTROL[0] |
| Thread (Unprivileged) | Thread (Privileged) | SVC instruction or exception entry then return |
| Thread (any) | Handler | Any exception/interrupt |
| Handler | Thread (any) | Exception return (pop of EXC_RETURN) |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**ARM Cortex-M Memory Map** *(refer to Section 4.3 for diagram)*.

The ARM Cortex-M architecture defines a **4 GB linear address space** divided into well-defined regions:

| Address Range | Region | Size |
|---|---|---|
| `0x00000000`–`0x1FFFFFFF` | Code | 512 MB |
| `0x20000000`–`0x3FFFFFFF` | SRAM | 512 MB |
| `0x40000000`–`0x5FFFFFFF` | Peripherals | 512 MB |
| `0x60000000`–`0x7FFFFFFF` | External RAM | 512 MB |
| `0x80000000`–`0x9FFFFFFF` | External Device | 512 MB |
| `0xA0000000`–`0xDFFFFFFF` | System | 1 GB |
| `0xE0000000`–`0xFFFFFFFF` | System Control Space | 512 MB |

**Significance of System Control Space (0xE0000000) (4 Marks):**

The **System Control Space (SCS)** at `0xE0000000` is a reserved 512 MB region that contains **core peripheral registers** that are implementation-defined but architecturally standardized:

1. **NVIC (Nested Vectored Interrupt Controller)**: Located at `0xE000E000`. Manages all exceptions and interrupts. Contains ISER, ICER, ISPR, ICPR, IABR, IPR registers.

2. **SysTick Timer**: Located at `0xE000E010`. A 24-bit down counter used for OS tick generation.

3. **MPU (Memory Protection Unit)**: Located at `0xE000ED90`. Defines memory regions with access permissions for privilege-based protection.

4. **SCB (System Control Block)**: Located at `0xE000ED00`. Contains configuration and control registers including the Vector Table Offset Register (VTOR), CPUID, and fault status registers.

5. **FPU (Floating Point Unit)** [Cortex-M4F only]: Located at `0xE000EF30`. Contains FPU configuration and status registers.

6. **Debug Components**: Including DWT, FPB, ITM, TPIU for instrumentation and debugging.

**Importance:**
- Provides **standardized access** to core peripherals across vendors (ST, NXP, TI).
- Enables **portable firmware** via CMSIS headers.
- Critical for **RTOS implementation** (SysTick, NVIC, MPU).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing APSR, IPSR, and EPSR**: Many students write that "PSR is a single register" — **incorrect**. It is a **combination of three sub-registers** accessed via MRS/MSR instructions. **Penalty: -2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Wrong bit-band alias offset**: The offset between bit-band region and alias region is **`0x02000000`**, NOT `0x01000000`. Students often write the wrong value. **Penalty: -1 to -2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 8**: In the bit-band alias address formula, students forget to multiply the word offset by 8. The formula is:
>    $$A_{alias} = A_{region\_base} + 0x02000000 + 8 \times (A_{bitword} - A_{region\_base}) + \text{bit\_number}$$
>    **Penalty: -2 marks for forgetting the `8×` factor**.
>
> 4. **Stating that Handler Mode can use PSP**: **WRONG**. Handler Mode **ALWAYS uses MSP**. PSP is only available in Thread Mode. **Penalty: -1 mark**.
>
> 5. **Confusing Cortex-M3 with Cortex-M4**: Cortex-M3 has **no DSP extensions** and **no FPU**. Cortex-M4 has both. **Penalty: -1 mark**.
>
> 6. **Writing addresses in decimal**: KTU expects hexadecimal notation. `0x20000000` is correct; `536870912` is **not acceptable** in KTU board exams. **Penalty: -0.5 mark**.

---

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Core and Programming Model**

- [x] The **ARM Cortex-M core** is a **32-bit RISC** processor with **Thumb-2 instruction set** for code density.
- [x] The **register set** has **13 GP registers (R0–R12)**, plus **SP, LR, PC**.
- [x] **SP (R13)** is banked into **MSP** (always available) and **PSP** (Thread Mode only).
- [x] **LR (R14)** stores return address OR **EXC_RETURN** on exception entry.
- [x] **PC (R15)** points to the next instruction (current + 4 in ARM, + 2 in Thumb).
- [x] **PSR = APSR + IPSR + EPSR** (three sub-registers, not one).
- [x] **APSR flags**: N, Z, C, V, Q. **EPSR** contains the **T-bit** (Thumb indicator).
- [x] **PRIMASK = 1** blocks all exceptions except NMI and HardFault.
- [x] **FAULTMASK = 1** blocks all exceptions except NMI.
- [x] **BASEPRI = N** blocks exceptions with priority $\geq N$.
- [x] **CONTROL register**: Bit 0 (nPRIV), Bit 1 (SPSEL), Bit 2 (FPCA).
- [x] **Thread Mode**: normal execution, can be privileged/unprivileged.
- [x] **Handler Mode**: exception context, always privileged, always uses MSP.
- [x] **Memory map = 4 GB** ($2^{32}$ bytes) divided into architecturally defined regions.
- [x] **Bit-band regions**: SRAM (`0x20000000`) and Peripheral (`0x40000000`).
- [x] **Bit-band alias formula**:
  $$A_{alias} = A_{base} + 0x02000000 + 8 \times (A_{word} - A_{base}) + \text{bit}$$
- [x] **System Control Space** at `0xE0000000` contains NVIC, SysTick, MPU, SCB.
- [x] **NVIC base address**: `0xE000E000`.
- [x] **Stack alignment**: must be **8-byte aligned** on exception entry.
- [x] **EXC_RETURN values**: `0xFFFFFFF1` (return to MSP), `0xFFFFFFFD` (return to PSP).
- [x] **Cortex-M3**: no DSP, no FPU. **Cortex-M4**: has DSP and optional FPU.
- [x] **Interrupt latency**: 12 cycles (deterministic) for Cortex-M3/M4.

Also, **mention the vendor (ST/NXP/TI)** and an example MCU (e.g., STM32F407, LPC1768, TM4C123) wherever possible to show applied knowledge.
